docs.title: Primary vs. Temporary Residence
docs.text:
• Multiple scholars' opinions on determining primary residence
  • Consideration of where most time is spent throughout the year
  • University students' residence classification depends on duration and intention
  • Work-related travel and its impact on residence determination

docs.title: Travel and Prayer Adaptations
docs.text:
• University students living on campus for 4+ years: campus becomes primary residence
  • Coming home for breaks less than 15 days: pray qasr at family home
  • Mail delivery location and work base help determine primary residence
  • Flight delays don't immediately change traveler status if departure intention remains

docs.title: Cultural Events and Islamic Boundaries
docs.text:
• Culture should not override Islamic principles
  • Advise event organizers with wisdom (hikmah) about Islamic guidelines
  • Consider mahram/non-mahram relationships even within family gatherings
  • Separate seating arrangements recommended for mixed family events
  • Gentle guidance preferred over harsh confrontation

docs.title: Making Up Missed Prayers
docs.text:
• No need to specify exact dates when making intention for qada prayers
  • General intention of "making up a missed prayer" is sufficient
  • New Muslims who lived in non-Muslim lands may be excused from pre-conversion makeup prayers
  • Priority should be given to making up prayers immediately to avoid complications

docs.title: Prayer During Illness
docs.text:
• Islam provides flexibility while maintaining the obligation
  • Adapt prayer method to physical ability rather than abandoning prayer
  • Only complete inability to move head absolves from prayer temporarily
  • Recovery periods require making up missed prayers unless specific conditions apply

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session covered detailed rulings about managing prayers when congregational prayer begins while praying individually, proper etiquette for entering the mosque during ongoing prayers, and comprehensive guidance on prostration of forgetfulness (sajda sahw). The discussion included practical scenarios for different prayer types, obligations when joining congregations, and remedying mistakes in prayer through specific procedures.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
• Prioritizing congregational prayer over individual prayer
  • Proper procedures for joining ongoing congregational prayers
  • Detailed rulings on prostration of forgetfulness (sajda sahw)
  • Managing prayer mistakes through specific remedial actions
  • Differences between obligatory (fard) and voluntary (nafl) prayer corrections
  • Maintaining even numbers in prayer units when corrections are needed

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Jamaah Congregation, congregational prayer Sajda Sahw Prostration of forgetfulness Nafl/Nawafil Voluntary prayers Sunnah Mu'akkada Emphasized sunnah prayers Rakah/Rakat Unit(s) of prayer Tashahud Sitting portion of prayer with specific recitations Qada Makeup prayer Makruh Tahrimi Severely disliked (close to forbidden) Wajib Obligatory/necessary Fard Obligatory pillar of prayer Salam Greeting that concludes prayer Ruku Bowing position in prayer Sajda Prostration

docs.title: When Jamaah Begins While Praying Alone
docs.text:
For 4-rakah prayers (Zuhr, Asr, Isha): - If not yet performed first prostration: Break prayer with one salam and join congregation - If completed first prostration but not more: Complete second rakah, say salam (counts as 2 nafl), then join congregation for obligatory prayer - If completed 3 rakahs: Finish all 4 rakahs, then join congregation intending voluntary prayer (except for Asr - no nafl after Asr)

For Fajr sunnah specifically: - Only sunnah prayer allowed while congregation is ongoing - Only pray if reasonably sure you can catch the congregation - If imam is in final tashahud, join directly without sunnah

For 3-rakah prayer (Maghrib): - No joining congregation after individual completion (no 3-rakah nafl exists)

docs.title: Entering Mosque When Prayer Already Started
docs.text:
• Do not pray any sunnah except 2 rakah before Fajr
  • Join congregation immediately for all prayers except Fajr sunnah
  • For Fajr sunnah: Only if confident you can catch congregation before it ends

docs.title: Merit of Congregation
docs.text:
• Thawab (reward) of congregation attained even by catching final tashahud
  • However, person must complete remaining rakahs to fulfill prayer obligation
  • Catching ruku means catching that rakah
  • Must spend at least a moment in ruku with imam to count the rakah

docs.title: Sajda Sahw Requirements
docs.text:
When Required: - Missing or delaying wajib acts due to forgetfulness or inattentiveness - NOT for missing fard acts (requires full prayer repetition) - Must be unintentional mistake

When NOT Required: - If disliked prayer times enter (zawal, sunrise, sunset) - If prayer time expires - If done intentionally (prayer becomes invalid)

Procedure: 1. Complete prayer normally 2. Say first salam (Assalamu alaykum wa rahmatullahi on right) 3. Perform two sajdahs 4. Sit up and recite complete tashahud 5. Complete with both salams

docs.title: Extra Rakah Scenarios
docs.text:
Standing for extra rakah without final sitting: - If no sajda performed yet: Sit immediately and complete - If sajda of extra rakah performed: Prayer becomes invalid, complete in pairs (add 6th for 4-rakah prayers, 4th for 3-rakah), then repeat entire obligatory prayer

For Maghrib specifically: - If extra rakah performed: Add only one more (total 4) to maintain even numbers - Must repeat Maghrib prayer afterward

docs.title: Alternative Views on Sunnah Interruption
docs.text:
• Some scholars allow completing 4 rakahs sunnah regardless of jamaah timing
  • Hanafi position: Break after 2 if jamaah begins, complete remaining 2 after obligatory prayer

docs.title: Sajda Sahw Timing
docs.text:
• Sunnah: After first salam
  • Valid but mildly disliked: Before any salam
  • Difference with other madhabs: Hanafi does salam first, others do sajda then salam

docs.title: Frequent Doubts in Prayer
docs.text: Three categories: 1. Rare doubts: Repeat entire prayer 2. Occasional doubts: Go with stronger assumption 3. Frequent doubts: Assume minimum certain amount, treat each rakah as potentially the last

docs.title: Mosque Etiquette
docs.text:
• If adhan called in mosque, remain until prayer completed (unless valid excuse)
  • After completing individual prayer: May leave except for Zuhr and Isha (should join as nafl)
  • Congregation takes precedence over individual prayer

docs.title: Prayer Management
docs.text:
• When unsure of rakah count: Go with confident assumption or assume minimum if no confidence
  • Hair showing during prayer: Fix when alerted (doesn't break prayer), but repeat if discovered after prayer
  • Breaking wudu during congregational prayer: Cannot make wudu mid-prayer and return

docs.title: Mistake Prevention
docs.text:
• Pay attention during prayer to avoid common errors
  • If mistake made unintentionally, follow sajda sahw procedure
  • If fundamental pillar (fard) missed, entire prayer must be repeated
  • Maintain even numbers when adding extra rakahs due to mistakes

docs.title: Following Imam
docs.text:
• Must catch at least a moment in ruku to count rakah
  • When making up missed rakahs: Will sit for tashahud multiple times (following both imam's pattern and your own completion pattern)
  • If imam makes uncorrected mistake affecting his prayer, entire congregation must repeat

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session covered three major types of prostration and prayer in Islamic jurisprudence: Sujood at-Tilawah (prostration of recital), Salat al-Jumu'ah (Friday prayer), Eid prayers, and special prayers including eclipse and rain prayers. The lesson emphasized the conditions, requirements, and proper methods for performing these acts of worship according to Hanafi fiqh.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
• Mandatory vs. Recommended Prostrations: Different types of sujood have varying levels of obligation
  • Communal Worship Requirements: Specific conditions must be met for congregational prayers to be valid
  • Flexibility in Worship: Islam provides alternatives and accommodations for different circumstances
  • Leadership in Prayer: The role of the imam and community authority in leading worship
  • Timing and Preparation: Proper timing and spiritual preparation are essential for valid worship

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Sujood at-Tilawah Prostration of recital - mandatory prostration when reciting specific Quranic verses Wajib Mandatory/obligatory Takbir Saying "Allahu Akbar" Tahrima Opening takbir of prayer with raised hands Salat al-Jumu'ah Friday congregational prayer Khutbah Sermon Qadi Judge Amir Governor/leader Misl City (for prayer purposes) Sa'i Hastening/rushing Zawwal Midday/zenith Takbirat at-Tashriq Special takbirs recited during Eid al-Adha period Salat al-Kusuf Solar eclipse prayer Salat al-Khusuf Lunar eclipse prayer Salat al-Istisqa Prayer for rain Salat al-Khawf Prayer during times of fear/battle

docs.title: Fiqh Rulings & Evidence
docs.text:
Sujood at-Tilawah: - Mandatory (wajib) when reciting any of 14 specific verses in the Quran - Can be delayed unless recited during prayer - Must be performed immediately within prayer - cannot be delayed until after prayer - Missing it within prayer is sinful - Hearing the verse (not just thinking it) is the condition for obligation

Friday Prayer: - Individual obligation (fard) with specific conditions - Replaces Dhuhr prayer even for those excused from attending - Late joiners who catch even the final tashahud complete it as Jumu'ah, otherwise pray Dhuhr

Eid Prayers: - Mandatory (wajib) upon those for whom Friday prayer is obligatory - Contains 6 extra takbirs (3 in each rak'ah) - Sermon (khutbah) is sunnah and delivered after the prayer

docs.title: Conditions & Categories
docs.text:
Conditions for Friday Prayer Validity: 1. City or its outskirts 2. Head of state or appointed leader 3. Time of Dhuhr 4. Preceding sermon (khutbah) delivered intentionally in Dhuhr time 5. At least one qualified listener for the sermon 6. General permission for all Muslims to attend 7. Congregation of at least 3 men plus the imam until first prostration

Who Must Attend Friday Prayer: - Male - Free - Resident in city/outskirts - Sound health - Safety from oppression - Sound eyesight - Ability to walk

Takbirat at-Tashriq: - According to Imam Abu Hanifa: From Fajr of Arafah until Asr of Nahr (10th Dhul-Hijjah) - According to Abu Yusuf and Muhammad: Until Asr of 13th Dhul-Hijjah - Recited after each obligatory prayer

docs.title: Scholarly Opinions
docs.text:
Language of Prostration Verses: - If heard in Arabic: prostration mandatory regardless of understanding - If heard in non-Arabic language: prostration only mandatory if meaning understood - If personally recited in any language: prostration mandatory regardless of understanding

Minimum Requirements: - Khutbah: One tasbih ("Subhanallah") or tahlil ("La ilaha illallah") suffices - Congregation: Minimum 4 people total (imam + 3 others) until first prostration - Multiple prostration verses in same sitting: Only one prostration required

Differences in Eclipse Prayer: - Solar eclipse: Performed in congregation with imam - Lunar eclipse: Can be performed individually

docs.title: Lessons & Practical Application
docs.text:
Spiritual Preparation: - For Eid al-Fitr: Eat odd number of dates before prayer, pay Zakat al-Fitr - For Eid al-Adha: Delay eating until after prayer - For rain prayer: Give charity, dress humbly, bring entire community including animals

Practical Guidelines: - Use different routes when going to and returning from Eid prayer - Remain silent during Friday sermon from when imam approaches pulpit - Recite takbir silently on way to Eid al-Fitr, loudly for Eid al-Adha - All 14 verses of prostration recited in one sitting with separate prostrations removes all worries (according to Imam Nasafi)

Prayer Accommodations: - During extreme fear: Pray individually while riding, facing any direction - Eclipse prayer: Prolong recitation, bowing, and prostration - Rain prayer: Community should go outside city limits for three days

Leadership Responsibilities: - Head of state should lead Friday prayer or appoint qualified leader - Imam must remain throughout prayer of fear while followers rotate - Community agreement on imam is acceptable when no state authority exists

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session covered the final chapter of the salah section, focusing on the complete funeral prayer (janazah) process. The lesson comprehensively addressed the three main components: ghusl (ritual washing), kafan (shrouding), salat al-janazah (funeral prayer), and burial procedures. The session concluded with an introduction to the chapter on fasting (sawm), including its legal definition and seven types of fasting.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
• Complete funeral procedures as acts of worship and community obligation
  • Proper treatment and dignity of the deceased according to Islamic law
  • The transition from life to death as a reminder of the hereafter
  • Fasting as the next pillar of Islam after completing the salah chapters
  • Community responsibilities and individual roles in Islamic funeral rites

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Janazah Funeral prayer Ghusl Ritual washing of the deceased Kafan Shrouding/burial cloth Muhtadar Person very close to passing away Talqeen Reciting shahada in presence of dying person Qamees Long shirt (part of male shroud) Izar Inner shroud Lifafa Outer shroud Khimar Head covering (female shroud) Khirqah Chest cloth (female shroud) Kafan al-sunnah Recommended shrouding Kafan al-kifaya Minimum sufficient shrouding Kafan al-darura Emergency shrouding Fard al-kifaya Communal obligation Lahad L-shaped grave Shiq Straight-down grave Sidr Lotus tree leaves (for washing) Masajid Places of prostration (for perfume application) Sawm Fasting

docs.title: Fiqh Rulings & Evidence
docs.text:
Pre-Death Procedures: - Direct dying person toward Qibla on right side or with feet toward Qibla - Recite shahada in their presence (talqeen) without commanding them directly - Close eyes and jaws after death with specific dua

Ghusl Requirements: - Perform wudu except rinsing mouth and nose - Three complete washes with warm water and sidr if available - Wash right side first, then left side, then complete body wash - No grooming - no cutting nails, combing hair, or trimming

Kafan (Shrouding) Rules: - Males: 3 cloths (qamees, izar, lifafa) - Females: 5 cloths (same as males plus khimar and khirqah) - White cotton preferred - Right side always on top when wrapping - Hair of females divided into two braids across chest

Salat al-Janazah: - 4 takbirat with specific recitations - After 1st takbir: thana - After 2nd takbir: salawat on Prophet - After 3rd takbir: dua for deceased (different for adults vs children) - After 4th takbir: salam

Burial Procedures: - Lahad (L-shaped) grave preferred over shiq (straight down) - Body placed on right side facing Qibla - Grave depth: minimum half body length - Dirt mounded like camel's hump

docs.title: Conditions & Categories
docs.text:
Who Requires Ghusl: - All deceased Muslims - Martyrs are exceptions (buried in their clothes without washing unless in state of janabah)

Shrouding Categories: 1. Kafan al-sunnah: Optimal shrouding (3 cloths male, 5 cloths female) 2. Kafan al-kifaya: Minimum sufficient (2 cloths + khimar for females) 3. Kafan al-darura: Emergency shrouding (whatever available)

Types of Fasting: 1. Obligatory (fard): Ramadan, makeup days, expiation, vowed fasts 2. Mandatory (wajib): Making up voluntary fasts 3. Sunnah: Day of Ashura (10th Muharram) 4. Recommended (mustahab): 3 days monthly, Mondays/Thursdays, 6 days Shawwal 5. Voluntary (nafl): Any permissible day 6. Prohibitively disliked (makruh tahrimi): Eid days, days of tashriq 7. Mildly disliked (makruh tanzihi): Singling out Saturdays, Persian holidays, consecutive fasting

docs.title: Scholarly Opinions
docs.text:
Talqeen After Burial: - Difference of opinion among Hanafi scholars whether to continue reciting shahada after burial - Authors' position: permissible if one can do it

Martyrdom Definition: - Legal martyrs (for burial purposes): killed by enemy combatants, rebels, highway brigands, robbers, or unjustly killed by Muslim with sharp object - Distinction between legal ruling and spiritual martyrdom (only Allah knows true martyrs) - Time factor: if conscious for entire prayer time after injury, treated as natural death

Grave Visitation: - Recommended to visit graves and recite Quran (especially Surah Yasin) - Disliked to sit at graves except for Quran recitation or dhikr

docs.title: Lessons & Practical Application
docs.text:
Community Responsibilities: - Funeral prayer is fard al-kifaya (communal obligation) - Participation in funeral rites provides significant reminders about hereafter - Proper procedures ensure dignity and respect for deceased

Practical Considerations: - Adjustments may be needed due to autopsy, accidents, or medical conditions - Ghusl still counts even if some steps must be modified due to circumstances - Transportation and burial logistics must maintain respect for deceased

Fasting Preparation: - Understanding different categories helps proper observance - Legal definition: withholding from eating, drinking, intimacy during daylight hours with intention - Must be performed by one who is capable and required to do so

Key Reminders: - Shrouds should not contain Quranic verses or names of Allah (will be disrespected underground) - No grooming of deceased - maintain natural state - Funeral procedures serve as powerful reminders of our temporary nature in this life

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session covered the intention (niyyah) for fasting, types of fasts, moon sighting for determining Ramadan, actions that invalidate fasting, exemptions from fasting, and the basics of spiritual retreat (i'tikaf). The lesson emphasized the critical importance of proper intention and provided detailed categorization of actions affecting the validity of fasting.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
• Proper intention (niyyah) is essential for all fasts and must be made at specific times depending on the type of fast
  • Distinction between specified (muayyan) and unspecified (ghayr muayyan) fasts determines timing requirements for intention
  • Moon sighting follows prophetic tradition with specific witness requirements
  • Actions that invalidate fasting are categorized by their consequences (makeup, expiation, or both)
  • Islamic calendar follows lunar months of either 29 or 30 days
  • Certain individuals have exemptions from fasting with obligation to make up missed days

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Niyyah Intention Muayyan Specified (fasts with specific days/times) Ghayr Muayyan Unspecified (fasts requiring specific intention) Zahwatul Kubra Midpoint of the day Qadha Makeup (of missed fasts) Kaffara Expiation Nadar Vow Yom Shak Day of doubt (30th of Sha'ban) Mufsidat Actions that invalidate the fast Hijama Blood cupping Miswak Toothstick Imsak Withholding/refraining Fidya Compensation payment Suhoor Pre-dawn meal I'tikaf Spiritual retreat Hilal Crescent moon

docs.title: Fiqh Rulings & Evidence
docs.text:
Intention Requirements: - All fasts require proper intention - Specified fasts (muayyan): intention can be made until midpoint of day (zahwatul kubra) - Unspecified fasts (ghayr muayyan): intention must be made between Maghrib of previous day and Fajr of fasting day

Moon Sighting Rules: - Based on Prophetic hadith: "Start your fast by sight of the moon and break your fast by sight of the moon" - If clouds obstruct: complete 30 days of Sha'ban - For beginning of Ramadan with obstruction: one upright witness sufficient (including slave or woman) - For Eid al-Fitr with obstruction: requires two male witnesses OR one male and two females - Without obstruction: large group must sight the moon

Fasting Violations - Four Categories: 1. Require makeup + expiation: eating, drinking, intercourse (when done intentionally) 2. Require makeup only: suppository use, accidentally swallowing water during wudu, coercion 3. Require nothing, not disliked: blood cupping (if not weakening), miswak, mouth/nose rinsing without swallowing 4. Require nothing, but disliked: tasting food without swallowing, showing affection to spouse without security

docs.title: Conditions & Categories
docs.text:
Types of Fasts: - Specified (Muayyan): Ramadan fasts, voluntary fasts for specific days, specified vows - Unspecified (Ghayr Muayyan): makeup fasts, expiation fasts, unspecified vows

Exemptions from Fasting: - Sick person fearing illness will worsen - Pregnant or nursing woman fearing harm to self or baby - Person fearing death from severe thirst - Traveler (though preferable to fast if able)

I'tikaf Categories: - Mandatory (wajib): when vowed - Communal sunnah (kifaya): last ten nights of Ramadan - Recommended: any time in masjid with intention

docs.title: Scholarly Opinions
docs.text:
Forgetfulness vs. Mistake: - Forgetfulness (nisyan): eating/drinking while forgetting one is fasting - no consequences - Mistake (khata'): accidentally swallowing water during wudu - requires makeup only

Voluntary Fast Breaking: - One narration allows breaking voluntary fast without excuse - If broken, mandatory to make up (except on prohibited days: two Eids, days of Tashreeq)

Fidya Payment: - For elderly unable to fast: half sa' of wheat or monetary equivalent per day - Only applicable when person is genuinely unable to fast due to age/permanent condition

docs.title: Lessons & Practical Application
docs.text:
Daily Practice: - Make intention before Fajr for unspecified fasts - Can make intention until midday for Ramadan and voluntary fasts - Eat suhoor close to Fajr time - Break fast immediately when Maghrib enters (unless cloudy day requiring certainty)

When Fast is Broken: - Must practice imsak (withholding) for remainder of day - Applies to: those who broke fast, returning travelers not previously fasting, women becoming pure from menstruation, new Muslims, children reaching puberty

Making Up Fasts: - Need not be consecutive (except for kaffara) - Should not be delayed unnecessarily - Reasonable estimation acceptable for calculating missed days over years

I'tikaf Guidelines: - Men: must be in designated masjid - Women: designated prayer area in home - Can eat, drink, sleep, talk appropriately - Cannot leave except for necessity (bathroom, emergency, Friday prayer) - Avoid idle talk and inappropriate speech

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session covered the fundamental principles of Zakah (obligatory charity) in Islamic jurisprudence, including its definition, conditions, calculation methods, eligible recipients, and prohibitions. The class also covered Sadaqat al-Fitr (charity at the end of Ramadan) and concluded with a brief overview of the five-day Hajj sequence from the 8th to 13th of Dhul-Hijjah.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
• Zakah as an act of worship requiring proper intention (niyyah)
  • Transfer of ownership (tamlik) as the core principle of Zakah
  • Nisab (threshold amount) and its calculation based on lunar year
  • Eight categories of eligible Zakah recipients
  • Prohibited recipients of Zakah including family members and wealthy individuals
  • Sadaqat al-Fitr as mandatory charity for Eid
  • Sequential overview of Hajj rituals and locations

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Zakah Obligatory charity; 2.5% of wealth above nisab Tamlik Transfer of ownership Milk Ownership Nisab Threshold amount for Zakah obligation Faqir Poor person with wealth below nisab Miskin Destitute person with no wealth at all Mukatab Slave working to purchase his freedom Ghani Wealthy person who possesses nisab Hashmite Member of Banu Hashim tribe (Prophet's lineage) Mithqal Weight measurement for gold (~4.374 grams) Dirham Weight measurement for silver Sadaqat al-Fitr Mandatory charity at end of Ramadan Sa' Measurement unit (~7.5 pounds or 4.4 kilograms) Rital Iraqi measurement standard Dhimmi Non-Muslim resident of Muslim lands Mudabbar Slave promised freedom upon master's death

docs.title: Fiqh Rulings & Evidence
docs.text:
Zakah Definition & Calculation: - Zakah is defined as transfer of ownership (tamlik) of specified portion of wealth to eligible person with proper intention - Rate: 2.5% of wealth above nisab after one lunar year - Current nisab thresholds: Gold standard $12,190, Silver standard $1,307 - Liabilities and debts are deducted before calculation - Gold nisab: 20 mithqal (approximately 86.48 grams) - Silver nisab: 200 dirhams

Timing Requirements: - Must pass full lunar year (Hijri calendar) from when nisab was first reached - Zakah calculated on amount possessed at end of year, not beginning - If below nisab at year-end, no Zakah due even if above during year

Sadaqat al-Fitr Rulings: - Becomes mandatory when Fajr begins on Eid al-Fitr day - Amount: Half sa' of wheat/flour OR one sa' of dates/raisins/barley - Monetary equivalent permissible and often superior in times of ease - Must be paid on behalf of self, minor children, and personal slaves

docs.title: Conditions & Categories
docs.text:
Zakah Obligation Conditions: - Free Muslim who is legally responsible (adult, sane) - Possesses nisab amount - Wealth held for full lunar year - Above basic personal needs and debts

Trade Goods & Assets: - Value of trade goods combined with gold/silver by value - Includes stocks, business assets, anything not for personal use - No Zakah on personal residence, car for personal use, etc.

Sadaqat al-Fitr Conditions: - Mandatory on behalf of: self, minor children, personal slaves - Not mandatory for: adult children, wife/husband, working slaves, co-owned slaves

docs.title: Scholarly Opinions
docs.text:
Nisab Calculation: - Question raised about gold vs. silver standard - requires consultation with qualified scholar - Imam Tahawi's position: Hashmites are eligible recipients (minority view) - Disagreement on splitting Sadaqat al-Fitr between multiple recipients - Hanafi jurisprudence differences on extra amounts above nisab

Geographic Distribution: - Preference for local distribution unless special circumstances - Acceptable to send elsewhere for relatives, more religious recipients, or greater benefit to Muslim community

docs.title: Lessons & Practical Application
docs.text:
Practical Zakah Guidance: - Complex business situations require consultation with qualified Mufti - Due diligence protects payer if recipient later found ineligible - Should not make recipient wealthy enough to owe Zakah themselves - Prefer giving enough so recipient doesn't need to ask for food

Distribution Priorities: 1. Eligible relatives (siblings, cousins - not parents/children/spouse) 2. Neighbors 3. Local district 4. Professional colleagues 5. Fellow citizens

Modern Considerations: - Credit card debt, home financing, car loans counted as liabilities - Multiple income streams require careful calculation - Stocks, business assets included in Zakah calculation - Monetary payment often more practical than goods for Sadaqat al-Fitr

Eight Eligible Recipients: 1. Faqir (poor with some wealth below nisab) 2. Miskin (destitute with no wealth) 3. Mukatab (slave buying freedom) 4. Person in debt 5. Soldier cut off from troop 6. Pilgrim separated from group 7. Wayfarer without sufficient funds 8. Zakah collector

Prohibited Recipients: - Non-Muslims, wealthy individuals (ghani) - Hashmites and their freed servants - Direct family: parents, grandparents, children, grandchildren, spouse - One's servants and slaves - For funeral expenses or deceased person's debts

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session introduced the chapter on Hajj, covering the fundamental definition, requirements, and detailed procedures of the pilgrimage. The class focused on the three types of Hajj (Qiran, Tamattu', and Ifrad), the specific conditions required for obligation, and the step-by-step process of performing the Hajj rites from entering Ihram through the farewell Tawaf.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
• Hajj as a journey requiring specific place (Makkah), state (Ihram), and time (days of Dhul Hijjah)
  • The urgency of performing Hajj when able - it is Fard and equally obligatory as Salah
  • Three distinct types of Hajj with different procedures
  • Detailed prohibitions and requirements while in the state of Ihram
  • The importance of avoiding harm to others during pilgrimage rites
  • Gender-specific differences in Hajj procedures

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Ihram State of pilgrim sanctity Miqat Designated boundaries for entering Ihram Qiran Hajj type: Umrah and Hajj performed in one Ihram Tamattu' Hajj type: Umrah and Hajj performed in two separate Ihrams Ifrad Hajj type: Hajj only, no Umrah (for local residents) Talbiya "Labbayka Allahumma Labbayk..." - pilgrim's response to Allah I'tiba Placing upper garment under right armpit, exposing right shoulder Ramal Walking briskly with gentle shoulder movement (first 3 rounds of Tawaf) Hatim Semicircular enclosure that is part of the Kaaba Sa'i Walking/running between Safa and Marwa (7 rounds) Milayn Akhdarayn The two green lines where pilgrims rush during Sa'i Wuquf Standing at Arafah (most significant pillar of Hajj) Jamarat Stone pillars for stoning ritual Tawaf al-Qudum Welcome Tawaf upon arrival Tawaf al-Ziyarah Obligatory Tawaf of visitation Tawaf al-Wida Farewell Tawaf

docs.title: Fiqh Rulings & Evidence
docs.text:
Obligation of Hajj: - Fard once in a lifetime when able - Equal in obligation to daily Salah - neglecting it when able is like neglecting Fajr or Dhuhr - Must be performed "al-fawr" (immediately/as soon as possible) when conditions are met

Essential Elements (Arkan): 1. Standing at Arafah (Wuquf) - most crucial pillar 2. Tawaf al-Ziyarah - second pillar

Prayer Combinations During Hajj: - In Arafah: Dhuhr and Asr combined in Dhuhr time (one Adhan, two Iqamahs) - In Muzdalifah: Maghrib and Isha combined in Isha time (one Adhan, one Iqamah) - Only valid when praying with the main Imam according to Imam Abu Hanifa - Imam Abu Yusuf and Muhammad allow combination even when praying alone

docs.title: Conditions & Categories
docs.text:
Conditions for Hajj Obligation: 1. Free (not enslaved) 2. Legally responsible (Mukallaf) 3. Able-bodied and healthy 4. Financial ability for provisions and transportation 5. Excess wealth beyond basic necessities and family support 6. Safe passage to Makkah 7. For women: not in Iddah, accompanied by husband or trustworthy Mahram

Prohibitions in Ihram: 1. Intercourse or speaking about it inappropriately (Rafath) 2. Vulgar speech (Fusuq) 3. Argumentation (Jidal) 4. Acts of disobedience 5. Killing or indicating land game to hunters 6. Wearing stitched garments (men) 7. Covering head (men) or face (both genders) 8. Applying scent to body or clothes 9. Shaving or plucking hair

Types of Hajj: 1. Qiran: Umrah and Hajj in one continuous Ihram 2. Tamattu': Umrah first, exit Ihram, then re-enter for Hajj 3. Ifrad: Hajj only (for Makkah residents)

docs.title: Scholarly Opinions
docs.text:
Prayer Combination Differences: - Imam Abu Hanifa: Combination only valid with main Imam and in Ihram state - Imam Abu Yusuf & Muhammad: Combination allowed even when praying alone (stronger opinion)

Touching the Black Stone: - Permissible only if done without harm to others - Caution needed due to fragrance (Itr) applied to it - touching would violate Ihram prohibitions - Can perform Istilam (greeting) from distance if crowded

docs.title: Lessons & Practical Application
docs.text:
Daily Schedule of Hajj: - 8th Dhul Hijjah: Depart to Mina after Fajr, stay entire day - 9th Dhul Hijjah: To Arafah, perform Wuquf until sunset, then to Muzdalifah - 10th Dhul Hijjah: Fajr in Muzdalifah, collect 70 pebbles, return to Mina, stone largest Jamara (7 stones), sacrifice animal, shave head, perform Tawaf al-Ziyarah - 11th-12th Dhul Hijjah: Stone all three Jamarat (21 stones each day) after midday - 13th Dhul Hijjah (optional): Stone all three Jamarat, can be done after true dawn

Practical Guidance: - Collect 70 pebbles total in Muzdalifah - Stop reciting Talbiya after throwing first stone on 10th - Everything becomes halal after head shaving except spousal relations - Spousal relations become permissible after Tawaf al-Ziyarah - Can leave after 12th stoning, but if staying past sunset of 12th until Fajr of 13th, must complete 13th day stoning

Gender Differences: - Women don't uncover heads, use face covering with distance from face - Women recite Talbiya quietly to themselves - Women don't perform Ramal or rush between green lines - Women trim hair instead of shaving - Women may wear stitched garments - Women should avoid crowded areas around Black Stone

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session covered the essential rulings of Umrah (lesser pilgrimage), expiation for Hajj/Umrah violations, hunting regulations during Ihram, Udhiyah (sacrifice) requirements, and general slaughtering principles. The class emphasized practical applications of these rulings with detailed discussions on violation penalties and proper procedures for religious observances.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
• Umrah Performance: Requirements, timing restrictions, and procedures
  • Expiation System: Different levels of penalties for Ihram violations
  • Sacred Boundaries: Respect for the Haram and its regulations
  • Animal Sacrifice: Religious and practical aspects of Udhiyah and general slaughtering
  • Hunting Laws: Permissible and impermissible practices during pilgrimage

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Umrah The lesser pilgrimage Makroo Tahrimi Prohibitively disliked Ihram State of pilgrim sanctity Tawaf Circumambulation around the Kaaba Sa'i Walking between Safa and Marwah Nisab Threshold amount for Zakat Wuquf Standing at Arafah Udhiyah Sacrifice on Eid al-Adha Dhabh Horizontal slaughtering method Nahr Vertical stabbing method (for camels) Basmala/Tasmiyyah Saying "Bismillah" before slaughter Thani Mature animal suitable for sacrifice Ittirari Emergency slaughtering of wounded game

docs.title: Fiqh Rulings & Evidence
docs.text:
Umrah Timing: - Valid throughout the year except five days (9th-13th Dhul Hijjah) - Prohibitively disliked (Makroo Tahrimi) during Hajj days - Best day is Arafah falling on Friday (worth more than 70 Hajj)

Umrah Components: - Tawaf: Seven rounds around Kaaba - Two Rakat after Tawaf - Sa'i: Seven rounds between Safa and Marwah - Shaving/trimming hair to exit Ihram

Zakat Nisab: - Measured by gold (~$9,400) or silver (~$700) standards - Standards apply universally regardless of local currency value

Slaughtering Requirements: - Must cut minimum 3 out of 4 vessels: trachea, esophagus, two carotid arteries - Basmala required unless forgotten unintentionally - Slaughterer must be Muslim, Christian, or Jew (not Majin, apostate, or idol worshiper)

docs.title: Conditions & Categories
docs.text:
Ihram Violations Requiring Sheep Sacrifice: - Applying scent to entire limb - Wearing stitched garment for full day/night (men) - Covering quarter of head for full day/night (men) - Shaving quarter of head/beard - Clipping all nails in one sitting - Missing entire day of stoning

Lesser Violations (Half Sa' of wheat): - Applying scent to less than entire limb - Wearing stitched garment for less than full day - Performing Tawaf in state of impurity

Animal Age Requirements for Sacrifice: - Camel: 5 years (Thani) - Cow: 2 years - Goat: 1 year - Sheep: 6 months (if appears mature)

Prohibited Animals for Sacrifice: - One-eyed or blind animals - Emaciated animals - Missing ears or most of tail - Cannot walk to slaughter place - Missing most teeth

docs.title: Scholarly Opinions
docs.text:
Hanafi Position on Nisab: - Difference of opinion exists regarding silver vs. gold nisab standards - Generally follows whichever is more beneficial to the poor

Imam Abu Yusuf vs. Imam Muhammad: - Abu Yusuf: Animal needs most of day's life remaining after injury - Muhammad: Any sign of life sufficient for slaughter validity

Horse Meat Ruling: - Imam Abu Hanifa: Prohibitively disliked - Two Companions: Mildly disliked

docs.title: Lessons & Practical Application
docs.text:
Umrah Renewal Process: - Travel to Masjid Aisha (main Miqat for residents/visitors) - Perform two Rakat, make intention - Return in Ihram state for new Umrah

Practical Slaughtering Guidelines: - Sharpen blade beforehand, away from animal's sight - Look for two blood streams and visible esophagus opening - Cover animal's eyes with ears for comfort - Avoid vertical cutting for cattle/sheep

Exam Preparation Advice: - Review daily, divide chapters manageable portions - Practice with someone testing you - Recite "Alam Nashrah" before exam - When stuck, recite Durud to jog memory

Precautionary Measures: - Set aside money for unintentional Ihram violations - Give to workers in Haram as expiation - Better to seek forgiveness than risk incomplete observance

docs.title: FQH 102 (Spring 2026)
docs.text:
Fiqh 102

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: Introduction to Fiqh al-Muyassir (Fiqh Made Easy), transitioning from previous semester's foundational topics (purification, prayer, zakah, fasting, hajj) to more advanced Arabic-based study. Focus on Chapter 1: Kitab al-Tahara (The Chapter of Purification) and types of water suitable for purification. Emphasis on applying Arabic language skills learned in previous semester while studying Islamic jurisprudence.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
• Purification as the foundation of all worship
  • Two fundamental categories of purification: ritual (hukmi) and physical (haqiqi)
  • Water as the primary agent of purification
  • Integration of Arabic language learning with fiqh study
  • Progressive difficulty in Islamic education while maintaining same subject matter

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning طَهَارَة (Tahara) Purification/cleanliness نَجَاسَة (Najasa) Physical filth/impurity حَدَث (Hadath) Ritual impurity (intangible) طَهَارَة حُكْمِيَّة (Tahara Hukmiyyah) De jure purification (from ritual impurity) طَهَارَة حَقِيقِيَّة (Tahara Haqiqiyyah) De facto purification (from physical filth) مَاء مُطْلَق (Ma'ul Mutlaq) Absolute water أَسَاس (Asas) Foundation مِفْتَاح (Miftah) Key تَحْصُلُ (Tahsulu) Is attained/obtained إِزَالَة (Izala) Removal وَسَائِل طَهَارَة (Wasail Tahara) Means/agents of purification دَبْغ (Dabgh) Animal hide tanning

docs.title: Fiqh Rulings & Evidence
docs.text:
Quranic Evidence: - "Indeed, Allah loves those who repent and those who keep themselves pure" (2:222)

Prophetic Evidence: - "Cleanliness is half of faith" (Sahih Muslim) - "The key to paradise is salah, and the key to salah is purification" (Ahmad)

Core Ruling: - Purification is the foundation of worship - Prayer (salah) is not valid without purification - Two methods exist for removing ritual impurity: wudu, ghusl, or tayammum (when water unavailable) - Physical impurities removed through washing with purifying agents

docs.title: Conditions & Categories
docs.text:
Two Categories of Purification:

  1. Tahara min al-Hadath (Purification from Ritual Impurity)
  2. Also called Tahara Hukmiyyah (De jure purification)
  3. Intangible state of impurity
  4. Examples: Breaking wudu, state of janaba

  5. Methods: Wudu, ghusl, or tayammum when water unavailable

  6. Tahara min al-Najasa (Purification from Physical Filth)

  7. Also called Tahara Haqiqiyyah (De facto purification)
  8. Tangible physical impurities
  9. Examples: Blood, urine, excrement on body/clothes
  10. Method: Physical removal using purifying agents

Types of Absolute Water (Ma'ul Mutlaq): - Rainwater (ma'ul samah) - River water (ma'ul nahar) - Well water (ma'ul bi'r) - Spring/fountain water (ma'ul ain) - Sea water (ma'ul bahr) - Melted snow water (ma'ul dhaba min al-thalj) - Melted hail water (ma'ul dhaba min al-barad)

docs.title: Scholarly Opinions
docs.text: Not covered in this session.

docs.title: Lessons & Practical Application
docs.text:
Educational Approach: - Progressive learning: same subjects with increasing complexity each year - Integration of Arabic language skills with Islamic jurisprudence - Importance of reading ahead and preparing for classes

Practical Purification: - Both types of purification may be needed simultaneously (e.g., using restroom requires both istinja for physical cleanliness and wudu for ritual purity) - Sometimes only one type needed (e.g., changing baby's diaper breaks physical purity but not wudu) - Water must retain original characteristics to be valid for purification - Alternative methods (tayammum) available when water unavailable or inaccessible

Study Method: - Emphasis on understanding Arabic terminology alongside English meanings - Application of sentence structure (tarkeeb) skills - Regular review and preparation expected for effective learning

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session continued the study of Tahara (purification) by covering the five categories of water and their rulings (ahkam). The lesson focused on understanding different types of water that can or cannot be used for purification, building upon previous lessons on the introduction to purification and types of water through which purity can be obtained.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
• Classification of water based on purification capabilities
  • Understanding the difference between pure (tahir) and purifying (mutahir) water
  • Degrees of dislike (makruh) in using certain types of water
  • Practical applications of water rulings in different circumstances
  • The concept of used water (ma'ul musta'mal) and its implications

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning أقسام المياه Types/categories of water أحكامها Their rulings طاهر Pure مطهر Purifying ماء مطلق Absolute water مكروه تنزيهي Mildly disliked مكروه تحريمي Prohibitively/majorly disliked هرة Cat دجاج Chicken سباع الطير Carnivorous birds حية Snake حمار Donkey بغل Mule ماء مستعمل Used water متوضئ One who has wudu محدث One who needs wudu (impure person) حدث Ritual impurity (intangible) قربة For reward تحصيل البرودة To obtain coolness/cool down راكد Stagnant نجاسة Impurity (tangible filth)

docs.title: Category 1: Pure, Purifying, Not Disliked
docs.text:
• Water type: Absolute water (ma'ul mutlaq)
  • Ruling: Pure (tahir), purifying (mutahir), not disliked (ghayr makruh)
  • Usage: Can be used for all purification purposes without any dislike

docs.title: Category 2: Pure, Purifying, but Disliked
docs.text:
• Water type: Water drunk from by cats, chickens, carnivorous birds, or snakes
  • Ruling: Pure and purifying but mildly disliked (makruh tanzihi) to use
  • Condition: Only disliked if absolute water is available as an alternative
  • Exception: No dislike if no other water is found

docs.title: Category 3: Pure but Doubtful in Purification
docs.text:
• Water type: Water drunk from by donkeys or mules
  • Ruling: Pure without doubt, but doubt exists whether it's purifying
  • Solution: If no other water available, person has option (khiyar) to perform either wudu first then tayammum, or tayammum first then wudu

docs.title: Category 4: Pure but Not Purifying
docs.text:
• Water type: Used water (ma'ul musta'mal)
  • Ruling: Pure in itself but cannot purify others; wudu and ghusl invalid with this water
  • Definition: Water used for wudu or ghusl either to remove ritual impurity (raf' hadath) or for reward (qurbah)

docs.title: Category 5: Impure Water
docs.text:
• Water type: Small amount of stagnant water mixed with impurity
  • Ruling: Impure (najis)
  • Scope: Whether the impurity is apparent in the water or not

docs.title: Used Water (Ma'ul Musta'mal) Conditions:
docs.text:
Water becomes used (musta'mal) when: 1. Used for wudu or ghusl to remove ritual impurity 2. Used for wudu upon wudu with intention of reward 3. Water separates from the body (fasala 'an jasad) - not when it touches, but when it drips off

Water does NOT become used when: - Person with wudu uses water to cool down or teach wudu - But if person without wudu uses water to cool down or teach, it becomes musta'mal

docs.title: Levels of Dislike:
docs.text:
• Makruh Tanzihi: Mildly disliked (can still be used)
  • Makruh Tahrimi: Prohibitively disliked (should be avoided)

docs.title: Scholarly Opinions
docs.text: The text follows the Hanafi madhab perspective on water categorization. The lesson mentioned that all four madhabs agree on the Hanafi position regarding Witr prayer, indicating scholarly consensus on certain rulings while acknowledging madhab-specific differences in others.

docs.title: Key Practical Points:
docs.text:
1. Hierarchy of water usage: Use absolute water when available; resort to other categories only when necessary
  2. Emergency situations: In absence of pure water, specific procedures (wudu + tayammum) provide valid alternatives
  3. Teaching and cooling: Intentions matter in determining whether water becomes used or remains usable
  4. Water conservation: Understanding when water can be reused helps in resource management during purification

docs.title: Modern Applications:
docs.text:
• Understanding these categories helps in travel situations or areas with limited water access
  • Provides framework for dealing with uncertainty about water purity
  • Guides proper water usage and conservation in Islamic contexts
  • Clarifies when alternatives like tayammum become necessary

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session continued the detailed discussion of the five categories of water for purification purposes, focusing primarily on category five (najis/impure water) and related rulings. The class covered definitions, conditions for water purity/impurity, measurements for classifying water bodies as large (kathir) or small (qalil), and various scenarios involving water mixed with pure substances.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
• Classification of najis (impure) water and its legal rulings
  • Distinction between large (ma'ul kathir) and small (ma'ul qalil) water bodies
  • Effects of mixing pure substances with water
  • Conditions that affect water's validity for purification
  • Natural changes in water due to environmental factors

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning najis impure, filthy qalil small amount kathir large amount, abundant rakid stagnant jari flowing hawd reservoir, basin, pond ma'ul kathir large body of water ma'ul qalil small body of water atharu'n-najasah traces of impurity dhira' cubit (measurement) riqqa thinness sayalan fluidity lawn color ta'am taste ra'iha smell hashaish algae awraq tree leaves

docs.title: Category Five - Najis Water (الْقِسْمُ الْخَامِس - نَجِس)
docs.text:
Definition: Small amount of stagnant water mixed with impurity, whether traces of najasah are apparent or not.

General Rule for Visible Impurity: When traces of najasah become apparent in water, the water becomes impure regardless of: - Amount (little or abundant) - Movement (stagnant or flowing)

Legal Ruling: - The water is impure (najis) - Cannot be used to obtain purity (la tahsulu bihi tahara) - Makes anything it touches impure - If mixed with other substances, renders those substances najis as well

docs.title: Water from Trees and Fruits
docs.text: Ruling: Wudu is not valid with water extracted from trees or fruits, whether: - It flows out naturally (bi nafsihi min ghairi 'asr) - Extracted through squeezing ('asr)

docs.title: Water That Has Lost Its Natural Qualities
docs.text: Ruling: Water used in cooking (tabakh) or made into beverages (ashribah) like soup (maraq) cannot be used for purification as it has lost its natural characteristics.

docs.title: Classification of Ma'ul Kathir (Large Body of Water)
docs.text:
Physical Test: Water in a large reservoir where one corner/side does not move when the other corner/side is disturbed.

Measurements: - Length: 10 cubits (ashru dhira') - Width: 10 cubits - Depth: Sufficient that the ground beneath doesn't become exposed when water is taken by hand

docs.title: Ma'ul Qalil (Small Body of Water)
docs.text: Definition: Any water less than the ma'ul kathir specifications above.

docs.title: Pure Substances Mixed with Water
docs.text:
Case 1 - Non-Overpowering Mixture: - Pure substances (e.g., soap, saffron) mixed with water - The pure substance does not overpower (ghalib) the water - Ruling: Water remains pure and can be used for purification

Case 2 - Overpowering Mixture: - Pure substance overwhelms water, removing its thinness (riqqa) and fluidity (sayalan) - Ruling: Water remains pure but cannot be used for wudu

docs.title: Natural Changes in Water
docs.text:
Environmental Changes: - Color (lawn), taste (ta'am), and smell (ra'iha) changes due to prolonged sitting - Ruling: Water remains pure and usable for purification

Common Natural Contaminants: - Things water is typically exposed to: algae (hashaish), tree leaves (awraq), fallen fruits - Ruling: Water remains pure and purification can be attained with it

docs.title: Scholarly Opinions
docs.text: The text presents the standard Hanafi position on water classification and purification. The detailed measurements for ma'ul kathir (10x10 cubits with adequate depth) represent established fiqh criteria for distinguishing between large and small water bodies, which affects the ruling when impurities are present.

docs.title: Lessons & Practical Application
docs.text:
1. Water Assessment: Muslims must be able to distinguish between different types of water bodies to make correct purification decisions.

  2. Practical Measurements: The 10x10 cubit rule provides a clear standard for determining when water can be considered "large enough" to potentially remain usable even with some impurity.

  3. Caution with Impurities: Any known impurity in small water bodies renders the entire amount unusable, emphasizing the importance of maintaining clean water sources.

  4. Natural vs. Artificial Changes: Water naturally changed by environmental factors remains usable, but water artificially altered (cooking, beverages) loses its purifying quality.

  5. Proportionality Principle: The ruling depends on whether pure additives overwhelm the water's natural properties, teaching balance in practical applications.

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session covered the rulings of water mixed with pure substances and the classifications of leftover water (su'r) from different animals. The discussion focused on determining when water remains valid for wudu based on the qualities (taste, color, smell) that are changed, and the specific rulings for water left over after various creatures have drunk from it.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
• Water Purity Classifications: Understanding when mixed water can still be used for purification vs. wudu
  • Quality-Based Rulings: How the number of changed characteristics (taste, color, smell) determines water's usability
  • Weight Determination: Using weight ratios to determine validity when qualities aren't distinguishable
  • Animal-Based Su'r Categories: Four distinct categories of leftover water based on the animal that drank from it
  • Practical Applications: Real-world scenarios involving detergent, milk, vinegar, and rose water

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning مَاءٌ مَائِعٌ Flowing liquid وَصْفٌ (أَوْصَافٌ) Quality/characteristic (qualities) مَغْلُوبٌ Overwhelmed لَبَنٌ Milk خَلٌّ Vinegar مَاءُ الْمُسْتَعْمَلُ Used water مَاءُ الْوَرْدُ Rose water سُؤْرٌ Leftover water نَجَاسَةٌ Impurity كَرَاهَةٌ Disliking/reprehensibility مَكْرُوهٌ تَنْزِيهًا Mildly disliked شَكٌّ Doubt بَغْلٌ Mule حِمَارٌ Donkey خِنْزِيرٌ Pig كَلْبٌ Dog سِبَاعُ الطَّيْرِ Predatory/carnivorous birds سَبُعٌ Predatory animal

docs.title: Mixed Water Classifications:
docs.text:
Two-Quality Substances (like milk): - If one quality (color, taste) changes → water invalid for wudu - Example: Milk has color and taste but no smell

Three-Quality Substances (like vinegar): - If two out of three qualities change → water invalid for wudu - Vinegar has color, taste, and smell

No-Quality Substances (like used water): - Determination by weight ratio (تُعْتَبَرُ الْغَلَبَةُ بِالْوَزْنِ) - If pure water outweighs impure → valid for wudu - If impure water outweighs pure → invalid for wudu

Used Water Status: - Pure but cannot be reused for wudu/ghusl - Can still remove najasa from objects - تُزَالُ بِهِ النَّجَاسَةُ وَلَكِنْ لَا تَحْصُلُ طَهَارَةٌ بِهِ الْحُكْمِيَّةُ

docs.title: Four Categories of Su'r (Leftover Water):
docs.text:
Category 1 - Pure and Permissible: - Human beings (Muslim or kafir, pure or junub) - if no najasa in mouth - Horses - without any karaha - Animals whose meat is permissible (camel, cow, goat)

Category 2 - Pure but Makruh Tanzihi: - Cats (if no najasa in mouth and other water available) - Predatory birds (falcon, eagle) - House-dwelling animals (mice)

Category 3 - Doubtful Purifying Ability: - Mules and donkeys - Pure water but doubt about validity for wudu - If no other water: perform wudu + tayammum, then pray

Category 4 - Impure (Najis): - Pigs - Dogs - All predatory land animals (lions, leopards, wolves) - Cannot be used for any purification

docs.title: Scholarly Opinions
docs.text:
The text presents the Hanafi position on these matters without extensive discussion of other madhabs. The rulings appear to be established scholarly consensus within the Hanafi school, particularly regarding:

  • The weight-based determination for substances without distinguishable qualities
  • The four-category classification system for animal leftover water
  • The principle that animals whose su'r is pure also have pure sweat, and vice versa

docs.title: Lessons & Practical Application
docs.text:
Daily Life Applications: - Detergent mixed with water: can clean clothes but not valid for wudu due to overwhelming qualities - Milk spilled in water: if taste changes, cannot use for wudu - Old rose water: if scent gone, weight determines usability

Travel Considerations: - When only questionable water available (mule/donkey leftover): combine wudu + tayammum - Prioritize finding pure water sources when possible - Understanding animal categories helps in rural/camping situations

Ramadan Preparation Message: - Consistency over quantity in worship (أَحَبُّ الْأَعْمَالِ إِلَى اللَّهِ أَدْوَمُهَا وَإِنْ قَلَّ) - Suggested Quran reading: 4 pages after each salah = 1 juz/day - Importance of tahajjud, avoiding idle talk during fasting - Making dua for others brings angelic intercession

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session covers the rulings of well water (أَحْكَامُ مِيَاهِ الْآبَارِ), focusing on what happens when various types of impurities or animals fall into wells. The discussion establishes clear guidelines for determining when well water becomes impure and the specific purification procedures required for different scenarios.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
• Classification of animals by their inherent purity status (نَجِسُ الْعَيْنِ vs. pure animals)
  • Connection to previous chapter on leftover water (سُؤْر) rulings
  • Practical purification procedures using bucket counts when complete water removal is impossible
  • Timeline considerations when contamination discovery date is unknown
  • Distinction between animals with flowing blood vs. insects/small creatures

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning أَحْكَامُ Rulings مِيَاهِ الْآبَارِ Well waters نَجَاسَة Impurity نَجِسُ الْعَيْنِ Inherently impure خِنزِير Pig/swine سُؤْر Leftover water (after drinking) لُعَاب Saliva دَلْو Bucket إِنْتِفَاخ Bloating (of dead animal) بَغْل Mule حِمَار Donkey صَقْر Falcon حِدَأَة Eagle هِرَّة Cat دَجَاج Chicken عُصْفُور Sparrow فَأْرَة Mouse رِشَاء Rope رَوْث Horse dung بَعْر Camel dung خَطَى Cow/buffalo dung

docs.title: When Pure Impurity Falls in Wells
docs.text: Ruling: If any amount of impurity (نَجَاسَة) falls into a well, even a drop of blood or wine, all water must be removed from the well.

docs.title: Animals that are Najis al-Ayn (Inherently Impure)
docs.text: Ruling: If an animal that is نَجِسُ الْعَيْنِ (like pig) falls into a well, all water must be removed regardless of: - Whether the animal died or came out alive - Whether its mouth touched the water or not

docs.title: Animals with Impure Saliva but Pure Bodies
docs.text: Ruling: If an animal falls in that is not inherently impure but whose leftover water (سُؤْر) is impure (like dogs, predatory animals), all water must be removed.

docs.title: Pure Animals and Humans
docs.text: Ruling: If a human or pure animal (mule, donkey, falcon, eagle) falls in and: - Comes out alive - Has no impurity on their body - Their mouth/beak doesn't touch the water Then the water remains pure.

docs.title: The Principle of Leftover Water Connection
docs.text: Key Principle: "وَإِذَا وَصَلَ لُعَابُ الْوَاقِعِ فِي الْمَاءِ فَهُوَ فِي حُكْمِ سُؤْرِهِ" If the saliva of a fallen creature reaches the water, then the water follows the ruling of that animal's leftover water.

docs.title: Animals Without Flowing Blood
docs.text: Examples: Bugs, flies, wasps, scorpions, honeybees, spiders, mosquitoes Ruling: Do not render well water impure when they die in it

docs.title: Aquatic Animals
docs.text: Examples: Fish, frogs, crabs - animals born and living in water Ruling: Do not render well water impure when they die in it

docs.title: Large Animals That Die in Wells
docs.text: Examples: Dogs, goats, humans Ruling: Water becomes impure and all water must be removed, but only if removed immediately before bloating occurs

docs.title: Practical Bucket Guidelines (When Complete Removal Impossible)
docs.text:
• General cases requiring full water removal: 200 average-sized buckets
  • Medium animals (cat, chicken) that died: 40 buckets
  • Small animals (sparrow, mouse) that died: 20 buckets

docs.title: Animal Dung Categories
docs.text: Pure dung (doesn't render well impure): Horse, camel, cow dung - unless amount is excessive Small bird droppings: Pigeon, sparrow droppings don't render well impure

docs.title: Scholarly Opinions
docs.text: The text presents the Hanafi position consistently without mentioning alternative scholarly opinions in this session.

docs.title: Timeline Rulings for Unknown Contamination
docs.text:
1. If animal found bloated with unknown entry time: Well considered impure for last 3 days and 3 nights
  2. If dead animal found before bloating with unknown entry time: Well considered impure for last 1 day and 1 night
  3. Consequence: Any prayers performed with wudu from that water must be repeated, clothes washed, body cleaned

docs.title: Purification of Equipment and Person
docs.text: After removing contaminated water, the rope, bucket, and hands of the person doing the removal automatically become pure - no separate washing required.

docs.title: Connection to Modern Applications
docs.text:
• Farmers dealing with harmful animals (boars, coyotes) may hunt them for crop protection
  • Payment for such services is permissible as it removes harm
  • Animal hides may be used if properly slaughtered and tanned
  • Forgetting to say "Bismillah" when hunting is excused if unintentional

docs.title: Practical Decision Framework
docs.text: When any creature falls in well water: 1. Identify the creature type and purity status 2. Determine if mouth/saliva contacted water 3. Check if creature came out alive or died 4. Apply appropriate purification procedure 5. Consider timeline factors if discovery timing unknown

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session covered آداب قضاء الحاجة (etiquettes of using the restroom). The class began with a hadith where Prophet Muhammad ﷺ compared himself to a father figure when teaching sensitive matters to his companions. The session systematically covered 22 specific etiquettes and rulings related to relieving oneself, emphasizing privacy, cleanliness, respect for sacred directions, and avoiding harm to oneself and others. The class concluded with a discussion on istibra (post-urination cleansing for males).

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
• Privacy and Modesty: Going away from people's sight and covering oneself appropriately
  • Cleanliness and Purity: Proper positioning, avoiding contamination, and post-relief cleansing
  • Respect for Sacred Space: Avoiding facing the qibla and not defiling places of worship or gathering
  • Consideration for Others: Not using areas where people benefit or gather
  • Health and Safety: Avoiding positions or places that could cause harm or splash-back

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning آداب Etiquettes, mannerisms قضاء الحاجة Fulfilling need (relieving oneself) الغائط To defecate, restroom استنجا Cleansing after using restroom بيت الخلاء Restroom أعوذ بالله من الخبث والخبائث "I seek refuge in Allah from male and female jinns" الصحراء Desert, open area استقبال Facing one's front toward استدبار Facing one's back toward مكروه تحريماً Prohibitively disliked المغتسل Place where ghusl is performed استبراء Post-urination cleansing to remove remaining impurity محل Organ, body part يغلب على ظنه Until one is certain

docs.title: Fiqh Rulings & Evidence
docs.text:
Primary Hadith: "إِنَّمَا أَنَا لَكُمْ بِمَنْزِلَةِ الْوَالِدِ أُعَلِّمُكُمْ" - "Verily I am for you at the level of a father, I teach you..." (Abu Dawud, narrated by Abu Huraira)

Key Prohibition: "فَلَا يَسْتَقْبِلِ الْقِبْلَةَ وَلَا يَسْتَدْبِرَهَا وَلَا يَسْتَطِبْ بِيَمِينِهِ" - "Do not face the qibla nor turn your back to it, and do not clean with the right hand"

Warning Hadith: "اسْتَنْزِهُوا مِنَ الْبَوْلِ فَإِنَّ عَامَّةَ عَذَابِ الْقَبْرِ مِنْهُ" - "Be careful/keep safe from urine, for verily most of the torment of the grave is because of it"

Quranic Foundation: "فِيهِ رِجَالٌ يُحِبُّونَ أَن يَتَطَهَّرُوا وَاللَّهُ يُحِبُّ الْمُتَطَهِّرِينَ" - "In it are men who love to purify themselves, and Allah loves those who purify themselves"

docs.title: Conditions & Categories
docs.text:
Mandatory Etiquettes (واجب): - Going away from people's sight until no one can see, hear sounds, or smell odors - Entering with left foot, exiting with right foot - Not facing qibla front or back (makruh tahrimi - prohibitively disliked) - Performing istibra (for males) before considering oneself pure

Recommended Etiquettes (مستحب): - Choosing soft, lower ground to avoid splash-back - Saying the dua: "أعوذ بالله من الخبث والخبائث" - Sitting while leaning on left foot (traditional toilets) - Covering the head during relief and istinja - Exiting with the dua: "الحمد لله الذي أذهب عني الأذى وعافاني"

Prohibited/Disliked Locations: - Holes in the ground (danger from animals/creatures) - Pathways and roads - Graveyards - Shaded areas where people gather - Places where people converse - Under fruit-bearing trees - Near wells, rivers, or water basins - Small amounts of stagnant water - Areas where ghusl is performed

docs.title: Scholarly Opinions
docs.text:
Modern Applications Discussed: - For contemporary toilets that face qibla: slightly turn body away if renovation impossible - Standing urination: strongly discouraged due to splash-back concerns - Shower urination: makruh due to potential water stagnation, though modern drainage may mitigate this - Traditional vs. modern toilet positioning: squatting position aids complete evacuation

Exception to Speaking Prohibition: Speaking is obligatory if one sees a blind person walking toward danger (like a ditch) while using the restroom - duty to warn overrides the general prohibition.

docs.title: Lessons & Practical Application
docs.text:
Parental Guidance: The Prophet's approach shows the importance of parents or guardians providing proper education about private matters in appropriate, fatherly/motherly manner, especially regarding purity for those reaching maturity.

Health Benefits: Many etiquettes have practical health benefits (proper positioning aids elimination, avoiding splash-back prevents contamination, choosing appropriate locations prevents harm).

Istibra Process: Males must ensure complete removal of remaining urine through methods like: - Walking briefly - Light coughing - Gentle pressure/squats - Whatever method works for the individual

Modern Considerations: - Use of "squatty potty" type devices aligns with traditional beneficial positioning - Toilet paper placement in water bowl can reduce splash-back - Drainage functionality affects rulings about urinating in ghusl areas

Post-Relief Dua: "الحمد لله الذي أذهب عني الأذى وعافاني" (All praise to Allah who has removed impurity from me and granted me good health) - expressing gratitude for bodily functions and health.

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session completed the discussion of istinja (cleansing after relieving oneself) with specific focus on when impurity exceeds the dirham amount, and introduced the comprehensive classification of najasa (ritual impurities). The class covered practical rulings for cleansing methods, the theological distinctions between fard, wajib, and sunnah, and began exploring the two main categories of impurity: hukmiyya (ritual) and haqiqiyya (physical).

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
1. Quantitative thresholds for istinja obligations - The dirham amount as the decisive factor
  2. Methodological preferences in purification - Combining dry and wet cleaning methods
  3. Theological categorization of obligations - Understanding fard vs wajib distinctions
  4. Comprehensive impurity classification - Ritual vs physical najasa
  5. Prerequisites for valid worship - Body, clothing, and place requirements

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning إِذَا تَجَاوَزَتِ النَّجَاسَةُ الْمَخْرَجَ When the impurity passes over/oversteps the private part قَدْرُ الدِّرْهَمِ The amount/size of a dirham الْمَخْرَجُ The private part (where filth exits from) الاِسْتِنْجَاءُ Istinja (cleansing after relieving oneself) الاِقْتِصَارُ To suffice/be content with نَجَاسَةٌ حُكْمِيَّةٌ Ritual impurity نَجَاسَةٌ حَقِيقِيَّةٌ Physical/actual impurity الْحَدَثُ الأَكْبَرُ Major ritual impurity الْحَدَثُ الأَصْغَرُ Minor ritual impurity النَّجَاسَةُ الْغَلِيظَةُ Heavy/severe filth دَلَكًا Rubbing thoroughly تَنْظِيفًا Thoroughly cleaning

docs.title: Fiqh Rulings & Evidence
docs.text:
Quranic Foundation: - وَثِيَابَكَ فَطَهِّرْ - "Purify your clothes" (Allah's command for purification)

Prophetic Evidence: - لَا يَقْبَلُ اللَّهُ صَلَاةً مِنْ غَيْرِ طَهُورٍ - "Allah does not accept salah without purification" (Bukhari & Muslim)

Istinja Rulings Based on Quantity:

  1. More than dirham amount: إِذَا تَجَاوَزَتِ النَّجَاسَةُ الْمَخْرَجَ وَكَانَتْ أَكْثَرَ مِنْ قَدْرِ الدِّرْهَمِ - Washing with water becomes fard (obligatory)

  2. Equal to dirham amount: وَكَانَتْ قَدْرَ الدِّرْهَمِ - Still wajib (necessary) to remove with water

  3. Less than dirham amount: إِذَا لَمْ تَتَجَاوَزِ النَّجَاسَةُ الْمَخْرَجَ - Istinja is sunnah, and alternatives to water are permissible

docs.title: Conditions & Categories
docs.text:
Three Essential Elements for Valid Salah: 1. البدن - Clean body 2. الثياب - Clean clothing 3. المكان - Clean place

Najasa Classifications:

A. Najasa Hukmiyya (Ritual Impurity): - Also called الحدث (hadath) - Subdivided into: 1. الحدث الأكبر (Major hadath) - Requires ghusl, prohibits salah, Quran recitation, and masjid entry 2. الحدث الأصغر (Minor hadath) - Requires wudu, prohibits salah but permits verbal Quran recitation

B. Najasa Haqiqiyya (Physical Impurity): - Subdivided into: 1. النجاسة الغليظة (Heavy filth) - Established with indubitable evidence 2. [Light filth - to be covered in next session]

docs.title: Scholarly Opinions
docs.text:
Hanafi Madhab Distinctions:

Fard vs Wajib: - Fard: Established by clear-cut evidence with no room for interpretation (e.g., establishing salah) - Wajib: Established evidence but with some interpretive possibilities due to multiple valid interpretations - Both carry equal practical obligations, but differ in certainty of establishment

Preferred Cleaning Methods: - Water only: Permissible for amounts less than dirham - Stones/toilet paper only: Permissible for amounts less than dirham - Combined method: Most preferable - dry cleaning first, then water washing - Three stones/pieces: Mustahab (recommended) minimum

docs.title: Lessons & Practical Application
docs.text:
Practical Istinja Procedure: 1. Use dry cleaning method first (stone/toilet paper) to remove bulk filth 2. Wash hands thoroughly before using water 3. Clean the area with water until تَنْقَطِعَ الرَّائِحَةُ (odor disappears) 4. After completion, wash hands thoroughly with دَلَكًا (vigorous rubbing) until odor is removed

Worship Prerequisites: - Major hadath prevents: salah, Quran recitation, masjid entry - Minor hadath prevents: salah only - Physical najasa must be removed from body, clothing, and prayer location

Flexibility in Application: - One or two stones permissible إِذَا حَصُلَتِ النَّظَافَةُ بِهِ (if cleanliness is achieved) - Water washing أَبْلَغُ فِي النَّظَافَةِ (most efficient in cleaning) in all cases

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session focused on the detailed study of najasa (ritual impurity), covering two main categories: najasa ghaliza (heavy/severe filth) and najasa khafifa (light/mild filth). The lesson included specific examples of each type, their rulings regarding prayer validity, and the conditions under which they are pardoned or require purification. Additionally, the transfer of najasa between objects and surfaces was discussed with practical scenarios.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
• Classification system of najasa into ghaliza (heavy) and khafifa (light) categories
  • Measurement standards for determining when najasa affects prayer validity
  • Conditions for pardoning small amounts of najasa
  • Transfer mechanisms of najasa between pure and impure objects
  • Practical applications in daily life regarding clothing, bedding, and contact with impurities

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning نجاسة غليظة (Najasa Ghaliza) Heavy/severe filth نجاسة خفيفة (Najasa Khafifa) Light/mild filth يُعفى عن (Yu'fa 'an) Is pardoned/forgiven قدر درهم (Qadr dirham) Size/amount of a dirham افترض غسلها (Iftarada ghasluha) It becomes necessary to wash it الدم المسفوح (Ad-dammu al-masfuh) Spilled blood الميتة (Al-mayta) Dead animal (not slaughtered according to Islamic law) فضلة (Fadla) Excrement خُرء (Khurr) Droppings رشاش البول (Rashash al-bawl) Droplets of urine رؤوس الإبر (Ru'us al-ibar) Needle-eye size أثر النجاسة (Athar an-najasa) Traces/effects of najasa

docs.title: Najasa Ghaliza Examples:
docs.text:
1. Ad-dammu al-masfuh - Spilled blood that exits the body
  2. Khamr - Wine (primarily from dates and grapes)
  3. Lahmul maytati wa jilduha - Meat and skin of dead animals not slaughtered according to Sharia
  4. Bawlul hayawan - Urine of animals whose meat cannot be consumed (predators, swine, etc.)
  5. Fadlatull kalb - Excrement of dogs
  6. Fadlatu saba'i wa luabuha - Excrement and saliva of predatory/carnivorous animals
  7. Khurru dajajati wa baqtata - Droppings of chickens and ducks
  8. Everything that breaks wudu - All substances that exit the human body and invalidate ablution

docs.title: Najasa Khafifa Examples:
docs.text:
1. Bawlul faras - Urine of horses
  2. Bawl of halal animals - Urine of animals whose meat can be consumed (camels, cows, goats)
  3. Khurru tayri haram - Droppings of birds whose meat cannot be consumed

docs.title: Najasa Ghaliza Rulings:
docs.text:
• Pardoned amount: Up to the size of a dirham
  • When pardoned: Prayer is valid but makrooh (disliked)
  • When not pardoned: If larger than dirham size, must be washed with water or removing substance
  • Prayer validity: Salah invalid if najasa exceeds dirham size

docs.title: Najasa Khafifa Rulings:
docs.text:
• Pardoned amount: As long as it's not kathir (abundant)
  • Definition of kathir: One-quarter of clothing or body
  • Special case: Droplets of urine pardoned if needle-eye size (ru'us al-ibar)
  • Prayer validity: Valid if within pardoned limits, but makrooh

docs.title: Transfer of Najasa:
docs.text:
• Wet to dry contact: Najasa transfers only if traces (athar) become apparent
  • Wringing test: If wet impure cloth cannot release water when wrung, it won't transfer najasa to pure dry cloth
  • Wind transfer: Wind carrying najasa particles affects wet garments only if traces are visible

docs.title: Imam Abu Hanifa's Position on Animal Branding:
docs.text:
• Initial opinion: Prohibited due to potential animal harm
  • Later position (rujuu): Permitted if no pain is caused to the animal
  • Modern application: Spray painting preferred over hot branding

docs.title: Hanafi School Methodology:
docs.text:
• Ibadat (worship) matters: Generally follow Imam Abu Hanifa's opinions as they are timeless
  • Mu'amalat (transactions): Often follow students' opinions (Sahibain) due to contemporary changes
  • Reasoning: Students lived later and witnessed societal changes affecting practical rulings

docs.title: Animal Diet Regulations:
docs.text:
• Detox requirement: Animals fed haram diet require purification period before slaughter
  • Investigation duty: For omnivorous animals, inquire about their diet before consumption
  • Herbivorous animals: No special inquiry needed due to natural grass-based diet

docs.title: Daily Life Applications:
docs.text:
1. Clothing cleanliness: Check for najasa size using dirham measurement (approximately 2.5cm diameter)
  2. Laundry practices: Separate washing for items with heavy vs. light najasa
  3. Prayer preparation: Remove clothing with najasa exceeding permitted limits
  4. Animal products: Verify halal diet for omnivorous animals before consumption

docs.title: Measurement Guidelines:
docs.text:
• Dirham size: Reference point for najasa ghaliza (roughly size of a quarter coin)
  • Quarter body/clothing: Measurement for najasa khafifa abundance
  • Needle-eye size: Acceptable limit for urine droplets

docs.title: Hygiene Principles:
docs.text:
• Visible traces rule: If najasa effects are not apparent, object remains pure
  • Wetness factor: Dry najasa less likely to transfer than wet najasa
  • Estimation method: Use reasonable judgment for scattered najasa across multiple body parts

docs.title: Modern Considerations:
docs.text:
• Farming practices: Humane treatment and proper diet essential for halal consumption
  • Animal welfare: Branding permitted only without causing pain
  • Food industry: Research animal treatment and diet in commercial farming operations

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session focused on the methods of removing najasa (impurities) from clothing, objects, and surfaces. The class covered two main categories of impurities: visible najasa (najasa mar'iya) that can be clearly identified, and invisible/non-discernible najasa (najasa ghair mar'iya) that might be confused with other substances. Various purification methods were discussed, including washing, scrubbing, burning, and transformation of the impurity's essence.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
• Two categories of najasa: Visible (mar'iya) and invisible/non-discernible (ghair mar'iya)
  • Primary goal: Remove the physical body (jirm) of the impurity
  • Different purification methods: Washing, scrubbing, burning, essence transformation
  • Material-specific rules: Different items require different purification approaches
  • Animal products: Complex rules regarding skin, bones, and other parts after death

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning كيف تزال النجاسة How are impurities removed تحصل الطهارة Purity is attained مرئية Visible غير المرئية Non-visible/invisible جرم Body (of the impurity) أثر النجاسة Traces/effects of impurity عصر To squeeze/wring out التقاطر Dripping مائع Liquid/flowing substance إزالة النجاسة Removal of impurity الخل Vinegar ماء الورد Rose water النعل Shoes الخف Leather socks الغسل Washing الدلك Scrubbing/rubbing السيف Sword السكين Knife المرآة Mirror الأواني المدهونة Oiled utensils المسح Wiping عين النجاسة The impurity itself ملح Salt احترقت Burned المني Seminal fluid يبس Dried الفرك Scraping رطبة Wet جافة Dry غليظ Thick دبغ/الدباغة Tanning حقيقية Real/chemical (tanning) حكمية Juridical/non-chemical (tanning) خنزير Pig كرامة Nobility شرف Honor الذبح الشرعي Proper Islamic slaughter لا يسري فيه الدم Does not have flowing blood الشعر Hair/fur الريش المقطوع Plucked feather القرن Horn الحافر Hooves العظم Bones دسم Fat العصب Nerves نافجة المسك Musk pouch

docs.title: Fiqh Rulings & Evidence
docs.text:
Visible Impurities (Najasa Mar'iya) - Examples: blood (dam), excrement (gha'it) - Method: Remove the physical body of the impurity through washing - Goal: Remove the impurity itself, whether it takes one wash or multiple washes - Traces (athar) remaining after body removal do not matter if removal is difficult

Invisible Impurities (Najasa Ghair Mar'iya) - Examples: urine (bawl) - called invisible because it can be confused with water or sweat - Method: Four-step process: 1. Wash three times 2. Squeeze/wring out completely each time 3. Continue squeezing until dripping stops 4. Use fresh, pure water for each wash

Water and Cleaning Agents - Water is the primary purifier for actual najasa (najasa haqiqiya) - Any flowing liquid that can remove filth may be used (vinegar, rose water, detergents, soap, bleach) - Important limitation: Vinegar and rose water cannot be used for wudu - only for removing physical impurities

Shoes and Leather Socks - Can be purified through washing (ghusal) or scrubbing (dalak) on pure ground - Scrubbing method only works when impurity has a physical body (jirm) - Works whether impurity is wet (ratba) or dry (jaffa)

Hard Surfaces - Swords, knives, mirrors, and oiled utensils: purified by wiping off the impurity

Ground Purification - Ground becomes pure when it dries and traces of najasa disappear - Salah is permissible on such ground - However, tayammum from such ground is not permissible

docs.title: Conditions & Categories
docs.text:
Transformation of Impurity Essence - When the essence (ayn) of najasa changes into something else (e.g., becomes salt), it becomes pure - Burning impurity with fire also purifies it

Seminal Fluid (Mani) Rules - If dry and thick: Can be purified by scraping (fark) - If wet or not thick: Must be washed, scraping is insufficient - Note: Modern diet makes fluid less thick, so washing is usually required today

Animal Skin Tanning - Dead animal skin becomes pure through tanning (dabagha) - Two types of tanning: - Chemical tanning (haqiqiya) - Sun/natural tanning (hukmiya) - Exception: Pig skin never becomes pure regardless of tanning - Human skin: Becomes pure through tanning but using it is forbidden as it violates human dignity (karama) and honor (sharaf)

Non-Edible Animals - Skin of animals whose meat is not consumed becomes pure through proper Islamic slaughter (dhabh shar'i)
