Parts Without Flowing Blood - Hair, plucked feathers, horns, hooves, and bones do not become impure upon animal's death - Condition: They must not have fat (dasam) on them - If fat is present: They become impure

Special Cases - Animal nerves (asab): Considered impure - Musk pouch (nafijat al-misk): Pure, and musk consumption is halal

docs.title: Scholarly Opinions
docs.text: The text presents the Hanafi position without mentioning alternative scholarly opinions. The rulings are presented as established fiqh principles without discussion of ikhtilaf (scholarly differences).

docs.title: Lessons & Practical Application
docs.text:
Modern Cleaning Agents - Contemporary detergents, bleach, and cleaning sprays fall under the category of "flowing substances that can remove filth" - These may be used for removing physical najasa but not for ritual purification (wudu/ghusl)

Practical Guidance for Parents - When a child's diaper leaks onto clothing: wash three times, squeeze each time, use fresh water - This does not break the adult's wudu as it's external contamination - Only the soiled clothing needs cleaning

Contemporary Applications - Taxidermy: Potentially problematic (makruh) due to resemblance to living beings and potential interference with prayer - Modern diet affects consistency of bodily fluids, requiring washing rather than scraping - Industrial tanning methods fall under "chemical tanning" (dabagha haqiqiya)

Key Principle The fundamental goal is removing the physical substance (jirm) of the impurity. Traces that remain after good faith effort to remove the substance do not invalidate purification when removal is genuinely difficult.

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session covered the ruling of wudu (Hukm al-Wudu), its linguistic and technical definitions, its integrals (Arkan), the conditions for its validity (Shurut al-Sihah), and the conditions that make wudu obligatory (Shurut al-Wujub). Students read aloud from the text in Arabic and translated passages into English, with the teacher providing explanations and answering questions throughout.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
1. Ruling of Wudu — established from Quranic verse and hadith; wudu is obligatory for Salah
  2. Definition of Wudu — linguistic vs. shari'i (technical) meanings
  3. Virtue of Maintaining Wudu — reward and elevation of rank for one who remains in a state of wudu
  4. Integrals (Arkan) of Wudu — the four obligatory acts that constitute wudu
  5. Conditions of Validity (Shurut al-Sihah) — three conditions required for wudu to count
  6. Conditions of Obligation (Shurut al-Wujub) — seven conditions that make wudu binding upon a person

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning الوُضُوء (al-Wudu) Wudu / ritual ablution الحَدَث (al-Hadath) State of ritual impurity الحَدَث الأَصْغَر (al-Hadath al-Asghar) Minor ritual impurity (requires wudu) الحَدَث الأَكْبَر (al-Hadath al-Akbar) Major ritual impurity (requires ghusl) الأَرْكَان (al-Arkan) Integrals / pillars (obligatory components) الفَرَائِض (al-Fara'id) Obligatory acts الشُّرُوط (al-Shurut) Conditions الصِّحَّة (al-Sihah) Validity الوُجُوب (al-Wujub) Obligation / being obligatory الحُسْن (al-Husn) Beauty النَّظَافَة (al-Nazafa) Cleanliness الطَّهَارَة (al-Taharah) Purification المَرَافِق (al-Marafiq) Elbows الكَعْبَيْن (al-Ka'bayn) Ankles الجَبْهَة (al-Jabha) Forehead الذَّقَن (al-Dhaqan) Chin شَحْمَتَا الأُذُنَيْن (Shahmat al-Udhnayn) Earlobes البَشَرَة (al-Basharah) Skin الشَّمْع (al-Sham') Wax العَجِين (al-'Ajin) Flour / dough البُلُوغ (al-Bulugh) Reaching the age of puberty العَقْل (al-'Aql) Sound intellect / sanity المَجْنُون (al-Majnun) An insane person الكَافِر (al-Kafir) A non-Muslim القُدْرَة (al-Qudrah) Ability / capability المُتَوَضِّئ (al-Mutawaddi) One who is already in a state of wudu ضِيق الوَقْت (Diyq al-Waqt) Shortage / tightness of time عَلَى الفَوْر ('Ala al-Fawr) Immediately / without delay مُتَّسِعًا (Muttasi'an) Spacious / ample (referring to time) وَاظَبَ (Wadhaba) To be consistent / particular about something اسْتِيفَاء (Istifa') Fulfilling / satisfying (conditions) الفَائِدَة المَطْلُوبَة (al-Fa'idah al-Matlubah) The sought-out / intended benefit الغُسْل (al-Ghusl) Full ritual bath التَّيَمُّم (al-Tayammum) Dry ablution (with dust/earth)

docs.title: Ruling of Wudu
docs.text:
Wudu is obligatory (fard) before performing Salah. It is also required before touching the Mus'haf (physical copy of the Quran).

Evidence 1 — Quranic Verse (Surah al-Ma'idah):

يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذَا قُمْتُمْ إِلَى الصَّلَاةِ فَاغْسِلُوا وُجُوهَكُمْ وَأَيْدِيَكُمْ إِلَى الْمَرَافِقِ وَامْسَحُوا بِرُؤُوسِكُمْ وَأَرْجُلَكُمْ إِلَى الْكَعْبَيْنِ "O you who believe, when you stand for Salah, wash your faces and your hands up to and including the elbows, and wipe your heads, and wash your feet up to and including the ankles."

  • فَاغْسِلُوا is a command form (Amr), indicating obligation
  • وَأَرْجُلَكُمْ grammatically connects back to فَاغْسِلُوا (not to وَامْسَحُوا), confirming feet are to be washed, not wiped
  • إِلَى in إِلَى الْمَرَافِقِ and إِلَى الْكَعْبَيْنِ is inclusive — the elbows and ankles must be included in the wash

Evidence 2 — Hadith of the Prophet ﷺ:

لَا يَقْبَلُ اللَّهُ صَلَاةَ أَحَدِكُمْ إِذَا أَحْدَثَ حَتَّى يَتَوَضَّأَ "Allah does not accept the Salah of any one of you when he enters into a state of hadath, until he performs wudu."

  • This confirms that the acceptance of Salah is conditioned upon being in a state of wudu
  • If one breaks wudu, they must renew it before praying

docs.title: Additional Rulings Derived
docs.text: Ruling Detail Salah is not permissible without wudu A person in a state of hadath may not pray Touching the Mus'haf is not permissible without wudu One must be in a state of wudu to handle the physical Quran A barrier on the skin invalidates wudu If water cannot reach the skin, wudu does not count Wudu performed while a nullifier occurs is invalid If a nullifier of wudu takes place during the act of wudu, wudu must be restarted Wudu is not sufficient when ghusl is required One in a state of Hadath al-Akbar must perform ghusl, not merely wudu

docs.title: Definition of Wudu
docs.text: Perspective Definition Linguistic (Lughawi) Al-Husn (beauty) and al-Nazafa (cleanliness) Shari'i (Technical) A ceremonial purification involving water (Taharatun Ma'iyah) that includes washing the face, hands, and feet, and wiping the head

docs.title: Arkan al-Wudu — The Four Integrals (All Obligatory)
docs.text:
These are the minimum obligatory acts of wudu. Each must be performed at least once:

# Integral Arabic Notes 1 Washing the face once غَسْل الوَجْه مَرَّة Boundaries defined below 2 Washing both arms up to and including the elbows once غَسْل الأَيْدِي إِلَى الْمَرَافِق المَرَافِق = elbows, included in the wash 3 Wiping one-fourth of the head مَسْح رُبُع الرَّأْس Note: wiping, not washing 4 Washing both feet up to and including the ankles once غَسْل الأَرْجُل إِلَى الكَعْبَيْن الكَعْبَيْن = ankles, included in the wash

Boundaries of the face: - In length (al-Tul): From the highest point of the forehead (أَعْلَى سَطْح الجَبْهَة) to the bottom of the chin (أَسْفَل الذَّقَن) - In width (al-'Ard): From earlobe to earlobe (مَا بَيْن شَحْمَتَي الأُذُنَيْن)

docs.title: Shurut al-Sihah — Three Conditions for Validity of Wudu
docs.text:
Wudu is not valid unless these three conditions are met. The sought-out benefit of wudu (reward and elevated rank) is also not attained without them:

# Condition Detail 1 Water must reach all body parts whose washing is obligatory Every required part must have water flow over it completely 2 Nothing must be present that prevents water from reaching the skin E.g. wax (شَمْع), flour/dough (عَجِين), superglue, caulking, oil-based paint, nail polish — any physical barrier invalidates wudu 3 No nullifier of wudu must occur during the wudu If one passes wind, bleeds, etc. while performing wudu, the wudu is invalidated and must be restarted

Note on barriers: Nail polish forms a complete layer over the nails and is considered a barrier — it must be removed for wudu to be valid. Flour is particularly noted because it creates a barrier both wet and dry, but especially when wet.

docs.title: Shurut al-Wujub — Seven Conditions for Wudu to be Obligatory
docs.text:
Wudu is only obligatory upon a person in whom all of the following conditions are found:

# Condition Arabic Detail 1 Puberty (al-Bulugh) البُلُوغ Wudu is not legally obligatory upon a child; however, children should be taught early for proper upbringing (tarbiyah) 2 Sound intellect (al-'Aql) العَقْل Wudu is not obligatory upon the insane (al-Majnun); this applies to those who cannot differentiate right from wrong or cannot perform basic functions. Conditions like severe autism, extreme developmental disorders, or severe dissociative disorders may fall under this category. Bipolar disorder alone, however, generally does not classify a person as majnun 3 Islam الإِسْلَام Wudu is not legally obligatory upon a non-Muslim (al-Kafir) 4 Ability to use water (al-Qudrah) القُدْرَة عَلَى اسْتِعْمَال المَاء One must be able to access and use sufficient water for all wudu limbs. If a person only has enough water for survival (drinking), they are not required to use it for wudu and may perform tayammum instead 5 Presence of Hadath al-Asghar وُجُود الحَدَث الأَصْغَر Wudu is not necessary upon a person already in the state of wudu (al-Mutawaddi) 6 Freedom from Hadath al-Akbar خُلُوُّه مِن الحَدَث الأَكْبَر If a person is in a state of major impurity (Hadath al-Akbar), wudu alone is insufficient — ghusl is required instead 7 Tightness of time (Diyq al-Waqt) ضِيق الوَقْت If there is ample time before the Salah's time expires, one is not obligated to perform wudu immediately (not 'ala al-fawr) — delay is permissible. However, if time is tight (the Salah is about to expire), wudu and Salah become immediately obligatory

docs.title: Scholarly Opinions
docs.text:
• The Hanafi position (consistent with this text) holds that إِلَى in the wudu verse is inclusive — the elbows and ankles are washed as part of the obligatory limbs.
  • The discussion of what constitutes a "majnun" for legal purposes acknowledges that modern psychiatric conditions must be assessed based on whether the person can reason and differentiate right from wrong — not merely based on a diagnosis.
  • The question of nail polish as a barrier is noted, with the teacher indicating it does constitute a barrier that must be removed for wudu to be valid.

docs.title: Lessons & Practical Application
docs.text:
1. Maintain wudu at all times. The one who is consistent (wadhaba) upon wudu — sleeping in wudu, renewing immediately after breaking it — earns great reward and elevation of rank in the Akhirah. This is a Prophetic practice to cultivate.

  2. Check for physical barriers before wudu. Before making wudu, ensure the skin is free of wax, heavy lotion residue, nail polish, superglue, paint, caulking, or any other substance that forms a physical layer over the skin.

  3. Do not interrupt wudu. If a nullifier of wudu (passing wind, bleeding, etc.) occurs mid-wudu, restart from the beginning.

  4. Teach children early. Even though wudu is not legally obligatory on pre-pubescent children, good tarbiyah requires instilling these habits early so they are second nature upon reaching puberty.

  5. Know when tayammum applies. If water is insufficient for all wudu limbs — or only enough for survival — tayammum is performed instead.

  6. Wudu vs. Ghusl. Understand the distinction between Hadath al-Asghar (minor impurity, remedied by wudu) and Hadath al-Akbar (major impurity, requiring full ghusl). Wudu in a state of Hadath al-Akbar does not suffice.

  7. Time awareness for Salah. If a Salah's time is running out and one has not yet prayed, it becomes obligatory to immediately perform wudu and pray. When ample time remains, one has flexibility in when to perform wudu.

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session covered two main areas: (1) a brief Q&A on the ruling of Qasr (shortened) prayer for travelers, clarifying conditions around residency duration and city limits; and (2) a detailed walkthrough of supplementary rulings (furu') related to wudu, followed by a full enumeration of the eighteen Sunnahs of wudu. The class also included nahw (grammatical) analysis of key sentences from the text, deepening understanding of the Arabic constructions used.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
1. Traveler vs. Temporary Resident — When a traveler's stay exceeds 15 days, he is reclassified and must pray full prayers.
  2. Supplementary Rulings of Wudu (Furu') — Detailed conditions about beards, nails, rings, and unusual circumstances that affect validity of wudu.
  3. Eighteen Sunnahs of Wudu — A comprehensive list of recommended acts that perfect the wudu.
  4. Grammatical Analysis (Nahw) — Application of shart-jaza (conditional) structure and the difference between fi'l and ism in context.

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning وَطَنُ الإِقَامَة Temporary place of residence قَصْر Shortening of prayers during travel خُلُوّ To be empty / free from (خالي in Urdu) حَدَث أَكْبَر Major ritual impurity requiring ghusl ضِيق الوَقْت Restriction/tightness of time عَلَى الفَوْر Immediately فُرُوع Supplementary / subsidiary rulings كَثَّة Thick (referring to a thick beard) خَفِيفَة Thin (referring to a thin beard) إِيصَال المَاء Making water reach (the skin) بَشَرَة Skin اِسْتَرْسَل To hang down (hairs hanging below beard) ظُفْر (أَظَافِر) Nail(s) أَنْمِلَة Fingertips / pores of the skin قَلْم Cutting (of nails) شَمَع Wax عَجِن Dough / flour تَحْرِيك Moving / rotating خَاتَم Ring ضَيِّق Tight شُقُوق Cleaves / cracks (e.g., cracked feet) سُنَن Sunnahs / recommended acts مَضْمَضَة Rinsing the mouth اِسْتِنْشَاق Cleaning / sniffing water into the nose يُبَالِغ To do something excessively / thoroughly صَائِم One who is fasting عُضْو (أَعْضَاء) Limb / body part(s) تَرْتِيب Order / sequence خِلَال Passing wet fingers through (beard or fingers) يُدَلِّك To rub جَفَاف Drying يُرَاعِي To be mindful/considerate of مِسْوَاك / سِوَاك Tooth-stick قَفَا The nape (back of the neck) حُلْقُوم The throat بِدْعَة Reprehensible innovation شَرْط وَجَزَاء Conditional clause and its consequence (if...then) تَرْقِيب Grammatical parsing/analysis حَلَق To shave/cut hair

docs.title: Qasr Prayer and the Traveler's Status
docs.text:
• A person who intends to stay 15 days or more at a destination is considered a temporary resident (وَطَن الإِقَامَة) and must pray full (4 raka'ah) prayers.
  • A person who intends to stay fewer than 15 days remains classified as a traveler (musafir) and prays Qasr (2 raka'ah).
  • The traveler status begins from the moment he leaves his city limits with the intention of travel.
  • Example given: Traveling from New Jersey → Boston → Maine. If Boston is merely a pit stop (under 15 days), one prays Qasr throughout. If staying in Boston for a month, upon reaching Boston he prays full.

docs.title: Condition 6 — Freedom from Major Impurity (حَدَث أَكْبَر)
docs.text:
• Wudu alone is not sufficient for someone upon whom ghusl (full ritual bath) is obligatory.
  • فَلَا يَكْفِي الوُضُوء لِلَّذِي قَدْ وَجَبَ عَلَيْهِ الغُسْل

docs.title: Condition 7 — Restriction of Time (ضِيق الوَقْت)
docs.text:
• If there is ample time remaining, wudu is not obligatory immediately (عَلَى الفَوْر); delaying is permissible.
  • If time is tight, wudu becomes immediately necessary.

docs.title: Supplementary Rulings of Wudu (فُرُوع تَتَعَلَّق بِالوُضُوء)
The Beard
docs.text:
1. Thick beard (كَثَّة): It is obligatory to wash only the outer surface. Khilal (running wet fingers through) is recommended.
  2. Thin beard (خَفِيفَة): Water must reach the skin beneath the beard (بَشَرَة). Washing just the outer surface is insufficient.
  3. Hanging hairs (مُسْتَرْسِل): The hairs that hang below the beard line — it is not obligatory to wash them, nor is it obligatory to wipe them.

docs.title: Nails
docs.text:
1. Obstructions under nails (wax, dough): If there is wax (شَمَع) or dough/flour (عَجِن) under the nails preventing water from reaching the skin, it must be removed and the area washed.
  2. Long nails: If nails grow so long that they cover the fingertips (أَنْمِلَة), it is obligatory to cut them so water can reach the skin beneath.
  3. Dirt and flea droppings: Regular dirt under the nails and droppings of a flea do not prevent water from reaching the skin and do not invalidate wudu.

docs.title: Rings
docs.text:
1. Tight ring (خَاتَم ضَيِّق): If a ring is tight and water cannot reach the skin beneath it without moving it, it is obligatory to rotate/move the ring during wudu.
  2. Loose ring: If the ring is loose and water readily reaches beneath it, moving it is not obligatory.

docs.title: Cracked Feet / Medicine
docs.text: 1. Cracked feet (شُقُوق رِجْلَيْه): If washing the cracks of one's feet would cause harm, it is permissible to pass water over the medicine/bandage applied to those feet (grammatical note: this is a shart-jaza construction — "if washing harms him [shart], then passing water over the medicine is permissible [jaza]").

docs.title: Post-Wudu Hair/Nail Changes
docs.text:
1. Wiping the head then cutting hair: If a person wipes his head in wudu and then gets a haircut or shaves his head, he does not need to re-wipe his head.
  2. Cutting nails or trimming mustache after wudu: Does not require repeating the wudu's washing of those parts.

docs.title: The Eighteen Sunnahs of Wudu (سُنَن الوُضُوء)
docs.text:
These acts are Sunnah — one should perform them so that wudu is done in the most perfect manner (عَلَى وَجْهٍ أَكْمَل):

# Sunnah Arabic 1 Making intention before beginning wudu النِّيَّة قَبْل الشُّرُوع 2 Reciting Bismillah at the start بِسْمِ اللهِ الرَّحْمٰنِ الرَّحِيْم 3 Washing both hands up to and including the wrists غَسْل اليَدَيْن إلى الرُّسْغَيْن 4 Using the miswak (tooth-stick) اَلاسْتِيَاك 5 Using the finger if no miswak is available فَبِالإِصْبَع إِن لَم يَجِد السِّوَاك 6 Rinsing the mouth (madmada) المَضْمَضَة 7 Cleaning the nose (istinshaq) الاِسْتِنْشَاق 8 Thoroughness in rinsing mouth and cleaning nose (not while fasting) المُبَالَغَة فِي المَضْمَضَة وَالاِسْتِنْشَاق إِذَا لَم يَكُن صَائِمًا 9 Washing each body part three times غَسْل كُل عُضْو ثَلَاث مَرَّات 10 Wiping the entire head once مَسْح جَمِيع الرَّأْس مَرَّة 11 Wiping the outer and inner ears مَسْح الأُذُنَيْن ظَاهِرَهُمَا وَبَاطِنَهُمَا 12 Performing khilal of the beard from below (upward) تَخْلِيل اللِّحْيَة مِن السُّفَل 13 Performing khilal of the fingers تَخْلِيل الأَصَابِع 14 Rubbing the limbs when washing them (not just pouring water) تَدْلِيك الأَعْضَاء عِنْد الغَسْل 15 Washing the second limb before the first dries غَسْل العُضْو الثَّانِي قَبْل جَفَاف العُضْو الأَوَّل 16 Maintaining the proper order (tartib) of washing: face → hands → wipe head → feet مُرَاعَاة التَّرْتِيب فِي غَسْل الأَعْضَاء 17 Washing the right hand before the left, and the right foot before the left غَسْل اليَد اليُمْنَى قَبْل اليُسْرَى وَكَذَا الرِّجْل 18 Beginning the head wipe from the front (forelock area) اِبْتِدَاء المَسْح مِن مُقَدَّم الرَّأْس 18+ Wiping the nape (back of neck) — قَفَا مَسْح القَفَا

Note: Wiping the throat (حُلْقُوم) is bid'ah (a reprehensible innovation) and should be avoided. Only the nape is the sunnah.

docs.title: Scholarly Opinions
docs.text:
• The rulings on the beard (thick vs. thin), nails, and rings are consistent with the Hanafi madhab as presented in the text (a continuation from Ascent to Felicity / Maraqi al-Falah, which was referenced as having covered these topics in detail).
  • The 15-day rule for establishing temporary residency and returning to full prayers is the standard Hanafi position.
  • The distinction between watan al-iqama (temporary place of residence where one prays full) and the musafir (traveler praying Qasr) is a well-established Hanafi categorization.

docs.title: Lessons & Practical Application
docs.text:
1. Travel planning matters for salah: Before traveling, determine your intended length of stay. If 15 days or more, plan to pray full prayers from the moment you enter that city. If less, you continue Qasr.

  2. Check your rings: If you wear a tight ring, make it a habit during wudu to slide it and rotate it to ensure water reaches beneath it. Forgetting this invalidates the wudu of that limb.

  3. Keep nails trimmed: Long nails that cover the fingertips are not just a hygiene issue — they make wudu invalid in those areas. Regular nail trimming is a practical Islamic obligation connected to purification.

  4. Beard khilal: During wudu, run wet fingers through the beard from the bottom upward. For a thin beard, ensure water reaches the skin of the face.

  5. Don't rush wudu: The Sunnahs of wudu — especially rubbing the limbs, thorough mouth/nose cleaning, washing three times, and maintaining order — elevate a valid wudu to a perfected one. Aim for these consistently.

  6. Don't wipe the throat: A common mistake is wiping the throat thinking it is sunnah — it is in fact bid'ah. The sunnah is to wipe the nape (back of the neck).

  7. Post-wudu changes don't break it: Getting a haircut, trimming your mustache, or cutting your nails after wudu does not require you to re-do any part of it.

  8. Grammatical awareness enhances understanding: The class illustrated how knowing Arabic grammar (e.g., shart-jaza structures, ism vs. fi'l after كَان) allows one to understand Islamic legal texts with precision and extract rulings correctly.

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session covered two major sections of Kitab al-Taharah (Book of Purification): the etiquettes (adab) of wudu, followed by the disliked actions (makruhat) of wudu, and then a full chapter on the categories of wudu — fard, wajib, and mustahab — including when each applies and the extensive list of situations where wudu is desirable.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
• Adab al-Wudu — 13 recommended etiquettes that a person should observe when performing wudu
  • Makruhat al-Wudu — 6 disliked actions to avoid during wudu
  • Aqsam al-Wudu — The three categories of wudu: obligatory (fard), necessary (wajib), and desirable (mustahab)
  • When wudu is fard (4 cases), wajib (1 case), and mustahab (22+ cases)

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning أَدَابُ الْوُضُوء (Adab al-Wudu) Etiquettes of wudu تَسْتَحِبُّ (Tastahib) Is preferred / recommended مَكَانٌ مُرْتَفِعٌ (Makan Murtafi') An elevated place مَاءٌ مُسْتَعْمَلٌ (Ma' Musta'mal) Used water مُسْتَقْبِلًا (Mustaqbilan) Facing (towards) نَحْوَ الْقِبْلَةِ (Nahwa al-Qiblah) Towards the qiblah دَعَوَاتٌ مَأْثُورَةٌ (Da'awat al-Ma'thurah) Supplications narrated from the Prophet ﷺ تَلَفُّظٌ بِاللِّسَانِ (Talaffuz bil-Lisan) Verbalizing with the tongue خِنْصَرٌ (Khinsari) Pinky finger / smallest finger مَبْلُولٌ (Mablul) Wet الصِّمَاخُ (Al-Simakh) The ear hole / inner ear خَاتَمٌ وَاسِعٌ (Khatam Wasi') A loose-fitting ring خَاتَمٌ ضَيِّقٌ (Khatam Dayyiq) A tight-fitting ring تَحْرِيكٌ (Tahrik) Moving around مَضْمَضَةٌ (Madmadah) Rinsing one's mouth اسْتِنْشَاقٌ (Istinshaq) Cleaning one's nose (inhaling water) امْتِخَاطٌ (Imtikhat) Cleaning/blowing one's nose (expelling) مَكْرُوهَاتُ الْوُضُوء (Makruhat al-Wudu) Disliked actions during wudu إِسْرَافٌ (Israf) Extravagance / wasting أَقْسَامُ الْوُضُوء (Aqsam al-Wudu) Categories/types of wudu فَرْضٌ (Fard) Obligatory وَاجِبٌ (Wajib) Necessary مُسْتَحَبٌّ (Mustahab) Desirable / preferred مُحْدِثٌ (Muhdith) One who is in need of wudu (state of minor impurity) أَدَاءُ الصَّلَاةِ (Ada' al-Salah) Performing/praying salah سُجُودُ تِلَاوَةٍ (Sujud al-Tilawah) Prostration of recitation صَلَاةُ الْجِنَازَةِ (Salat al-Janazah) Funeral prayer طَوَافٌ (Tawaf) Circumambulation of the Ka'bah الْمَعْذُورُ (Al-Ma'dhur) Person with a chronic excuse (e.g., chronic condition that breaks wudu) مُدَاوَمَةٌ (Mudawamah) Constantly maintaining / being persistent ثَوَابٌ (Thawab) Reward نَمِيمَةٌ (Namimah) Slander / tale-bearing غُسْلُ الْجَنَابَةِ (Ghusl al-Janabah) The obligatory bath for major ritual impurity الْجُنُبُ (Al-Junub) Person in state of major ritual impurity شِعْرٌ قَبِيحٌ (Shi'r Qabeeh) Sinful / obscene poetry

docs.title: Ring During Wudu
docs.text:
• Loose ring: It is an etiquette (mustahab) to move it around so water reaches the skin beneath.
  • Tight ring: It is necessary (lazim) to move it around so water reaches the skin, otherwise wudu is invalid.
  • Exception: If a person is certain water has already reached under the ring, moving it is not required even if tight.

docs.title: Touching the Qur'an Without Wudu
docs.text:
• Wudu is fard (obligatory) before touching the Qur'an.
  • This applies to an ayah written on:
  • A wall
  • Paper (قِرْطَاسٌ)
  • A coin (دِرْهَمٌ)
  • For books containing ayahs alongside other text: If the majority of content is Qur'anic ayahs, the entire book carries the ruling of the Qur'an. If the majority is non-Qur'anic, one must still avoid touching the specific pages or parts that contain ayahs.

docs.title: Laughing Loudly
docs.text:
• Outside of salah: Wudu remains intact; however, it is mustahab to make wudu afterwards.
  • During salah: Both wudu and salah are invalidated.

docs.title: Wudu Upon Wudu (Wudu 'ala Wudu)
docs.text:
• Performing wudu again while already in a state of wudu is mustahab only when a person uses the new wudu for an act of ibadah (e.g., praying salah, reciting Qur'an) and then remakes wudu.
  • Simply repeating wudu in quick succession without performing an ibadah in between falls under israf (wasteful extravagance in water).

docs.title: The Ma'dhur
docs.text:
• A ma'dhur is a person with a chronic condition that continuously breaks wudu (e.g., chronic bleeding, incontinence).
  • For such a person, wudu is necessary for each prayer time — i.e., the wudu lasts only for that prayer's time.
  • The etiquette of making wudu before the prayer time enters does not apply to the ma'dhur.

docs.title: Wudu While in State of Janabah
docs.text:
• A junub (person in major ritual impurity) performing wudu does not remove the state of janabah.
  • However, it is mustahab before eating, drinking, and sleeping, as it raises the person to a degree of purity.

docs.title: Adab al-Wudu — 13 Etiquettes
docs.text:
1. Sit in an elevated place — so that droplets of used water (ma' musta'mal) do not fall back onto the person.
  2. Face the qiblah while performing wudu.
  3. Do not seek assistance from others — unless physically unable to perform wudu alone, in which case it is permissible.
  4. Do not engage in conversation (words of worldly talk) during wudu. One should focus on dhikr and adhkar.
  5. Recite the da'awat al-ma'thurah — the supplications narrated from the Prophet ﷺ during wudu.
  6. Combine intention of the heart with verbal expression — make the niyyah internally and also verbalize it with the tongue.
  7. Recite Bismillah ar-Rahman ar-Raheem at the washing of every single body part.
  8. Insert the wet pinky finger into the ear hole (simakh) when wiping the ears.
  9. Move a loose-fitting ring around during wudu.
  10. Take water for madmadah and istinshaq with the right hand.
  11. Use the left hand for imtikhat — cleaning/blowing the nose.
  12. Perform wudu before the prayer time enters — except for the ma'dhur.
  13. After completing wudu, stand facing the qiblah and recite: > أَشْهَدُ أَنْ لَا إِلَهَ إِلَّا اللَّهُ وَحْدَهُ لَا شَرِيكَ لَهُ وَأَشْهَدُ أَنَّ سَيِّدَنَا مُحَمَّدًا عَبْدُهُ وَرَسُولُهُ اللَّهُمَّ اجْعَلْنِي مِنَ التَّوَّابِينَ وَاجْعَلْنِي مِنَ الْمُتَطَهِّرِينَ

docs.title: Makruhat al-Wudu — 6 Disliked Actions
docs.text:
1. Israf — Being extravagant or wasteful in water usage.
  2. Stinginess with water — Using so little water that one is uncertain it reached all required body parts.
  3. Striking the face with water — Throwing or splashing water harshly onto one's face.
  4. Talking during wudu — Speaking worldly matters (kalam al-nas) during wudu.
  5. Seeking assistance without need — Taking help from others when physically capable of performing wudu independently.
  6. Wiping the head three times with new water each time — The masah of the head is performed once only; repeating it multiple times with new water each time is makruh.

docs.title: 1. Fard (Obligatory) — 4 Cases
docs.text:
Wudu is fard upon a muhdith for any of the following:

# Situation 1 Performing any salah — whether fard or nafl 2 Performing Salat al-Janazah (funeral prayer) 3 Performing Sujud al-Tilawah (prostration of recitation) 4 Touching the Holy Qur'an (or an ayah written on a wall, paper, or coin)

docs.title: 2. Wajib (Necessary) — 1 Case
docs.text:
Wudu is wajib upon a muhdith for:

# Situation 1 Performing Tawaf (circumambulation) of the Ka'bah

docs.title: 3. Mustahab (Desirable) — 22 Cases
docs.text: # Situation 1 Sleeping in a state of purity (with wudu) 2 Upon waking from sleep 3 Constantly maintaining the state of wudu (mudawamah 'ala al-wudu) 4 Performing wudu upon wudu with the intention of thawab (after performing an ibadah with the first wudu) 5 After committing backbiting (ghibah) 6 After committing slander (namimah) or lying (kidhb) 7 After committing any sin 8 After reciting sinful/obscene poetry (shi'r qabeeh) 9 After laughing loudly outside of salah 10 To wash a deceased person (ghusl al-mayyit) 11 To carry a deceased person 12 Before every salah time 13 Before performing the obligatory bath (ghusl al-janabah) 14 For a junub before eating 15 For a junub before drinking 16 For a junub before sleeping 17 To verbally recite the Qur'an 18 To recite or narrate ahadith of the Prophet ﷺ 19 While studying Islamic knowledge ('ilm shari'i) 20 To call the adhan 21 To call the iqamah 22 To deliver the khutbah of Jumu'ah 23 To visit the Prophet ﷺ (ziyarat al-Nabi ﷺ) 24 While standing at Arafah (wuquf bi Arafah) 25 While performing sa'i between Safa and Marwah

docs.title: Scholarly Opinions
docs.text:
• The Hanafi position on wudu categories divides them into fard, wajib, and mustahab — reflecting the classic Hanafi distinction between fard (established by definitive evidence) and wajib (established by probable evidence).
  • The ruling of the ma'dhur (person with chronic excuse) is an established Hanafi ruling: their wudu is valid for the duration of that prayer's time, regardless of their condition continuing.
  • Tawaf is listed as wajib (not fard) in this madhhab, reflecting the Hanafi position that tawaf requires wudu but its obligation is at a lower degree than salah.

docs.title: Lessons & Practical Application
docs.text:
1. Elevation when performing wudu is a practical hygiene and ritual concern — used water is considered impure (in terms of ritual usage), and sitting at a higher level prevents it from splashing back.

  2. Facing the qiblah during wudu reflects the broader principle of orienting acts of worship toward the qiblah. Even preparatory acts like wudu carry this spiritual dimension.

  3. Maintaining constant wudu was highlighted through the example of a scholar's elder who maintained wudu for 40 years — immediately renewing it whenever it broke. This is a high level of spiritual discipline and is deeply rewarded.

  4. The niyyah should be both internal and verbal — combining the intention of the heart (niyyat al-qalb) with verbalization (talaffuz bil-lisan) is the fuller, more complete form of intention for wudu.

  5. Bismillah at every limb — not just at the start of wudu, but at each individual body part being washed. This is a level of detail many people overlook.

  6. Using the correct hands: Right hand for taking water for the mouth and nose (madmadah/istinshaq), left hand for cleaning/blowing the nose (imtikhat) — following the general Sunnah principle of using the right for noble acts and left for cleansing.

  7. Ring awareness: Anyone wearing a ring should be conscious of whether it is loose or tight. A tight ring must be moved during wudu for validity; this is not merely etiquette but a condition of the wudu.

  8. Performing wudu after sins (ghibah, namimah, lying, sinful speech) is spiritually meaningful — as the hadith indicates, the droplets of water are like sins falling off the body, making minor sins fall away. This provides a practical spiritual reset after slipping into sin.

  9. Wudu before Islamic study and ibadah — the recommendation to be in wudu while studying shari'i knowledge, reciting hadith, making adhan, delivering khutbah, and performing Hajj rituals underscores that wudu is not merely for salah but is a state of spiritual readiness and respect.

  10. Class schedule note: Final classes run until June 27, 2026. Exam prep break: June 28 – July 5. Exams: July 6–11. The class aims to complete Kitab al-Taharah (approximately 20 more pages) before Kitab al-Salah begins at page 59.

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session covered two major topics: (1) the invalidators of wudu (Nawaqid al-Wudu) — nine things which, if they occur, break one's wudu and place a person in a state of hadath; and (2) things that resemble invalidators of wudu but do not actually break it. The session also began the chapter on the obligatory acts (fara'id) and sunnahs of ghusl, covering the three obligatory acts and nine sunnahs of ghusl.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
1. Nawaqid al-Wudu — what breaks wudu and the specific conditions under which each invalidator applies
  2. Things resembling invalidators but not breaking wudu — important distinctions in conditions (e.g., blood staying in its place vs. flowing)
  3. Fara'id al-Ghusl — the three obligatory acts that make ghusl valid
  4. Sunnan al-Ghusl — nine recommended acts that complete ghusl in the best manner

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning نَوَاقِضُ الْوُضُوء Nawaqid al-Wudu — invalidators of wudu يَنْتَقِضُ Yantaqidu — to be invalidated / to break الْحَدَث Hadath — state of ritual impurity (opposite of wudu) السَّبِيلَانِ Al-Sabilayn — the two passages (front and back private parts) الْبَوْل Al-Bawl — urine الْغَائِط Al-Gha'it — excrement الرِّيح Al-Rih — wind/gas الدَّم Al-Dam — blood الْقَيْح Al-Qayh — pus تَجَاوَزَ Tajawaza — to cross over, to pass beyond its place الْبُصَاق Al-Busaq — saliva غَلَبَ Ghalaba — to overpower, to be more than سَوَاهُ Sawahu — equivalent to it قَاءَ Qa'a — to vomit الْعَلَق Al-'Alaqa — blood clot الْمَرَّة Al-Murra — bile الْبَلْغَم Al-Balgham — phlegm/mucus مِلْءُ الْفَمِ Mil' al-Fam — a mouthful (filling the mouth) الْمَقْعَدَة Al-Maq'ada — the buttocks/backside أُغْمِيَ عَلَيْهِ Ughmiya 'alayhi — to faint/lose consciousness جُنَّ Junna — to lose one's sanity, become insane سَكِرَ Sakira — to become intoxicated قَهْقَهَ Qahqaha — to laugh out loud الْيَقْظَان Al-Yaqzan — one who is awake, non-sleeping الْبَالِغ Al-Baligh — a mature person (one who has reached puberty) صَلَاةُ الْجَنَازَة Salat al-Janaza — funeral prayer سَجْدَةُ التِّلَاوَة Sajdat al-Tilawa — prostration of recitation دَوْدَة Dawda — worm عِرْقُ الْمَدَنِي 'Irq al-Madani — a skin disease (known as "Naru" in Urdu) causing flesh to fall off الْإِغْتِسَال Al-Ightisaal — ghusl, ritual bath الْمَضْمَضَة Al-Madmada — rinsing the mouth الِاسْتِنْشَاق Al-Istinshaq — cleaning/sniffing water into the nose إِيصَالُ الْمَاء Isal al-Ma' — making water reach (every part of the body) مُتَوَالِيًا Mutawalyan — continuously, without pause مَنْكِب Mankib — shoulder يَجِفّ Yajiff — to dry up مُنْخَفِض Munkhafid — a lowered/steep place where water gathers

docs.title: Nawaqid al-Wudu (Invalidators of Wudu)
docs.text:
Wudu is broken (yantaqidu al-wudu) when any one of the following nine things occurs:

1. Something exits from one of the two private parts (السَّبِيلَيْن) - Urine, excrement, or wind — all break wudu - Applies to exit from the front or back private part

2. Blood, pus, or wound fluid that moves from its place - If blood, pus, or clear wound liquid comes out and crosses (تَجَاوَزَ) to an area beyond the wound itself (i.e., reaches skin that would normally require cleaning), wudu is broken - Key condition: The substance must move on its own from its place - If the blood stays within the wound and does not flow outward, wudu remains intact - This same ruling applies to pus and the clear fluid that seeps from a healing or unhealed wound

3. Blood from the mouth that equals or exceeds the saliva - If blood from anywhere in the mouth is more than (غَلَبَ) the saliva, or equal to it (أَوْ سَوَاهُ), wudu is broken - If saliva is more than the blood, wudu remains intact - Practical example: brushing teeth after wudu — if gums bleed heavily (blood ≥ saliva), wudu must be remade

4. Vomiting a mouthful - Wudu is broken if one vomits: food, water, a blood clot (عَلَق), bile (مَرَّة), or phlegm — provided it is a mouthful (مِلْءُ الْفَمِ) - Definition of mouthful: the vomit fills the mouth to the point that speech is difficult or impossible - A small burp that brings up a little food is NOT a mouthful — wudu is not broken - Stomach acid is included in this ruling, as it is something being vomited up

5. Falling asleep such that the buttocks leave the ground - If a person falls asleep while sitting and their backside does not remain stable on the ground (they keep tipping/swaying over), wudu is broken - Likewise, if the buttocks of a sleeping person rises off the ground before they awaken (i.e., they are in a deep sleep and fall over), wudu is broken - The key criterion: buttocks are no longer firm and stable on the ground

6. Fainting (الإغماء) - If a person loses consciousness (أُغْمِيَ عَلَيْهِ), wudu is broken

7. Losing one's sanity (الجنون) - If a person becomes insane (جُنَّ), wudu is broken

8. Becoming intoxicated (السُّكر) - If a person becomes intoxicated (سَكِرَ), wudu is broken

9. Laughing out loud during salah (القَهْقَهَة) - Conditions for this to break wudu: 1. The person must be mature (بَالِغ — has reached puberty) 2. The person must be awake (يَقْظَان — not asleep) 3. It must occur during salah (فِي صَلَاة) 4. The salah must contain bowing and prostration (ذَاتُ رُكُوعٍ وَسُجُود) — i.e., any fard or nafl salah (not Salat al-Janaza or Sajdat al-Tilawa) - Wudu is not broken by laughing out loud in the following cases: - A child laughs out loud - A sleeping person laughs out loud during salah (even in a lighter sleep, as it is not a conscious act) - Laughing out loud during Salat al-Janaza (no ruku/sujud) - Laughing out loud during Sajdat al-Tilawa (no ruku/sujud)

docs.title: Things That Resemble Nawaqid but Do NOT Break Wudu
docs.text:
1. Blood appears but does not move from its place - Blood comes to the surface of a wound but stays within it — wudu intact

2. Flesh falls from the body without blood flowing - Example: 'Irq al-Madani (عِرْقُ الْمَدَنِي / "Naru") — a skin disease, common in hot regions, where pieces of flesh fall off - As long as no blood flows from the wound, wudu is intact - The main condition is: blood does not come out (لَمْ يَسِلْ مِنْهُ الدَّم)

3. A worm exits from a wound or from the ear - This does not break wudu

4. Vomiting less than a mouthful - If a person vomits, but it is not a mouthful, wudu does not break

5. Vomiting phlegm (بَلْغَم) - Whether the phlegm is a little or a lot, vomiting phlegm alone does not break wudu

6. A person performing salah falls asleep while in a valid prayer position - If a person falls asleep while standing, sitting, bowing (ruku), or prostrating (sujud), and remains in the correct form (على صِفَةِ السُّنَّة) — i.e., still appears to be praying — wudu does not break - If, however, his hands drop loose (no longer in proper form while standing), wudu breaks, and therefore salah also breaks

7. A person with wudu falls asleep with their buttocks firm on the ground - If a person with wudu falls asleep while sitting and their backside remains firm (مُتَمَكِّنَة) on the ground, wudu does not break

8. Touching one's own private part - Touching one's own private part with the hand does not break wudu (Hanafi position)

9. Touching a woman - Touching a woman does not break wudu (Hanafi position)

10. Swaying while asleep - If a sleeping person sways (as long as their backside remains on the ground), wudu does not break

docs.title: Fara'id al-Ghusl (Obligatory Acts of Ghusl)
docs.text:
There are three obligatory acts of ghusl. If any one is missed, ghusl is invalid:

  1. Al-Madmada (الْمَضْمَضَة) — Rinsing the mouth
  2. Al-Istinshaq (الِاسْتِنْشَاق) — Cleaning/sniffing water into the nose
  3. Isal al-Ma' ila Jami' al-Badan (إِيصَالُ الْمَاءِ إِلَى جَمِيعِ الْبَدَنِ) — Making water reach every part of the body, such that not a single dry spot remains

docs.title: Sunnan al-Ghusl (Recommended Acts of Ghusl)
docs.text:
Observing these sunnahs completes the ghusl in the best manner (عَلَى وَجْهٍ أَكْمَل):

  1. Reciting Bismillah before beginning the ghusl
  2. Making the intention (niyyah) for ghusl — to attain purity (tahara)
  3. Washing the hands up to and including the wrists first, just as one does in wudu
  4. Washing away any impurities on the body or clothing before beginning the ghusl
  5. Performing wudu before ghusl — however, if standing in a lowered/steep place where water gathers (مُنْخَفِض) at the feet, one delays washing the feet until the end (so the feet are washed clean after all impure pooled water has been cleared)
  6. Pouring water over the entire body three times
  7. Pouring water in the following order: first over the head, then right shoulder (مَنْكِب أَيْمَن), then left shoulder (مَنْكِب أَيْسَر)
  8. Rubbing the body (دَلْك) while washing — not merely pouring water over it
  9. Washing the body continuously (مُتَوَالِيًا) — such that the first limb/area does not dry before the second is washed

Note on flowing water: If a person enters a flowing body of water (river, stream) or a large reservoir that is considered in the ruling of flowing water, remains in it, and rubs their body — they have completed the sunnah of ghusl.

docs.title: Conditions for Laughing Out Loud (Qahqaha) Breaking Wudu
docs.text:
All four conditions must be met simultaneously:

Condition Detail Maturity (بُلُوغ) Must be a mature person (post-puberty); does not apply to children Wakefulness (يَقَظَة) Must be awake; sleeping person laughing is not a conscious act During Salah Must occur while in the prayer Prayer contains Ruku & Sujud Applies to all fard and nafl salah; excludes Janaza and Sajdat al-Tilawa

docs.title: Conditions for Blood/Pus Breaking Wudu
docs.text: State of Blood/Pus Ruling Appears on wound surface but stays in place Wudu intact Moves (flows) from its place by itself Wudu broken From the mouth — saliva is more Wudu intact From the mouth — blood = or > saliva Wudu broken

docs.title: Conditions for Sleep Breaking Wudu
docs.text: Sleep Scenario Ruling Asleep sitting, backside firm on ground Wudu intact Asleep sitting, swaying/tipping (backside unstable) Wudu broken Asleep, buttocks rise off ground before waking Wudu broken Asleep lying down Wudu broken Asleep in salah in correct form (standing, sitting, ruku, sujud) Wudu intact Asleep in salah, hands fall (loses sunnah form) Wudu broken

docs.title: Scholarly Opinions
docs.text:
• The rulings covered follow the Hanafi madhab, as is the standard for this course.
  • The ruling that touching a woman or touching one's own private part does not break wudu is specific to the Hanafi position (contrasting with Shafi'i and Maliki positions where these do break wudu).
  • The ruling on qahqaha (loud laughter in salah breaking wudu) is a position particular to the Hanafi school. Other madhabs do not hold this view.

docs.title: Lessons & Practical Application
docs.text:
1. Brushing teeth after wudu: Check the amount of blood vs. saliva when brushing. If gums bleed lightly and saliva is clearly more, wudu remains. If there is a significant amount of blood (equal to or more than saliva), remake wudu before salah.

  2. Wounds during wudu/salah: A cut or wound does not break wudu unless the blood actually flows out from the wound. As long as blood stays within the wound site, one may continue salah.

  3. Vomiting: A small reflux or burp with a little food is not a mouthful and does not break wudu. Only when the vomit fills the mouth (making speech difficult) is it considered a mouthful.

  4. Dozing off: Nodding off while seated with one's backside on the ground (e.g., during a lecture or after salah) does not break wudu. Falling into a deep sleep where one falls over does.

  5. Salah with drowsiness: If a person is very sleepy in salah and sways but maintains proper form, their wudu and salah are intact. The moment the proper form is lost (e.g., hands drop while standing), wudu breaks.

  6. Ghusl practice: Begin ghusl with Bismillah and niyyah, wash hands first, remove any impurities, perform wudu (delaying feet if water pools), then pour water over the head, right shoulder, and left shoulder — rubbing the body throughout — and wash continuously without letting any part dry.

  7. Ghusl in a shower: The sunnah of delaying washing the feet applies when water pools at one's feet (e.g., a clogged drain). Wash feet last so that accumulated used water on the floor can be washed away cleanly.

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session covered Aqsam al-Ghusl (the types/categories of ritual bath). The lesson systematically categorized ghusl into three levels of obligation — fard, sunnah, and mandub/mustahab — and detailed the specific occasions that fall under each category. A brief discussion also addressed the ruling on washing a martyr (shaheed) and the ruling on a new Muslim performing ghusl.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
• The three-tier classification of ghusl: obligatory, recommended (sunnah), and preferred (mustahab)
  • The four causes that make ghusl obligatory (fard)
  • The four occasions that make ghusl a sunnah
  • The eighteen occasions where ghusl is mustahab/mandub
  • The distinction between the ruling on a shaheed dying in battle vs. other forms of martyrdom
  • The ruling on a new Muslim performing ghusl based on their state of purity at the time of conversion

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning أَقْسَامُ الْغُسْل (Aqsam al-Ghusl) The categories/types of ritual bath غُسْل (Ghusl) Full ritual bath/purification فَرْض (Fard) Obligatory سُنَّة (Sunnah) Prophetically recommended مَنْدُوب / مُسْتَحَب (Mandub / Mustahab) Preferred/desirable (not obligatory) جَنَابَة (Janabah) State of major ritual impurity جُنُب (Junub) A person in the state of janabah حَيْض (Haydh) Menstrual bleeding نِفَاس (Nifas) Postnatal bleeding (after childbirth) شَهِيد (Shaheed) Martyr إِحْرَام (Ihram) State of consecration for Hajj or Umrah عَرَفَة (Arafah) The plain of Arafah (Hajj station) زَوَال الشَّمْس (Zawal al-Shams) Midday (when the sun passes its zenith) لَيْلَة النِّصْف مِنْ شَعْبَان (Laylat al-Nisf min Sha'ban) The 15th night of Sha'ban اسْتِسْقَاء (Istisqa) Prayer for rain (performed during drought) فَزَع (Faza') Fear/panic ظُلْمَة (Dhulmah) Darkness رِيح شَدِيدَة (Rih Shadida) Severe/strong wind تَوْبَة (Tawbah) Repentance حِجَامَة (Hijamah) Cupping therapy مُزْدَلِفَة (Muzdalifah) Station between Arafah and Mina during Hajj يَوْم النَّحْر (Yawm al-Nahr) Day of sacrifice (10th of Dhul Hijjah) طَوَاف الزِّيَارَة (Tawaf al-Ziyarah) The obligatory tawaf of Hajj صَبِيحَة (Sabihah) The morning of الْمَدِينَة الْمُنَوَّرَة (al-Madinah al-Munawwarah) The illuminated city of Madinah مَكَّة الْمُشَرَّفَة (Makkah al-Musharrafah) The noble city of Makkah

docs.title: Classification of Ghusl
docs.text: Ghusl is divided into three categories: 1. فَرْض (Fard) — Obligatory 2. سُنَّة (Sunnah) — Recommended 3. مَنْدُوب / مُسْتَحَب (Mandub / Mustahab) — Preferred/desirable

docs.title: Fard Ghusl — The Four Causes
docs.text:
يُفْتَرَضُ الْغُسْلُ بِوَاحِدٍ مِنْ أَرْبَعَةِ أُمُورٍ "Ghusl is obligatory due to one of the following four reasons:"

# Cause Arabic 1 A person is in the state of janabah (major ritual impurity) إِذَا كَانَ جُنُوبًا 2 A woman becomes pure from her menstrual cycle (haydh) إِذَا طَهُرَتْ مِنَ الْحَيْض 3 A woman becomes pure from postnatal bleeding (nifas) إِذَا طَهُرَتْ مِنَ النِّفَاس 4 It is obligatory upon the living to wash a deceased Muslim يُفْتَرَضُ تَغْسِيل الْمَيِّت عَلَى الْأَحْيَاء

Note on washing the deceased (Cause #4): - The obligation of washing the deceased falls on the living, not the deceased person himself. - A shaheed (martyr) who dies in battle is not washed; he is buried in his clothes and salah is performed over him. - There are two types of shaheed: one who died in actual battlefield combat (kukman shaheed) and one who died through other means (e.g., drowning, collapsing building, killed in self-defense). Their rulings differ regarding washing, salah, and burial — to be confirmed.

docs.title: Sunnah Ghusl — The Four Occasions
docs.text:
يُسَنَّ الْغُسْلُ لِأَرْبَعَةِ أَشَاءٍ "Ghusl is sunnah for four things:"

# Occasion 1 For Salat al-Jumu'ah (Friday prayer) 2 For Eid prayer (Eid al-Fitr and Eid al-Adha) 3 For a person entering into the state of Ihram 4 For a pilgrim (haji) in the plain of Arafah after zawal al-shams (mid-afternoon)

docs.title: Mustahab/Mandub Ghusl — The Eighteen Occasions
docs.text: # Occasion 1 On the 15th night of Sha'ban (Laylat al-Nisf min Sha'ban) 2 On Laylat al-Qadr (the Night of Power — on the nights most likely to be it) 3 Before the solar eclipse prayer (Kusuf) 4 Before the lunar eclipse prayer (Khusuf) 5 Before Salat al-Istisqa (prayer for rain during drought) 6 During a time of fear (عند فَزَع) 7 During a time of darkness (عند ظُلْمَة) 8 During severe winds (عند رِيح شَدِيدَة) 9 When wearing new clothing (عند لُبْس ثَوْب جَدِيد) 10 When making tawbah (repentance) from a sin 11 Upon returning from a journey (للَّذِي قَدِمَ مِنْ سَفَر) 12 When intending to enter al-Madinah al-Munawwarah 13 When intending to enter Makkah al-Musharrafah 14 When staying in Muzdalifah on the morning of Yawm al-Nahr (10th of Dhul Hijjah) 15 Before performing Tawaf al-Ziyarah (the obligatory tawaf of Hajj) 16 After washing a deceased person (للَّذِي غَسَّلَ مَيِّتًا) 17 After hijamah (cupping therapy) 18 Upon recovering from insanity, fainting, or intoxication

docs.title: Ruling on the New Muslim Performing Ghusl
docs.text:
Scenario Ruling A person accepts Islam while already in a state of purity Ghusl is mustahab (preferred, not obligatory) A person accepts Islam while in a state of janabah (major ritual impurity) Ghusl is fard (obligatory) upon him

Arabic text: - أَمَّا إِذَا كَانَ الَّذِي أَسْلَمَ جُنُوبًا فَيُفْتَرَضُ عَلَيْهِ الْغُسْلُ - "As for the one who accepts Islam while in a state of janabah, ghusl is obligatory upon him."

docs.title: Summary Table — Three Levels of Ghusl
docs.text: Level Arabic Number of Occasions Obligatory فَرْض 4 causes Sunnah سُنَّة 4 occasions Preferred مُسْتَحَب / مَنْدُوب 18 occasions

docs.title: Key Conditions to Note
docs.text:
• The obligation to wash the deceased (Cause #4 of fard ghusl) is a communal obligation (fard kifayah) — it falls on the living as a community, not on the deceased.
  • The mustahab occasions of ghusl are largely connected to worship, acts of obedience, or entry into sacred spaces — indicating that a person should present himself in a state of complete cleanliness before acts of worship or drawing near to Allah.
  • Non-Muslims do not have Islamic purification procedures for janabah. If they embrace Islam while in that state, ghusl immediately becomes fard upon them.

docs.title: Scholarly Opinions
docs.text:
• The distinction between the two types of shaheed (battlefield martyr vs. other forms of martyrdom, e.g., drowning, crushed under rubble, killed in self-defense) results in differing rulings regarding:
  • Whether ghusl is performed
  • Whether janazah salah is prayed
  • How the body is buried
  • The teacher noted he would verify the precise distinctions between the two types (kukman shaheed vs. actual battlefield martyr) for a future class.

docs.title: Lessons & Practical Application
docs.text:
1. Ghusl has a hierarchy — not every occasion requiring ghusl carries the same legal weight. Knowing the difference between fard, sunnah, and mustahab allows a Muslim to prioritize correctly and avoid sin where there is an obligation.

  2. Cleanliness before worship — the 18 mustahab occasions of ghusl are largely tied to moments of worship or approaching Allah (tawbah, salah, entering sacred cities). This reinforces the Islamic principle that external purity reflects and supports internal readiness for worship.

  3. Istisqa reminder — Salat al-Istisqa is performed during drought as a communal act of humility before Allah, going out in simple clothing to make du'a for rain. Ghusl before this salah is mustahab.

  4. New Muslims — if someone accepts Islam while in janabah, ghusl is immediately fard. Da'ees (callers to Islam) and community members should be aware of this ruling to guide new Muslims properly.

  5. This chapter (Aqsam al-Ghusl) is the last chapter before the chapter of Salah. The class will continue from here in the next session, possibly after completing the remaining four hadiths in the Hadith class.

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This was the final Fiqh class of the academic year. The session covered three major topics: (1) the legal basis (mashru'iyyah) and conditions (shurut) of Tayammum, its integrals (arkan), sunnahs, method of performance, and its invalidators; (2) wiping over leather/thick socks (masah 'ala al-khuffayn) — its conditions, time periods, and invalidators; and (3) wiping over wound dressings and casts (masah 'ala al-'isabah wal-jabirah). The session also included miscellaneous rulings connecting intention to validity of Tayammum for various acts of worship.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
• Tayammum is a divinely granted concession enabling worship when water is unavailable or harmful to use
  • The validity of Tayammum depends strictly on eight conditions, particularly the correctness of intention (niyyah)
  • Intention must be made for an ibadah maqsudah (a purposive act of worship) for Tayammum to be valid for Salah
  • Masah 'ala al-khuffayn is a concession granted by Shari'ah to ease the practice of Wudu — not an independent act of worship
  • Islamic law consistently removes hardship (haraj) from worship, as evidenced by rulings on tayammum, masah, and wound dressings
  • The same invalidators that break Wudu break Tayammum and Masah al-khuffayn

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning تَيَمُّم (Tayammum) Linguistically: intention / to intend. Legally: purification using clean earth, involving wiping the face and hands including elbows صَعِيد (Sa'id) Dirt / earth طَيِّب (Tayyib) Clean / pure مَشْرُوعِيَّة (Mashru'iyyah) Legality within Shari'ah; the subject's place in Islamic law شُرُوط (Shurut) Conditions (plural of shart) أَرْكَان (Arkan) Integrals / pillars (plural of rukn) سُنَن (Sunan) Recommended acts / traditions نَوَاقِض (Nawaqid) Invalidators (plural of naqid) حَدَث (Hadath) State of ritual impurity حَدَث أَصْغَر (Hadath Asghar) Minor ritual impurity (requiring Wudu) إِبَاحَة الصَّلَاة (Ibahatu al-Salah) Permissibility of performing Salah عِبَادَة مَقْصُودَة (Ibadah Maqsudah) A purposive act of worship — one that yields reward and cannot be validly performed without purification عِوَض ('Iwad) In replacement of / in lieu of مَفْقُود (Mafqud) Unavailable / missing مَعْذُور (Ma'dhur) One who has a valid excuse حَائِل (Ha'il) Barrier (between skin and wiping) مَسْح عَلَى الخُفَّيْن (Masah 'ala al-Khuffayn) Wiping over leather/thick socks in place of washing the feet during Wudu خُفَّيْن (Khuffayn) Leather socks (dual) كَعْبَيْن (Ka'bayn) The two ankles خَرْق (Kharq) Hole / tear (in a sock) عِصَابَة ('Isabah) Wound dressing / bandage / gauze جَبِيرَة (Jabirah) Splint or cast رَمَدَ (Ramada) To suffer from sore eyes حَادِق (Hadiq) Skilled / experienced التِّلَاوَة (Al-Tilawah) Recitation of the Qur'an مُحْدِث (Muhdith) One in a state of hadath مُقِيم (Muqim) Resident (non-traveler) مُسَافِر (Musafir) Traveler صُفُوف (Sufuf) Rows تُرْبَة (Turbah) Soil / earth طَهُور (Tahur) Purifying (that which purifies) مَسْجِد (Masjid) Place of prostration / prayer (in the hadith, meaning any place on earth) شَمَّرَ (Shammara) To pull up one's sleeves سَاعِد (Sa'id) Forearm مِرْفَق (Mirfaq) Elbow اسْتِبَاحَة (Istibahah) Making something permissible for oneself through a valid act

docs.title: Qur'anic Evidence for Tayammum
docs.text:
Surah al-Nisa (4:43) / al-Ma'idah (5:6):

"...wa in kuntum marda aw 'ala safarin aw ja'a ahadun minkum min al-gha'it aw lamastumu al-nisa'a falam tajidu ma'an fatayyamamu sa'idan tayyiban famsahu biwujuhikum wa aydikum. Inna Allaha kana 'afuwwan ghafura."

"If you are sick, or on a journey, or any of you has used the restroom, or has had intimate relations and you find no water, then perform Tayammum with clean earth — wipe your faces and hands. Indeed Allah is Most Pardoning, Most Forgiving."

docs.title: Hadith Evidence for Tayammum
docs.text:
The Prophet ﷺ said:

"Fuddiina 'ala al-naas bi-thalathin: ju'ilat sufufuna kasufuf al-mala'ikah, wa ju'ilat lana al-ardu kulluha masjidan, wa ju'ilat turbataha lana tahuran idha lam najid al-ma'."

"We have been given virtue over people in three things: our rows have been made like the rows of the angels; the entire earth has been made a place of prayer for us; and its soil has been made purifying for us when we do not find water."

docs.title: Reason Tayammum Was Legislated
docs.text: Tayammum was prescribed because human beings sometimes cannot use water — either due to water being unavailable (mafqud) or due to illness — so that people are not deprived of performing acts of worship (like Salah) that require purification.

docs.title: Hadith Evidence for Masah al-Khuffayn
docs.text:
The Prophet ﷺ said:

"Al-mashu 'ala al-khuffayn lil-musafir thalathu ayyamin wa layaliyha, wa lil-muqim yawm wa laylah."

"Wiping over leather socks is permissible for a traveler for three days and nights, and for a resident for one day and one night."

docs.title: Qur'anic Evidence for Concessions (Masah over Wound Dressings)
docs.text: "Wa ma ja'ala 'alaykum fi al-dini min haraj." "He has not placed any hardship upon you in the religion." (Surah al-Hajj 22:78)

docs.title: Conditions of Valid Tayammum (8 Conditions)
docs.text:
1. Niyyah (Intention) Tayammum is invalid without intention. The intention must be for one of three things: - Purification from hadath - The permissibility of Salah (istibahatu al-salah) - An ibadah maqsudah — a purposive act of worship that cannot be validly performed without purification (e.g., Salah, Sajdatu al-Tilawah)

Key rulings on intention: - It is not necessary to specify which hadath in the intention - If a person makes Tayammum with the intention of touching the Mus'haf: Salah will not be valid, because merely touching the Qur'an is not an ibadah in itself — the actual ibadah is tilawah (recitation) - If a person makes Tayammum with the intention of calling the Adhan or Iqamah: Salah will not be valid, as Adhan and Iqamah are not stand-alone ibadah maqsudah - If a person makes Tayammum with the intention of recitation of Qur'an while in a state of hadath asghar: Salah will not be valid, because recitation of Qur'an — though it is an ibadah maqsudah — can be performed without Wudu - If a person makes Tayammum with the intention of Salah: all other acts (touching the Qur'an, reciting, Sajdatu al-Tilawah) become permissible under that same Tayammum

2. Presence of a Valid Excuse One of the following excusable circumstances must exist: 1. Water is more than a mile away 2. A skilled Muslim doctor informs the person that using water will cause illness, worsen existing illness, or delay recovery 3. The person overwhelmingly fears that using cold water (the only water available) will cause death or severe harm 4. The person fears thirst for himself or a companion if water is used for Wudu (water is insufficient for both drinking and purification) 5. No instrument (bucket, rope) is available to extract water from a well 6. An enemy — human or carnivorous animal (hayawan muftaris) — stands between the person and the water 7. The person overwhelmingly fears that performing Wudu will cause him to miss 'Eid Salah or Salatu al-Janazah (prayers with no qada)

Important distinction: If a person fears missing a regular Fard Salah or Jumu'ah due to performing Wudu, Tayammum is not permissible. He must perform Wudu, pray, and make qada of the missed obligatory prayer. For Jumu'ah specifically, he will pray Zuhr as a substitute ('iwadan 'an al-Jumu'ah).

3. Use of a Pure Earthy Substance Tayammum must be performed with something from the earth (jins al-ard): dirt (turab), stone (hajar), or thick dust (raml). Tayammum is not valid with wood, silver, or gold.

4. Wiping the Entire Face and Both Arms Including Elbows The entirety (or majority) of the full hand must be used. If a person uses only two fingers to wipe — even if he covers the entire arm — the Tayammum is not valid because the majority of the hand was not used.

5. [Implicit from text] The wiping must be with both hands on both body parts.

6. Two Strikes with the Inner Palms (Darbatayn bi-batin al-kaffayn) A person must perform two strikes — one for the face, one for the arms. If a person strikes twice in the same place, or dirt falls onto his body and he wipes it without striking, the Tayammum remains valid.

7. No Barrier Between Wiping and Skin Nothing must be present between the hand and the skin: wax (sham'), grease (shahm), thick paint, super glue, nail polish (for sisters), etc. These must be removed before wiping. If present and not removed, Tayammum is invalid.

8. No Ongoing Invalidator Nothing that invalidates Tayammum should occur during the performance of Tayammum, such as: - Menstrual bleeding (hayd) - Postnatal bleeding (nifas) - Hadath occurring mid-performance (passing wind, vomiting, bleeding — unless the person is ma'dhur)

docs.title: Integrals (Arkan) of Tayammum
docs.text: There are only two physical integrals (plus intention as the internal integral): 1. Wiping the entirety of the face (mashu jami' al-wajh) 2. Wiping both arms including the elbows (mashu al-yadayn ma'a al-mirfaqayn)

docs.title: Sunnahs of Tayammum
docs.text:
1. Reciting Bismillah al-Rahman al-Rahim at the beginning
  2. Following the order: face first, then right arm, then left arm
  3. Not separating the wiping of the face and hands with a foreign action (fi'l ajnabi)
  4. Moving the hands forwards and backwards (iqbal wa idbar) in the dirt to get a good amount on the hands
  5. Dusting off the hands (nafd) after lifting them from the dirt, before wiping
  6. Keeping the fingers spread apart when placing the hands in the dirt

docs.title: Method of Performing Tayammum (Kayfiyyah)
docs.text:
1. Pull sleeves up above the elbows (shammara 'an sa'idayhi)
  2. Recite Bismillah al-Rahman al-Rahim
  3. Make intention (niyyah) — e.g., for permissibility of Salah (istibahatu al-salah)
  4. Place the inner palms on pure earth with fingers spread apart, moving hands forward and backward
  5. Raise hands and dust them off
  6. Wipe the entire face with both hands
  7. Place hands on earth a second time in the same manner
  8. Wipe the right arm entirely (including elbow) with the entire left hand
  9. Wipe the left arm entirely (including elbow) with the right hand
  10. Tayammum is now complete — one may now perform as many Fard and Nafl prayers as desired

docs.title: Invalidators (Nawaqid) of Tayammum
docs.text:
1. Everything that invalidates Wudu also invalidates Tayammum
  2. Finding and gaining the ability to use water (al-qudrah 'ala isti'mal al-ma')
  3. The disappearance of the excuse that originally made Tayammum permissible (e.g., enemy is gone, illness resolved, water found)

docs.title: Miscellaneous Rulings on Tayammum
docs.text: Situation Ruling Tayammum made for entering a Masjid Cannot perform Salah with it (not an ibadah maqsudah; also Wudu not required to enter Masjid) Tayammum made for I'tikaf I'tikaf itself is not the issue — but Ghusl (if in janabah) still does not permit Salah without intention for Salah Tayammum made with intention of visiting graves or burying the deceased Salah is not permissible with this Tayammum Person hopes to find water before Salah time exits It is mustahab to delay Tayammum and wait Someone has promised to bring the person water It is wajib (yajib) to wait for the water Person has little water needed for kneading flour (making bread) He should make Tayammum and use the water for food Person has little water needed for making gravy/curry He must use the water for Wudu — gravy is liquid and not a substitute survival need in the same category Seeking water from a companion Obligatory to ask if in a place where people are generally not stingy with water; not obligatory if people in the area are known to be stingy Performing Tayammum before Salah time enters Permissible if the person is not in the ma'dhur category and has no expectation of finding water Person whose hands and feet are amputated and whose face is injured Prays without any purification Majority of limbs are injured Performs Tayammum, then wipes over the injured parts Majority of limbs are uninjured Performs Wudu and wipes over the injured parts

docs.title: Conditions for Masah 'ala al-Khuffayn (6 Conditions)
docs.text:
1. The socks must have been put on while in a state of Taharah (Wudu) — specifically, the feet must have been washed before putting them on. If a person washes his feet, puts on the socks, then completes Wudu — this is valid, as long as Wudu was not broken between washing the feet and completing Wudu.
  2. The socks must cover the ankles (ka'bayn) — ankle socks do not qualify.
  3. The socks must be free of holes equal to or larger than the size of the three smallest toes (thalathu asabi' min asghar asabi' al-qadam). If the holes are that large or larger, the socks cannot be used for masah.
  4. The socks must stay on the feet without needing to be tied (yastamsika 'ala al-rijlayn biduni shadd) — they must not fall off when the foot is lifted.
  5. The socks must prevent water from reaching the feet — they must be waterproof.
  6. A person must be able to walk consistently while wearing them.

docs.title: The Obligatory (Fard) and Sunnah Amount of Masah al-Khuffayn
docs.text: Ruling Fard (minimum): Equivalent of the three smallest fingers of the hand (min asghar asabi' al-yad) wiped on the top (zahir) of each foot Sunnah: Keep fingers spread wide and wipe from the tips of the toes up to the shin (saq), starting from the bottom upward on the top of the foot

docs.title: Time Periods for Masah al-Khuffayn
docs.text:
Person Duration Resident (Muqim) 1 day and 1 night = 24 hours Traveler (Musafir) 3 days and 3 nights = 72 hours

When does the clock start? The time period begins from the first time Wudu breaks after putting on the socks while in a state of Taharah — not from when the socks were put on or when Wudu was completed.

docs.title: Transitioning Between Resident and Traveler Status
docs.text: Situation Ruling Resident begins traveling before his 24 hours expire He completes the remaining time up to 72 hours (the traveler's period) Traveler returns home (becomes resident) after 48 hours of wiping He must remove socks and restart; his time period ends when he becomes a resident Traveler returns home within 24 hours (still within resident period) He completes the 24-hour resident period and then is done Musafir wiped for less than 1 day then becomes a resident He completes 1 full day and night (yawm wa laylah) as the resident period

docs.title: Invalidators of Masah al-Khuffayn
docs.text:
1. Everything that invalidates Wudu
  2. Removing either or both socks (naz' al-khuff) — even one sock requires restarting
  3. Majority of the foot slipping out of the top of the sock (saq al-khuff)
  4. Expiry of the time period (intiha' muddatihi)
  5. Water reaching the majority of one foot inside the sock (sock is no longer waterproof)

docs.title: What Cannot Be Used for Masah
docs.text:
Item Ruling Turban ('imamah) Masah over it is not permissible in place of wiping the head Topi / head cap (qalansuwah) Not permissible for masah of the head Burqa / face veil (burqu') Not permissible for masah of the head Gloves (quffazayn) Not permissible for masah in place of washing the hands

The only concession for wiping over a covering is for the feet, using socks that meet the specified conditions.

docs.title: Masah over Wound Dressings ('Isabah) and Casts (Jabirah)
docs.text:
Conditions and Rulings:

  • If a person cannot wash or wipe a body part due to injury, he may wipe the majority of the wound dressing or cast from on top
  • This continues until the wound heals (ila an yalta'ima al-jurh)
  • It is not a condition that the dressing be tied upon a state of Taharah (impurity such as blood under the bandage does not affect validity)
  • The same applies to a cast (jabirah): wipe over it until healed; no condition of Taharah for putting it on
Situation Ruling One foot has a cast, the other is healthy Wipe over the cast on the injured foot; wash the other foot normally Cast falls off before wound heals Masah remains valid; no need to rewipe (if already wiped once) Cast is changed to a new one Not obligatory to wipe the new cast, but it is better (afadal) to wipe it Person has sore eyes and skilled Muslim doctor forbids washing eyes Wiping over the eyes is permissible

docs.title: Niyyah in Masah
docs.text: Act Niyyah Required? Masah 'ala al-Khuffayn Not required Masah 'ala al-Jabirah (cast) Not required Masah of the head (in Wudu) Not required Tayammum Required — the only act among these where Niyyah is a condition

docs.title: Scholarly Opinions
docs.text:
• The Hanafi position (implied throughout) is that Tayammum is a full substitute for Wudu and Ghusl when valid excuses exist, and a single Tayammum made for Salah covers all subsidiary acts of worship
  • The ruling that adhan and iqamah are not ibadah maqsudah is consistent with the position that Wudu is not obligatory for them (though it is recommended), and missing them does not require qada
  • The ruling distinguishing between fear of missing 'Eid/'Janazah (permits Tayammum) versus fear of missing Fard/Jumu'ah (does not permit Tayammum) reflects the principle that prayers with qada can be made up, while those without cannot

docs.title: Lessons & Practical Application
docs.text:
1. Intention determines validity: Before performing Tayammum, be clear about what ibadah you are performing it for. Intending Salah covers the broadest scope of subsequent worship acts.

  2. Know your excuses: Tayammum is not freely done — a specific, valid excuse from the list must be present. The most common in daily life are unavailability of water, illness, or fear of harm from water.

  3. The earth is your purifier: The Prophet ﷺ reminded us that the entire earth is a Masjid and its soil is tahur. This is a unique blessing of this ummah.

  4. Masah al-Khuffayn is a real concession: In cold weather or travel, wiping over thick socks that meet the six conditions is a fully valid and legitimate alternative to washing the feet. Learn the conditions and time periods carefully.

  5. Injured individuals are not excused from worship: Rather, they are given accommodation. A person with a cast does not skip Wudu — they simply wipe over the cast. A person with amputated limbs still performs Salah.

  6. The deen removes hardship: All of these concessions — Tayammum, masah, wiping over casts — reflect the Qur'anic principle: "Allah intends ease for you and does not intend hardship." Fiqh is not a set of obstacles but a framework of facilitation.

  7. Practical Tayammum sequence to memorize: Roll up sleeves → Bismillah → Niyyah → Strike earth (fingers spread) → Move hands forward and back → Lift and dust off → Wipe face → Strike earth again → Wipe right arm with left hand → Wipe left arm with right hand.

  8. Seeking water is itself obligatory: If a companion has water, asking for it is wajib (in places where people do not withhold water). If promised water, waiting is wajib. Tayammum is not a shortcut — it is a last resort.

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: Introduction to Hadith 101 focusing on the 40 Hadith of Imam Nawawi (rahmatullahi wabarakatuh). The session covered the foundational understanding of hadith as a science, the different types of divine revelation (wahi), and a comprehensive biography of Imam Nawawi. Emphasis was placed on the importance of hadith as a source of Islamic guidance alongside the Quran, and the immense scholarly contributions of Imam Nawawi despite his relatively short life.

docs.title: Hadith Text and Chain of Narration
docs.text: Not covered in this session.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
1. The Definition and Classification of Hadith: Understanding hadith as actions, sayings, or tacit approval of Prophet Muhammad (ﷺ)
  2. Divine Revelation (Wahi): Distinction between wahi matlu (recited revelation - Quran) and wahi ghayr matlu (non-recited revelation - hadith)
  3. Scholarly Excellence: The remarkable achievements of Imam Nawawi in his brief 45-year life
  4. The Importance of Learning: Drawing inspiration from the scholars' dedication to knowledge
  5. Preservation of Islamic Knowledge: The rigorous science of hadith authentication and narrator evaluation

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Hadith Saying, action, or tacit approval of Prophet Muhammad (ﷺ) Wahi Divine revelation Wahi matlu Recited revelation (referring to the Quran) Wahi ghayr matlu Non-recited revelation (referring to hadith) Taqreer Tacit approval Qawl Saying/statement Fi'l Action Shama'il Hadith describing the physical characteristics and mannerisms of the Prophet (ﷺ) Hadith Qudsi Sacred hadith where the Prophet (ﷺ) quotes Allah directly Jarh wa ta'dil Science of narrator criticism and authentication Asma al-rijal Study of hadith narrator biographies Ism mansub Name attributed to a place of origin Qubuliyah Divine acceptance Shuhra Fame/reputation

docs.title: Explanation and Commentary
docs.text:
The session established that hadith represents one of two forms of divine revelation. While the Quran constitutes wahi matlu (recited revelation) preserved through mass transmission (tawatur), hadith represents wahi ghayr matlu (non-recited revelation) based on Quran 53:3-4: "He does not speak from desire. It is nothing but revelation revealed to him."

The three categories of hadith are: 1. Sunnah al-Qawliyyah - Prophetic statements 2. Sunnah al-Fi'liyyah - Prophetic actions 3. Sunnah al-Taqririyyah - Prophetic tacit approval

The distinction between Quranic preservation and hadith preservation was explained: while the Quran maintained exact wording through mass transmission, hadith sometimes preserved meaning through paraphrasing, leading to the development of rigorous authentication sciences.

Imam Nawawi's biography demonstrates extraordinary scholarly dedication. Born in Nawa (near Damascus) in 631 AH, he moved to Damascus at age 19 to study under renowned scholars. His methodology included reading 12 lessons daily while writing commentaries and marginal notes - a practice that enhanced retention and understanding.

docs.title: Fiqh Rulings Derived
docs.text: Not covered in this session.

docs.title: Lessons and Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Continuous Learning: Following Imam Nawawi's example of lifelong dedication to knowledge acquisition
  2. Quality over Quantity: Despite living only 45 years, Imam Nawawi's works remain globally influential, teaching us that impact matters more than duration
  3. Comprehensive Approach: The importance of not just reading but also writing and reflecting on knowledge
  4. Divine Acceptance: When Allah accepts someone's work, it gains lasting benefit for humanity - as evidenced by the enduring popularity of works like Riyadh al-Saliheen and the 40 Hadith
  5. Scholarly Rigor: The importance of maintaining high standards in preserving and transmitting Islamic knowledge
  6. Practical Application: The value of taking detailed notes and actively engaging with the material for better retention

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session focused on introducing the first hadith in Imam Nawawi's collection: the famous hadith of niyyah (intention). The instructor emphasized the importance of proper note-taking habits early in Islamic studies, as teachers will move more quickly in advanced classes. The session covered the Arabic text reading, basic translation, and introductory commentary on the significance of intention in Islamic actions. The hadith was presented as foundational for understanding the correct mindset needed before studying any Islamic text.

docs.title: Hadith Text and Chain of Narration
docs.text:
Arabic Text: عَنْ أَمِيرِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَبِي حَفْصٍ عُمَرَ بِنِ الْخَطَّابِ رَضِيَ اللَّهُ تَعَالَى عَنْهُ قَالَ سَمِعْتُ رَسُولَ اللَّهِ صَلَّى اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ يَقُولُ إِنَّمَا الْأَعْمَالُ بِالنِّيَّاتِ وَإِنَّمَا لِكُلِّ امْرِئٍ مَا نَوَى فَمَنْ كَانَتْ هِجْرَتُهُ إِلَى اللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ فَهِجْرَتُهُ إِلَى اللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ وَمَنْ كَانَتْ هِجْرَتُهُ لِدُنْيَا يُصِيبُهَا أَوِ امْرَأَةٍ يَنْكِحُهَا فَهِجْرَتُهُ إِلَى مَا هَاجَرَ إِلَيْهِ

Translation: It is narrated by Amir al-Mu'minin Abu Hafs Umar ibn al-Khattab (may Allah be pleased with him) who said: "I heard the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him) saying: 'Verily, actions are according to their intentions, and every person receives what they intend. So whosoever migrated to Allah and His Messenger, then his migration is to Allah and His Messenger. And whosoever migrated to obtain something worldly or to marry a woman, then his migration will be for the sake of whatever he migrated for.'"

Chain of Narration: - Narrator: Umar ibn al-Khattab (رضي الله عنه) - Title: Amir al-Mu'minin Abu Hafs - Direct statement: "I heard the Messenger of Allah saying..."

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
1. Centrality of Intention (Niyyah): All actions are judged by the intentions behind them
  2. Divine Justice: Each person receives according to their true intention
  3. Migration (Hijrah) as Example: The Prophet used migration as a practical example to illustrate the principle
  4. Sincerity vs. Worldly Motives: Contrast between those who act for Allah's sake versus worldly gains
  5. Universal Principle: This hadith applies to all Islamic actions and worship

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning إِنَّمَا (innama) Verily, indeed (restrictive particle) الْأَعْمَالُ (al-a'mal) Actions, deeds بِالنِّيَّاتِ (bil-niyyat) According to intentions امْرِئٍ (imri') Individual person نَوَى (nawa) Intended هِجْرَة (hijrah) Migration يُصِيبُهَا (yusibuha) To obtain/acquire it يَنْكِحُهَا (yankihuha) To marry her هَاجَرَ (hajara) Migrated

docs.title: Historical Context and Placement
docs.text:
• This hadith is known as "hadith al-niyyah" (the hadith of intention)
  • Scholars traditionally place this hadith at the beginning of their books
  • Purpose: To establish the correct mindset and understanding before readers proceed with the text
  • Ensures that whatever knowledge follows will be approached with the proper heart condition

docs.title: Linguistic Analysis
