docs.title: Translation Exercises:
docs.text:
Arabic to English: - اذهب (Go) - لا تذهب (Don't go) - لا تمنعوا (Don't prevent/stop - plural male) - لا تبدئي (Don't start - fem. sing.)

English to Arabic: - Don't go to the market, but go to the mosque → لا تذهبوا إلى السوق بل اذهبوا إلى المسجد - Open the box → افتح الصندوق - Work for yourselves → اعملوا لأنفسكم - Don't laugh a lot → لا تضحكوا كثيراً

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Nahi formation is simpler than Amr - only 2 steps vs 4 steps for imperative
  2. Always use second person for both Amr and Nahi (person being addressed)
  3. Preposition usage varies by verb - must be memorized, no set rules
  4. Vocabulary memorization is crucial for proper sentence construction
  5. Practice tables regularly - essential for fluent conjugation
  6. Mudare knowledge required - Nahi patterns depend on knowing present tense forms
  7. Context determines translation - Arabic prepositions don't always match English equivalents

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session covered Lesson 10 focusing on Arabic number forms: singular (al-wahid), dual (al-tasniya), and plural (al-jama'). The lesson emphasized the unique dual form in Arabic and the rules for forming sound masculine plural (jama' mudhakkar salim). Practice exercises reinforced these concepts with vocabulary application.

docs.title: Dual Form (Tasniya) Rules:
docs.text:
• Rafa' case: Add الف نون (alif noon) with kasra under noon
  • Nasab/Jar case: Add يا نون (ya noon) with kasra under noon
  • Examples: كتابان (kitabani) → كتابين (kitabayni)

docs.title: Sound Masculine Plural (Jama' Mudhakkar Salim) Rules:
docs.text:
• Rafa' case: Add واو نون (waw noon) with fatha under noon
  • Nasab/Jar case: Add يا نون (ya noon) with fatha under noon
  • Examples: مسلمون (muslimuna) → مسلمين (muslimina)

docs.title: Case Recognition:
docs.text:
• In dual forms: الف indicates rafa', يا indicates nasab/jar
  • In masculine plural: واو indicates rafa', يا indicates nasab/jar
  • Short vowels (damma, fatha, kasra) don't determine case in dual/plural forms

docs.title: Mudaf Rule:
docs.text:
• When dual or masculine plural words become mudaf, the noon is dropped
  • Examples: كتابا زيد (kitaba Zayd), مسلمو المدينة (muslimi al-Madina)

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning سجن jail/prison مصلح peacemaker سيد master/chief مفسد corrupt person مهين humiliating/disgraceful مظلة umbrella سنة year أمر he ordered ناجح successful/prosperous خاتم seal/ring صاحب companion جميع all ظالم oppressor سارق thief أليم painful حبس he imprisoned خبز bread خال maternal uncle صابر patient دفتر notebook درس he studied أخذ he took

docs.title: I'rab (Sentence Analysis) Examples
docs.text:
1. ذهب رجلان إلى السوق (Two men went to the market)

  2. رجلان: فاعل مرفوع بالألف (fa'il in rafa' case, marked by alif)

  3. خطب العالمون في المسجد (Scholars gave a speech in the mosque)

  4. العالمون: فاعل مرفوع بالواو (fa'il in rafa' case, marked by waw)

  5. نصر خالد مظلومين (Khalid helped two oppressed persons)

  6. مظلومين: مفعول منصوب بالياء (maf'ul in nasab case, marked by ya)

  7. ضرب نصير الظالمين (Nasir hit the oppressors)

  8. الظالمين: مفعول منصوب بالياء (maf'ul in nasab case, marked by ya)

docs.title: Dual Examples:
docs.text:
• كتب الطالب بقلمين (The student wrote with two pens)
  • قرأت كتابي زيد (I read Zayd's two books) - mudaf construction

docs.title: Masculine Plural Examples:
docs.text:
• جاء المسلمون (The Muslims came) - rafa' case
  • رأيت المسلمين (I saw the Muslims) - nasab case
  • مسلمو مصر (Muslims of Egypt) - mudaf construction

docs.title: Quranic References:
docs.text: • إن المؤمنين والمؤمنات - demonstrating back-to-back masculine and feminine plurals in nasab case due to إن

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Arabic uniquely has three number forms: singular, dual, and plural
  2. Dual formation is consistent across all nouns (unlike irregular plurals)
  3. Long vowels (الف، واو، يا) determine grammatical case in dual and masculine plural forms
  4. When dual or masculine plural becomes mudaf, the final noon is dropped
  5. Sound masculine plural (جمع مذكر سالم) follows predictable patterns unlike broken plurals (جمع مكسر)
  6. These grammatical structures appear frequently in Quranic Arabic and formal texts

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This was a comprehensive review session and Q&A to prepare students for the upcoming exam. The session covered key concepts from previous lessons including dual and plural forms, pronouns (damir), sentence structures, and various grammatical rules. Students asked clarification questions on topics they found challenging, with particular focus on pronoun classifications, case endings, and sentence analysis.

docs.title: Dual Forms (Tasniyah)
docs.text:
• Masculine singular to dual: Add alif noon (ان) - muslimun → muslimani
  • Feminine singular to dual: Add alif noon (ان) - muslimatun → muslimatani
  • Dual in nasab/jar cases: Change alif to ya - muslimayni, muslimatayni

docs.title: Sound Masculine Plural (Jam'a Mudhakkar Salim)
docs.text:
• Rafa case: Add waw noon (ون) - muslimuna
  • Nasab/Jar cases: Add ya noon (ين) - muslimina

docs.title: Pronouns (Damir) Classification
docs.text:
• Damir Muttasil (Attached): Visible (baris) and hidden (mustatir)
  • Damir Munfasil (Detached): Separate pronoun words like iyyaka

docs.title: Hidden Pronouns (Damir Mustatir) in Verbs
docs.text:
Past tense (Madhi): 2 places where damir is mustatir - Siga 1: fa'ala - Siga 4: fa'alat (ta is for feminine marker, not damir)

Present tense (Mudari): 5 places where damir is mustatir - Siga 1: yaf'alu - Siga 4: taf'alu - Siga 7: taf'alu - Siga 10: taf'alina - Siga 13-14: af'alu, naf'alu

docs.title: Prepositions (Huruf Jar)
docs.text:
• Prepositions like fi (في) put following nouns in jar case
  • Example: fi al-bayti (in the house) - bayti takes kasra due to fi

docs.title: Alif al-Jama'ah (Distinguishing Alif)
docs.text:
• Added to distinguish plural verb forms from singular
  • Example: yad'u (he prays) vs yad'uwa (they pray)
  • Also called al-alif al-fariqah (separating alif)

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning damir muttasil attached pronoun damir munfasil detached pronoun damir baris visible pronoun damir mustatir hidden pronoun tasniyah dual form jam'a mudhakkar salim sound masculine plural huruf mudareh present tense particles noon al-wiqaya protective noon noon an-niswa feminine plural noon alif al-jama'ah group alif al-alif al-fariqah distinguishing alif mudaf first part of possessive mudaf ilayh second part of possessive harf jar preposition jumla fi'liya verbal sentence jumla ismiya nominal sentence

docs.title: Possessive Construction
docs.text:
• Qalamu Zaydin (Zaid's pen)
  • Qalamu: mudaf (rafa case as mubtada)
  • Zaydin: mudaf ilayh (jar case)

docs.title: Preposition + Noun
docs.text:
• Fi al-bayti (in the house)
  • Fi: harf jar (preposition)
  • Al-bayti: jar case due to preposition

docs.title: Nominal Sentence with Verbal Predicate
docs.text:
• Zaydun dhahaba (Zaid went/Zaid, he went)
  • Zaydun: mubtada (rafa case)
  • Dhahaba: khabar (contains hidden pronoun huwa as fa'il)

docs.title: Multiple Choice Format Questions
docs.text:
• Vocabulary: Given English word "book", choose: Kitabun, Kalamun, or Sayyaratun
  • Sentence correction: Choose correct form:
  • Katabtu bil-qalama
  • Katabtu bil-qalami ✓
  • Katabtu bil-qalamu

docs.title: Pronoun Identification
docs.text:
• Nasartu (I helped): Contains damir baris in rafa case
  • Nasartahu (I helped him): Contains damir baris in nasab case
  • Fa'ila (he did): Contains damir mustatir in rafa case

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Case recognition comes through grammar rules: Understanding why words take specific endings requires knowing grammatical concepts like mudaf/mudaf ilayh, prepositions, and sentence types.

  2. Systematic study required: Review all 10-15 concepts covered in the course, as each lesson builds understanding of case endings and sentence structure.

  3. Technical terms are important: Arabic grammatical terminology (damir muttasil, baris, mustatir, etc.) should be learned through understanding concepts, not rote memorization.

  4. Exam format: Multiple choice questions covering vocabulary, sentence identification, and grammatical analysis.

  5. Distinguishing features matter: Elements like alif al-jama'ah serve specific grammatical purposes to differentiate meaning and should be understood in context.

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session focused on Al-Huroof Al-Mushabbaha bil-Fi'l (particles that resemble verbs), also known as Inna wa Akhawatuha (Inna and its sisters). These are six Arabic particles that govern two words like transitive verbs do, affecting the i'rab of mubtada and khabar in nominal sentences. The lesson covered their meanings, grammatical effects, and practical applications.

docs.title: Al-Huroof Al-Mushabbaha bil-Fi'l (Particles Resembling Verbs)
docs.text:
Definition: Six particles that have resemblance with verbs because they govern two words, similar to how transitive verbs (fi'l muta'adi) govern both a subject (fa'il) and object (maf'ul).

The Six Particles: 1. Inna - certainly, verily, indeed, definitely 2. Anna - that (connecting particle) 3. Ka anna - as if 4. Lakinna - but, however 5. Layta - if only, I wish (for impossible things) 6. La'alla - maybe, hopefully, perhaps (for possible things)

Grammatical Effects: - Appear before mubtada and khabar (nominal sentences) - Effect on Mubtada: - Loses its name (becomes ism of inna/anna/etc.) - Loses its i'rab (changes from rafa' to nasab case) - Effect on Khabar: - Loses only its name (becomes khabar of inna/anna/etc.) - Retains its i'rab (stays in rafa' case)

Why They Resemble Verbs: Like transitive verbs (fi'l muta'adi) that govern fa'il and maf'ul, these particles govern two elements (the ism and khabar), making them similar in grammatical behavior.

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Al-Huroof Al-Mushabbaha bil-Fi'l Particles that resemble verbs Inna wa Akhawatuha Inna and its sisters Mushabbaha Having resemblance (from tashbih) Fi'l Lazim Intransitive verb Fi'l Muta'adi Transitive verb Jumlah Ismiyyah Nominal sentence Jumlah Fi'liyyah Verbal sentence Ism of Inna The subject governed by inna (in nasab case) Khabar of Inna The predicate governed by inna (in rafa' case) Harf Mushabbah bil-Fi'l Particle resembling a verb

docs.title: I'rab (Sentence Analysis) Examples
docs.text:
Example 1: إنّ الله عليم (Inna Allaha 'Aleem) - إنّ: Harf mushabbah bil-fi'l - الله: Ism of inna (nasab case) - عليم: Khabar of inna (rafa' case) - Translation: "Verily, Allah is All-Knowing"

Example 2: أعرف أنّ الامتحان قريب (A'rifu anna al-imtihana qareeb) - أعرف: Fi'l mudari', first person singular - أنّ: Harf mushabbah bil-fi'l - الامتحان: Ism of anna (nasab case) - قريب: Khabar of anna (rafa' case) - Translation: "I know that the exam is near"

Example 3: كأنّ البيت جديد (Ka anna al-bayta jadeed) - كأنّ: Harf mushabbah bil-fi'l - البيت: Ism of ka anna (nasab case) - جديد: Khabar of ka anna (rafa' case) - Translation: "As if the house is new"

docs.title: Practice Examples
docs.text:
The class was assigned to complete exercises from the textbook involving: 1. Reading sentences with correct harakat (vowel marks) 2. Translating the sentences 3. Analyzing each sentence grammatically 4. Identifying the harf and its effects on ism and khabar

Note: Students must use dictionaries (recommended: Hans Wehr) to understand word meanings rather than AI tools, as this helps retention.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Core Concept: These six particles transform nominal sentences by changing the case and naming of mubtada and khabar
  2. Memorization: The ism of these particles is ALWAYS in nasab case, never rafa'
  3. Usage Context:
  4. Layta: Used for impossible wishes
  5. La'alla: Used for possible hopes
  6. Lakinna: Used mid-sentence for clarification/contrast
  7. Anna: Connects sentences/phrases
  8. Analysis Method: Always identify the harf first, then its ism (nasab), then its khabar (rafa')
  9. Sentence Type: Despite containing harf, these remain jumlah ismiyyah (nominal sentences) because the main components are still nouns
  10. Practical Application: Essential for understanding Quranic verses and classical Arabic texts that frequently use these particles for emphasis and connection

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This class covered a comprehensive review of إِنَّا وَأَخَوَاتُهَا (inna and its sisters) and introduced الأَفْعَالُ النَّاقِصَةُ (defective verbs). The session focused on advanced rules for the inna family, including word order variations, emphasis particles, and contextual usage. The class concluded with an introduction to kāna and its sisters as auxiliary verbs.

docs.title: إِنَّا وَأَخَوَاتُهَا (Inna and Its Sisters)
docs.text:
Six particles: إِنَّا، أَنَّا، لَكِنَّا، لَيْتَا، لَعَلَّةِ، كَأَنَّا

Basic Rule: These are حُرُوف مُشَبَّهَة بِالفِعْل (letters resembling verbs) that: - Enter upon مُبْتَدَأ and خَبَر - The مُبْتَدَأ becomes اِسْمُ إِنَّا (in نَصْب case) - The خَبَر becomes خَبَرُ إِنَّا (remains in رَفْع case)

docs.title: Word Order Rules
docs.text:
Normal Order: إِنَّا + اِسْم + خَبَر

Switched Order: When خَبَر is جَارّ مَجْرُور and اِسْم is either: 1. مُضَاف مُضَاف إِلَيْه 2. نَكِرَة

Example: إِنَّا إِلَيْنَا إِيَابَهُمْ - إِلَيْنَا = خَبَر مُقَدَّم - إِيَابَهُمْ = اِسْم مُؤَخَّر

docs.title: Emphasis Rules
docs.text:
مَا الكَافَّة: When added to any of these particles, it cancels their grammatical effect: - إِنَّمَا، أَنَّمَا، etc. - Both اِسْم and خَبَر return to رَفْع case

لاَم التَّوْكِيد: Can be added before the خَبَر for additional emphasis: - إِنَّكَ لَرَسُولُ اللَّهِ (Indeed you are the messenger of Allah)

docs.title: الأَفْعَالُ النَّاقِصَةُ (Defective Verbs)
docs.text:
Introduction to كَانَ and Sisters: - Called نَاقِص because despite being فِعْل لاَزِم, they require two مَعْمُول - Enter upon مُبْتَدَأ and خَبَر like إِنَّا - Opposite effect: اِسْم in رَفْع case, خَبَر in نَصْب case

Conjugation Rule for Hollow Verbs: - Check the مُضَارِع to identify middle letter - If وَاو in مُضَارِع → first letter gets ضَمَّة from صِيغَة 6 onwards - If يَاء in مُضَارِع → first letter gets كَسْرَة from صِيغَة 6 onwards

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning حُرُوف مُشَبَّهَة بِالفِعْل Letters resembling verbs مَا الكَافَّة The nullifying "ma" لاَم التَّوْكِيد Lam of emphasis خَبَر مُقَدَّم Advanced predicate اِسْم مُؤَخَّر Delayed subject الأَفْعَالُ النَّاقِصَةُ Defective verbs فِعْل نَاقِص Defective verb جُمْلَة إِسْمِيَّة خَبَرِيَّة Nominal sentence serving as predicate جُمْلَة فِعْلِيَّة خَبَرِيَّة Verbal sentence serving as predicate

docs.title: I'rab (Sentence Analysis) Examples
docs.text:
Example 1: إِنَّا زَيْدًا أُمُّهُ صَالِحَة - إِنَّا = حَرْف مُشَبَّه بِالفِعْل - زَيْدًا = اِسْمُ إِنَّا مَنْصُوب - أُمُّهُ صَالِحَة = جُمْلَة إِسْمِيَّة في مَحَلّ رَفْع خَبَر إِنَّا

Example 2: إِنَّا زَيْدًا أَكَلَ الطَّعَام - إِنَّا = حَرْف مُشَبَّه بِالفِعْل - زَيْدًا = اِسْمُ إِنَّا مَنْصُوب - أَكَلَ الطَّعَام = جُمْلَة فِعْلِيَّة في مَحَلّ رَفْع خَبَر إِنَّا

Example 3: إِنَّا إِلَيْنَا إِيَابَهُمْ - إِنَّا = حَرْف مُشَبَّه بِالفِعْل - إِلَيْنَا = جَارّ مَجْرُور خَبَر إِنَّا مُقَدَّم - إِيَابَهُمْ = اِسْمُ إِنَّا مُؤَخَّر مَنْصُوب

docs.title: Practice Examples
docs.text:
Translation Exercises Covered:

  1. لَعَلَّ المَرِيضَ نَائِم (Hopefully the patient is asleep)
  2. أَعْلَمُ أَنَّا زَيْدًا عَاقِل (I know that Zayd is intelligent)
  3. إِنَّا رَجُلاً دَخَلَ البَيْت (Indeed a man entered the house)
  4. كَأَنَّا القَمَرَ مِصْبَاح (As if the moon is a lamp)
  5. لَيْتَ القَمَرَ طَالِع (I wish the moon would rise)
  6. إِنَّا عَلَيْنَا بَيَانَه (Indeed upon us is its explanation)

Conjugation Practice for كَانَ: - كَانَ، كَانَت، كَانُوا، كَانَت، كَانَتَا - From صِيغَة 6: كُنَّا، كُنْتَ، كُنْتُمَا، كُنْتُمْ، كُنْتِ، كُنْتُمَا، كُنْتُنَّ، كُنْتُ

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. إِنَّا vs أَنَّا Usage: إِنَّا generally begins sentences; أَنَّا appears in the middle and serves as a connecting particle meaning "that" or "indeed that"

  2. Exception to أَنَّا Rule: After verbs derived from ق-و-ل (qāla), use إِنَّا instead of أَنَّا in the middle of sentences

  3. Complete Sentences as خَبَر: Both جُمْلَة إِسْمِيَّة and جُمْلَة فِعْلِيَّة can serve as the خَبَر of إِنَّا, and sentences themselves don't take case endings

  4. Word Order Flexibility: When خَبَر is جَارّ مَجْرُور and اِسْم meets specific conditions (مُضَاف مُضَاف إِلَيْه or نَكِرَة), the word order switches

  5. مَا الكَافَّة Effect: Adding مَا to any of these particles nullifies their grammatical effect and adds emphasis

  6. لاَم التَّوْكِيد Placement: This emphatic lām is added specifically to the خَبَر, not the اِسْم

  7. كَانَ Introduction: These defective verbs work oppositely to إِنَّا - اِسْم takes رَفْع, خَبَر takes نَصْب

  8. Conjugation Pattern: For hollow verbs, check the مُضَارِع middle letter to determine which vowel the first letter receives in conjugation

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session completed the discussion of إنّ (inna) and its sisters, then focused extensively on كان (kana) and its sisters - the أفعال ناقصة (af'al naqisa/defective verbs). The class covered conjugation patterns, timing-specific verbs, and practical sentence analysis with these constructions.

docs.title: الأفعال الناقصة (Al-Af'al An-Naqisa) - The Defective Verbs
docs.text:
Definition: Called ناقصة (naqisa/defective) because they are لازم (lazim) in meaning but require two nouns (اسم and خبر) to complete their meaning, acting like متعدّي (muta'addi) verbs.

I'rab Rules: Complete opposite of إنّ وأخواتها: - اسم كان receives رفع (rafa) - خبر كان receives نصب (nasab)

docs.title: The 13 Defective Verbs
docs.text:
1. كان - was | يكون - to become/becoming
  2. صار - became | يصير - to become (shows change of state)
  3. أصبح - became in the morning
  4. أمسى - became in the evening
  5. أضحى - became at mid-morning
  6. ظلّ - became/remained during the day
  7. بات - became/remained during the night
  8. ما دام - as long as (must be in middle of sentence)
  9. ما زال - continuously/always
  10. ما بَرِح - continuously/always
  11. ما فَتِئ - continuously/always
  12. ما انفكّ - continuously/always
  13. ليس - is not/not (no مضارع form)

docs.title: Conjugation Patterns
docs.text:
Rule for Past Tense Conjugation: - Look at مضارع form to determine voweling - If مضارع has واو (like يكون), use ضمّة from صيغة 6 onward - If مضارع has ياء (like يصير), use كسرة from صيغة 6 onward

Examples: - كان، كانا، كانوا، كانت، كانتا، كُنّ (dhamma because of waw in yakunu) - صار، صارا، صاروا، صارت، صارتا، سِرن (kasra because of ya in yasiru)

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning أفعال ناقصة Defective verbs فعل ناقص Defective verb فعل لازم Intransitive verb فعل متعدّي Transitive verb الألف المقصورة Shortened alif (written as ya, pronounced as alif) ما دام As long as ما زال Continuously ليس Is not صار Became (change of state) أصبح Became in the morning أمسى Became in the evening أضحى Became at mid-morning ظلّ Remained during the day بات Remained during the night

docs.title: I'rab (Sentence Analysis) Examples
docs.text:
Example 1: كان البيت نظيفًا - كان - فعل ناقص - البيت - اسم كان مرفوع - نظيفًا - خبر كان منصوب

Example 2: صار الرجل غنيًا - صار - فعل ناقص - الرجل - اسم صار مرفوع - غنيًا - خبر صار منصوب

Example 3: ليس الخادم قويًا - ليس - فعل ناقص للنفي - الخادم - اسم ليس مرفوع - قويًا - خبر ليس منصوب

docs.title: Practice Examples
docs.text:
1. ليس العامل نشيطًا - The worker is not active
  2. كان منامه منامًا صادقًا - His dream was a true dream
  3. أمسى الغني فقيرًا - The rich man became poor (in the evening)
  4. نويت الاعتكاف ما دمت في المسجد - I intended i'tikaf as long as I was in the mosque
  5. صار الكبش سمينًا - The ram became fat
  6. لا يزال لسانك رطبًا من ذكر الله - Your tongue continues to be moist with the remembrance of Allah
  7. يصبح المداد جافًا - The ink becomes dry

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. كان with مضارع gives past continuous or habitual meaning: كان زيد يكتب = "Zayd was writing/used to write"

  2. Verb Availability:

  3. كان through بات: Have ماضي، مضارع، and أمر forms
  4. ما زال group: Have ماضي and مضارع only (no أمر)

  5. ما دام and ليس: Have ماضي only

  6. Special Rules:

  7. ما دام cannot start a sentence
  8. ليس sometimes takes بـ prefix on خبر for emphasis

  9. الألف المقصورة converts to ياء during conjugation

  10. Timing-specific verbs (أصبح، أمسى، أضحى، ظلّ، بات) can be used generally without time restrictions

  11. Next class preparation: Read Chapter 2 المعرب والمبني, pages 27-30, focusing on section 2.2 المبني

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: Introduction to Al-Mu'rab wal-Mabni (Declinable and Indeclinable Words), covering the fundamental distinction between words that change their endings based on grammatical position versus those that remain constant. This is a crucial concept in Arabic grammar that forms the foundation for understanding sentence structure and word function.

docs.title: Al-Mu'rab (Declinable Words)
docs.text:
• Definition: A word whose ending can change based on its grammatical position
  • Can accept all three ḥarakāt (Ḍamma, Fatḥa, Kasra) or two out of three
  • Changes according to case: Rafʿ (Ḍamma), Naṣb (Fatḥa), Jarr (Kasra)
  • Examples: Zaydun → Zaydan → Zaydin; Al-Masjidu → Al-Masjida → Al-Masjidi

docs.title: Al-Mabnī (Indeclinable Words)
docs.text:
• Definition: A word whose ending never changes regardless of grammatical position
  • Maintains constant ending in all three cases (Rafʿ, Naṣb, Jarr)
  • Case determined by context and position, not by visible ending changes
  • Example: Hādhā remains Hādhā whether subject, object, or after preposition

docs.title: Original Mabnī (Al-Mabnī Al-Aṣl)
docs.text:
1. All particles (Ḥurūf): Min, ʿalā, fī, an - never change
  2. Fiʿl Māḍī: All 14 forms remain constant (Faʿala, Faʿalā, Faʿalū, etc.)
  3. Fiʿl Amr: All 6 forms of imperative verbs (Ifʿal, Iqra', Uḍrib, Unṣur)

docs.title: Derived Mabnī
docs.text:
1. Certain Fiʿl Muḍāriʿ forms:
  2. Jamʿ Mu'annas forms (Yafʿalna, Tafʿalna)

  3. Forms with Nūn at-Tawkīd (heavy and light nūn)

  4. Nouns with resemblance to original Mabnī (4 types of resemblance):

a) Resemblance in Meaning - Nouns giving meaning of verbs - Example: Ruwaydā (meaning "give respite") - resembles Amhil

b) Resemblance in Dependency - Nouns dependent on other words for complete meaning - Example: Hādhā (this) - needs Mushār ilayh like Hādhā al-baytu - Resembles particles which also need other words

c) Resemblance in Having Less Than Three Letters - Example: Man (who) - resembles Min (from) in having only two letters

d) Resemblance in Originally Containing a Ḥarf - Example: Aḥada ʿashara (eleven) - originally Aḥadun wa ʿashrūn - The wāw (conjunction) was originally present

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Al-Muʿrab Declinable (word whose ending changes) Al-Mabnī Indeclinable (word whose ending never changes) Ḥarakāt Vowel marks (Ḍamma, Fatḥa, Kasra) Al-Mabnī Al-Aṣl Original indeclinable words Mushār ilayh The thing pointed to (referent) Fiʿl Māḍī Past tense verb Fiʿl Amr Imperative verb Fiʿl Muḍāriʿ Present/future tense verb Jamʿ Mu'annas Feminine plural Nūn at-Tawkīd Emphatic nūn (heavy and light) Ruwaydā Give respite/grace (noun with verb meaning)

docs.title: Demonstrating Mabnī Words:
docs.text:
1. Jāʾa Hādhā (This came)

  2. Hādhā: Fāʿil, Rafʿ case, but ending doesn't change

  3. Ra'aytu Hādhā (I saw this)

  4. Hādhā: Mafʿūl, Naṣb case, but ending doesn't change

  5. Marartu bi Hādhā (I passed by this)

  6. Hādhā: Majrūr, Jarr case, but ending doesn't change

docs.title: Context Determines Case:
docs.text:
• Case recognition through sentence position and context
  • No visible ḥaraka changes in Mabnī words
  • Grammatical function understood through syntax

docs.title: Sentence Analysis (identifying Mabnī words):
docs.text:
1. Qara'tu Hādhā al-Kitāba (I read this book)
  2. Qara'tu: Mabnī (Fiʿl Māḍī + pronoun)
  3. Hādhā: Mabnī (Ism Ishāra)

  4. al-Kitāba: Muʿrab (changes based on case)

  5. Dhahaba ilā ʿAmmihi (He went to his uncle)

  6. Dhahaba: Mabnī (Fiʿl Māḍī)
  7. ilā: Mabnī (Ḥarf Jarr)

  8. ʿAmmihi: Muʿrab (changes based on case)

  9. Kitābuhu fī Baytihi (His book is in his house)

  10. fī: Mabnī (Ḥarf Jarr)
  11. Other words: Muʿrab

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Fundamental Distinction: Every Arabic word is either Muʿrab (declinable) or Mabnī (indeclinable)

  2. Recognition Strategy: Mabnī words maintain constant endings; context determines their grammatical case

  3. Five Categories: Original Mabnī includes particles, past tense verbs, and imperatives; derived Mabnī includes certain present tense forms and nouns with specific resemblances

  4. Four Types of Resemblance: Meaning, dependency, letter count, and original ḥarf content determine when nouns become Mabnī

  5. Practical Application: Understanding this concept is essential for proper sentence analysis and grammatical comprehension

  6. Upcoming Topics: Eight types of indeclinable nouns to be covered, starting with pronouns (Aḍ-Ḍamāʾir) in the next session

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session provided a comprehensive review of Mabani (indeclinable words) and Mu'rab (declinable words) concepts, with detailed explanations of the five types of Mabani words and their classification criteria. The class included practical exercises to identify Mabani vs Mu'rab words in sentences, reinforcing understanding through application.

docs.title: Mabani (المبني) - Indeclinable Words
docs.text:
Words that do not change their ending regardless of their grammatical case (Rafa, Nasb, or Jar).

Five Types of Mabani:

  1. All Particles (Huruf) - Originally Mabani (Al-Mabniyyu al-Asl)

  2. Examples: ila, ala, fi, an, min

  3. Al-Fi'lul-Madi - Past tense verbs (all 14 forms)

  4. Examples: fa'ala, fa'alu, fa'alat, fa'alata, fa'alna, etc.

  5. Al-Amru al-Hadir al-Ma'roof - Present imperative commands (6 forms)

  6. Examples: if'al, if'ala, if'alu, if'ali, if'ala, if'alna

  7. Specific Fi'l Mudareh forms - Present tense verbs with:

  8. Jama' Mu'annas Ghaib (feminine plural absent)
  9. Jama' Mu'annas Hadir (feminine plural present)

  10. Forms with Noon Taqeed (thaqeela and khafifah)

  11. Nouns with resemblance to original Mabani in four ways:

  12. Meaning resemblance: Nouns that give meaning of verbs (Ismul Fi'il)
  13. Dependency resemblance: Dependent on other words like particles (e.g., Hadha, Dhalika)
  14. Letter count resemblance: Having less than three letters (e.g., Man)
  15. Original Harf presence: Originally contained a particle (e.g., Ahada Ashara from Ahadun wa Ashrun)

docs.title: Mu'rab (المعرب) - Declinable Words
docs.text:
Words that change their ending according to their grammatical case.

Two Types of Mu'rab:

  1. Among Fi'l: 12 forms of Fi'l Mudareh (excluding Jama' Mu'annas Ghaib and Hadir)

  2. Among Ism: Most nouns (Mutamakkin) that accept I'rab changes when used in sentences

Important Note: Nouns in dictionaries are Mabni by default (with Dhamma), but become Mu'rab when used in sentences.

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Mabani (المبني) Indeclinable word Mu'rab (المعرب) Declinable word Al-Mabniyyu al-Asl (المبني الأصل) Originally indeclinable Mutamakkin (متمكن) Giving place to I'rab changes Ghayrul Mutamakkin (غير المتمكن) Not giving place to I'rab changes Ismul Ishara (اسم الإشارة) Demonstrative pronoun Ismul Fi'il (اسم الفعل) Verbal noun Kathratul Isti'mal (كثرة الاستعمال) Excessive usage Noon Taqeed (نون التأكيد) Emphatic Noon Jama' Mu'annas (جمع مؤنث) Feminine plural

docs.title: Mabani Examples:
docs.text:
• Ja'a Hadha - Hadha (subject) remains unchanged despite being Marfu'
  • Ra'aytu Hadha - Hadha (object) remains unchanged despite being Mansub
  • Marartu bi Hadha - Hadha remains unchanged despite being Majrur

docs.title: Mu'rab Examples:
docs.text:
• Ja'a Rajulun - Rajulun (Marfu' with Dhamma)
  • Ra'aytu Rajulan - Rajulan (Mansub with Fatha)
  • Marartu bi Rajulin - Rajulin (Majrur with Kasra)

docs.title: Exercise 1 - Identifying Mabani vs Mu'rab:
docs.text:
1. Sami'a (He heard) - Mabani (Fi'l Madi)
  2. Mundhu (Since) - Mabani (Harf/Particle)
  3. Yamsuru (He helps) - Mu'rab (Fi'l Mudareh)
  4. Yadribna (They [fem] hit) - Mabani (Jama' Mu'annas Ghaib)
  5. Yaqsirna (They [fem] shorten) - Mabani (Jama' Mu'annas Ghaib)
  6. Lakinna (But) - Mabani (Harf)
  7. Dhalika (That) - Mabani (Ismul Ishara - dependency resemblance)
  8. Isma'i (Listen!) - Mabani (Fi'l Amr Hadir)

docs.title: Exercise 2 - Sentence Translation and Analysis:
docs.text:
1. Nazartu ila az-Zahra - "I looked at the flower"
  2. Nazartu (Mabani - Fi'l Madi), ila (Mabani - Harf)
  3. Akhadhtu al-Kitab min as-Sadeeq - "I took the book from the friend"
  4. Akhadhtu (Mabani - Fi'l Madi), min (Mabni - Harf)

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Mabani Criteria: A word is Mabni if it's a particle, past tense verb, imperative command, specific present tense forms, or a noun resembling original Mabani.

  2. Resemblance Types: Nouns become Mabni through resemblance in meaning, dependency, letter count, or original Harf presence.

  3. Default State: Nouns are Mabni in dictionaries but become Mu'rab in sentences when they can accept different Harakat.

  4. Identification Method: To determine if a noun is Mabni, check if it matches any of the five Mabni categories; if not, it's Mu'rab.

  5. Next Topic: Al-Asma'ul Mawsoola (Relative Pronouns) - pages 38-41 assigned for pre-reading.

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session covered the remaining types of Damir (pronouns) and introduced al-Asma'ul-Mawsoola (relative pronouns). The class reviewed previously learned concepts of Damir from the "10 Lessons" book and focused on three special types: Damir al-Sha'an, Damir al-Qissa, and Damir al-Fasl. The second half introduced relative pronouns and their usage with Sila (relative clauses).

docs.title: Three Special Types of Damir
docs.text:
1. Damir al-Sha'an (ضمير الشأن) - Singular, third person masculine pronoun (مفرد غائب مذكر) - Appears at beginning of sentence without prior antecedent (مرجع) - Adds emphasis and beauty to the sentence - The sentence after it clarifies the pronoun - Example: إنه زيد قائم (Indeed, the matter is that Zaid is standing)

2. Damir al-Qissa (ضمير القصة) - Same as Damir al-Sha'an but feminine - Example: إنها فاطمة قائمة (Indeed, the matter is that Fatima is standing)

3. Damir al-Fasl (ضمير الفصل) - Separates Mubtada from Khabar for emphasis and restriction - Must be compatible with Mubtada in number and gender - Creates exclusive meaning (only they, not others) - Example: أولئك هم المفلحون (It is they who are successful) - Structure: Mubtada + Damir al-Fasl + al-Khabar with Alif-Lam

docs.title: Al-Asma'ul-Mawsoola (الأسماء الموصولة)
docs.text:
Basic Rules: - Always Ma'rifa (definite), cannot be made Nakira - Must have Sila (صلة) - a complete sentence following it - The Sila must contain A'id (عائد) - a pronoun referring back to the Ism Mawsool - A'id must match the Ism Mawsool in number and gender

Masculine Forms: - Singular: الذي (alladhi) - Dual: اللذان (alladhani) - Rafa case / اللذين (alladhaini) - Nasab/Jar cases - Plural: الذين (alladheena)

Feminine Forms: - Singular: التي (allati) - Dual: اللتان (allatani) - Rafa case / اللتين (allataini) - Nasab/Jar cases - Plural: اللاتي/اللواتي/اللائي (allati/allawati/alla'i)

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning ضمير الشأن Damir al-Sha'an (pronoun of matter/affair) ضمير القصة Damir al-Qissa (pronoun of story/narrative) ضمير الفصل Damir al-Fasl (separating pronoun) مرجع Marja' (antecedent) الأسماء الموصولة al-Asma'ul-Mawsoola (relative pronouns) صلة Sila (relative clause) عائد A'id (returning pronoun) اسم الإشارة Ism al-Ishara (demonstrative pronoun) المشار إليه al-Mushar ilayh (what is pointed to) مبني Mabni (indeclinable)

docs.title: I'rab (Sentence Analysis) Examples
docs.text:
Example 1: جاء الذي نصرك - جاء: Fi'l (verb) - الذي نصرك: Fa'il (subject) - composed of Ism Mawsool + Sila - الذي: Ism Mawsool - نصرك: Sila (relative clause) - Hidden pronoun "هو" in نصر is the A'id referring to الذي

Example 2: رأيت الذين نصروني - رأيت: Fi'l + Fa'il - الذين نصروني: Maf'ool (object) in Nasab case - الذين: Ism Mawsool (plural masculine) - نصروني: Sila - The واو in نصروا represents هم (A'id) referring to الذين

Example 3: أولئك هم المفلحون - أولئك: Mubtada - هم: Damir al-Fasl (not I'rab position) - المفلحون: Khabar in Rafa case

docs.title: Practice Examples
docs.text:
Exercise from pages 38-41 (mentioned but not completed in class): 1. القطار قدم في وقته (The train arrived on time) - ه in وقته: Damir Muttasil, Nasab case

  1. سنرزقكم (We will provide you sustenance)

  2. نا in سنرزقكم: Damir Munfasil, Rafa case

  3. الشرطي نصرنا (The policeman helped us)

  4. Hidden pronoun هو in نصر: Damir Mustatir, Rafa case (Fa'il)

  5. نا: Damir Muttasil, Nasab case (Maf'ool)

  6. الله يرزقها وإياكم (Allah provides for her and you all)

  7. ها in يرزقها: Damir Muttasil, Nasab case

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Special Damair add emphasis: Damir al-Sha'an/Qissa create more elaborate and emphatic sentence structures compared to simple constructions.

  2. Damir al-Fasl creates exclusivity: It transforms a general statement into an exclusive one, limiting the quality to specific subjects only.

  3. Relative pronouns are always definite: Al-Asma'ul-Mawsoola cannot be made indefinite under any circumstances.

  4. Sila is mandatory: Every Ism Mawsool must be followed by a complete sentence (Sila) to give it meaning.

  5. A'id ensures coherence: The pronoun in the Sila must grammatically match the Ism Mawsool in number and gender.

  6. Mabni nature: Relative pronouns don't change form based on their grammatical case - they remain constant.

  7. Fi'l number rule: When the Fa'il comes after the Fi'l, the Fi'l remains singular regardless of the Fa'il's number.

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: Continued study of Ism al-Mawsool (relative pronouns) with detailed sentence analysis (tarkeeb) practice. Introduced additional relative pronouns (man and ma) and their usage rules. Covered Dhul as a relative pronoun in Banu Tay dialect. Began introduction to Asma al-Ishara (demonstrative pronouns) with focus on near and far distance distinctions.

docs.title: Ism al-Mawsool (Relative Pronouns)
docs.text:
• Every Ism al-Mawsool requires silah (relative clause)
  • Silah must contain a dameer (pronoun) that refers back to the Ism al-Mawsool, called al-'aid
  • Dual forms follow tathniya rules: Rafa case with alif-nun, Nasab/Jarr cases with ya-nun
  • All other forms are mabni (indeclinable)

docs.title: Additional Relative Pronouns
docs.text:
• Man (مَنْ): Used for dhawi al-uqool (intelligent beings) - humans, jinn, angels
  • Ma (مَا): Used for non-intelligent beings - animals, objects, things
  • Both work for all genders and numbers
  • Dhul (ذُو): In Banu Tay dialect only, functions as relative pronoun for all genders/numbers

docs.title: Shibh al-Jumla Exception
docs.text:
• When silah is a semi-sentence (jarr-majroor combination), no 'aid dameer is required
  • Exception to the standard rule requiring a referring pronoun

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Ism al-Mawsool Relative pronoun Silah Relative clause Al-'aid The referring pronoun Dhawi al-uqool Intelligent beings Shibh al-jumla Semi-sentence Tarkeeb Sentence analysis Mabni Indeclinable Tathniya Dual form Mushar ilaih The thing being pointed to Qareeb Near distance Ba'eed Far distance

docs.title: Example 1: Ja'a alladhi nasarakah
docs.text: Translation: The one who helped you came Analysis: - Ja'a: fi'l - Alladhi nasarakah: fa'il (entire unit) - Alladhi: Ism mawsool - Nasarakah: silah (jumla fi'liya with fa'il mustatir + ka'af maf'ul)

docs.title: Example 2: Ra'aytu alladhayni nasarakah
docs.text: Translation: I saw the two who helped you Analysis: - Ra'aytu: fi'l + fa'il - Alladhayni nasarakah: maf'ul (entire unit) - Alladhayni: Ism mawsool (Nasab case with ya-nun) - Nasarakah: silah

docs.title: Example 3: Ja'a alladhi abuhu 'alimun
docs.text: Translation: The person whose father is knowledgeable came Analysis: - Ja'a: fi'l - Alladhi abuhu 'alimun: fa'il (entire unit) - Alladhi: Ism mawsool - Abuhu 'alimun: silah (jumla ismiya: abuhu mubtada + 'alimun khabar)

docs.title: Exercise Sentences Covered:
docs.text:
1. Ayna alladhi nasara Zayd? - Where is the one who helped Zayd?
  2. Allah ya'lamu ma fil-ard - Allah knows what is on earth
  3. Ja'at allatani taskanan amamana - The two females who live in front of us came
  4. Inna allatina kafaru la yadkhuluna al-jannah - Indeed, those who disbelieved will not enter Paradise

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Sentence analysis (tarkeeb) requires breaking down sentences into components first, then reassembling to understand grammatical relationships
  • Practice with 50-100 varied sentences is recommended for mastery
  • Dual forms of Ism al-Mawsool follow standard tathniya declension rules
  • Man/ma distinction based on intelligence level of referent is crucial
  • Shibh al-jumla (semi-sentences) have different rules than full sentences for silah requirements
  • Regular practice with tarkeeb analysis strengthens overall Arabic grammar comprehension

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session continued the discussion of ismul-ishara (demonstrative pronouns) rules, covering advanced concepts including mudaf constructions, sentence vs. phrase distinctions, and spatial demonstratives. The class included extensive practice with sentence analysis and i'rab identification.

docs.title: Rule 3: Mudaf with Ismul-Ishara
docs.text:
• When musharun-ilayh is a mudaf (construct phrase), ismul-ishara comes after the mudaf-ilayh
  • Example: kitabu-ka hadha (this book of yours) - NOT hadha kitabu-ka
  • Reason: Placing hadha before creates a complete sentence (mubtada-khabar), not a phrase

docs.title: Rule 4: Ismul-Ishara as Mubtada
docs.text:
• When ismul-ishara functions as mubtada, the khabar is generally nakira (indefinite)
  • Example: hadha kitabun (this is a book)
  • When khabar is ma'rifah, add dameer al-fasil: hadha huwa al-kitabu (this is the book)

docs.title: Rule 5: Mudaf as Khabar
docs.text:
• When mudaf-mudaf-ilayh serves as khabar, no dameer is needed between mubtada and khabar
  • Example: hadha ibn al-maliki (this is the son of the king)
  • For ismul-ishara construction: ibn al-maliki hadha (this son of the king)

docs.title: Rule 6: Spatial Demonstratives
docs.text:
• huna or hahunah = here (near)
  • hunaka = there (far)
  • These are also asmul-ishara but have no special grammatical rules
  • Word order depends on emphasis: mata huna (he died here) vs huna mata (HERE he died)

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning ismul-ishara demonstrative pronoun musharun-ilayh the thing being pointed to mudaf first part of construct phrase mudaf-ilayh second part of construct phrase murakab naqis incomplete phrase mubtada subject khabar predicate dameer al-fasil separating pronoun nakira indefinite noun ma'rifah definite noun huna/hahunah here hunaka there

docs.title: Example 1: hadha al-qalamu nafees
docs.text:
• hadha = ismul-ishara
  • al-qalam = musharun-ilayh
  • Together: mubtada
  • nafees = khabar (nakira)
  • Translation: This pen is precious

docs.title: Example 2: tilka sayyaratun qadeemah
docs.text:
• tilka = ismul-ishara
  • sayyarat = musharun-ilayh
  • Together: mubtada
  • qadeemah = khabar
  • Translation: That car is old

docs.title: Example 3: ula'ika tujjarun sadiqoon
docs.text:
• ula'ika = mubtada
  • tujjarun sadiqoon = mudaf-sifah (khabar)
  • Translation: Those are truthful businessmen

docs.title: Sentence vs. Phrase Distinction:
docs.text:
• hadha kitabun (sentence) = This is a book
  • hadha al-kitab (phrase) = This book
  • hadha huwa al-kitabu (sentence) = This is the book

docs.title: Mudaf Constructions:
docs.text:
• kitabu-ka hadha = this book of yours (phrase)
  • hadha kitabu-ka = this is your book (sentence)
  • ibn al-maliki hadha = this son of the king (phrase)
  • hadha ibn al-maliki = this is the son of the king (sentence)

docs.title: Dual Forms:
docs.text:
• hadhani waladani muhathabani = these two are well-mannered boys
  • hadhani al-waladani muhathabani = these two boys are well-mannered

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Word order matters critically - changing position of ismul-ishara can transform phrases into sentences and vice versa

  2. Presence or absence of alif-lam determines whether a construction is ismul-ishara/musharun-ilayh (phrase) or mubtada/khabar (sentence)

  3. Mudaf constructions require special attention to word order and cannot accept alif-lam on the mudaf

  4. Emphasis in spatial demonstratives (huna, hunaka) is shown through word order flexibility

  5. Sentence analysis requires identifying where "is/are" would be inserted in English translation to distinguish mubtada from khabar

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session covered Asma'ul Af'al (isms that have the meaning of fi'il) and Asma'ul Aswat (isms that denote sound). The lesson focused on understanding how certain nouns function like verbs while maintaining their noun status, and the specific i'rab rules that apply to the words that follow them.

docs.title: Asma'ul Af'al (2.4.4)
docs.text:
• Definition: Isms (nouns) that have the meaning of fi'il (verb) but do not accept the signs of fi'il
  • Key principle: These nouns cannot be conjugated like verbs (no madhi, mudareh, amr, nahi forms)
  • Two categories:
  • Meaning of fi'il madhi: The noun following will be in rafa case (like fa'il)
  • Meaning of amr al-hazir: The noun following will be in nasab case (like maf'ool)

docs.title: I'rab Rules for Asma'ul Af'al
docs.text:
1. Fi'il Madhi meaning → Following noun in rafa
  2. Amr meaning → Following noun in nasab

docs.title: Asma'ul Aswat (2.4.5)
docs.text:
• Definition: Isms that denote sounds
  • Status: All are mabni (indeclinable)
  • No grammatical rules: Just vocabulary to memorize

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning أَسْمَاءُ الأَفْعَال Nouns with verb meanings هَيْهَاتَ Far from, beyond capability شَتَّانَ What a difference, far apart سَرْعَانَ Hastened, rushed رُوَيْدَكَ Give respite, slowly بَلْهَ Leave, give up دُونَكَ / هَا Take عَلَيْكَ Hold on to, incumbent upon حَيَّ / هَلُمَّ Come, hasten تَعَالَ Come هَاتِ Bring, give صَهْ Keep quiet أُفّ Expression of pain/annoyance آهِ Expression of happiness/pleasure إِخْ Sound to make camel sit غَاقْ Sound of crow's cawing أَسْمَاءُ الأَصْوَات Nouns denoting sounds مُتَصَرِّف Inflectable/changeable

docs.title: Nouns with Fi'il Madhi Meaning (Following noun in rafa):
docs.text:
1. هَيْهَاتَ زَيْدٌ - "Far from it is Zaid" (Zaid in rafa)
  2. شَتَّانَ بَيْنَ الْعَالِمِ وَالْجَاهِلِ - "What a difference between the learned and ignorant"
  3. سَرْعَانَ زَيْدٌ - "Zaid hastened" (Zaid in rafa)

docs.title: Nouns with Amr Meaning (Following noun in nasab):
docs.text:
1. رُوَيْدَكَ زَيْدًا - "Give Zaid respite" (Zaid in nasab)
  2. بَلْهَ التَّفَكُّرَ - "Give up thinking" (at-tafakkur in nasab)
  3. دُونَكَ اللَّبَنَ - "Take the milk" (al-laban in nasab)

docs.title: Quranic Examples:
docs.text:
• حَيَّ عَلَى الصَّلَاةِ - "Come to prayer" (from adhan)
  • قُلْ هَاتُوا بُرْهَانَكُمْ - "Say, bring your proof"
  • تَعَالَوْا إِلَى كَلِمَةٍ سَوَاءٍ - "Come to a common word"

docs.title: Conjugation of Hati and Ta'ala:
docs.text:
هَاتِ (Hati - Bring): - هَاتِ (masculine singular) - هَاتِيَا (dual) - هَاتُوا (masculine plural) - هَاتِي (feminine singular) - هَاتِيَا (feminine dual) - هَاتِينَ (feminine plural)

تَعَالَ (Ta'ala - Come): - تَعَالَ (masculine singular) - تَعَالَيَا (dual) - تَعَالَوْا (masculine plural) - تَعَالَيْ (feminine singular) - تَعَالَيَا (feminine dual) - تَعَالَيْنَ (feminine plural)

docs.title: Sound Words (Asma'ul Aswat):
docs.text:
• أُحّ - sound of coughing
  • أُفّ - expression of pain (mentioned in Quran regarding parents)
  • آهِ - expression of happiness/pleasure
  • إِخْ - sound to make camel sit
  • غَاقْ - sound of crow cawing
  • نَخْ - sound used for animals

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Asma'ul Af'al are unique Arabic constructions that blur the line between nouns and verbs
  2. Only two asma'ul af'al can be conjugated: هَاتِ and تَعَالَ
  3. The i'rab of the following noun depends on whether the ism al-fi'l has past or command meaning
  4. Asma'ul Aswat are simply vocabulary items with no grammatical rules - they are all mabni
  5. These constructions appear frequently in the Quran and daily Arabic usage
  6. Understanding these helps in proper recitation and meaning comprehension of Quranic verses

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session focused on Zuruf (adverbs), specifically section 2.4.6 covering Zuruf al-Zaman (adverbs of time). The fundamental principle was established that Ism al-Zarf equals Maf'ul Fi and defaults to the Nasab case unless affected by a governing agent ('amil). Two primary adverbs were covered in detail: إذ (Idh) and إذا (Idha), including their rules, meanings, and usage patterns. The class emphasized the distinction between question forms and statement forms of temporal expressions.

docs.title: Basic Principles of Zarf
docs.text:
• Definition: Zarf is a noun that gives an idea of when or where work is done
  • Alternative name: Zarf is also called Maf'ul Fi
  • Default case: Zarf is always in Nasab case by default (since Maf'ul is always Nasab)
  • Exception: Only changes case when there is an 'amil (governing agent) causing a different case

docs.title: Types of Zuruf
docs.text:
1. Zuruf al-Zaman (adverbs of time) - can be specific or unspecified
  2. Zuruf al-Makan (adverbs of place)

docs.title: Rules for إذ (Idh)
docs.text:
1. Meaning: "When" (in statements, not questions)
  2. Tense rule: Gives meaning of Madi even when appearing before Fi'l Mudareh
  3. Sentence types: Can be entered upon Jumla Ismiya or Jumla Fi'liya
  4. Three meanings:
  5. When (temporal statement)
  6. Suddenly (إذ الفجائية - Idh al-Fuja'iya)
  7. Because (إذ التعليلية - Idh al-Ta'liliya)

docs.title: Rules for إذا (Idha)
docs.text:
1. Meanings: "When" or "If"
  2. Tense rule: Gives meaning of Mudareh even when appearing before Fi'l Madhi (opposite of إذ)
  3. Condition rule: Gives meaning of Shart and Jaza but does NOT give Jazm (because verbs are Madhi, and Madhi cannot accept Jazm)
  4. Sentence preference: Can be entered upon Jumla Ismiya or Jumla Fi'liya, but Jumla Fi'liya is preferred
  5. Suddenness: Can give meaning of suddenness, but only when entered upon Jumla Ismiya

docs.title: Comparison: إذ vs إذا
docs.text:
• إذ: Madi meaning even with Mudareh verbs
  • إذا: Mudareh meaning even with Madhi verbs
  • Both: Can express suddenness and can be entered upon both sentence types

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning ظروف (Zuruf) Adverbs ظرف (Zarf) Adverb (singular) ظروف الزمان (Zuruf al-Zaman) Adverbs of time ظروف المكان (Zuruf al-Makan) Adverbs of place اسم الظرف (Ism al-Zarf) Noun of adverb مفعول فيه (Maf'ul Fi) Object of circumstance إذ (Idh) When (statement form) إذا (Idha) When/If متى (Mata) When (question form) إذ الفجائية (Idh al-Fuja'iya) إذ of suddenness إذ التعليلية (Idh al-Ta'liliya) إذ of reasoning/because شرط (Shart) Condition جزاء (Jaza) Result of condition جزم (Jazm) Jussive case فجأ (Faja'a) To shock, suddenness عامل ('Amil) Governing agent

docs.title: إذ Examples:
docs.text:
1. واذكروا إذ أنتم قليلون (Quran)
  2. Wazkuru idh antum qalilun
  3. "Remember when you were few"

  4. إذ entered upon Jumla Ismiya, gives Madi meaning

  5. وإذ يرفع إبراهيم القواعد من البيت (Quran)

  6. Wa idh yarfa'u Ibrahim al-qawa'id min al-bayt
  7. "And when Ibrahim was raising the foundations of the house"
  8. إذ gives Madi meaning despite يرفع being Mudareh

docs.title: إذا Examples:
docs.text:
1. إذا طلعت الشمس آتيك
  2. Idha tala'at ash-shamsu atayk
  3. "When the sun rises, I will come to you"

  4. إذا entered upon Jumla Ismiya

  5. إذا نزل المطر فارجع إلى البيت

  6. Idha nazal al-matar farji' ila al-bayt
  7. "If it rains, return home"

  8. نزل is Madhi but gives Mudareh meaning due to إذا

  9. إذا جاء نصر الله (Surat an-Nasr)

  10. Idha ja'a nasr Allah
  11. "When the victory of Allah comes"
  12. جاء is Madhi but meaning is Mudareh

docs.title: Suddenness Examples:
docs.text:
1. خرجت فإذا مدير المدرسة ناظر
  2. Kharajtu fa-idha mudir al-madrasa nazir

  3. "I came out and suddenly the principal was watching"

  4. خرجت فإذا الكلب واقف

  5. Kharajtu fa-idha al-kalb waqif
  6. "I came out and suddenly encountered the dog standing"

docs.title: Conditional Examples:
docs.text:
• إن تذهب أذهب (with Jazm)
  • In tadhhab adhhab
  • "If you go, I go"
  • Both verbs receive Jazm

docs.title: Comparison with Different Question Forms:
docs.text:
• متى صليت؟ - "When did you pray?" (question)
  • متى تقرأ؟ - "When will you read?" (question)
  • متى تجيء؟ - "When will you come?" (question)

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Never confuse إذ with متى: إذ is for statements, متى is for questions
  2. Opposite tense rules: إذ gives Madi meaning, إذا gives Mudareh meaning
  3. Context determines meaning: Whether إذ/إذا means "when," "suddenly," or "because/if" depends on context
  4. Default case rule: All Zuruf default to Nasab case as they are Maf'ul Fi
  5. Jazm distinction: إن gives Jazm to conditional sentences, إذا does not (because it uses Madhi verbs)
  6. Sentence compatibility: Both إذ and إذا can be entered upon Jumla Ismiya and Jumla Fi'liya
  7. Study focus: Special attention needed for Qablu and Ba'du (number 10 in upcoming lessons) as they are key to understanding Zuruf al-Makan

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session covered adverbs of time (zarf al-zaman) and place (zarf al-makaan) in Arabic grammar. The class began with a clarification on sifa (adjectives) in mudaf constructions, then proceeded through various temporal and spatial adverbs, focusing on their i'rab rules and dual natures. Key emphasis was placed on understanding when certain adverbs are mu'rab (declinable) versus mabni (indeclinable) based on whether their mudaf ilaih is explicitly mentioned or merely intended.

docs.title: Sifa in Mudaf Constructions
docs.text:
• If sifa describes the mudaf: comes after mudaf ilaih with alif lam
  • If sifa describes the mudaf ilaih: matches properties of mudaf ilaih and is in jar case

docs.title: Temporal Adverbs (Zarf al-Zaman)
docs.text:
1. Mata (متى) - dual usage:
  2. Interrogative: "when" (questions)

  3. Conditional: causes both shart and jaza to be majzoom (like in)

  4. Munzu/Mud (منذ/مذ) - dual nature:

  5. As harf jar: following word is majroor

  6. As ism (mubtada): following word is khabar (marfu)

  7. Qabl/Ba'd (قبل/بعد) - dual nature:

  8. Mu'rab when mudaf ilaih is mentioned in words
  9. Mabni ala al-dham when mudaf ilaih is intended but not mentioned

docs.title: Spatial Adverbs (Zarf al-Makaan)
docs.text:
1. Haythu (حيث) - generally mudaf to entire sentences
  2. Quddam/Khalf, Tahta/Fawq (قدام/خلف، تحت/فوق) - follow same rules as qabl/ba'd
  3. Inda (عند) - standard mudaf behavior

docs.title: General Zarf Rules
docs.text:
• Zarf is mafu'ul fihi (adverbial object)
  • By default in nasab case unless governed by harf jar
  • Ismuz-zarf accepts other i'rab if there's reason, otherwise defaults to nasab

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning متى when (interrogative/conditional) كيف how (condition inquiry) أيان when (for extraordinary events) أمس yesterday (mabni with kasra) منذ/مذ since/for قط never (for past tense with negation) عود never (for present/future with negation) قبل before بعد after حيث where قدام in front of خلف behind تحت under فوق above/on top عند with/at/near ظرف الزمان adverb of time ظرف المكان adverb of place مبني على الضم indeclinable with damma

docs.title: I'rab (Sentence Analysis) Examples
docs.text:
Mata tasum asum (متى تصم أصم) - متى: conditional noun causing jazm - تصم: fi'l mudari majzoom (shart) - أصم: fi'l mudari majzoom (jaza)

Ma ra'aytuhu munzu yawmil jumu'ati (ما رأيته منذ يوم الجمعة) - منذ: harf jar - يوم الجمعة: majroor bi munzu

Min qablu wa min ba'du (من قبل ومن بعد) - قبل/بعد: majroor bi min, but mabni ala al-dham (mudaf ilaih intended)

Ijlis haythu Zaydun jalisun (اجلس حيث زيد جالس) - اجلس: fi'l amr - حيث: zarf makaan mudaf - زيد جالس: jumla ismiyya (mubtada khabar) acting as mudaf ilaih

docs.title: Practice Examples
docs.text:
1. Temporal negation emphasis:
  2. ما ضربته قط (I never hit him - past)

  3. لن أضربه عود (I will never hit him - future)

  4. Qabl/Ba'd variations:

  5. من قبل الفتح (min qabli al-fath) - mudaf ilaih mentioned, majroor

  6. من قبل (min qablu) - mudaf ilaih intended, mabni ala al-dham

  7. Spatial positioning:

  8. جلس زيد تحت وعمرو فوق (Zayd sat under and Amr above)
  9. المال عند زيد (The money is with Zayd)

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Dual Nature Concept: Many adverbs (munzu/mud, qabl/ba'd, quddam/khalf, tahta/fawq) can be either mu'rab or mabni depending on whether their mudaf ilaih is explicitly mentioned or merely intended.

  2. Default Zarf I'rab: All adverbs default to nasab case as mafu'ul fihi unless governed by a harf jar.

  3. Conditional vs Interrogative: Mata can function both ways - as interrogative (questions) and conditional (causing jazm like 'in').

  4. Mabni Consistency: When adverbs are mabni (indeclinable), they maintain their form regardless of grammatical context, though their i'rab function may change.

  5. Sentence-level Mudaf: Haythu uniquely takes entire sentences as its mudaf ilaih, demonstrating Arabic's structural flexibility.

  6. Emphasis Particles: Qattu (past) and audu (present/future) require prior negation to function as emphasis for "never."

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session continued the study of zuruf (adverbs), focusing on the remaining three adverbs of place (inda, ayna/anna, ladun/lada). The class also covered how adverbs can be mudaf to entire sentences, introduced kinayat (words indicating unspecified quantity), and began the crucial topic of Aqsamul Asma'il Mu'raba (types of declinable nouns), specifically the difference between munsarif and ghayr munsarif nouns.

docs.title: Zuruf al-Makan (Adverbs of Place) - Remaining Three:
docs.text:
4. Inda (عند) - "by, at, near, with" - Shows possession and nearness, but NOT companionship - For companionship, use ma'a (مع) - Example: صديقي عندي (my friend is by me/near me) - Gets kasra when preceded by min: من عند الله - If preceded by harf jar, it becomes majroor; otherwise it's zarf (maf'ul fihi) in nasab case

5. Ayna/Anna (أين/أنّى) - "where" - Both used for istifham (interrogation) - Anna is used for emphasis, ayna without emphasis - Can be used as adawat al-shart (conditional tools) - When used for shart, both verbs get jazm: أين تجلس أجلس (wherever you sit, I sit)

6. Ladun/Lada (لدن/لدى) - "at, by, near, with" - Similar meaning to inda but with important difference - Lada/ladun: possessed thing must be PRESENT with the person - Inda: possessed thing need not be present (can be in bank account, etc.) - Al-ilmul ladunni (العلم اللدني): knowledge that comes directly from Allah without acquisition - Ladun is most eloquently used with min: من لدن حكيم خبير

docs.title: Mudaf to Sentences Rule:
docs.text:
• Words can be mudaf not only to other words but to entire sentences
  • When a sentence becomes mudaf ilayh, it doesn't get kasra
  • Each word in the sentence gets i'raab according to its position
  • Example: يوم ينفع الصادقين صدقهم (the day when the truth of the truthful will benefit them)

docs.title: Mabni ala al-Fath Rule:
docs.text:
• Adverbs that are mu'rab and mudaf to sentences have two options:
  • Treat as mabni ala al-fath (always fatha) - like Qira'at Hafs
  • Give i'raab according to governing agent (more complex analysis)

docs.title: Kinayat (Words Indicating Unspecified Quantity):
docs.text:
• Kam (كم) - "how many, how much"
  • Katha (كذا) - "this many, so many"
  • Following noun must be singular and in nasab case
  • Example: كم درهماً (how many dirhams) - note singular form
  • Kayt wa dayt (كيت وذيت) - "such and such" (mabni, used as maf'ul)

docs.title: Aqsamul Asma'il Mu'raba (Types of Declinable Nouns):
docs.text:
• Munsarif (منصرف): Triptote - accepts all three harakat and tanwin
  • Ghayr Munsarif (غير منصرف): Diptote - accepts only dhamma and fatha, never kasra or tanwin
  • Nine causes that prevent full declension
  • If two causes exist (or one equivalent to two), noun becomes ghayr munsarif
  • In jar case, ghayr munsarif gets fatha instead of kasra
  • Example: في مكة (fi Makkata) - Makkah gets fatha despite being in jar case

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning عند (inda) by, at, near, with (without companionship) مع (ma'a) with (companionship) أين (ayna) where (without emphasis) أنّى (anna) where (with emphasis) لدن/لدى (ladun/lada) at, by, near, with (thing must be present) العلم اللدني (al-ilmul ladunni) knowledge directly from Allah ظروف الزمان (zuruf al-zaman) adverbs of time ظروف المكان (zuruf al-makan) adverbs of place كنايات (kinayat) words indicating unspecified quantity كم (kam) how many, how much كذا (katha) this many, so many كيت وذيت (kayt wa dayt) such and such منصرف (munsarif) triptote (accepts all harakat) غير منصرف (ghayr munsarif) diptote (limited harakat) مبني على الفتح (mabni ala al-fath) indeclinable with fatha

docs.title: I'rab (Sentence Analysis) Examples
docs.text:
Example 1: هذا يوم ينفع الصادقين صدقهم - هذا: mubtada (rafa case) - يوم: mudaf, khabar (rafa case) - ينفع الصادقين صدقهم: entire sentence is mudaf ilayh - ينفع: fi'l mudariq - الصادقين: maf'ul (nasab case) - صدقهم: fa'il (rafa case) - mudaf + mudaf ilayh

Example 2: المال عند زيد - المال: mubtada - عند: zarf, khabar - زيد: mudaf ilayh (jar case)

Example 3: في مكة - في: harf jar - مكة: majroor but with fatha (ghayr munsarif rule)

docs.title: Practice Examples
docs.text: Quranic Ayat Translation Exercise: 1. يوم يكون الناس كالفراش المبثوث - "The day when people will be like scattered moths" (yawma = zarf zaman) 2. وما جعلنا لبشر من قبلك الخلد - "We have not granted immortality to anyone before you" (qablika = zarf zaman) 3. فسبحان الله حين تمسون وحين تصبحون - "Glory be to Allah when you are in evening and morning" (hina tumsuna, hina tusbihuna = zarf zaman) 4. من بعدكم - "after you" (min ba'di = zarf zaman) 5. إذ جاؤوكم من فوقكم - "when they came to you from above you" (idh = zarf zaman, min fawqikum = zarf makan) 6. ما عندي ما تستعجلون - "I do not have what you are hastening for" (indi = zarf makan) 7. وعلمناه من لدنا علماً - "We taught him knowledge from Us" (min ladunna = zarf makan) 8. يعلم ما بين أيديهم وما خلفهم - "He knows what is before them and what is behind them" (bayna aydihim, khalfahum = zarf makan)

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Precision in Meaning: Distinguish between inda (nearness without presence required) and lada/ladun (nearness with presence required), and between inda (no companionship) and ma'a (companionship).

  2. Conditional Usage: Ayna and anna can function as both interrogative particles and conditional tools (adawat al-shart), requiring jazm on both verbs when used conditionally.

  3. Mudaf to Sentences: Advanced grammatical concept where entire sentences can serve as mudaf ilayh, requiring careful analysis of each word's position within the sentence.

  4. Kinayat Rules: When using kam or katha, the following noun must be singular and in nasab case, contrary to English which uses plural forms.

  5. Munsarif vs Ghayr Munsarif: This fundamental distinction affects how nouns behave throughout Arabic grammar. Ghayr munsarif nouns (like female names and place names like Makkah) never take kasra or tanwin, using fatha in jar case instead.

  6. Nine Causes: There are nine specific causes that can make a noun ghayr munsarif - understanding these is crucial for advanced Arabic grammar mastery.

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session provided an in-depth exploration of Al-Asma ul-Murabatu (الأسماء المُعرَبة - the declinable nouns) with emphasis on analytical methodology for studying Arabic grammar. The class focused on understanding the fundamental differences between regular plurals (Jama Salim) and broken plurals (Jama Taksir), comprehensive pronoun systems, and the critical distinctions between sentences and phrases. The instructor demonstrated a parsing methodology for deep grammatical understanding.

docs.title: 1. Al-Asma ul-Murabatu (الأسماء المُعرَبة)
docs.text:
• Definition: Nouns that can accept all three grammatical states (Rafa, Nasb, Jarr) and tanween
  • Condition: Must not have two of the nine causes that prevent declension occurring together
  • Opposite: Ghayr Munsarif (غير منصرف) - partially declined nouns

docs.title: 2. Broken Plural Treatment Rule
docs.text:
• Key Rule: Broken plurals (Jama Taksir) are treated as feminine singular for grammatical purposes
  • Application: When using adjectives (Sifah) or pronouns (Damayir) to refer to broken plurals, use feminine singular forms
  • Example: Asma (أسماء) + Murabah (مُعرَبة) - both feminine singular despite "Asma" being plural in meaning

docs.title: 3. Ta Marbutah Rule
docs.text:
• Rule: Any word ending in Ta Marbutah (ة) is always singular
  • Cannot be: Dual or plural
  • Harakat Rule: The letter immediately before Ta Marbutah must have Fathah

docs.title: 4. Mausuf-Sifah Agreement (DINZ Formula)
docs.text:
• D: Definiteness (both definite or both indefinite)
  • I: I'rab (same grammatical ending)
  • N: Number (singular, dual, plural)
  • G: Gender (masculine/feminine)
  • Exception: With broken plurals, treat as feminine singular

docs.title: 5. Ism al-Isharah Rules
docs.text:
• With Al: Forms a phrase (Tilka Rusulu - تلك الرسل)
  • Without Al: Forms a sentence (Hadha Yaumukum - هذا يومكم)
  • Requirement: Noun after Ism al-Isharah must be definite with "Al" for phrase formation

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning الأسماء المُعرَبة (Al-Asma ul-Murabatu) The declinable nouns جمع تكسير (Jama Taksir) Broken plural جمع سالم (Jama Salim) Regular/sound plural موصوف وصفة (Mausuf wa Sifah) Described and descriptor اسم الإشارة (Ism al-Isharah) Demonstrative pronoun مشار إليه (Mushar Ilayh) The thing pointed to مُعرَب (Mu'rab) Declinable, showing full grammatical endings غير منصرف (Ghayr Munsarif) Partially declined, diptote ضمائر (Damayir) Pronouns متصل (Muttasil) Attached (pronouns) منفصل (Munfasil) Detached (pronouns) مرفوع (Marfu) In nominative state منصوب (Mansub) In accusative state مجرور (Majrur) In genitive state

docs.title: Example 1: الأسماء المُعرَبة
docs.text:
• Analysis: Mausuf (موصوف) + Sifah (صفة) relationship
  • Agreement: Both definite, both feminine, same i'rab (dhamma)
  • Special Note: "Asma" is broken plural treated as feminine singular

docs.title: Example 2: أسماءٌ مُعرَبةٌ
docs.text:
• Analysis: Mubtada (مبتدأ) + Khabar (خبر)
  • Translation: "Names are declinable"
  • Rule: When definiteness breaks (removing Al), phrase becomes sentence

docs.title: Example 3: جَعَلْنا vs جَعَلَنا
docs.text:
• جَعَلْنا: "We made" (نا = attached marfu pronoun, subject)
  • جَعَلَنا: "He made us" (نا = attached mansub pronoun, object)
  • Key: Same letters, different grammatical roles and meanings

docs.title: Example 4: إيّاكَ نَعْبُدُ
docs.text:
• Analysis: Object fronting for emphasis
  • Normal order: نَعْبُدُكَ (We worship you)
  • Emphatic order: إيّاكَ نَعْبُدُ (You alone we worship)
  • Effect: Emphasizes exclusive worship (Tawhid)

docs.title: Broken Plural Recognition
docs.text:
1. اسْم (singular) → اسْمان (dual) → أسْماء (broken plural)
  2. مُسْلِم (singular) → مُسْلِمان (dual) → مُسْلِمون (regular plural)

docs.title: Ism al-Isharah Applications
docs.text:
1. تِلْكَ الرُّسُل - "Those messengers" (phrase)
  2. هذا يَوْمُكُم - "This is your day" (sentence)
  3. هذا اليَوْم - "This day" (phrase)

docs.title: Pronoun Count System
docs.text:
• Attached Marfu: 12 (part of verb conjugation)
  • Detached Marfu: 12 (أنا، أنتَ، هو، etc.)
  • Attached Mansub: 12 (object pronouns with verbs)
  • Detached Mansub: 12 (إيّاي، إيّاكَ، إيّاه، etc.)
  • Attached Majrur: 12 (with prepositions only)
  • Total: 60 pronouns in Arabic language

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Deep Analysis Method: Parse every word completely - number, gender, definiteness, grammatical state before moving to sentence-level analysis.

  2. Broken Plural Rule: Always treat broken plurals as feminine singular for grammatical agreement, regardless of the actual gender/number of the referent.

  3. Ism al-Isharah Distinction: Presence or absence of "Al" determines whether you have a phrase or sentence structure.

  4. Pronoun Mastery: Understanding the 60-pronoun system (attached/detached × marfu/mansub/majrur) is fundamental to Arabic comprehension.

  5. Ta Marbutah Recognition: Instant recognition that Ta Marbutah = singular + preceding letter has Fathah.

  6. DINZ Agreement: Master this formula for all Mausuf-Sifah relationships, with special attention to broken plural exceptions.

  7. Parsing Priority: Understanding word-level grammar before attempting sentence-level translation prevents confusion and builds unshakeable foundations.

  8. Quranic Application: These rules are essential for understanding Quranic Arabic, where precision in grammatical analysis reveals theological and rhetorical meanings.

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session focused on understanding I'rab (grammatical inflection) and the fundamental distinction between Munsarif (fully declined nouns) and Ghayr Munsarif (indeclinable nouns). The instructor emphasized that understanding I'rab is essential before studying the specific rules of indeclinable nouns. The class covered the definition of I'rab, characteristics of Munsarif nouns, and introduced the nine reasons that prevent nouns from being fully declined.

docs.title: I'rab (الإعراب)
docs.text:
• Definition: The sign or method of indicating the grammatical role of a word in a sentence through changes in the final letter
  • Purpose: Shows whether a noun functions as subject (مرفوع), object (منصوب), or is in genitive case (مجرور)
  • Mechanism: Changes in harakat (vowel marks) on the last letter reflect the word's grammatical position

docs.title: Munsarif (المنصرف)
docs.text:
• Definition: Nouns that show full variation of harakat on their final letter
  • Characteristics:
  • Shows damma (ضمة) or equivalent for rafa state (مرفوع)
  • Shows fatha (فتحة) or equivalent for nasb state (منصوب)
  • Shows kasra (كسرة) or equivalent for jar state (مجرور)
  • Example: كتاب becomes كتابٌ (nominative), كتابًا (accusative), كتابٍ (genitive)

docs.title: Ghayr Munsarif (غير المنصرف)
docs.text:
• Definition: Nouns that do not accept kasra and never take tanwin
  • Key characteristics:
  • Cannot have kasra on the final letter
  • Cannot take tanwin (nunation)
  • In jar state, takes fatha instead of kasra
  • Requirement: Generally needs two reasons from the nine categories to be ghayr munsarif

docs.title: Nine Reasons for Indeclension
docs.text:
1. 'Adl (العدل) - Derivation/modification from original form
  2. Ta'nith (التأنيث) - Femininity
  3. 'Ajamiyyah (العجمية) - Foreign origin
  4. Wazan al-Fi'l (وزن الفعل) - Verbal pattern
  5. Jam' (الجمع) - Specific plural forms
  6. Tarkib (التركيب) - Compound structure
  7. Ziyada (الزيادة) - Extra letters
  8. 'Udool 'an Qiyaas (العدول عن القياس) - Deviation from analogy
  9. Alifaan aw Waawaani (الألفان أو الواوان) - Double alif or waw

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning إعراب (I'rab) Grammatical inflection, declension منصرف (Munsarif) Fully declined noun غير منصرف (Ghayr Munsarif) Indeclinable noun مرفوع (Marfu') Nominative case منصوب (Mansub) Accusative case مجرور (Majrur) Genitive case تنوين (Tanwin) Nunation عدل ('Adl) Derivation, modification تأنيث (Ta'nith) Femininity عجمية ('Ajamiyyah) Foreign origin ألف مقصورة (Alif Maqsura) Shortened alif (ى) ألف ممدودة (Alif Mamduda) Extended alif (اء) زائدتان (Zaidatan) Two extra letters

docs.title: Munsarif Examples:
docs.text:
• زيدٌ يكتبُ كتابًا يوميًا (Zaidun yaktubu kitaban yawmiyan)
  • زيدٌ: مبتدأ مرفوع (subject, nominative)
  • كتابًا: مفعول به منصوب (direct object, accusative)
  • يوميًا: ظرف زمان منصوب (time adverb, accusative)

docs.title: Ghayr Munsarif Examples:
docs.text:
• صحف إبراهيمَ وموسى (Suhufu Ibrahima wa Musa)
  • إبراهيمَ: مضاف إليه مجرور بالفتحة نيابة عن الكسرة (genitive with fatha instead of kasra)
  • عمرُ خليفة ('Umaru khalifa)
  • عمرُ: مبتدأ مرفوع (subject) - no tanwin due to being ghayr munsarif

docs.title: 'Adl (Derivation) Examples:
docs.text:
• عمر (derived from عامر) - no kasra, no tanwin
  • ثُلاث (derived from ثلاثة) - modified form

docs.title: Femininity Examples:
docs.text:
• زينب - feminine name, more than 3 letters
  • فاطمة - feminine name with ta marbuta

docs.title: Foreign Names:
docs.text:
• إبراهيم - foreign origin name
  • إسماعيل - foreign origin name

docs.title: Alif Endings:
docs.text:
• Words ending in ألف مقصورة (ى)
  • Words ending in ألف ممدودة (اء) - these have two reasons, always ghayr munsarif

docs.title: Color Patterns:
docs.text:
• أحمر/حمراء (red) - pattern أفعل for masculine colors
  • Pattern indicates comparative/superlative form

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. I'rab Foundation: Understanding I'rab is essential before studying indeclinable nouns - it shows how words function grammatically through final letter changes.

  2. Two-Reason Rule: Most ghayr munsarif nouns require two of the nine reasons to be indeclinable, though some exceptions exist with just one strong reason.

  3. Practical Recognition: Instead of memorizing all rules, focus on recognizing common patterns and examples, then connect back to the underlying principles.

  4. Kasra Indicator: The key test for ghayr munsarif is inability to take kasra in the genitive case - it takes fatha instead.

  5. Tanwin Absence: Ghayr munsarif nouns never accept tanwin (nunation), which helps in identification.

  6. Strong Reasons: Some reasons like alif maqsura/mamduda are so strong they alone can make a noun ghayr munsarif.

  7. Quranic Examples: Many examples can be found in Quranic text, such as إبراهيم in "صحف إبراهيم وموسى" showing fatha in genitive position.

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session continued the discussion on غير منصرف (ghayr munsarif - non-declining) nouns and their nine causes. The focus was on understanding how these nouns behave differently in the جر (jar) state, practical examples using proper names like أحمد (Ahmad), and the relationship between تنوين (tanween) and grammatical states. The class also covered different types of nouns (صحيح، منقوص، مقصور) and their إعراب patterns.

docs.title: غير منصرف (Non-declining) Nouns - Key Principles:
docs.text:
• A غير منصرف noun has two out of nine possible causes that prevent complete declension
  • In المرفوع (marfu) and المنصوب (mansub) states: shows normal signs (ضمة/فتحة)
  • In المجرور (majrur) state: cannot show كسرة - instead shows فتحة (recycling the mansub sign)
  • Never takes تنوين in any state

docs.title: The Nine Causes (الأسباب التسعة):
docs.text:
1. العدل (al-'adl) - morphological shift/substitution
  2. العجمة (al-'ujma) - foreign origin
  3. التأنيث (at-ta'neeth) - femininity
  4. زيادة الألف والنون (ziyadat al-alif wa'n-nun) - added alif and nun
  5. العلمية (al-'alamiyya) - being a proper name
  6. الجمع (al-jam') - certain plural forms
  7. التركيب (at-tarkeeb) - compound structure
  8. وزن الفعل (wazn al-fi'l) - verb-like pattern (فعلان/فعلون)
  9. الوصف (al-wasf) - descriptive quality

docs.title: Color Patterns and Gender:
docs.text:
• Male colors: أحمر، أسود، أخضر (pattern: أفعل)
  • Female colors: حمراء، سوداء، خضراء (pattern: فعلاء)
  • Both patterns are غير منصرف

docs.title: Types of Nouns by Ending:
docs.text:
• الصحيح: Ends with a consonant (no و، ي، ا)
  • المنقوص: Ends with ي preceded by كسرة
  • المقصور: Ends with ألف

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning غير منصرف Non-declining (noun that doesn't fully decline) منصرف Fully declining العدل Morphological substitution تنوين Nunation (double short vowels) إعراب Grammatical declension مضاف ومضاف إليه Genitive construct (possessive structure) الحجر الأسود The Black Stone (in Kaaba) صحيح Sound/regular (noun ending in consonant) منقوص Defective (noun ending in ي) مقصور Shortened (noun ending in ا) مستتر Hidden (referring to grammatical markers)

docs.title: Ahmad (أحمد) - غير منصرف proper name:
docs.text:
• Marfu: جاء أحمدُ (Ahmad came) - ضمة without تنوين
  • Mansub: رأيت أحمدَ (I saw Ahmad) - فتحة without تنوين
  • Majrur: مررت بأحمدَ (I passed by Ahmad) - فتحة (not كسرة!)

docs.title: Father (أب) - منقوص noun:
docs.text:
• Marfu: جاء أبوك (Your father came) - shows و
  • Mansub: رأيت أباك (I saw your father) - shows ا
  • Majrur: مررت بأبيك (I passed by your father) - shows ي

docs.title: Comparison with Regular Nouns:
docs.text:
• Regular noun: زيدٌ، زيداً، زيدٍ (with تنوين)
  • غير منصرف: أحمدُ، أحمدَ، أحمدَ (no تنوين, فتحة in jar)

docs.title: Color Examples:
docs.text:
• الحجر الأسود (The Black Stone - masculine)
  • المرأة الحمراء (The red woman - feminine)

docs.title: Nested إضافة Structure:
docs.text: إعراب أقسام الأسماء المعربات - Analysis: "I'rab of types of declined nouns" - Two levels of مضاف ومضاف إليه structures

docs.title: Three Sentence Pattern Examples:
docs.text:
• جاء زيدٌ (Zayd came - marfu with ضمة)
  • رأيت زيداً (I saw Zayd - mansub with فتحة)
  • مررت بزيدٍ (I passed by Zayd - majrur with كسرة)

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Recognition Rule: If a noun has 2 out of 9 specific causes, it becomes غير منصرف
  2. Critical Difference: غير منصرف nouns use فتحة (not كسرة) when in jar state
  3. No تنوين Rule: غير منصرف nouns never take double vowels (تنوين)
  4. States Still Exist: The three grammatical states (رفع، نصب، جر) remain - only the visible signs change
  5. Context Clues: Use sentence structure and prepositions to determine the actual grammatical state when signs are ambiguous
  6. Gender Patterns: Colors follow predictable masculine (أفعل) and feminine (فعلاء) patterns
  7. Practical Application: Names like أحمد, محمد are common غير منصرف examples in daily usage

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session covered advanced noun declensions focusing on sound plurals (Jamʿ Sālim), relative adjectives (Ism Mansūb), diminutive forms (Taṣghīr), and the distinction between definite and indefinite nouns. The class emphasized practical application through examples and detailed analysis of grammatical states.

docs.title: Sound Plurals (Jamʿ Sālim)
docs.text:
Masculine Sound Plural (Jamʿ Mudhakkar Sālim): - Rafaʿ (nominative): ends with ون (wāw + nūn) - Naṣb and Jarr (accusative/genitive): ends with ين (yāʾ + nūn) - Example: مُسْلِمُونَ (Muslims came), رَأَيْتُ مُسْلِمِينَ (I saw Muslims)

Feminine Sound Plural (Jamʿ Muʾannath Sālim): - Rafaʿ: ends with ات (alif + tāʾ + ḍammatān) - Naṣb and Jarr: ends with ات (alif + tāʾ + kasratān) - Example: جَاءَتْ مُسْلِمَاتٌ, رَأَيْتُ مُسْلِمَاتٍ

docs.title: Numbers (20-99) with Nouns
docs.text:
• The numbered noun remains in naṣb (accusative) singular form
  • Example: جَاءَ عِشْرُونَ رَجُلاً (Twenty men came)
  • The noun stays fixed regardless of the sentence's grammatical requirement

docs.title: Relative Adjectives (Ism Mansūb)
docs.text:
• Formation: Add ي (yāʾ) + ّ (shaddah) to place names or concepts
  • Purpose: To relate someone to a place, school of thought, or characteristic
  • Examples: بَغْدَادِيّ (from Baghdad), شَافِعِيّ (follower of Shāfiʿī)

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning جَمْعُ مُذَكَّرٍ سَالِم Masculine sound plural جَمْعُ مُؤَنَّثٍ سَالِم Feminine sound plural اِسْمُ مَنْسُوب Relative adjective تَصْغِير Diminutive form مَعْرِفَة Definite noun نَكِرَة Indefinite noun مُذَكَّر Masculine مُؤَنَّث Feminine حَقِيقِيّ Real/actual (gender) لَفْظِيّ Grammatical/formal (gender) تَقْدِيرِيّ Conventional/traditional (gender)

docs.title: I'rab (Sentence Analysis) Examples
docs.text:
Sound Plural Examples: - جَاءَ مُسْلِمُونَ (Muslims came) - مُسْلِمُونَ is marfūʿ (nominative) - رَأَيْتُ عَامِلِينَ (I saw workers) - عَامِلِينَ is manṣūb (accusative) - مَرَرْتُ بِمُسْلِمِينَ (I passed by Muslims) - مُسْلِمِينَ is majrūr (genitive)

Counting Examples: - جَاءَ عِشْرُونَ رَجُلاً (Twenty men came) - رَجُلاً remains manṣūb despite جَاءَ - رَأَيْتُ عِشْرِينَ رَجُلاً (I saw twenty men) - رَجُلاً remains manṣūb

Relative Adjectives: - عَبْدُ الرَّحْمٰنِ الْبَغْدَادِيُّ (Abdurrahman al-Baghdadi) - الرَّجُلُ الشَّافِعِيُّ (The Shāfiʿī man)

docs.title: Diminutive Forms (Taṣghīr)
docs.text:
• رَجُل → رُجَيْل (little man)
  • حَسَن → حُسَيْن (little Hassan - showing affection)
  • عَبْد → عُبَيْد (little slave)
  • جَعْفَر → جُعَيْفِر (little Jaʿfar)

docs.title: Definite vs. Indefinite
docs.text:
Definite categories: - Pronouns (ضَمَائِر): هُوَ، هِيَ، أَنْتَ - Proper names (أَعْلام): أَحْمَد، مَكَّة، زَمْزَم - Demonstrative pronouns: هٰذَا، تِلْكَ، أُولٰئِكَ - Relative pronouns: الَّذِي، الَّتِي، الَّذِينَ - Nouns with definite article: الرَّجُل، الْكِتَاب - Vocative nouns: يَا رَجُلُ (without ال or tanwīn)

Indefinite: - Common nouns without ال: رَجُل، كِتَاب، بَيْت

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Sound plurals maintain their characteristic endings regardless of their grammatical function, making them easier to identify than broken plurals

  2. Context determines meaning when the same form appears in both naṣb and jarr cases - look at the prepositions and sentence structure

  3. Relative adjectives are crucial for distinguishing between people with similar names in Islamic texts, especially in ḥadīth chains

  4. Counting from 20-99 requires the counted noun to be in singular naṣb form, regardless of the sentence's grammatical requirements

  5. Feminine markers (tāʾ marbūṭah, alif maqṣūrah, alif mamdūdah) indicate grammatical gender, but actual usage depends on the referent's real gender

  6. Diminutive forms serve both grammatical and emotional functions, often expressing affection or endearment

  7. Definiteness in Arabic has multiple sources beyond just the definite article ال, including proper names, pronouns, and demonstratives

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session covered Arabic grammatical number (العدد) in depth, focusing on how Arabic distinguishes between singular, dual, and plural. The class walked through the formation rules for the dual (التثنية), sound plurals (الجمع السالم), and broken plurals (الجمع المكسّر), with special attention to the restricted plural (جمع القلة) and unrestricted plural (جمع الكثرة). The session also touched on counting rules for numbers 3–10, the gender-flipping rule in counting, and the concept of plural of plurals (جمع الجمع). I'rab analysis methodology (sentence vs. phrase identification) was practiced throughout using live examples.

docs.title: 1. Arabic Grammatical Number (العدد)
docs.text:
Arabic distinguishes three distinct numbers:
