Number Arabic Term Definition Singular المفرد Default form; refers to exactly one Dual المثنى / التثنية Refers to exactly two Plural الجمع Refers to three or more

docs.title: 2. Forming the Dual (التثنية)
docs.text:
Rule: Add ألف + نون مكسورة (Alif + Noon with Kasra) to the end of the singular word.

Critical sub-rule — the Alif constraint: - Alif cannot carry any haraka (it is always preceded by a Fatha). - Therefore, whatever letter immediately precedes the Alif must have a Fatha — even if the original letter had a Dhamma, Kasra, Dhammatain, or Kasratain. - The Noon carries a Kasra (Noon Maksura) — this is fixed by design.

Formation by grammatical state:

State (الحال) Ending Added Example (رجل → two men) Marfu (مرفوع) ـَانِ (Alif + Noon Kasra) رَجُلَانِ Mansub / Majrur (منصوب / مجرور) ـَيْنِ (Ya Sukun + Noon Kasra) رَجُلَيْنِ

Why the Dhamma disappears: When Alif is inserted after the Lam of رجل, the Lam's double Dhamma (ضمتان) is replaced by a single Fatha to accommodate the Alif. This is not optional — it is a structural requirement.

Examples of dual formation:

Singular Meaning Dual (Marfu) Dual (Majrur/Mansub) رَجُلٌ one man رَجُلَانِ رَجُلَيْنِ كِتَابٌ one book كِتَابَانِ كِتَابَيْنِ دَارٌ one house دَارَانِ دَارَيْنِ مِصْبَاحٌ one lamp مِصْبَاحَانِ مِصْبَاحَيْنِ أَحْمَد Ahmad (a name) أَحْمَدَانِ أَحْمَدَيْنِ

docs.title: 3. The Noon Drops in Idhafa (الإضافة)
docs.text:
Rule: When a dual word becomes the Mudaf (المضاف) in an Idhaafa construction, the Noon is dropped.

Examples:

Original Dual Idhaafa Form Meaning قَلَمَانِ قَلَمَا زَيْدٍ The two pens of Zayd مِصْبَاحَانِ مِصْبَاحَا حَسَّانٍ The two lamps of Hassan

Note: Noon dropping in Idhafa is not exclusive to the dual. It also occurs in the sound male plural (جمع المذكر السالم) when it becomes Mudaf. Example: مُسْلِمُو مِصْرَ (the Muslims of Egypt) — original was مُسْلِمُونَ but Noon dropped due to Idhafa.

docs.title: A. Sound Plural (الجمع السالم)
docs.text:
The singular word's structure remains intact (سالم = sound/unbroken).

i. Sound Male Plural (جمع المذكر السالم): - Marfu: add ـُونَ → مُسْلِمُونَ - Mansub/Majrur: add ـِينَ → مُسْلِمِينَ

ii. Sound Female Plural (جمع المؤنث السالم): - Marfu: add ـَاتٌ (Alif + Ta + Damma) → مُسْلِمَاتٌ - Majrur/Mansub: add ـَاتٍ (Alif + Ta + Kasratain) → مُسْلِمَاتٍ

docs.title: B. Broken Plural (الجمع المكسَّر / جمع التكسير)
docs.text: The internal structure of the singular word is broken and reformed. There is no single predictable rule — the pattern (وزن) must be learned word by word, though scholars have identified common scales (أوزان).

docs.title: 5. Restricted Plural — Jam'ul Qilla (جمع القلة)
docs.text:
Definition: A plural that denotes a number from 3 to 10. It signals a relatively small (though still plural) quantity.

The four common Awzan (scales) of Jam'ul Qilla:

Wazan Example Singular Example Plural Meaning أَفْعُل — أَقْلَام pens (قَلَم → أَقْلَام) أَفْعَال قَوْل أَقْوَال statements أَفْعِلَة طَعَام أَطْعِمَة foods فِعْلَة غُلَام غِلْمَة young men/boys

Note: This does not mean these words cannot refer to more than 10 in context. In Quranic usage especially, context determines the precise meaning. The classification is a grammatical and morphological label used by scholars to group plural patterns.

Related term — بِضْع (Bid'): A specific Arabic word meaning any number between 3 and 9 (sometimes stated as 3–10). Example usage: in Surah Ar-Rum, فِي بِضْعِ سِنِينَ ("within a few years") — this was the context of the famous wager between the Muslims and the disbelievers regarding the Roman-Persian war.

docs.title: 6. Unrestricted Plural — Jam'ul Kathira (جمع الكثرة)
docs.text:
Definition: A plural that denotes 10 or more (or an unspecified large quantity).

Common Awzan (scales) of Jam'ul Kathira:

Wazan Example Singular Example Plural Meaning فِعَال عَبْد عِبَاد servants/slaves فُعُول رَسُول رُسُل messengers فُعَلَاء عَالِم عُلَمَاء scholars فُعَلَاء فَقِيه فُقَهَاء jurists فُعَلَاء شَهِيد شُهَدَاء martyrs فُعَلَاء فَقِير فُقَرَاء poor people

Important pedagogical note from the Sheikh: Do not over-focus on memorizing the Wazan labels at the early stage. Instead, learn the pair — singular and its plural — and gradually recognize the pattern through exposure. The Wazan is a reference model (like a "model house") that helps you identify the pattern, not something to be mechanically applied.

docs.title: 7. Plural of Plurals (جمع الجمع)
docs.text:
Definition: A plural formed from another plural word, indicating an exceptionally large or emphatic quantity.

Example:

Singular First Plural Plural of Plural Meaning نِعْمَة أَنْعُم أَنَاعِم countless/overwhelming blessings بَيْت (house) بُيُوت أَبْيَات (contextual) very many houses

docs.title: 8. Noun Gender (التأنيث) — Sami'i Feminines (التأنيث السماعي)
docs.text:
Some nouns are grammatically feminine without any visible sign (no تاء مربوطة or other marker). These are called السماعي (heard/oral) — we know they are feminine because the Arabs used them as feminine words.

Examples of Sami'i feminines: - نَار (fire) — feminine, no sign - رُوح (spirit/soul) — feminine - دَار (house) — feminine - نَفْس (self/soul) — feminine - قَلْب (heart) — feminine

docs.title: 9. Counting Rule: Gender Flip for Numbers 3–10
docs.text:
Rule: When counting with numbers 3 to 10, the gender of the number is opposite to the gender of the singular of the noun being counted.

Singular Noun Gender of Singular Gender Used for Number Example كِتَاب (book) Masculine Feminine number ثَلَاثَةُ كُتُبٍ نَفْس (soul) Feminine Masculine number ثَلَاثُ أَنْفُسٍ رَجُل (man) Masculine Feminine number أَرْبَعَةُ رِجَالٍ

Critical point: The gender of the number is determined by the gender of the singular of that word — NOT the plural. So even if the broken plural of كِتَاب (which is كُتُب) is treated as non-human feminine, you still look at كِتَاب (masculine) to determine the number's gender.

Counting buckets (different rules apply for each range): - 1–2: Standard singular/dual - 3–10: Gender-flip rule applies - 11–19: Different rule - 20–99: Different rule - 100+: Different rule - 1000+: Different rule

The Sheikh noted this is a challenging area even for non-native Arabic scholars and requires extensive practice and exposure to become natural.

docs.title: 10. I'rab Analysis Methodology
docs.text:
Step-by-step process for any group of words:

  1. Identify word types — Is any word a verb (فعل)? If not, all words are nouns (أسماء).
  2. Ask: sentence or phrase? — A sentence requires a verb, or a Mubtada-Khabar structure.
  3. If phrase: what type? — At least 15 types of phrases exist. Use the D.I.N.G. framework to identify adjective phrases (المركّب الوصفي / الموصوف والصفة):

D.I.N.G. — Four agreement conditions for Mausuf-Sifa (موصوف وصفة):

Letter Condition Meaning D تعريف / تنكير Definiteness (both definite or both indefinite) I إعراب Same case ending (Raf', Nasb, or Jarr) N عدد Same number (singular, dual, or plural) G جنس Same gender (masculine or feminine)

Idhafa identification clues: - The Mudaf (المضاف) has no Alif-Lam (ال) and no Tanwin - The Mudaf ilayh (المضاف إليه) is in the Majrur (مجرور) state - The Mudaf ilayh may or may not have Alif-Lam

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning التثنية / المثنى Dual; the grammatical form for exactly two الجمع Plural (three or more) الجمع السالم Sound plural (structure of singular preserved) جمع المذكر السالم Sound masculine plural جمع المؤنث السالم Sound feminine plural الجمع المكسَّر / جمع التكسير Broken plural (internal structure altered) جمع القلة Restricted plural (3–10) جمع الكثرة Unrestricted plural (10+) جمع الجمع Plural of plurals الوزن (pl. الأوزان) Morphological scale / pattern السماعي Heard/oral; referring to grammatical gender known only by transmission from Arabs الإضافة Possessive/genitive construction المضاف First word in an Idhafa (the possessed) المضاف إليه Second word in an Idhafa (the possessor), always Majrur المرفوع Nominative case المنصوب Accusative case المجرور Genitive case الموصوف والصفة Noun-adjective phrase (described + descriptor) نَار Fire (feminine, no visible sign — Sami'i) نَفْس Soul/self (feminine, Sami'i) مِصْبَاح Lamp/lantern (appears in Ayat al-Nur: اللهُ نُورُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالأَرْضِ) بِضْع A specific number between 3 and 9 عُلَمَاء Scholars (plural of عَالِم, on Fuʿalaʾ scale) فُقَهَاء Jurists (plural of فَقِيه) شُهَدَاء Martyrs (plural of شَهِيد) فُقَرَاء Poor people (plural of فَقِير) رُسُل Messengers (plural of رَسُول) أَنْعُم / أَنَاعِم Blessings / overwhelming blessings (plural of plural)

docs.title: Example 1: الجمعُ السالمِ
docs.text: Analysis: - Two words. No verb present → this is a phrase, not a sentence. - الجمعُ: has Alif-Lam, last letter has Dhamma → Marfu, singular masculine - السالمِ: has Alif-Lam, last letter has Kasra → Majrur state - The Mudaf cannot have Alif-Lam, but الجمع does have Alif-Lam → so this is NOT Idhafa - Both words agree in definiteness (both have ال), case would differ for Idhafa, but here السالم has Kasra suggesting Majrur... - Conclusion: Both words share Alif-Lam, same number (singular), same gender (masculine). الجمع is Marfu and السالم agrees → this is a Mausuf-Sifa (موصوف وصفة) construction — adjective phrase. The word الجمع itself is singular (meaning is plural, but the word is singular; if the word were plural, its Sifa would be singular feminine for non-human plural).

docs.title: Example 2: الجمعُ المكسَّرِ
docs.text: Same analysis as above → Mausuf-Sifa. Both words have Alif-Lam, both singular, both masculine. Adjective phrase.

docs.title: Example 3: أقسامُ الجمعِ (Idhafa)
docs.text: Analysis: - Two words. No verb → phrase. - أقسامُ: no Alif-Lam, no Tanwin, last letter Dhamma → Marfu, and this absence of ال and Tanwin signals it is a Mudaf - الجمعِ: has Alif-Lam, last letter Kasra → Majrur → this is the Mudaf ilayh - Conclusion: Idhafa construction. "The types/kinds of the plural."

docs.title: Example 4: قَلَمَا زَيْدٍ (Dual in Idhafa)
docs.text: Analysis: - قَلَمَا: original was قَلَمَانِ (two pens, Marfu dual) → Noon dropped because it became Mudaf - زَيْدٍ: Kasra + Tanwin → Majrur Mudaf ilayh (no Alif-Lam, proper noun) - Meaning: The two pens of Zayd

docs.title: Example 5: رَأَيْتُ أَرْبَعَةَ رِجَالٍ (Counting example)
docs.text: Analysis: - رَأَيْتُ: verb "I saw" + subject pronoun - أَرْبَعَةَ: number four, feminine form (تاء مربوطة) because رَجُل (singular of رِجَال) is masculine → gender flip rule - رِجَالٍ: Majrur because it is Mudaf ilayh to the number (number + counted noun is Idhafa structure) - Common mistake to avoid: Do not say أَرْبَعُ رِجَالٍ (would imply رجال is feminine) or use the gender of the plural رِجَال rather than the singular رَجُل.

docs.title: Practice Examples
docs.text:
Exercise 1 — Form the dual (Marfu and Majrur/Mansub):

Singular Marfu Dual Majrur/Mansub Dual رَجُلٌ (man) رَجُلَانِ رَجُلَيْنِ كِتَابٌ (book) كِتَابَانِ كِتَابَيْنِ دَارٌ (house) دَارَانِ دَارَيْنِ مِصْبَاحٌ (lamp) مِصْبَاحَانِ مِصْبَاحَيْنِ قَلَمٌ (pen) قَلَمَانِ قَلَمَيْنِ

Exercise 2 — Form dual Idhafa (drop the Noon):

Two of X belonging to Y Answer Two pens of Zayd قَلَمَا زَيْدٍ Two lamps of Hassan مِصْبَاحَا حَسَّانٍ Two books of the student كِتَابَا الطَّالِبِ

Exercise 3 — Counting with gender flip (3–10):

Number + Noun Singular Gender Number Gender Used Correct Form 3 books (كُتُب، singular كِتَاب) Masculine Feminine ثَلَاثَةُ كُتُبٍ 4 men (رِجَال، singular رَجُل) Masculine Feminine أَرْبَعَةُ رِجَالٍ 3 souls (أَنْفُس، singular نَفْس) Feminine Masculine ثَلَاثُ أَنْفُسٍ

Exercise 4 — Identify the construction (Idhafa or Mausuf-Sifa):

Phrase Construction Clue الجمعُ السالمُ Mausuf-Sifa Both have ال, same case, same gender, same number أقسامُ الجمعِ Idhafa First word has no ال and no Tanwin; second is Majrur طَالِبُو العِلمِ Idhafa (Noon dropped) Original طَالِبُونَ, Noon dropped in Idhafa

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. The Alif constraint is foundational: Whenever Alif enters a word (as in dual formation), the letter immediately before it is forced to have a Fatha. This is why رَجُل (Lam had Dhammatain) becomes رَجُلَانِ — the Dhammatain is replaced by a single Fatha.

  2. Dual has two forms: ـَانِ for Marfu, and ـَيْنِ for Mansub/Majrur. The Noon always drops when the dual becomes a Mudaf in Idhafa.

  3. Arabic number has three levels: Singular (1), Dual (2), and Plural (3+). Arabic uniquely maintains distinct forms for all three — a feature not found in most languages.

  4. Broken plural has no single rule but follows identifiable scales (أوزان). At this stage, learn singular-plural pairs by heart. Recognize patterns through exposure rather than mechanical wazan application.

  5. Jam'ul Qilla (3–10) vs. Jam'ul Kathira (10+): Arabic formally distinguishes small plurals from large plurals morphologically. The four Qilla scales (أَفْعُل، أَفْعَال، أَفْعِلَة، فِعْلَة) signal limited quantity; Kathira scales signal abundance.

  6. Gender-flip rule for counting (3–10): Always look at the gender of the singular of the noun being counted — not the plural — and use the opposite gender for the number. This is one of the most commonly misapplied rules.

  7. D.I.N.G. framework for adjective phrases: Definiteness, I'rab (case), Number, Gender — all four must agree between Mausuf and Sifa. This is the checklist for identifying and constructing adjective phrases correctly.

  8. The analysis habit: Before labeling any multi-word phrase, always ask: (a) Is there a verb? (b) Is it a sentence or a phrase? (c) If a phrase, what type? This systematic approach prevents common errors and builds lasting grammatical intuition.

  9. Noun-Adjective word is singular even when meaning is plural: الجمع السالم — the word الجمع is singular even though its meaning is "plural." Evidence: its Sifa السالم is singular. If الجمع were grammatically plural, a non-human plural Sifa would be singular feminine (as-Salima), not السالم.

  10. Homework: Read through the remaining sections of the broken plural chapter. Focus on recognizing singular-plural pairs. Note any words you encounter that seem to fit the four Jam'ul Qilla scales.

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session focused on Section 3.7 — Marfu'at (roles of isms that are always in the state of Rafa'). Before diving into the specific marfu roles, the instructor laid crucial conceptual groundwork: explaining how many roles an ism can play in Arabic, why only three grammatical states are needed to identify all of them, and how grammarians categorized these roles into 8 marfu'at, 12 mansubat, and 2 majruurat (totaling 22). The session concluded with a brief introduction to Section 3.8 (Mansubat), which will be the starting point for the next class. The instructor also noted this may be his last session with the class.

docs.title: 1. The 22 Roles of an Ism
docs.text:
An ism (noun) — including pronouns (damir), demonstrative pronouns (ism al-ishara), proper names, and anything functioning as a noun — can play 22 distinct roles in Arabic sentences.

Examples of roles mentioned: - Mubtada' (subject of a nominal sentence) - Khabar (predicate of a nominal sentence) - Fa'il (doer/subject of a verbal sentence) - Na'ib al-fa'il (deputy doer / passive subject) - Maf'ul bihi (direct object) - Maf'ul mutlaq (absolute object) - Tamyiz (specification/distinction) - Tawkid (emphasis) - Jar-majrur (prepositional phrase) - Mudaf ilayhi (possessive/genitive)

docs.title: 2. Only Three States (Harakat) Are Needed
docs.text:
Despite 22 possible roles, only three grammatical states are needed to identify any role:

State Name Marker Rafa' Marfu' Dhamma (ـُ) or equivalent Nasb Mansub Fathah (ـَ) or equivalent Jarr Majrur Kasra (ـِ) or equivalent

Why three are sufficient: The 22 roles never appear in combinations that would require more than three distinct markers to distinguish them. Roles that could cause confusion never appear side by side in the same sentence context.

docs.title: 3. The 8 Marfu'at — Roles Always in the State of Rafa'
docs.text:
The following 8 roles are always marfu' (carry dhamma or its equivalent on the final letter):

# Role Definition/Context 1 Fa'il The doer in an active-voice verbal sentence (Jumla Fi'liya ma'ruf) 2 Na'ib al-Fa'il The deputy doer in a passive-voice verbal sentence (Jumla Fi'liya majhul) 3 Mubtada' The subject of a nominal sentence (Jumla Ismiya) 4 Khabar The predicate of a nominal sentence (Jumla Ismiya) 5 Khabar Inna wa Akhawatiha The predicate of an Inna-type sentence (Inna gives nasb to the mubtada', but khabar stays marfu') 6 Ism Kana The subject of a Kana-type sentence (Kana gives nasb to the khabar, but ism stays marfu') 7 Ism Ma and La al-Mushabbaha bi-Laysa Subject of Ma and La that resemble Laysa 8 Khabar La al-Nafi lil-Jins Predicate of La that negates the entire class (e.g., لا إله إلا الله — "illallah" carries dhamma)

docs.title: 4. Fa'il vs. Na'ib al-Fa'il — Cannot Coexist
docs.text:
A single verb cannot be simultaneously active (ma'ruf) and passive (majhul). Therefore: - If verb is ma'ruf (active) → Fa'il is present, no Na'ib al-fa'il - If verb is majhul (passive) → Na'ib al-fa'il is present, no Fa'il

This means Fa'il and Na'ib al-fa'il can never appear together for the same verb in one sentence.

docs.title: 5. The Effect of Inna and Kana on Jumla Ismiya
docs.text:
Particle Affects Effect What Remains Marfu' Inna (and sisters) Mubtada' (closest) Gives Nasb Khabar remains marfu' Kana (and sisters) Khabar (farthest) Gives Nasb Ism remains marfu'

Sisters of Inna include: أنَّ، كأنَّ، لكنَّ، ليتَ، لعلَّ

docs.title: 6. One Word, One Role Only
docs.text: A single word in a sentence can only play one and only one grammatical role. If a word were allowed to play two roles simultaneously (e.g., both mubtada' and na'ib al-fa'il), translation and comprehension would become impossible. This is an inviolable rule of Arabic grammar.

docs.title: 7. The 12 Mansubat and 2 Majruurat (Overview)
docs.text:
The instructor gave a summary of the full breakdown:

Category Count Total Marfu'at (Rafa' roles) 8 Mansubat (Nasb roles) 12 Majruurat (Jarr roles) 2 (Jar-Majrur & Mudaf Ilayhi) Total 22

The Mansubat (Section 3.8) will be covered in the next class.

docs.title: 8. The Implicit Fa'il (Damir Mustatir)
docs.text: When a verb is in a form that has no explicit (visible) fa'il — such as the 4th conjugation pattern (e.g., كُوِّرَتْ — feminine passive, tasukun ending) — the fa'il is a damir mustatir (hidden/implicit pronoun). The tasukun on the ta' is not a damir; it is simply a structural element of the conjugation pattern. The implicit damir must be extracted for full i'rab analysis.

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning إسم (Ism) Noun (includes pronouns, proper names, demonstratives, etc.) مرفوع (Marfu') Noun in the nominative state, carries dhamma منصوب (Mansub) Noun in the accusative state, carries fathah مجرور (Majrur) Noun in the genitive state, carries kasra مرفوعات (Marfu'at) The category of roles that are always marfu' منصوبات (Mansubat) The category of roles that are always mansub مجرورات (Majrurat) The category of roles that are always majrur فاعل (Fa'il) Doer; subject of an active-voice verbal sentence نائب الفاعل (Na'ib al-Fa'il) Deputy doer; subject of a passive-voice verbal sentence مبتدأ (Mubtada') Subject of a nominal sentence خبر (Khabar) Predicate of a nominal sentence جملة اسمية (Jumla Ismiya) Nominal sentence (begins with a noun) جملة فعلية (Jumla Fi'liya) Verbal sentence (begins with a verb) معروف (Ma'ruf) Active voice مجهول (Majhul) Passive voice إنَّ وأخواتها (Inna wa Akhawatiha) Inna and its sisters (particles that give nasb to mubtada') كان وأخواتها (Kana wa Akhawatiha) Kana and its sisters (particles that give nasb to khabar) ما ولا المشبهة بليس Ma and La that resemble Laysa لا النافية للجنس (La al-Nafiya lil-Jins) La that negates the entire class/genus خبر إنَّ (Khabar Inna) Predicate of an Inna sentence (remains marfu') اسم كان (Ism Kana) Subject of a Kana sentence (remains marfu') ضمير مستتر (Damir Mustatir) Hidden/implicit pronoun مضاف إليه (Mudaf Ilayhi) Genitive noun in a possessive construction (always majrur)

docs.title: Example 1: إِذَا الشَّمْسُ كُوِّرَتْ (Surah al-Takwir, 81:1)
docs.text:
Word Role State Reason إذا Condition particle — Not analyzed as ism here الشَّمْسُ Mubtada' Marfu' (dhamma) Subject of the nominal sentence كُوِّرَتْ Khabar (Jumla Fi'liya) — Predicate, here as a verbal clause كُوِّرَ (verb) Fi'l Majhul (passive) — Pattern of passive (4th conjugation feminine: كُوِّرَتْ) Ta' in كُوِّرَتْ Not a damir — structural — Tasukun is a pattern feature, not a damir Damir Mustatir (هي) Na'ib al-Fa'il Marfu' Implicit pronoun referring to الشمس

Full reading: الشمس is mubtada', كوّرت is khabar as a Jumla Fi'liya. Inside that jumla: the verb is majhul, the na'ib al-fa'il is a hidden damir (هي) referring to الشمس.

docs.title: Example 2: إِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ
docs.text: Word Role State Reason إنَّ Particle of emphasis — Acts on the following ism اللَّهَ Ism Inna Mansub (fathah) Inna gives nasb to mubtada' غَفُورٌ Khabar Inna Marfu' (dhamma) Khabar remains in rafa' رَّحِيمٌ Na't (adjective) of khabar Marfu' (dhamma) Follows its mawsuf in state

docs.title: Example 3: لا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا اللَّهُ
docs.text: Word Role State Reason لا La al-Nafiya lil-Jins — Class negation particle إلٰه Ism La Mansub (fathah, no tanwin) La gives nasb to its ism إلا الله Khabar La (or badal) Marfu' (dhamma) Khabar of La al-Nafiya lil-Jins is marfu'

docs.title: Example 4: جَاءَ زَيْدٌ
docs.text: Word Role State Reason جَاءَ Fi'l ma'ruf — Active verb زَيْدٌ Fa'il Marfu' (dhamma + tanwin) Doer in active verbal sentence

docs.title: Practice Examples
docs.text:
Identify the role and state of the underlined word:

  1. جَاءَ زَيْدٌ — What is زيد? → Fa'il, Marfu'
  2. قُتِلَ أَصْحَابُ الْأُخْدُودِ (Surah al-Buruj, 85:4) — What is أصحاب? → Na'ib al-Fa'il, Marfu' (verb قُتِلَ is passive/majhul)
  3. إنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ — What is غفور? → Khabar Inna, Marfu'
  4. كَانَ زَيْدٌ عَالِمًا — What is زيد? → Ism Kana, Marfu'; What is عالمًا? → Khabar Kana, Mansub
  5. لا إِلَهَ إِلَّا اللَّهُ — What is the khabar? → Marfu' (La al-Nafiya lil-Jins)

Determine whether Fa'il or Na'ib al-Fa'il is present: - نَصَرَ زَيْدٌ → Active voice → Fa'il = زيد - نُصِرَ زَيْدٌ → Passive voice → Na'ib al-Fa'il = زيد - Can both appear for the same verb? No — never.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. An ism can play 22 distinct roles in Arabic. Knowing this total is foundational for understanding why we need grammatical states.

  2. Only 3 grammatical states (Rafa', Nasb, Jarr) are sufficient to identify all 22 roles — a profound miracle of the Arabic language. Roles are distributed such that three markers never cause ambiguity.

  3. The 22 roles are categorized as: 8 Marfu'at + 12 Mansubat + 2 Majrurat = 22 total.

  4. Every word in a sentence plays one and only one grammatical role. Dual roles are grammatically forbidden — this is what makes Arabic parsing unambiguous.

  5. Fa'il and Na'ib al-Fa'il can never coexist for the same verb, because a verb is either active or passive — never both.

  6. Inna gives nasb to the mubtada' (closer element); Kana gives nasb to the khabar (farther element). The element not affected remains marfu'.

  7. Tasukun (ـْ) on the ta' of a verb form is NOT a damir. When no explicit fa'il or na'ib al-fa'il follows such a verb, the doer is a damir mustatir (hidden pronoun) that must be identified in full i'rab.

  8. Section 3.8 (Mansubat — 12 roles) is the starting point for the next class.

  9. Critical study habit: Always ask — What role is this word playing? Why is it in this state? This approach prevents rote memorization and builds deep grammatical understanding that connects to Quranic analysis.

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text:
This session covered Section 3.8 — المنصوبات (words that are always in the nasab/accusative case). The instructor introduced the twelve categories of منصوبات, noting which had already been covered in previous sections, and then taught in detail:

  1. مفعول به (Maf'ul Bihi) — the direct object, with special focus on cases where the governing verb is dropped
  2. مفعول مطلق (Maf'ul Mutlaq) — the absolute object (masdar used for emphasis, type, or number)
  3. مفعول له / مفعول لأجله (Maf'ul Lahu) — the causal object
  4. مفعول معه (Maf'ul Ma'ahu) — the associative object (introduced via واو المعية)
  5. مفعول فيه / ظرف (Maf'ul Fihi / Zarf) — the adverbial object of time or place

The session also covered المنادى (Munada — the vocative) in detail, including Table 3.7 on its six grammatical states, and introduced ترخيم (Tarkhim — abbreviation in address).

docs.title: 1. المفاعيل الخمسة — The Five Maf'uls
docs.text:
All five types of مفعول fall under the umbrella term المفاعيل الخمسة (al-Mafa'il al-Khamsa). All مفعولs are always منصوب (in the nasab case).

# Term Description 1 مفعول به Direct object — the thing upon which the action of the فاعل takes place 2 مفعول مطلق Absolute object — the مصدر of the governing verb, used for emphasis, type, or number 3 مفعول له / لأجله Causal object — explains the reason/purpose of the action 4 مفعول معه Associative object — what accompanies the subject, following واو المعية 5 مفعول فيه Adverbial object — denotes time or place; also called ظرف

docs.title: 2. مفعول به (Maf'ul Bihi — Direct Object)
docs.text:
Definition: The word upon which the action of the فاعل (doer) takes place.

  • Previously taught as simply "مفعول," the full correct term is مفعول به to distinguish it from the other four types.
  • Always منصوب.
  • Example: شَرِبَ زَيْدٌ مَاءً — Zaid drank water. مَاءً is مفعول به (منصوب).

docs.title: Cases Where the Governing Verb (فعل) Is Dropped:
docs.text:
Case 1 — المنادى (The Vocative)

When calling upon someone, the implied verb أَدْعُو (I call) is dropped. The called person is the مفعول به of that dropped verb.

  • Particles of Address (حروف النداء):
  • أَ / أَيْ — used when the person addressed is near
  • أَيَا / هَيَا — used when far

  • يَا — used for both near and far (most common)

  • The harf of nida substitutes the dropped فعل.

Table 3.7 — The Six States of المنادى:

# Condition Grammatical State Example 1 مضاف (Mudaf — genitive construct) منصوب يَا عَبْدَ اللَّهِ ، يَا طَالِبَ الْعِلْمِ 2 شبيه بالمضاف (resembling mudaf) منصوب (advanced — not covered) 3 نكرة غير معيّنة (unspecified indefinite) منصوب يَا رَجُلًا (a blind man calling an unknown person) 4 نكرة معيّنة (specified indefinite) مبني على الضم يَا رَجُلُ (you can see the man you're calling) 5 معرفة مفردة (definite singular proper noun) مبني على الضم يَا زَيْدُ ، يَا اللَّهُ ، يَا خَالِدُ 6 معرّف بالألف واللام (definite with ال) مبني على الضم يَا أَيُّهَا الرَّجُلُ ، يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ

Important correction: The textbook labels states 4–6 as "مرفوع" — this is misleading. They are not truly مرفوع; they are مبني على الضم (indeclinable with a single ضمة). They are still مفعول به of the implied verb in terms of meaning, but they are mabni and do not change their vowel ending.

Why the distinction? Words that are مبني always retain their fixed vowel regardless of grammatical position. So يَا زَيْدُ appears with a ضمة not because it is مرفوع, but because proper nouns as منادى become مبني على الضم.

Case 2 — أَهْلًا وَسَهْلًا (Welcoming Expression)

  • Full original sentence: أَتَيْتَ أَهْلًا وَوَطِئْتَ سَهْلًا (You have come to your own people and trodden comfortable ground)
  • Both verbs أَتَيْتَ and وَطِئْتَ are dropped; only the مفعول به remains.
  • أَهْلًا وَسَهْلًا are منصوب because they are مفعول به of dropped verbs.

Case 3 — Warning (التحذير)

  • When warning someone urgently, the governing verb is dropped to save time.
  • Example: الطَّرِيقَ الطَّرِيقَ instead of اتَّقِ الطَّرِيقَ (Beware of the road!)
  • The implied verb is اتَّقِ — الطَّرِيقَ is منصوب as مفعول به.
  • Another example: إِيَّاكَ وَالْأَسَدَ — save yourself from the lion (instead of اتَّقِ نَفْسَكَ مِنَ الْأَسَدِ).

docs.title: 3. ترخيم (Tarkhim — Abbreviation in Address)
docs.text:
When calling someone by name frequently, the final letter may be dropped for brevity or as an expression of affection.

  • يَا مَالِكُ → يَا مَالُ or يَا مَالِ
  • Option 1: Drop the كاف and give its ضمة to the now-final letter → يَا مَالُ
  • Option 2: Drop the كاف and keep the original vowel of the لام → يَا مَالِ
  • يَا مَنْصُورُ → يَا مَنْصُ

docs.title: 4. مفعول مطلق (Maf'ul Mutlaq — Absolute Object)
docs.text:
Definition: The مصدر of the very فعل that governs it in the same sentence.

Three Purposes:

Purpose Arabic Term Example Meaning Emphasis تأكيد ضَرَبْتُهُ ضَرْبًا I beat him (severely) Description of type بيان النوع جَلَسْتُ جِلْسَةَ الْقَارِئِ I sat as a reciter would sit Number of times بيان العدد ضَرَبْتُهُ ضَرْبَتَيْنِ I beat him twice
  • ضَرْبًا is the مصدر of ضَرَبَ — منصوب as مفعول مطلق.
  • جِلْسَةَ الْقَارِئِ: جِلْسَةَ is مضاف, الْقَارِئِ is مضاف إليه; together they are the مفعول مطلق.
  • ضَرْبَتَيْنِ: the dual form of ضَرْبَة; منصوب shown by يَاء + نون (sign of dual in nasab).

docs.title: When the فعل Governing مفعول مطلق Is Dropped:
docs.text:
• خَيْرَ مَقْدَمٍ (Welcome) — from قَدِمْتَ قُدُومًا خَيْرَ مَقْدَمٍ
  • قَدِمْتَ (verb) dropped
  • قُدُومًا (مفعول مطلق) dropped
  • Only the صفة of the مفعول مطلق remains: خَيْرَ مَقْدَمٍ (منصوب because it is the sifah of the dropped مفعول مطلق)

docs.title: 5. مفعول له / لأجله (Maf'ul Lahu — Causal Object)
docs.text:
Definition: A noun (generally a مصدر) that explains the reason or purpose for which an action was performed.

  • Always منصوب.
  • Example: ضَرَبْتُهُ تَأْدِيبًا — I beat him (in order) to discipline him.
  • تَأْدِيبًا is the مصدر of أَدَّبَ يُؤَدِّبُ — منصوب as مفعول لأجله.
  • It answers the question: لماذا؟ (Why?)

docs.title: 6. مفعول معه (Maf'ul Ma'ahu — Associative Object)
docs.text:
Definition: A noun that follows واو المعية (the waw of companionship), indicating what accompanies the subject in the action.

  • واو in Arabic has multiple meanings:
  • واو العطف — conjunction (and), which would put the following word in رفع to match the subject

  • واو المعية — "with," which causes the following noun to be منصوب as مفعول معه

  • Example: جَاءَ زَيْدٌ وَالْكِتَابَ — Zaid came with the book.

  • Here واو = واو المعية (meaning مع/with)
  • الْكِتَابَ is منصوب as مفعول معه
  • Contrast: جَاءَ زَيْدٌ وَالْكِتَابُ — Zaid and the book came (واو العطف; الْكِتَابُ is مرفوع as a second فاعل)

docs.title: 7. مفعول فيه / ظرف (Maf'ul Fihi / Zarf — Adverbial Object)
docs.text:
Definition: A noun denoting the time (زمان) or place (مكان) in which an action occurs.

  • Also called ظرف (Zarf).
  • By default, ظرف is منصوب (as مفعول فيه), unless it follows a حرف جر, in which case it takes كسرة (jar case).
  • Two types:
  • ظرف زمان — time adverb: سَافَرْتُ شَهْرًا (I traveled for a month — شَهْرًا is منصوب)
  • ظرف مكان — place adverb
  • Each type is further divided into محدود (bounded/specific) and مبهم (vague/unspecified) — to be covered in the next class.

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning المنصوبات Words in the accusative/nasab case المفاعيل الخمسة The five types of مفعول مفعول به Direct object (the thing acted upon) مفعول مطلق Absolute object (مصدر of the governing verb) مفعول له / لأجله Causal object (explains reason for action) مفعول معه Associative object (accompanies subject via واو المعية) مفعول فيه Adverbial object (time or place); also called ظرف المنادى The one being called/addressed (vocative) حرف النداء Particle of address (يَا، أَ، أَيْ، أَيَا، هَيَا) أَدْعُو I call — the implied verb dropped in the vocative مبني على الضم Indeclinable with a single ضمة (no tanwin) ترخيم Abbreviation — dropping the last letter of a name in address مصدر Verbal noun / infinitive تأكيد Emphasis بيان النوع Description of type/manner بيان العدد Description of number/frequency واو المعية The waw of companionship (meaning مع — "with") واو العطف Conjunctive waw (meaning "and") ظرف زمان Time adverb ظرف مكان Place adverb محدود Bounded/specific (type of zarf) مبهم Vague/unspecified (type of zarf) التحذير Warning — a context where the governing verb is dropped مضاف The first term in a genitive construct مضاف إليه The second term in a genitive construct نكرة غير معيّنة Unspecified indefinite (e.g., calling an unknown person) نكرة معيّنة Specified indefinite (you can see who you're calling) معرفة مفردة Definite singular (proper noun)

docs.title: Example 1: مفعول به
docs.text:
شَرِبَ زَيْدٌ مَاءً

Word Term I'rab شَرِبَ فعل ماضٍ مبني على الفتح زَيْدٌ فاعل مرفوع، علامته الضمة مَاءً مفعول به منصوب، علامته الفتحة

docs.title: Example 2: المنادى — مضاف (منصوب)
docs.text:
يَا عَبْدَ اللَّهِ

Word Term I'rab يَا حرف نداء مبني، لا محل له من الإعراب عَبْدَ منادى مضاف / مفعول به للفعل المحذوف أَدْعُو منصوب، علامته الفتحة اللَّهِ مضاف إليه مجرور، علامته الكسرة

docs.title: Example 3: المنادى — معرفة مفردة (مبني على الضم)
docs.text:
يَا زَيْدُ

Word Term I'rab يَا حرف نداء مبني، لا محل له من الإعراب زَيْدُ منادى معرفة مفردة مبني على الضم في محل نصب (مفعول به للفعل المحذوف)

docs.title: Example 4: مفعول مطلق للتأكيد
docs.text:
ضَرَبْتُهُ ضَرْبًا

Word Term I'rab ضَرَبْ فعل ماضٍ مبني على السكون تُ فاعل (ضمير) مبني في محل رفع هُ مفعول به مبني في محل نصب ضَرْبًا مفعول مطلق (للتأكيد) منصوب، علامته الفتحة

docs.title: Example 5: مفعول مطلق لبيان النوع
docs.text:
جَلَسْتُ جِلْسَةَ الْقَارِئِ

Word Term I'rab جَلَسْتُ فعل + فاعل — جِلْسَةَ مفعول مطلق (لبيان النوع)، مضاف منصوب، علامته الفتحة الْقَارِئِ مضاف إليه مجرور، علامته الكسرة

docs.title: Example 6: مفعول مطلق لبيان العدد
docs.text:
ضَرَبْتُهُ ضَرْبَتَيْنِ

Word Term I'rab ضَرَبْتُهُ فعل + فاعل + مفعول به — ضَرْبَتَيْنِ مفعول مطلق (لبيان العدد)، مثنى منصوب، علامته الياء (لأنه مثنى)

docs.title: Example 7: مفعول له
docs.text:
ضَرَبْتُهُ تَأْدِيبًا

Word Term I'rab ضَرَبْتُهُ فعل + فاعل + مفعول به — تَأْدِيبًا مفعول له (يبين سبب الفعل) منصوب، علامته الفتحة

docs.title: Example 8: مفعول معه
docs.text:
جَاءَ زَيْدٌ وَالْكِتَابَ

Word Term I'rab جَاءَ فعل ماضٍ مبني على الفتح زَيْدٌ فاعل مرفوع، علامته الضمة وَ واو المعية حرف، مبني الْكِتَابَ مفعول معه منصوب، علامته الفتحة

docs.title: Example 9: مفعول فيه (ظرف زمان)
docs.text:
سَافَرْتُ شَهْرًا

Word Term I'rab سَافَرْتُ فعل + فاعل — شَهْرًا مفعول فيه / ظرف زمان منصوب، علامته الفتحة

docs.title: Practice Examples
docs.text:
1. يَا طَالِبَ الْعِلْمِ — Identify: What type of منادى? Why is it منصوب? (Answer: مضاف — منصوب because it is مفعول به of the dropped verb أَدْعُو)

  2. يَا أَيُّهَا الْمُسْلِمُونَ — Identify: What type of منادى? Why does it have ضمة? (Answer: معرّف بالألف واللام — مبني على الضم; جمع مذكر سالم shows رفع with واو normally, but here مبني)

  3. أَهْلًا وَسَهْلًا — What are the dropped verbs? Why are these words منصوب? (Answer: أَتَيْتَ and وَطِئْتَ are dropped; منصوب as مفعول به of those implied verbs)

  4. الطَّرِيقَ الطَّرِيقَ — What is the implied verb? What grammatical category is this? (Answer: اتَّقِ — this is التحذير; الطَّرِيقَ is مفعول به of the dropped اتَّقِ)

  5. Distinguish between these two sentences:

  6. جَاءَ زَيْدٌ وَالْكِتَابُ (واو العطف — the book also came)

  7. جَاءَ زَيْدٌ وَالْكِتَابَ (واو المعية — Zaid came with the book)

  8. From the assigned reading: Read the last page of Section 3.8, understand the distinction between ظرف محدود and ظرف مبهم, and complete the translation/i'rab exercise identifying مفعول types.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. All مفاعيل الخمسة are منصوب — this is the defining grammatical feature uniting all five types of مفعول.

  2. مفعول به vs. مفعول — "مفعول" is the general umbrella term; "مفعول به" is the specific term for the direct object. Use the full term to distinguish it from the other four.

  3. The governing verb can be dropped in three contexts for مفعول به: النداء (vocative), set expressions like أَهْلًا وَسَهْلًا, and urgent warnings (التحذير). The مفعول به remains منصوب in all three.

  4. المنادى has six grammatical states — states 1–3 are منصوب; states 4–6 are مبني على الضم (not truly مرفوع as the textbook misleadingly labels them). This distinction is critical: the textbook's use of "مرفوع" for these states is an error — they should read مبني على الضم.

  5. مفعول مطلق must be the مصدر of the governing verb — this is the definitional requirement. Its three purposes are: تأكيد (emphasis), بيان النوع (type), بيان العدد (number).

  6. واو المعية vs. واو العطف — the same و can create مفعول معه (nasab) or conjunction (rafa'). Context and intended meaning determine which واو is present.

  7. ظرف = مفعول فيه — these are two names for the same thing. ظرف is منصوب by default; it only goes into jar if preceded by a حرف جر.

  8. Recommended supplementary resource: لسان القرآن (Lisan al-Quran) — developed by teachers of Jami'ah Aisha Siddiqah, Karachi. Available in English. Focuses on Quranic Arabic with extensive exercises. Good for additional practice beyond this textbook.

  9. Next class (Thursday): Complete مفعول فيه — specifically the distinction between ظرف محدود and ظرف مبهم — and go through the assigned translation/i'rab exercise before attending.

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session completed the chapter on the five types of Maf'ool (objects) by covering Maf'ool Fihi — the last of the five. The teacher explained that Maf'ool Fihi and Zarf are identical concepts, clarifying why Zarf is always in the Nasb case (it is a Maf'ool, and Maf'ool is Mansoob). The session covered the two subtypes of both Zarf al-Zaman and Zarf al-Makan (Mahdood and Mubham), and concluded with a full class exercise where students identified all five types of Maf'ool across a series of Arabic sentences. The teacher also previewed upcoming topics: Hal, Tamiz, and Arabic numbers (1–1,000,000).

docs.title: 1. Maf'ool Fihi (مفعول فيه) — The Fifth Type of Maf'ool
docs.text:
• Definition: An ism (noun) that denotes the time (zaman) or place (makan) in which an action took place.
  • It is identical to Zarf — the terms are interchangeable.
  • Since it is a Maf'ool, it is always Mansoob (in the Nasb case).
  • In Arabic, no preposition is needed where English uses "for": سَافَرْتُ شَهْرًا (I traveled for a month) — not لِشَهْرٍ.

docs.title: 2. Types of Zarf al-Zaman (ظرف الزمان) — Adverbs of Time
docs.text:
Both Zarf al-Zaman and Zarf al-Makan are divided into two subtypes:

Type Meaning Description Mahdood (محدود) Limited / Restricted Denotes a specific, defined period of time Mubham (مبهم) Unlimited / Unrestricted / Ambiguous Denotes a vague or open-ended period of time

Zarf al-Zaman Mahdood examples: - يَوْم — a day (24 hours; defined) - لَيْل — a night (defined) - شَهْر — a month (29–30 days) - سَنَة — a year

Examples in sentences: - صُمْتُ يَوْمًا — I fasted for one day - عَمِلْتُ لَيْلًا — I worked for one night - صُمْتُ شَهْرًا — I fasted for a month

Zarf al-Zaman Mubham examples: - دَهْر — a long (unspecified) period of time (could be decades, centuries, millennia) - حِين — some time (usually short, but unspecified)

Examples: - صُمْتُ دَهْرًا — I fasted for a long time (exact duration unknown) - دَعَا نُوحٌ قَوْمَهُ حِينًا مِنَ الدَّهْرِ — Nuh called his people for some time - Note: Nuh (AS) called his people for ~910 years; the Quran uses حِين because relative to all of human civilization, even this is a short span.

docs.title: 3. Types of Zarf al-Makan (ظرف المكان) — Adverbs of Place
docs.text:
Same two subtypes apply: Mahdood and Mubham.

Key rule: - Zarf al-Makan Mahdood — the preposition فِي is explicitly stated in speech/writing. The following word is then Majroor (governed by the harf jarr فِي), not Mansoob. - Zarf al-Makan Mubham — the preposition فِي is implied/assumed, not written. The word remains Mansoob as Maf'ool Fihi.

Zarf al-Makan Mahdood examples (فِي is mentioned — limited/defined space): - صَلَّيْتُ فِي الْمَسْجِدِ — I prayed in the mosque - جَلَسْتُ فِي الدَّارِ — I sat in the house - فِي السُّوقِ ، فِي السَّمَاءِ ، فِي الْأَرْضِ

Zarf al-Makan Mubham examples (فِي is implied — directional/unlimited): - أَمَامَ — in front of - خَلْفَ — behind - يَمِينَ — to the right of - شِمَالَ — to the left of - فَوْقَ — above - تَحْتَ — beneath/under

These words denote direction but the actual distance/space is unspecified (in front of a mountain = hundreds of miles; in front of me = a few feet). Therefore فِي is not written, and these words are Mansoob as Maf'ool Fihi.

Do NOT say فِي خَلْفِ or فِي أَمَامِ — just say خَلْفَ and أَمَامَ.

docs.title: 4. All Five Maf'ools in One Sentence
docs.text:
A combined example sentence containing all five types of Maf'ool:

حَمِدْتُ حَمْدًا حَمِيدًا وَحَمِيدًا رِعَايَةَ شُكْرِهِ دَهْرًا مَدِيدًا

I praised Hamid a lot, along with Hamid, out of regard for thanking him, for an extended period of time.

Word Type of Maf'ool حَمْدًا Maf'ool Mutlaq (masdar of the same verb) حَمِيدًا (first) Maf'ool Bihi (the one being praised) وَحَمِيدًا (with وَاو المعية) Maf'ool Ma'ahu رِعَايَةَ شُكْرِهِ Maf'ool Lahu (reason/purpose — out of gratitude) دَهْرًا مَدِيدًا Maf'ool Fihi / Zarf al-Zaman Mubham

Notes on the irab of رِعَايَةَ شُكْرِهِ: - رِعَايَةَ = Mudaf - شُكْرِ = Mudaf ilayhi (and also itself a Mudaf) - هِ = Mudaf ilayhi of شُكْر

Notes on دَهْرًا مَدِيدًا: - دَهْرًا = Mawsoof (described noun) - مَدِيدًا = Sifa (adjective/description)

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning مفعول فيه The fifth type of Maf'ool; noun denoting time or place of an action ظرف Adverb (of time or place); identical to Maf'ool Fihi ظرف الزمان Adverb of time ظرف المكان Adverb of place محدود Limited, restricted (Mahdood) مبهم Unlimited, unrestricted, ambiguous (Mubham) دَهْر A long, unspecified period of time حِين Some time (often short, but unspecified) أَمَامَ In front of خَلْفَ Behind فَوْقَ Above تَحْتَ Beneath / under يَمِينَ To the right of شِمَالَ To the left of مَدِيد Extended, prolonged رِعَايَة Regard, consideration شُكْر Gratitude, thanks نَائِب الفاعل Substitute for the Fa'il (used in passive voice sentences) تَمْيِيز Tamiz — topic previewed for next class حَال Hal — topic previewed for next class

docs.title: Example 1: سَافَرْتُ شَهْرًا
docs.text:
• سَافَرْتُ — Fi'l + Fa'il (doer implied: I)
  • شَهْرًا — Maf'ool Fihi / Zarf al-Zaman Mahdood → Mansoob

docs.title: Example 2: صَلَّيْتُ فِي الْمَسْجِدِ
docs.text:
• صَلَّيْتُ — Fi'l + Fa'il
  • فِي الْمَسْجِدِ — Jar Majroor; فِي is Harf Jarr, الْمَسْجِدِ is Majroor
  • Type: Zarf al-Makan Mahdood (فِي is explicit)

docs.title: Example 3: جَلَسْتُ أَمَامَ الْبَيْتِ
docs.text:
• جَلَسْتُ — Fi'l + Fa'il
  • أَمَامَ — Maf'ool Fihi / Zarf al-Makan Mubham → Mansoob (فِي is implied)
  • الْبَيْتِ — Mudaf ilayhi of أَمَامَ

docs.title: Example 4 (from exercise): تُوقَدُ الْمَصَابِيحُ لَيْلًا
docs.text:
• تُوقَدُ — Fi'l (passive)
  • الْمَصَابِيحُ — Na'ib al-Fa'il (substitute for the Fa'il in passive)
  • لَيْلًا — Maf'ool Fihi / Zarf al-Zaman Mahdood → Mansoob

docs.title: Example 5 (from exercise): قَرَأْتُ الدَّرْسَ صَبَاحًا أَمَامَ الْمُعَلِّمِ
docs.text:
• قَرَأْتُ — Fi'l + Fa'il
  • الدَّرْسَ — Maf'ool Bihi → Mansoob
  • صَبَاحًا — Maf'ool Fihi / Zarf al-Zaman Mahdood → Mansoob
  • أَمَامَ الْمُعَلِّمِ — Maf'ool Fihi / Zarf al-Makan Mubham → Mansoob

docs.title: Example 6 (from exercise): تَدُورُ الْأَرْضُ دَوْرَةً فِي الْيَوْمِ
docs.text:
• تَدُورُ — Fi'l
  • الْأَرْضُ — Fa'il → Marfoo'
  • دَوْرَةً — Maf'ool Mutlaq → Mansoob
  • فِي الْيَوْمِ — Jar Majroor; also functions as Maf'ool Fihi / Zarf al-Zaman Mahdood

docs.title: Practice Examples
docs.text:
The following sentences were analyzed in class (students translated and identified Maf'ool types):

Arabic Sentence Translation Key Maf'ool Identified أَكَلَ زَيْدٌ أَكْلَتَيْنِ Zaid ate two meals Maf'ool Mutlaq (Tasniyya of masdar أَكْلَة) رَكِبَ إِبْرَاهِيمُ وَالْحِصَانَ Ibrahim rode along with the horse وَالْحِصَانَ = Maf'ool Ma'ah (وَاو المعية) يَثِبُ الْأَمِيرُ وُثُوبَ الْأَسِيرِ The panther leaps like the leap of a lion وُثُوبَ الْأَسِيرِ = Maf'ool Mutlaq (Mudaf + Mudaf ilayhi) يُسَافِرُ خَالِدٌ إِلَى مِصْرَ طَلَبًا لِلْعِلْمِ Khalid traveled to Egypt seeking knowledge طَلَبًا لِلْعِلْمِ = Maf'ool Lahu (reason/purpose) لَا تَبْخَلْ خَشْيَةَ الْفَقْرِ Do not be stingy out of fear of poverty خَشْيَةَ الْفَقْرِ = Maf'ool Lahu تُوقَدُ الْمَصَابِيحُ لَيْلًا The lamps are lit at night لَيْلًا = Maf'ool Fihi / Zarf al-Zaman سَارَتِ السَّيَّارَةُ سَاعَةً The car traveled for an hour سَاعَةً = Maf'ool Fihi / Zarf al-Zaman Mahdood جَلَسَتِ الْهِرَّةُ تَحْتَ الْمَكْتَبِ The cat sat under the desk تَحْتَ الْمَكْتَبِ = Maf'ool Fihi / Zarf al-Makan Mubham افْعَلِ الْخَيْرَ رُبًّا فِي الْخَيْرِ Do good for the love of good رُبًّا فِي الْخَيْرِ = Maf'ool Lahu وَقَفَ الشُّرْطِيُّ وُقُوفًا نَشِيطًا The policeman stood actively وُقُوفًا نَشِيطًا = Maf'ool Mutlaq (with Sifa)

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Maf'ool Fihi = Zarf — they are the same thing. Zarf is Mansoob because it is a Maf'ool.

  2. No preposition needed in Arabic where English uses "for" with time expressions: سَافَرْتُ شَهْرًا, not سَافَرْتُ لِشَهْرٍ.

  3. Mahdood vs. Mubham applies to both Zarf al-Zaman and Zarf al-Makan:

  4. Mahdood = defined, limited (يَوْم، لَيْل، شَهْر، سَنَة / a specific room, mosque, house)

  5. Mubham = undefined, open-ended (دَهْر، حِين / directional words like أَمَامَ، خَلْفَ، تَحْتَ)

  6. Critical rule for Zarf al-Makan:

  7. Mahdood → use فِي explicitly → following noun is Majroor

  8. Mubham → فِي is implied → word stays Mansoob as Maf'ool Fihi

  9. All five Maf'ools can appear in a single sentence — the combined example حَمِدْتُ حَمْدًا حَمِيدًا وَحَمِيدًا رِعَايَةَ شُكْرِهِ دَهْرًا مَدِيدًا demonstrates this.

  10. Coming up next class (Thursday — extended session):

  11. Section 3.8.6 + exercise
  12. Hal (حَال) and Tamiz (تَمْيِيز)
  13. Arabic numbers 1–1,000,000 will be assigned for memorization (students encouraged to begin early — treat like English spelling rules, which require repeated practice)
  14. Target: memorize and practice numbers 1–100 a minimum of 100 times

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text:
This session introduced two major grammatical concepts:

  1. Hal and Dhul-Hal — A word (or sentence) that describes the condition/state of the doer (fa'il) or object (maf'ul bihi) at the time an action takes place. Six detailed notes were covered, followed by sentence analysis practice.

  2. Tamiz and Mumayyaz — An indeterminate noun that removes vagueness or ambiguity created by a preceding noun, particularly in contexts of distance, weight, measure, or number. An overview of the rules for Arabic numerals was given as a preview for the next class.

docs.title: Concept 1: Hal (حال) and Dhul-Hal (ذو الحال)
docs.text:
Definition: Hal is an ism that describes the condition or state of either the fa'il or the maf'ul bihi at the time the action takes place. The word whose condition is being described is called the Dhul-Hal.

Note 1 — Definitions - Hal (حال): The word describing the condition/state. Linguistic meaning: state or condition. - Dhul-Hal (ذو الحال): The word whose condition is being described. - Pattern: Dhul-Hal generally comes first, Hal comes second.

Note 2 — Hal is Mansub (in the Nasab case) - Hal is always in the nasab case. - Since Hal is generally nakira (indefinite, no alif-lam), it takes two fathas (tanwin nasb).

Note 3 — Dhul-Hal is Ma'rifah; Hal is Nakira - Dhul-Hal is generally ma'rifah (definite). - Hal is generally nakira (indefinite). - The word generally indicates exceptions are possible.

Note 4 — Switching Order When Dhul-Hal is Nakira - Normally: Dhul-Hal → Hal. - Exception: If the Dhul-Hal is nakira, the Hal may be brought before the Dhul-Hal (order is flipped for emphasis). - Example: جَاءَنِي رَاكِبًا رَجُلٌ — A man came to me while riding. (Hal comes first because Dhul-Hal is nakira.)

Note 5 — Dhul-Hal Can Be a Dhamir (Pronoun) - The Dhul-Hal does not have to be an explicit noun; it can be a hidden/implied pronoun within a verb. - Example: زَيْدٌ أَكَلَ جَالِسًا — The implied huwa in akala is the Dhul-Hal; jalisan is the Hal.

Note 6 — Hal Can Be an Entire Sentence (Jumlah) - Hal does not have to be a single noun. It can be a full sentence.

A. If Hal is a Jumlah Ismiyyah (nominal sentence): - A waw haliyah (وَاو حَالِيَّة) is added before the mubtada. - Example (Quran): لَا تَقْرَبُوا الصَّلَاةَ وَأَنْتُمْ سُكَارَى — Do not come near salah while you are intoxicated. - وَ = waw haliyah | أَنْتُمْ = mubtada | سُكَارَى = khabar | The whole jumlah ismiyyah = Hal - Dhul-Hal = the dhamir inside لَا تَقْرَبُوا

B. If Hal is a Jumlah Fi'liyyah (verbal sentence) with a Madi verb: - Both waw haliyah AND قَدْ must appear before the madi verb. - Example: جَاءَ زَيْدٌ وَقَدْ خَرَجَ خَادِمُهُ — Zaid came while his servant had left. - وَ = waw haliyah | قَدْ = harf | خَرَجَ = fi'l | خَادِمُهُ = fa'il of kharaja | Whole phrase = Hal - Dhul-Hal = زَيْدٌ

docs.title: Concept 2: Tamiz (تَمْيِيز) and Mumayyaz (مُمَيَّز)
docs.text:
Definition: Tamiz is an ism nakira that removes the ambiguity or vagueness created by the preceding ism (the mumayyaz). The ambiguity may be in distance, weight, measure, or number.

  • Mumayyaz (مُمَيَّز): The ambiguous word — comes first.
  • Tamiz (تَمْيِيز): The clarifying word — comes second.
  • Like Hal, Tamiz is nakira and in the nasab case (two fathas if a regular singular indefinite noun).
  • Mumayyaz and Tamiz together function as Fa'il or Maf'ul Bihi, just like Dhul-Hal and Hal.

Rule regarding numbers: If the mumayyaz is a number, the rules specific to Arabic numerals apply. The tamiz does not always have to be nakira mansub in this case — the rules vary by number range:

Number Range Notes 1–2 Special rules 3–10 Special rules (noun after them is in jar + plural) 11–12 Special rules (noun is singular mansub) 13–19 Special rules 20, 30 ... 90 (tens) Special rules (noun is singular mansub) 21–29, 31–39, etc. (compound) Special rules 100 Noun is singular majrur 1000 Special rules

(Full number rules to be covered in the next class — memorization of 1–100 assigned as homework.)

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning حَال (Hal) State / condition — the word describing the condition of the fa'il or maf'ul ذُو الْحَال (Dhul-Hal) The one whose condition is being described فَاعِل (Fa'il) The doer of the action / subject مَفْعُول بِهِ (Maf'ul Bihi) The object of the action مَعْرِفَة (Ma'rifah) Definite noun نَكِرَة (Nakira) Indefinite noun مَنْصُوب (Mansub) Noun in the nasab case وَاو حَالِيَّة (Waw Haliyah) The waw that converts a jumlah into a Hal جُمْلَة اسْمِيَّة (Jumlah Ismiyyah) Nominal sentence (mubtada + khabar) جُمْلَة فِعْلِيَّة (Jumlah Fi'liyyah) Verbal sentence (fi'l + fa'il) ضَمِير مُسْتَتِر (Dhamir Mustatir) Hidden/implied pronoun تَمْيِيز (Tamiz) Noun that clarifies/specifies the mumayyaz مُمَيَّز (Mumayyaz) The ambiguous noun whose vagueness tamiz removes رَاكِبًا (Rakiban) Riding / mounted (hal in nasab) نَائِمًا (Na'iman) Sleeping (hal in nasab) جَالِسًا / جَالِسَيْنِ (Jalisan / Jalisayni) Sitting — singular / dual (dual in nasab) كَوْكَب (Kawkab) Star أَحَدَ عَشَرَ (Ahada Ashara) Eleven ذِرَاع (Dhira') Arm span / cubit (unit of measure) حَرِير (Harir) Silk مِلْء الْكَفّ (Mil' al-Kaff) A handful شِبْر (Shibr) A hand-span (unit of length) رَطْل (Ratl) A unit of measurement (roughly a pound) قُبَّة / قَدَح (Qubba / Qadah) Cup / vessel

docs.title: Example 1: جَاءَ زَيْدٌ رَاكِبًا
docs.text:
Zaid came while mounted.

Word Role Reason جَاءَ Fi'l (verb) Past tense verb زَيْدٌ Dhul-Hal Ma'rifah (proper noun); not in nasab رَاكِبًا Hal Nakira + mansub (two fathas) زَيْدٌ + رَاكِبًا Fa'il of جَاءَ Dhul-Hal + Hal together = Fa'il

docs.title: Example 2: جِئْتُ عَمْرًا نَائِمًا
docs.text:
I came to Amr while he was asleep.

Word Role Reason جِئْتُ Fi'l + Fa'il (dhamir tu) عَمْرًا Dhul-Hal Maf'ul bihi of ji'tu — ma'rifah proper noun نَائِمًا Hal Nakira + mansub عَمْرًا + نَائِمًا Maf'ul Bihi of جِئْتُ Dhul-Hal + Hal together = Maf'ul

docs.title: Example 3: لَقِيتُ بَكْرًا وَهُوَ جَالِسٌ
docs.text:
I met Bakr while he was sitting. (Hal = Jumlah Ismiyyah)

Word Role Reason لَقِيتُ Fi'l + Fa'il بَكْرًا Dhul-Hal Maf'ul bihi; ma'rifah وَ Waw Haliyah Converts jumlah into Hal هُوَ Mubtada جَالِسٌ Khabar وَهُوَ جَالِسٌ Hal (Jumlah Ismiyyah) Full nominal sentence functioning as Hal بَكْرًا + وَهُوَ جَالِسٌ Maf'ul Bihi of لَقِيتُ Dhul-Hal + Hal together

docs.title: Example 4: زَيْدٌ أَكَلَ جَالِسًا
docs.text:
Zaid ate while sitting. (Dhul-Hal = hidden dhamir)

Word Role Reason زَيْدٌ Mubtada أَكَلَ Fi'l هُوَ (implied in أَكَلَ) Dhul-Hal Hidden dhamir referring to Zaid جَالِسًا Hal Nakira + mansub هُوَ (dhamir) + جَالِسًا Fa'il of أَكَلَ Dhul-Hal + Hal together أَكَلَ جَالِسًا Khabar of زَيْدٌ Fi'l + Fa'il = Jumlah Fi'liyyah functioning as Khabar

docs.title: Example 5: جَاءَ زَيْدٌ وَقَدْ خَرَجَ خَادِمُهُ
docs.text:
Zaid came while his servant had left. (Hal = Jumlah Fi'liyyah with Madi)

Word Role Reason جَاءَ Fi'l زَيْدٌ Dhul-Hal → Fa'il وَ Waw Haliyah قَدْ Harf (required before madi in Hal) خَرَجَ Fi'l (madi) خَادِمُهُ Fa'il of خَرَجَ وَقَدْ خَرَجَ خَادِمُهُ Hal (Jumlah Fi'liyyah)

docs.title: Tamiz Example: رَأَيْتُ أَحَدَ عَشَرَ كَوْكَبًا
docs.text:
I saw eleven stars.

Word Role Reason رَأَيْتُ Fi'l + Fa'il أَحَدَ عَشَرَ Mumayyaz The number creates ambiguity — eleven of what? كَوْكَبًا Tamiz Nakira + mansub; clarifies what the eleven refers to أَحَدَ عَشَرَ + كَوْكَبًا Maf'ul Bihi of رَأَيْتُ

docs.title: Hal/Dhul-Hal Exercise (from class)
docs.text: Sentence Translation Hal Dhul-Hal أُحِبُّ الطَّالِبَ مُجْتَهِدًا I love the student while he is hardworking مُجْتَهِدًا الطَّالِب لَقِيتُ زَيْدًا رَاكِبَيْنِ I met Zaid while we were both riding رَاكِبَيْنِ (dual) Dhamir in لَقِيتُ (fa'il) + زَيْدًا (maf'ul) — two dhul-hals لَا تَأْكُلُوا الْفَاكِهَةَ وَهِيَ حَامِضَة Do not eat fruit while it is unripe وَهِيَ حَامِضَة (jumlah ismiyyah with waw haliyah) الْفَاكِهَة رَجَعَ الزَّعِيمُ مُنْتَصِرًا The leader returned victorious مُنْتَصِرًا الزَّعِيم قَطَفَ التَّاجِرُ الْعِنَبَ نَاضِجًا The merchant picked the grapes while they were ripe نَاضِجًا الْعِنَب لَا تَأْكُلِ الطَّعَامَ حَارًّا Do not eat the food while hot حَارًّا الطَّعَام نَصَرْتُ زَيْدًا مَجْدُودًا I helped Zaid while he was tied up مَجْدُودًا زَيْدًا غَبَا أَخُوكَ وَقَدْ حَضَرَ جَمِيعُ أَصْدِقَائِهِ Your brother left while all his friends had arrived وَقَدْ حَضَرَ جَمِيعُ أَصْدِقَائِهِ (jumlah fi'liyyah with waw + qad) أَخُوكَ دَخَلَ اللِّصُّ الْمَنْزِلَ وَأَهْلُهُ نَائِمُونَ The thief entered the house while the people were sleeping وَأَهْلُهُ نَائِمُونَ (jumlah ismiyyah with waw haliyah) الْمَنْزِل نَامَتِ الْأَشْجَارُ وَلَمَّا تُثْمِرْ The trees grew but had not yet borne fruit وَلَمَّا تُثْمِرْ الْأَشْجَار

docs.title: Tamiz Exercise (from class)
docs.text: Sentence Translation Mumayyaz Tamiz فِي الْحَقْلِ عِشْرُونَ بَقَرَةً In the field there are twenty cows عِشْرُونَ بَقَرَةً بِعْتُهُ ذِرَاعًا حَرِيرًا I sold him an arm's length of silk ذِرَاعًا حَرِيرًا الْفِيلُ أَكْبَرُ مِنَ الْجَمَلِ جِسْمًا The elephant is bigger than the camel in body أَكْبَرُ جِسْمًا أَطْعَمْتُ الدَّجَاجَةَ مِلْءَ الْكَفِّ حَبًّا I fed the chicken a handful of grain مِلْءَ الْكَفِّ حَبًّا لَا أَمْلِكُ شِبْرًا أَرْضًا I do not own a hand-span of land شِبْرًا أَرْضًا شَرِبْتُ رَطْلًا لَبَنًا I drank a measure of milk رَطْلًا لَبَنًا شَرِبْتُ قَدَحًا مَاءً I drank a cup of water قَدَحًا مَاءً

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Hal and Dhul-Hal always come in a pair — just like fi'l/fa'il, mubtada/khabar, sifah/mawsuf, and mudaf/mudaf ilayhi.

  2. Hal has two essential properties:

  3. It is nakira (indefinite).

  4. It is in the nasab case (usually two fathas for a regular singular noun; ya-noon for dual in nasab).

  5. Dhul-Hal is generally ma'rifah (definite) — this is how you distinguish Hal from Dhul-Hal when both appear together.

  6. Dhul-Hal + Hal together function as a grammatical unit — they jointly become either the Fa'il or the Maf'ul Bihi of the verb.

  7. Hal can be an entire sentence:

  8. Jumlah Ismiyyah → add waw haliyah before the mubtada.

  9. Jumlah Fi'liyyah with a madi verb → add waw haliyah + قَدْ before the verb.

  10. Waw has multiple meanings in Arabic — waw al-'atf (conjunction), waw al-qasam (oath), waw al-haliyah (hal), waw al-ma'iyyah (accompaniment). Context determines which meaning applies.

  11. The Dhul-Hal can be a hidden dhamir (dhamir mustatir) — e.g., the implied huwa inside a verb form.

  12. Tamiz and Mumayyaz parallel Hal and Dhul-Hal — Tamiz is nakira and mansub; Mumayyaz comes first; together they become Fa'il or Maf'ul.

  13. Number rules are critical for Tamiz — when the mumayyaz is a number, each range (1–2, 3–10, 11–12, 13–19, the tens, the compounds, 100, 1000) has its own rule for the following noun.

  14. Sentence analysis (i'rab) is the "lab" of Nahw — practicing it forces application of every grammar rule simultaneously and leads to deeper, lasting understanding.

  15. Homework for next class: Memorize Arabic numbers 1–100 fluently (say them 100 times). Mastery of the numbers is required before the number rules (which govern Tamiz) can be properly taught.

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text:
This session focused on the rules of Arabic numbers (Al-Adad wal-Ma'dud) — the grammatical relationship between a number and the noun being counted. The teacher began by reviewing homework (memorizing numbers 1–100 in Arabic) and testing several students. The bulk of the class covered the distinct grammatical rules for numbers 1–2, 3–10, 11–12, and 13–19, with Quranic examples throughout.

Key terms established at the outset: - Adad (العدد) — the number itself - Ma'dud (المعدود) — the noun/object being counted

docs.title: Rule Set 1: Numbers 1 and 2 (Wahid and Ithnan)
docs.text:
• Adad (1 or 2) acts as Sifah (صفة) — a descriptive adjective
  • Ma'dud acts as Mausuf (موصوف) — the described noun
  • Mausuf comes first, Sifah comes second (same as standard sifah-mausuf structure)
  • They agree in four things: number, gender, ma'rifah/nakirah, and i'rab
  • In most cases, the number is not needed — a singular noun implies "one" and a dual noun implies "two"; use the number only for emphasis
  • When kitabun → al-kitabu, both mausuf and sifah take al: al-kitabu al-wahidu
Case Muzakkar Mu'annas Raf' كِتَابٌ وَاحِدٌ سَيَّارَةٌ وَاحِدَةٌ Nasb كِتَاباً وَاحِداً سَيَّارَةً وَاحِدَةً Jarr كِتَابٍ وَاحِدٍ سَيَّارَةٍ وَاحِدَةٍ

For two (tasniyah), the dual form of the noun is sufficient on its own: - كِتَابَانِ (Raf') / كِتَابَيْنِ (Nasb/Jarr) — "two books" (without needing إثنان unless emphasizing)

docs.title: Rule Set 2: Numbers 3 to 10
docs.text:
• Adad acts as Mudaf (مضاف); Ma'dud acts as Mudaf Ilayh (مضاف إليه)
  • Adad comes first (reversed from 1–2)
  • Ma'dud is always plural and majroor (because mudaf ilayh is always majroor)
  • Adad does not accept alif-lam or tanwin (because it is mudaf)
  • Adad can take all three harakaat depending on its position in the sentence
  • CRITICAL RULE: Adad and Ma'dud do NOT agree in gender — they are opposite
  • If ma'dud is muzakkar → adad is mu'annas
  • If ma'dud is mu'annas → adad is muzakkar
  • Gender of ma'dud is determined by looking at its singular form
Number Muzakkar Ma'dud (books) Mu'annas Ma'dud (cars) 3 ثَلَاثَةُ كُتُبٍ ثَلَاثُ سَيَّارَاتٍ 4 أَرْبَعَةُ كُتُبٍ أَرْبَعُ سَيَّارَاتٍ 5 خَمْسَةُ أَقْلَامٍ خَمْسُ سَيَّارَاتٍ 6 سِتَّةُ أَقْلَامٍ — 7 سَبْعَةُ أَقْلَامٍ — 8 ثَمَانِيَةُ أَقْلَامٍ — 9 تِسْعَةُ أَقْلَامٍ — 10 عَشَرَةُ أَقْلَامٍ —

Quranic references: - أَرْبَعَةُ أَشْهُرٍ — "four months" (Ash-hur = plural of Shahr, muzakkar → adad is mu'annas: أَرْبَعَةُ) - سَبْعَةٌ إِذَا رَجَعْتُمْ — "seven [days of fasting] when you return" (Siyam implied, Surah Al-Baqarah) - عَلَيْهَا تِسْعَةَ عَشَرَ — "over it are nineteen [angels]" (Surah Al-Muddaththir)

docs.title: Rule Set 3: Number 11 (Ahada Asharah / Ihda Asharata)
docs.text:
Forms:

Gender Form Muzakkar أَحَدَ عَشَرَ Mu'annas إِحْدَى عَشَرَةَ

Rules: - Adad comes first, Ma'dud comes second - Ma'dud is singular and mansoob (Nasb case) - Adad and Ma'dud agree in gender - Both parts of the adad are mabni (indeclinable) — they do not change regardless of sentence position - Ma'dud from 11 onwards is always singular (unlike 3–10 where it is plural)

Examples: - أَحَدَ عَشَرَ كِتَاباً — eleven books - إِحْدَى عَشَرَةَ سَيَّارَةً — eleven cars

docs.title: Rule Set 4: Number 12 (Ithna Asharah / Ithnataa Asharata)
docs.text:
Origin: Originally إثْنَانِ عَشَرَ / إثْنَتَانِ عَشَرَةَ — the noon of tasniyah is dropped.

Forms:

Case Muzakkar Mu'annas Raf' اثْنَا عَشَرَ اثْنَتَا عَشَرَةَ Nasb/Jarr اثْنَيْ عَشَرَ اثْنَتَيْ عَشَرَةَ

Rules: - Unlike 11, the first part of 12 is NOT mabni — it changes in i'rab (follows dual declension: alif in raf', ya in nasb/jarr) - The sheen of عَشَرَ has fathah for muzakkar; sukoon for mu'annas (عَشْرَة) - Ma'dud is singular and mansoob - Adad and Ma'dud agree in gender

Examples: - اثْنَا عَشَرَ كِتَاباً (Raf') → اثْنَيْ عَشَرَ كِتَاباً (Nasb/Jarr) - اثْنَتَا عَشَرَةَ سَيَّارَةً (Raf') → اثْنَتَيْ عَشَرَةَ سَيَّارَةً (Nasb/Jarr)

docs.title: Rule Set 5: Numbers 13 to 19
docs.text:
Forms: Numbers are compound — two parts (e.g., ثَلَاثَةَ عَشَرَ = thalatha + ashara)

Rules: - Adad comes first, Ma'dud comes second - Ma'dud is singular and mansoob - First part of adad disagrees with ma'dud in gender - Second part of adad agrees with ma'dud in gender - Ma'dud muzakkar → first part mu'annas (has ta'), second part (عَشَرَ) muzakkar (sheen with fathah) - Ma'dud mu'annas → first part muzakkar (no ta'), second part (عَشْرَةَ) mu'annas (sheen with sukoon)

Number Muzakkar Ma'dud Mu'annas Ma'dud 13 ثَلَاثَةَ عَشَرَ كِتَاباً ثَلَاثَ عَشَرَةَ سَيَّارَةً 14 أَرْبَعَةَ عَشَرَ قَلَماً أَرْبَعَ عَشَرَةَ بِنْتاً 15 خَمْسَةَ عَشَرَ وَلَداً خَمْسَ عَشَرَةَ بِنْتاً 16 سِتَّةَ عَشَرَ وَلَداً — 17 سَبْعَةَ عَشَرَ غُرْفَةً — 18 ثَمَانِيَةَ عَشَرَ شُبَّاكاً — 19 تِسْعَةَ عَشَرَ مَلَكاً —

Quranic example: عَلَيْهَا تِسْعَةَ عَشَرَ (Surah Al-Muddaththir) — "over it are nineteen [angels]" — Malak (angel) is implied and muzakkar, so تِسْعَةَ (mu'annas/disagrees) and عَشَرَ (muzakkar/agrees).

docs.title: Summary Comparison Table
docs.text: Number Range Adad Position Ma'dud Form Gender Agreement Adad Structure 1–2 After ma'dud (sifah) Singular Agrees Simple 3–10 Before ma'dud (mudaf) Plural, majroor Opposite (disagrees) Simple 11 Before ma'dud Singular, mansoob Agrees Mabni (indeclinable) 12 Before ma'dud Singular, mansoob Agrees Partly declinable (dual) 13–19 Before ma'dud Singular, mansoob 1st part: disagrees; 2nd part: agrees Compound

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning عَدَد (Adad) The number مَعْدُود (Ma'dud) The noun/object being counted مَوْصُوف (Mausuf) The described noun (in sifah-mausuf structure) صِفَة (Sifah) The describing adjective مُضَاف (Mudaf) The first noun in an idafah (possessive) construction مُضَاف إِلَيْه (Mudaf Ilayh) The second noun in an idafah — always majroor مَبْنِي (Mabni) Indeclinable — does not change with i'rab مُذَكَّر (Muzakkar) Masculine مُؤَنَّث (Mu'annas) Feminine تَاء مَرْبُوطَة (Ta' Marbuta) The ة at the end of feminine words مَرْفُوع (Marfu') Nominative case (Raf') مَنْصُوب (Mansub) Accusative case (Nasb) مَجْرُور (Majrur) Genitive case (Jarr) نَكِرَة (Nakirah) Indefinite مَعْرِفَة (Ma'rifah) Definite تَثْنِيَة (Tasniyah) Dual form وَاحِد / وَاحِدَة One (m/f) اثْنَانِ / اثْنَتَانِ Two (m/f) — base dual forms ثَلَاثَة / ثَلَاث Three (used with muzakkar/mu'annas ma'dud respectively) مِئَة (Mi'ah) One hundred تِسْعُون (Tis'un) Ninety

docs.title: I'rab (Sentence Analysis) Examples
docs.text:
Example 1: كِتَابٌ وَاحِدٌ - كِتَابٌ — Mausuf, mubtada or subject, marfu', muzakkar, nakirah - وَاحِدٌ — Sifah, agrees with mausuf in raf', singular, muzakkar, nakirah

Example 2: ثَلَاثَةُ كُتُبٍ - ثَلَاثَةُ — Adad, mudaf, marfu' (no tanwin, no alif-lam), mu'annas (ta' marbuta present), opposite of ma'dud - كُتُبٍ — Ma'dud, mudaf ilayh, majroor (tanwin kasra), plural of كِتَاب, muzakkar

Example 3: أَحَدَ عَشَرَ كِتَاباً - أَحَدَ عَشَرَ — Adad, mabni (indeclinable), muzakkar, agrees with ma'dud - كِتَاباً — Ma'dud, mansoob (fathah tanwin), singular

Example 4: اثْنَيْ عَشَرَ كِتَاباً (in Nasb/Jarr) - اثْنَيْ — First part of adad, ya replaces alif because nasb/jarr (follows dual declension), muzakkar - عَشَرَ — Second part, mabni, sheen with fathah (muzakkar) - كِتَاباً — Ma'dud, mansoob, singular

Example 5: ثَلَاثَةَ عَشَرَ كِتَاباً - ثَلَاثَةَ — First part of adad, mu'annas (disagrees with muzakkar ma'dud), mansoob - عَشَرَ — Second part, muzakkar (agrees with muzakkar ma'dud), sheen with fathah - كِتَاباً — Ma'dud, singular, mansoob

Example 6: ثَلَاثَ عَشَرَةَ سَيَّارَةً - ثَلَاثَ — First part of adad, muzakkar/no ta' (disagrees with mu'annas ma'dud) - عَشَرَةَ — Second part, mu'annas (agrees with mu'annas ma'dud), sheen with sukoon - سَيَّارَةً — Ma'dud, singular, mansoob

docs.title: Practice Examples
docs.text:
Numbers 1–2: - قَلَمٌ وَاحِدٌ — one pen - سَيَّارَةٌ وَاحِدَةٌ — one car - قَلَمَانِ — two pens (dual form sufficient) - قَلَمَانِ اثْنَانِ — two pens (emphatic)

Numbers 3–10 (opposite gender rule): - ثَلَاثَةُ أَقْلَامٍ — three pens (Qalam = muzakkar → ثَلَاثَة mu'annas) - أَرْبَعَةُ أَقْلَامٍ — four pens - خَمْسَةُ أَقْلَامٍ — five pens - ثَلَاثُ سَيَّارَاتٍ — three cars (Sayyara = mu'annas → ثَلَاث muzakkar)

Numbers 11–12: - أَحَدَ عَشَرَ كِتَاباً — eleven books - إِحْدَى عَشَرَةَ سَيَّارَةً — eleven cars - اثْنَا عَشَرَ كِتَاباً — twelve books (Raf') - اثْنَتَيْ عَشَرَةَ سَيَّارَةً — twelve cars (Nasb/Jarr)

Numbers 13–19: - خَمْسَةَ عَشَرَ وَلَداً — fifteen boys (walad = muzakkar) - خَمْسَ عَشَرَةَ بِنْتاً — fifteen girls (bint = mu'annas) - ثَمَانِيَةَ عَشَرَ شُبَّاكاً — eighteen windows (shubak = muzakkar) - تِسْعَةَ عَشَرَ مَلَكاً — nineteen angels (malak = muzakkar; Quranic reference: عَلَيْهَا تِسْعَةَ عَشَرَ)

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. The two core terms: Adad (number) and Ma'dud (counted noun) — master these before anything else.

  2. Numbers 1–2 follow sifah-mausuf rules: Ma'dud comes first, adad comes second; they agree in all four things (number, gender, ma'rifah/nakirah, i'rab). These numbers are usually omitted unless emphasis is needed.

  3. Numbers 3–10 follow mudaf-mudaf ilayh rules: Adad comes first (as mudaf), ma'dud is plural and majroor (as mudaf ilayh). The most critical rule: adad and ma'dud are opposite in gender.

  4. From 11 onwards, ma'dud is always singular — a key contrast with English where counted nouns are always plural above one.

  5. From 11 onwards, ma'dud is mansoob (Nasb case).

  6. Numbers 11–12: adad and ma'dud agree in gender. Number 11 is fully mabni; number 12 partially declines (follows dual pattern — alif in Raf', ya in Nasb/Jarr).

  7. Numbers 13–19: Compound adad — the first part disagrees with ma'dud in gender; the second part agrees. The sheen of عَشَر has fathah with muzakkar and sukoon with mu'annas.

  8. Practical shortcut: To identify gender, always look at the singular form of the noun, not the plural.

  9. Next class will cover: Rules for 20, 30 ... 90; compound numbers 21–22, 31–32 ... 91–92; 23–29, 33–39 ... 93–99; and 100 and beyond (105, 110, etc.). Continue memorizing the number sequence 1–100 before next class (minimum 100 repetitions of 1–30 to achieve fluency).

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session completed the rules of Arabic numbers (أعداد), covering the decades (20–90), compound numbers (21–99), hundreds (100–900), thousands, and millions. By the end, students have a full framework for forming and parsing any number from 1 to 1,000,000+ in Arabic, including gender agreement rules, case endings, and the مضاف/مضاف إليه structure.

docs.title: Rule Summary: Numbers 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90 (Decades)
docs.text:
1. Gender: عدد is gender-neutral (same form for male and female ma'dud).
  2. Case ending: Follows the pattern of جمع مذكر سالم — رفع with واو, نصب/جر with ياء.
  3. عِشْرُونَ (رفع) → عِشْرِينَ (نصب/جر)
  4. ثَلَاثُونَ → ثَلَاثِينَ
  5. تِسْعُونَ → تِسْعِينَ
  6. Ma'dud: Always singular and منصوب.

Example: عِشْرُونَ طَالِبًا / عِشْرُونَ طَالِبَةً (رفع) | عِشْرِينَ طَالِبًا (نصب/جر)

docs.title: Rule Summary: 21 and 22 (Compound Numbers with واو)
docs.text:
• Structure: unit + وَ + decade (e.g., وَاحِدٌ وَعِشْرُونَ)
  • First part (unit): Must agree with the ma'dud in gender.
  • Male ma'dud → وَاحِدٌ وَعِشْرُونَ | Female ma'dud → إِحْدَى وَعِشْرُونَ
  • Male ma'dud → اثْنَانِ وَعِشْرُونَ | Female ma'dud → اثْنَتَانِ وَعِشْرُونَ
  • Second part (decade): Gender-neutral; changes case like جمع مذكر سالم.
  • Ma'dud: Always singular and منصوب.
Case Masculine (21 students) Feminine (21 students) رفع وَاحِدٌ وَعِشْرُونَ طَالِبًا إِحْدَى وَعِشْرُونَ طَالِبَةً نصب وَاحِدًا وَعِشْرِينَ طَالِبًا إِحْدَى وَعِشْرِينَ طَالِبَةً جر وَاحِدٍ وَعِشْرِينَ طَالِبًا إِحْدَى وَعِشْرِينَ طَالِبَةً

Note: إِحْدَى ends in ألف مقصورة, making it مبني (indeclinable). The case is shown only on the decade word.

Case Masculine (22 students) Feminine (22 students) رفع اثْنَانِ وَعِشْرُونَ طَالِبًا اثْنَتَانِ وَعِشْرُونَ طَالِبَةً نصب/جر اثْنَيْنِ وَعِشْرِينَ طَالِبًا اثْنَتَيْنِ وَعِشْرِينَ طَالِبَةً

This same rule extends to 31/32, 41/42, 51/52 … all the way to 91/92.

docs.title: Rule Summary: 23–29 (and 33–39, 43–49 … 93–99)
docs.text:
• Structure: unit + وَ + decade
  • First part (unit, 3–9): Must disagree with the ma'dud in gender (same rule as 3–10 standalone).
  • Second part (decade): Gender-neutral.
  • Ma'dud: Always singular and منصوب (this rule holds through 99).
  • Both parts can accept all three case vowels (but the decade follows جمع مذكر سالم pattern).
Example Arabic Note 23 male students (رفع) ثَلَاثَةٌ وَعِشْرُونَ طَالِبًا ثَلَاثَة disagrees with طَالِب (masc) 24 female students (رفع) أَرْبَعٌ وَعِشْرُونَ طَالِبَةً أَرْبَع disagrees with طَالِبَة (fem) 24 female students (نصب) أَرْبَعًا وَعِشْرِينَ طَالِبَةً First digit declines 24 female students (جر) أَرْبَعٍ وَعِشْرِينَ طَالِبَةً Same change on decade

docs.title: Rule Summary: مِئَة (100)
docs.text:
• Spelling note: Written مِئَة — the alif after mim is silent (do not pronounce مَاَة; correct pronunciation is مِئَة).
  • Structure: مِئَة is مضاف; ma'dud is مضاف إليه.
  • Gender: Gender-neutral (used for both male and female ma'dud).
  • Ma'dud: Singular and مجرور (because مضاف إليه).
  • مِئَة accepts all three case vowels: مِئَةُ / مِئَةَ / مِئَةِ
Example Arabic 100 male students (رفع) مِئَةُ طَالِبٍ 100 female students (رفع) مِئَةُ طَالِبَةٍ 100 male students (نصب) مِئَةَ طَالِبٍ 100 male students (جر) مِئَةِ طَالِبٍ

docs.title: Rule Summary: مِئَتَان (200)
docs.text:
• تثنية of مِئَة → مِئَتَانِ (رفع) / مِئَتَيْنِ (نصب/جر)
  • When مضاف, the noon drops: مِئَتَانِ → مِئَتَا / مِئَتَيْنِ → مِئَتَيْ
Case Example رفع مِئَتَا طَالِبٍ / طَالِبَةٍ نصب/جر مِئَتَيْ طَالِبٍ / طَالِبَةٍ

docs.title: Rule Summary: 300–900
docs.text:
• Structure: unit (3–9) + مِئَة
  • The unit acts as مضاف; مِئَة acts as مضاف إليه.
  • Gender of unit: مِئَة is feminine (has تاء مربوطة), so the unit must be opposite in gender → masculine form (i.e., no تاء on the unit).
  • مِئَة stays singular (this is the key exception — unlike other ma'dud from 3–10 which require plural, مِئَة stays singular).
Number Arabic 300 ثَلَاثُ مِئَةٍ 500 خَمْسُ مِئَةٍ
  • Adding a ma'dud (e.g., 300 students): apply rule of مِئَة to طَالِب, and unit applies to مِئَة.
Example Arabic 300 male students ثَلَاثُ مِئَةِ طَالِبٍ 500 female students خَمْسُ مِئَةِ طَالِبَةٍ 600 male students سِتُّ مِئَةِ طَالِبٍ

docs.title: Rule Summary: أَلْف (1,000)
docs.text:
• Gender: Gender-neutral.
  • Structure: أَلْف is مضاف; ma'dud is مضاف إليه → singular and مجرور.
  • أَلْف accepts all three case vowels.
  • تثنية: أَلْفَانِ (رفع) → أَلْفَا when مضاف (noon drops) | أَلْفَيْنِ (نصب/جر) → أَلْفَيْ when مضاف
Case 1,000 students 2,000 students رفع أَلْفُ طَالِبٍ / طَالِبَةٍ أَلْفَا طَالِبٍ / طَالِبَةٍ نصب/جر أَلْفَ طَالِبٍ أَلْفَيْ طَالِبٍ

docs.title: Rule Summary: 3,000–10,000
docs.text:
• Plural of أَلْف → آلَاف (masculine)
  • Apply rules of 3–10: unit is مضاف, آلَاف is مضاف إليه → plural and مجرور; unit disagrees in gender with آلَاف (آلَاف is masc, so unit takes feminine form).
  • Adding a ma'dud: آلَاف's rule applies to it, and the last number's rule applies to the ma'dud.
Example Arabic 10,000 (رفع) عَشَرَةُ آلَافٍ 10,000 (نصب) عَشَرَةَ آلَافٍ 10,000 (جر) عَشَرَةِ آلَافٍ 10,000 students عَشَرَةُ آلَافِ طَالِبٍ 9,000 students تِسْعَةُ آلَافِ طَالِبٍ

docs.title: Rule Summary: مِلْيُون (Million)
docs.text:
• Arabized (no native Arabic origin) — borrowed and adapted.
  • Same rule as أَلْف: مضاف, ma'dud is مضاف إليه → singular and مجرور, gender-neutral.
  • تثنية: مِلْيُونَانِ → مِلْيُونَا when مضاف (noon drops) | مِلْيُونَيْنِ → مِلْيُونَيْ
  • Plural: مَلَايِين
Case 1M students 2M students رفع مِلْيُونُ طَالِبٍ مِلْيُونَا طَالِبٍ نصب/جر مِلْيُونَ طَالِبٍ مِلْيُونَيْ طَالِبٍ

docs.title: Rule for Compound Large Numbers (e.g., 2,523 students)
docs.text:
• Read right to left in blocks: thousands → hundreds → tens & units.
  • Connect blocks with وَ.
  • The last number's rule governs the ma'dud.

Example: 1,003 male students → أَلْفٌ وَثَلَاثَةُ طُلَّابٍ (ثَلَاثَة disagrees with طَالِب; ma'dud becomes plural جمع تكسير طُلَّاب)

Example: 1,003 female students → أَلْفٌ وَثَلَاثُ طَالِبَاتٍ

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning عَدَد Number مَعْدُود The counted noun (ma'dud) مُضَاف The first noun in an إضافة construction مُضَاف إِلَيْه The second noun in an إضافة construction (always مجرور) جَمْع مُذَكَّر سَالِم Sound masculine plural (case marked by و/ي) أَلْف One thousand آلَاف Thousands (plural of أَلْف) مِئَة One hundred مِئَتَانِ Two hundred مِلْيُون One million مَلَايِين Millions (plural of مِلْيُون) إِحْدَى Feminine form of "one" (indeclinable — ألف مقصورة) اثْنَانِ / اثْنَتَانِ Two (masc/fem), رفع form اثْنَيْنِ / اثْنَتَيْنِ Two (masc/fem), نصب/جر form تَثْنِيَة Dual form of a noun أَلِف مَقْصُورَة Short alif (ى) that makes a word مبني (indeclinable) مَبْنِي Indeclinable — case does not change

docs.title: I'rab (Sentence Analysis) Examples
docs.text:
Example 1: عِشْرُونَ طَالِبًا - عِشْرُونَ — عدد، مرفوع، علامة رفعه الواو (جمع مذكر سالم) - طَالِبًا — مَعْدُود، مفرد، منصوب، تنوين فتح

Example 2: وَاحِدٌ وَعِشْرُونَ طَالِبًا - وَاحِدٌ — الجزء الأول من العدد، مرفوع، يتفق مع المعدود في الجنس - وَ — حرف عطف - عِشْرُونَ — الجزء الثاني، مرفوع بالواو - طَالِبًا — مَعْدُود، مفرد، منصوب

Example 3: مِئَةُ طَالِبٍ - مِئَةُ — عدد، مضاف، مرفوع - طَالِبٍ — مَعْدُود، مُضَاف إليه، مجرور، مفرد

Example 4: ثَلَاثُ مِئَةِ طَالِبٍ - ثَلَاثُ — عدد، مضاف، مرفوع (بدون تاء لأن مِئَة مؤنث، والعدد يخالف المعدود) - مِئَةِ — مضاف إليه (من ثَلَاث)، ومضاف (إلى طَالِب)، مجرور، مفرد - طَالِبٍ — مضاف إليه، مجرور، مفرد

Example 5: أَلْفُ طَالِبٍ - أَلْفُ — عدد، مضاف، مرفوع - طَالِبٍ — مضاف إليه، مجرور، مفرد

Example 6: عَشَرَةُ آلَافِ طَالِبٍ - عَشَرَةُ — عدد، مضاف، مرفوع (مؤنث لأن آلَاف مذكر — يخالف) - آلَافِ — مضاف إليه (من عَشَرَة)، مجرور، جمع تكسير - طَالِبٍ — مضاف إليه، مجرور، مفرد

docs.title: Practice Examples
docs.text:
Work through these before the next class (Tuesday):

Number Masculine Students (رفع) Feminine Students (رفع) 30 ثَلَاثُونَ طَالِبًا ثَلَاثُونَ طَالِبَةً 31 وَاحِدٌ وَثَلَاثُونَ طَالِبًا إِحْدَى وَثَلَاثُونَ طَالِبَةً 32 اثْنَانِ وَثَلَاثُونَ طَالِبًا اثْنَتَانِ وَثَلَاثُونَ طَالِبَةً 35 خَمْسَةٌ وَثَلَاثُونَ طَالِبًا خَمْسٌ وَثَلَاثُونَ طَالِبَةً 100 مِئَةُ طَالِبٍ مِئَةُ طَالِبَةٍ 200 مِئَتَا طَالِبٍ مِئَتَا طَالِبَةٍ 700 سَبْعُ مِئَةِ طَالِبٍ سَبْعُ مِئَةِ طَالِبَةٍ 1,000 أَلْفُ طَالِبٍ أَلْفُ طَالِبَةٍ 2,000 أَلْفَا طَالِبٍ أَلْفَا طَالِبَةٍ 5,000 خَمْسَةُ آلَافِ طَالِبٍ خَمْسَةُ آلَافِ طَالِبَةٍ 1,000,000 مِلْيُونُ طَالِبٍ مِلْيُونُ طَالِبَةٍ

Homework (due Tuesday): Textbook p. 91 — two exercises: 1. Write the following in Arabic (numbers like: 2 schools, 12 women, 17 doors, etc.) 2. Translate sentences and fill in the i'rab (8 sentences)

Self-practice tip from the teacher: Do not try to memorize rules in isolation — throw random numbers at yourself and practice forming them with different ma'dud words.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Ma'dud from 11 through 99 is always singular and منصوب — this never changes across that range.
  2. Decades (20–90) are gender-neutral and decline like جمع مذكر سالم (واو in رفع, ياء in نصب/جر).
  3. Units in 21–22 agree in gender with the ma'dud; units in 23–99 disagree in gender with the ma'dud.
  4. مِئَة, أَلْف, مِلْيُون all follow the مضاف/مضاف إليه rule — ma'dud is singular and مجرور.
  5. مِئَة is the key exception for 300–900: despite being a ma'dud of the unit (3–9), it stays singular (normally 3–9 requires plural ma'dud).
  6. Noon drops in تثنية when the word becomes مضاف: مِئَتَانِ → مِئَتَا، أَلْفَانِ → أَلْفَا، مِلْيُونَانِ → مِلْيُونَا.
  7. إِحْدَى is indeclinable (مبني) due to ألف مقصورة — its case is shown only by the decade word.
  8. In large compound numbers, the rule governing the ma'dud comes from the last number in the sequence.
  9. مِلْيُون is an Arabized loanword with no classical Arabic root; its plural is مَلَايِين.

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session had two main parts. The first part was a review exercise session on Arabic numbers (العدد), where students translated English number phrases into Arabic and vice versa. The second and primary part introduced a major new concept: Istithna (الاستثناء) — the grammar of exception/exclusion. The teacher introduced all key terminologies, types of Istithna, and the rules governing the I'rab of المستثنى through a structured table (Table 3.8). Students are expected to read through these rules two to three times before the next class, where exercises will be done.

docs.title: Part 1 — Numbers Review (العدد) Key Reminders
docs.text:
• When a number has multiple digits, connect them with واو (and).
  • The معدود (ma'dud) — the counted noun — follows the rule of the last number.
  • In most cases, the معدود is singular and in the نصب case.

docs.title: Core Terminologies
docs.text:
Term Definition الاستثناء (Istithna) The grammatical concept of excluding/exempting something from a general statement المستثنى منه (Mustathna Minh) The group/set from which the exclusion is made; appears before the particle of exception المستثنى (Mustathna) The item/person that is excluded; appears after the particle of exception حرف الاستثناء (Harful Istithna) The particle of exception (e.g., إلا)

Order in a sentence: المستثنى منه → حرف الاستثناء → المستثنى

docs.title: Particles of Exception (حروف الاستثناء)
docs.text: Particle Notes إلا Most common غير Always used as مضاف سوى Always used as مضاف حاشا Can be harfujar or harful istithna (dual nature) خلا Dual nature (harfujar or harful istithna) عدا Dual nature (harfujar or harful istithna) ما خلا ما added makes it exclusively harful istithna ما عدا ما added makes it exclusively harful istithna

docs.title: Classification 1 — Muttasil vs. Munqati' (Connected vs. Disconnected)
docs.text:
المستثنى المتصل (Mustathna Muttasil — Connected) - The مستثنى and the مستثنى منه belong to the same group/species (even before the exclusion was made). - Example: جَاءَ الْقَوْمُ إِلَّا زَيْدًا — "The people came except Zayd." (Zayd is part of the people.)

المستثنى المنقطع (Mustathna Munqati' — Disconnected) - The مستثنى and the مستثنى منه do not belong to the same group even before the exclusion. - Example: سَجَدَ الْمَلَائِكَةُ كُلُّهُمْ أَجْمَعُونَ إِلَّا إِبْلِيسَ — "The angels all prostrated except Iblis." (Iblis was never from the angels; he is from the Jinn.)
