docs.title: Classification 2 — Kalam Mujab vs. Kalam Ghayru Mujab (Positive vs. Negative Statement)
docs.text:
الكلام الموجب / المثبت (Kalam Mujab — Positive Statement) - A sentence that contains no نفي (negation), no نهي (prohibition), and no استفهام (question). - Example: جَاءَ الْقَوْمُ إِلَّا زَيْدًا — "The people came except Zayd." (No negation, no prohibition, no question.)

الكلام غير الموجب / المنفي (Kalam Ghayru Mujab — Negative/Non-positive Statement) - A sentence that contains نفي (e.g., ما, لا), نهي (prohibition), or استفهام (question). - Example: مَا جَاءَ الْقَوْمُ إِلَّا زَيْدًا — "The people did not come except Zayd." (Contains ما النافية.)

docs.title: Classification 3 — Mufarragh vs. Ghayru Mufarragh (Void vs. Non-void)
docs.text:
المستثنى المفرَّغ (Mustathna Mufarragh — Void/Empty) - The مستثنى منه is NOT mentioned in the sentence in words; it is only implied/understood. - مُفَرَّغ is derived from فَرَاغ (emptiness/void) — the مستثنى منه slot is empty. - Example: مَا جَاءَ إِلَّا زَيْدٌ — "No one came except Zayd." (The word "no one"/أحد is not stated.)

المستثنى غير المفرَّغ (Mustathna Ghayru Mufarragh — Non-void) - The مستثنى منه IS mentioned explicitly in words. - Example: مَا جَاءَ الْقَوْمُ إِلَّا زَيْدًا — "The people did not come except Zayd." (القوم is explicitly mentioned.)

docs.title: Table 3.8 — The I'rab of المستثنى (Seven Scenarios)
docs.text: # Harful Istithna Type Kalam Mufarragh? I'rab of Mustathna 1 إلا مُنقطع (Munqati') Any Any منصوب (always nasab) 2 إلا مُتَّصِل (Muttasil) مُوجَب (Mujab) غير مُفَرَّغ منصوب (always nasab) 3 إلا مُتَّصِل غير موجب (Ghayru Mujab) غير مُفَرَّغ منصوب (preferred) OR same i'rab as مستثنى منه 4 إلا مُتَّصِل غير موجب مُفَرَّغ According to the عامل (as if إلا doesn't exist) 5 ما خلا / ما عدا Any Any Any منصوب (always nasab) 6 خلا / عدا / حاشا Any Any Any منصوب OR مجرور (dual option due to dual nature) 7 غير / سوى Any Any Any مجرور (always jar — as مضاف إليه)

docs.title: Detailed Notes on Each Scenario
docs.text:
Scenario 1 — Munqati', with إلا:

مستثنى is always منصوب, no further analysis needed. Example: سَجَدَ الْمَلَائِكَةُ إِلَّا إِبْلِيسَ — إبليس is منصوب (note: no tanwin because it is غير منصرف).

Scenario 2 — Muttasil, Kalam Mujab, with إلا:

مستثنى is منصوب. Example: جَاءَ الْقَوْمُ إِلَّا زَيْدًا — زيدًا is منصوب.

Scenario 3 — Muttasil, Kalam Ghayru Mujab, Ghayru Mufarragh, with إلا:

Option 1 (preferred): مستثنى is منصوب — مَا جَاءَ أَحَدٌ إِلَّا زَيْدًا Option 2: مستثنى follows the same i'rab as مستثنى منه — مَا جَاءَ أَحَدٌ إِلَّا زَيْدٌ (rafa, because أحد is فاعل, i.e., in rafa)

Scenario 4 — Muttasil, Kalam Ghayru Mujab, Mufarragh (مستثنى منه not mentioned), with إلا:

Imagine إلا does not exist. What would be the i'rab of المستثنى if it were in the position of مستثنى منه? Give it that same i'rab.

Three sub-examples: - مَا جَاءَ إِلَّا زَيْدٌ (no one came except Zayd) → if أحد were there: مَا جَاءَ أَحَدٌ → أحد is فاعل → رفع → زيدٌ is مرفوع - مَا رَأَيْتُ إِلَّا زَيْدًا (I didn't see anyone except Zayd) → if أحد were there: مَا رَأَيْتُ أَحَدًا → أحد is مفعول → نصب → زيدًا is منصوب - مَا مَرَرْتُ إِلَّا بِزَيْدٍ (I didn't pass by anyone except Zayd) → if أحد were there: مَا مَرَرْتُ بِأَحَدٍ → أحد is مجرور by بِ → زيدٍ is مجرور, and even the حرف جر بِ remains.

Scenario 5 — ما خلا / ما عدا:

مستثنى is always منصوب, regardless of any other classification. Example: جَاءَ الْقَوْمُ مَا خَلَا زَيْدًا

Scenario 6 — خلا / عدا / حاشا (without ما):

These three have dual nature: they can function as حرف الاستثناء OR as حرف الجر. - If treated as حرف الاستثناء → مستثنى is منصوب: جَاءَ الْقَوْمُ خَلَا زَيْدًا - If treated as حرف الجر → مستثنى is مجرور: جَاءَ الْقَوْمُ خَلَا زَيْدٍ

Scenario 7 — غير / سوى:

These are always used as مضاف. The مستثنى is مضاف إليه → always مجرور. Examples: - جَاءَ الْقَوْمُ غَيْرَ زَيْدٍ - جَاءَ الْقَوْمُ سِوَى زَيْدٍ

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning الاستثناء Exception, exclusion المستثنى The excluded item (comes after حرف الاستثناء) المستثنى منه The group from which exclusion is made (comes before حرف الاستثناء) حرف الاستثناء Particle of exception إلا Except (most common particle of exception) غير Except (always مضاف) سوى Except (always مضاف) حاشا / خلا / عدا Except (dual nature: حرف استثناء or حرف جر) ما خلا / ما عدا Except (ما makes them exclusively harful istithna) مُتَّصِل Connected — مستثنى and مستثنى منه are from the same group مُنْقَطِع Disconnected — مستثنى and مستثنى منه are from different groups الكلام الموجب / المثبت Positive statement (no نفي, نهي, or استفهام) الكلام غير الموجب / المنفي Non-positive/negative statement (has نفي, نهي, or استفهام) المفرَّغ Void — مستثنى منه is not mentioned in words غير المفرَّغ Non-void — مستثنى منه is mentioned in words فراغ Emptiness, void (root of مُفَرَّغ) النفي Negation النهي Prohibition الاستفهام Question منصوب In the nasab (accusative) case مرفوع In the rafa (nominative) case مجرور In the jar (genitive) case مضاف إليه The second term in a genitive construct (always مجرور) بيضة / بيضات Egg / eggs وردة Rose نخلة Palm tree مسافة Distance, area قنطار Quintal (unit of measurement) العامل The governing element that determines i'rab

docs.title: I'rab (Sentence Analysis) Examples
docs.text:
Example 1: Simple Muttasil, Kalam Mujab

جَاءَ الْقَوْمُ إِلَّا زَيْدًا

Word I'rab جَاءَ فعل ماضٍ الْقَوْمُ فاعل، مرفوع — also المستثنى منه إِلَّا حرف الاستثناء زَيْدًا مستثنى، منصوب (Scenario 2)

Example 2: Kalam Ghayru Mujab, Ghayru Mufarragh, Muttasil — Option 2 (follows مستثنى منه)

مَا جَاءَ أَحَدٌ إِلَّا زَيْدٌ

Word I'rab مَا حرف نفي جَاءَ فعل ماضٍ أَحَدٌ فاعل، مرفوع — also المستثنى منه إِلَّا حرف الاستثناء زَيْدٌ مستثنى، مرفوع — following the i'rab of أحد (Scenario 3, option 2)

Example 3: Kalam Ghayru Mujab, Mufarragh — مستثنى as مفعول به

مَا رَأَيْتُ إِلَّا زَيْدًا

Word I'rab مَا حرف نفي رَأَيْتُ فعل وفاعل إِلَّا حرف الاستثناء زَيْدًا مستثنى، منصوب — because if أحد were present (مَا رَأَيْتُ أَحَدًا), it would be مفعول به منصوب (Scenario 4)

Example 4: Kalam Ghayru Mujab, Mufarragh — مستثنى مجرور with حرف جر

مَا مَرَرْتُ إِلَّا بِزَيْدٍ

Word I'rab مَا حرف نفي مَرَرْتُ فعل وفاعل إِلَّا حرف الاستثناء بِ حرف جر (carried over from مستثنى منه position) زَيْدٍ مستثنى، مجرور بِ — because if أحد were present: مَا مَرَرْتُ بِأَحَدٍ (Scenario 4)

Example 5: Munqati', with إلا (Scenario 1)

سَجَدَ الْمَلَائِكَةُ إِلَّا إِبْلِيسَ

Word I'rab سَجَدَ فعل ماضٍ الْمَلَائِكَةُ فاعل، مرفوع إِلَّا حرف الاستثناء إِبْلِيسَ مستثنى منقطع، منصوب (no tanwin: غير منصرف)

Example 6: غير / سوى — Scenario 7

جَاءَ الْقَوْمُ غَيْرَ زَيْدٍ

Word I'rab جَاءَ فعل ماضٍ الْقَوْمُ فاعل، مرفوع غَيْرَ مستثنى، منصوب، مضاف زَيْدٍ مضاف إليه، مجرور

docs.title: Numbers Exercise (Arabic to English — Part 2 of the session)
docs.text: Arabic English فِي الْبُسْتَانِ تِسْعٌ وَتِسْعُونَ نَخْلَةً In the garden there are 99 palm trees (نخلة is منصوب as معدود) مِسَاحَةُ الدَّارِ أَلْفَا ذِرَاعٍ The area of the house is 2,000 cubits فِي الْغُرْفَةِ ثَلَاثُ نَوَافِذَ The room has 3 windows بَاعَ التَّاجِرُ أَرْبَعَةَ عَشَرَ قِنْطَارًا مِنَ الْقُطْنِ The merchant sold 14 quintals of cotton فِي الْكِتَابِ أَرْبَعٌ وَعِشْرُونَ صَفْحَةً In the book there are 24 pages فِي الْعَامِ اثْنَا عَشَرَ شَهْرًا There are 12 months in a year عُمْرُ أَخِيكَ الآنَ خَمْسٌ وَثَلَاثُونَ سَنَةً Your brother is now 35 years old بَاضَتِ الدَّجَاجَةُ ثَلَاثَ بَيْضَاتٍ The hen laid 3 eggs

docs.title: Istithna Identification Practice (to be done next class)
docs.text: Students are expected to: 1. Read the Arabic sentence with Istithna. 2. Translate it into English. 3. Identify which scenario number from Table 3.8 applies (1 through 7). 4. State the i'rab of the مستثنى and justify it.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Istithna has two core actors: مستثنى منه (the group) comes first; مستثنى (the excluded item) comes after حرف الاستثناء.

  2. Three classification axes for Istithna:

  3. Muttasil vs. Munqati' (same group or different group)
  4. Kalam Mujab vs. Kalam Ghayru Mujab (positive vs. negative/prohibitive/interrogative)

  5. Mufarragh vs. Ghayru Mufarragh (مستثنى منه stated or implied)

  6. In most scenarios, مستثنى is منصوب — this is the default safe rule. Scenarios 1, 2, 5 are always منصوب with no exceptions.

  7. Scenario 4 (Mufarragh) is the trickiest: imagine إلا doesn't exist and determine what i'rab the مستثنى would have in the position of مستثنى منه — that is its i'rab. Even حروف الجر carry over (as seen in بِزَيْدٍ).

  8. غير and سوى always produce مجرور because they are always مضاف and the مستثنى becomes مضاف إليه.

  9. خلا, عدا, حاشا have dual nature: adding ما before them (ما خلا, ما عدا) locks them as حرف الاستثناء and the مستثنى must be منصوب.

  10. Munqati' is simple: whenever مستثنى and مستثنى منه are from different species/groups, the مستثنى is always منصوب, regardless of all other factors.

  11. Academic calendar note: Nahw 102 classes run through June 27, followed by exam preparation break June 28 – July 5, with the new semester beginning July 13.

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text:
This session covered two main topics:

  1. Completion of Mustasna (Exception) chapter — specifically the I'rab of غَيْر (ghayr) and سِوَى (siwā), followed by a full class exercise on all seven Mustasna scenarios.
  2. Introduction to Majrūrāt (Section 3.9–3.10) — a brief review of the two foundational reasons for jarr, then launching into the five Tawābi' concepts, beginning with Na't Haqīqī (direct description) and its grammar rules, including when a sifah is a complete sentence.

Na't Sababi (indirect description) was introduced conceptually but deferred to the next class.

docs.title: Rule 1: I'rāb of Ghayр (غَيْر)
docs.text:
• غَيْر and سِوَى, when used as particles of exception, follow Rule 7 of the Mustasna table.
  • For all types of Mustasna (muttasil, munqati', mufarragh, ghayр mufarragh; mujab or ghayр mujab), the noun following غَيْر/سِوَى is always majrūr (because it is mudhāf ilayh of غَيْر).
  • سِوَى is mabnī (indeclinable) — it never changes; always سِوَى.
  • غَيْر is mu'rab (declinable) — it can take any of the three harakāt (ghayra / ghayru / ghayri), so its I'rāb must be determined.

Two-step method to determine the I'rāb of غَيْر:

Step 1: Replace غَيْر with إِلَّا. Identify which of the four Mustasna scenarios applies, and determine what I'rāb the Mustasna (the noun after إِلَّا) would receive.

Step 2: Remove إِلَّا and restore غَيْر. Give غَيْر the I'rāb that the Mustasna would have had. The original noun after غَيْر becomes majrūr as mudhāf ilayh.

Applied examples:

Original Sentence Replace غَيْر with إِلَّا Scenario I'rāb of Mustasna I'rāb of غَيْر Final Form جَاءَ الْقَوْمُ غَيْرَ زَيْدٍ جَاءَ الْقَوْمُ إِلَّا زَيْدًا #1 (mujab, muttasil) Mansūb Mansūb → غَيْرَ جَاءَ الْقَوْمُ غَيْرَ زَيْدٍ مَا جَاءَ غَيْرُ زَيْدٍ مَا جَاءَ إِلَّا زَيْدٌ #4 (ghayр mujab, mufarragh) Marfū' (per 'āmil) Marfū' → غَيْرُ مَا جَاءَ غَيْرُ زَيْدٍ

docs.title: Rule 2: Two Foundational Reasons for Jarr (Section 3.9)
docs.text:
Words are always majrūr for one of two reasons: 1. Preceded by a harf al-jarr — the noun following any harf al-jarr is majrūr (e.g., فِي الْكِتَابِ). 2. Mudhāf ilayh — the second noun in an idāfa construction is always majrūr (e.g., كِتَابُ زَيْدٍ).

These are foundational and not re-explained in detail (covered extensively in earlier lessons).

docs.title: Rule 3: Tawābi' — Introduction (Section 3.10)
docs.text:
Tābi' (تَابِع): the word that follows another word and matches it in I'rāb. Matbū' (مَتْبُوع): the word that is followed.

The word that comes second follows the first in one or more of four categories: 1. I'rāb (case ending) 2. Being Ma'rifa or Nakira 3. Gender (masculine/feminine) 4. Number (singular/dual/plural)

Five concepts fall under Tawābi': 1. Na't (نَعْت) — description ← covered today 2. 'Atf (عَطْف) 3. Tawkīd (تَوْكِيد) 4. Badal (بَدَل) 5. (fifth unnamed in this session)

docs.title: Rule 4: Terminology — Mawsūf/Sifah = Man'ūt/Na't
docs.text:
Term Pair Role مَوْصُوف (Mawsūf) = مَنْعُوت (Man'ūt) The noun being described → is the matbū' صِفَة (Sifah) = نَعْت (Na't) The adjective/descriptor → is the tābi'

In Na't Haqīqī, the sifah follows the mawsūf in all four of the above categories.

docs.title: Rule 5: Na't Haqīqī (نَعْت حَقِيقِي) — Direct Description
docs.text:
• The sifah comes after the mawsūf.
  • They agree in four things: I'rāb, being ma'rifa/nakira, gender, and number.
  • Example: اَلْبَيْتُ الْكَبِيرُ — al-kabīru agrees with al-bayt in all four.

When the Sifah is a complete sentence:

Two conditions apply: 1. The mawsūf must be nakira (no alif-lam). 2. The sentence functioning as sifah must contain a dhamīr (pronoun) that refers back to the nakira mawsūf.

Example: جَاءَنِي وَلَدٌ يَرْكَبُ الدَّرَّاجَةَ - وَلَدٌ = mawsūf (nakira, fā'il of جَاءَ) - يَرْكَبُ الدَّرَّاجَةَ = sifah (complete verbal sentence; hidden dhamīr in يَرْكَبُ refers to وَلَد) - Translation: A boy who was riding the bicycle came to me.

Contrast — when the noun is ma'rifa:

جَاءَنِي الْوَلَدُ يَرْكَبُ الدَّرَّاجَةَ - الْوَلَدُ is ma'rifa → يَرْكَبُ الدَّرَّاجَةَ cannot be sifah - يَرْكَبُ الدَّرَّاجَةَ becomes Hāl; الْوَلَدُ becomes Dhul-hāl - Translation: The boy came to me while riding the bicycle.

When ma'rifa is followed by a sentence — two possibilities: | Preceding noun | Sentence becomes | Example | |---|---|---| | Ma'rifa (with alif-lam) | Khabar | اَلْوَلَدُ يَرْكَبُ الدَّرَّاجَةَ (The boy is riding...) | | Ma'rifa (with alif-lam) | Hāl | جَاءَ الْوَلَدُ يَرْكَبُ الدَّرَّاجَةَ (The boy came while riding...) | | Nakira | Sifah | جَاءَنِي وَلَدٌ يَرْكَبُ الدَّرَّاجَةَ (A boy who was riding came...) |

docs.title: Rule 6: Na't with a Jumlah Ismiyyah as Sifah
docs.text: جَاءَ رَجُلٌ أَبُوهُ عَالِمٌ - رَجُلٌ = mawsūf (nakira) — fā'il of جَاءَ together with its sifah - أَبُوهُ عَالِمٌ = sifah (complete nominal sentence) - أَبُوهُ: أَبُو = mudhāf, هُ = mudhāf ilayh → together = mubtada' - عَالِمٌ = khabar - هُ in أَبُوهُ is the dhamīr referring back to رَجُل (satisfying the dhamīr rule) - Translation: A man whose father is a scholar came.

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning مُسْتَثْنَى (Mustathna) The excluded noun in an exception construction مُسْتَثْنَى مِنْهُ (Mustasna minh) The group from which exclusion is made غَيْر (Ghayr) Except / other than (declinable particle of exception) سِوَى (Siwā) Except / other than (indeclinable particle of exception) مَبْنِي (Mabnī) Indeclinable — form never changes مُعْرَب (Mu'rab) Declinable — form changes with I'rāb مُضَاف (Mudhāf) First noun in an idāfa (construct) مُضَاف إِلَيْهِ (Mudhāf ilayhi) Second noun in an idāfa — always majrūr مَجْرُور (Majrūr) Noun in the jarr/genitive case مَجْرُورَات (Majrūrāt) Words that are always in the jarr case (Section 3.9) تَوَابِع (Tawābi') Followers — words that follow another in I'rāb (umbrella term) تَابِع (Tābi') The follower (the word that comes second) مَتْبُوع (Matbū') The followed word (the word that comes first) نَعْت (Na't) Description / adjective (synonym of Sifah) مَنْعُوت (Man'ūt) The described noun (synonym of Mawsūf) نَعْت حَقِيقِي (Na't Haqīqī) Direct description — adjective directly describes the noun نَعْت سَبَبِي (Na't Sababi) Indirect description — to be covered next class مَوْصُوف (Mawsūf) The described noun (matbū' in na't) صِفَة (Sifah) The adjective/descriptor (tābi' in na't) ضَمِير (Dhamīr) Pronoun ضَمِير مُسْتَتِر (Dhamīr Mustatir) Hidden/implied pronoun حَال (Hāl) Circumstantial descriptor — describes the state ذُو الْحَال (Dhul-Hāl) The noun whose state is being described جُمْلَة فِعْلِيَّة (Jumlah Fi'liyya) Verbal sentence جُمْلَة اسْمِيَّة (Jumlah Ismiyya) Nominal sentence مُفَرَّغ (Mufarragh) Emptied/incomplete — negative sentence with no Mustasna minh stated مُجَاب (Mujab) Affirmative/positive sentence غَيْر مُجَاب (Ghayр Mujab) Negative sentence عَامِل (Āmil) The governing word that determines I'rāb

docs.title: Example 1: جَاءَ الْقَوْمُ غَيْرَ زَيْدٍ
docs.text:
(The people came except Zayd)

Word I'rāb Reason جَاءَ Fi'l mādhī Verb الْقَوْمُ Fā'il — marfū' Subject of جَاءَ غَيْرَ Mustathna — mansūb Replaces إِلَّا in Scenario #1 (mujab, muttasil); gets the nasb the mustathna would have received زَيْدٍ Mudhāf ilayhi — majrūr Is the mudhāf ilayhi of غَيْر

docs.title: Example 2: مَا جَاءَ غَيْرُ زَيْدٍ
docs.text:
(No one came except Zayd)

Word I'rāb Reason مَا Harf nafy Negation particle جَاءَ Fi'l mādhī Verb غَيْرُ Fā'il — marfū' Scenario #4 (ghayр mujab, mufarragh); the 'āmil جَاءَ requires rafa' for its fā'il زَيْدٍ Mudhāf ilayhi — majrūr Mudhāf ilayhi of غَيْر

docs.title: Example 3: جَاءَنِي وَلَدٌ يَرْكَبُ الدَّرَّاجَةَ
docs.text:
(A boy who was riding the bicycle came to me)

Word I'rāb Reason جَاءَ Fi'l mādhī Verb نِ (nī) Maf'ūl bihi — majrūr Object (came to me) وَلَدٌ Mawsūf + Fā'il — marfū' Nakira noun; fā'il of جَاءَ together with its sifah يَرْكَبُ Fi'l mudhāri' (sifah sentence) Fā'il = dhamīr mustatir (هُو) referring to وَلَد الدَّرَّاجَةَ Maf'ūl bihi — mansūb Object of يَرْكَبُ يَرْكَبُ الدَّرَّاجَةَ Sifah (jumlah fi'liyya) Sentence describes the nakira وَلَدٌ

docs.title: Example 4: جَاءَ رَجُلٌ أَبُوهُ عَالِمٌ
docs.text:
(A man whose father is a scholar came)

Word I'rāb Reason جَاءَ Fi'l mādhī Verb رَجُلٌ Mawsūf + Fā'il — marfū' Nakira; fā'il of جَاءَ (with its sifah sentence) أَبُوهُ Mubtada' — marfū' (mudhāf + mudhāf ilayhi) أَبُو = mudhāf; هُ = mudhāf ilayhi referring to رَجُل عَالِمٌ Khabar — marfū' Completes the nominal sifah sentence أَبُوهُ عَالِمٌ Sifah (jumlah ismiyya) Nominal sentence describing the nakira رَجُلٌ; contains dhamīr هُ referring to مَوْصُوف

docs.title: Example 5 (Class Exercise): مَا عَادَ الْمَرِيضَ عَائِدٌ غَيْرَ الطَّبِيبِ
docs.text:
(No visitor visited the patient except the doctor)

Word I'rāb Reason مَا Harf nafy Negation عَادَ Fi'l mādhī Verb الْمَرِيضَ Maf'ūl bihi — mansūb Patient = object (comes before fā'il here) عَائِدٌ Fā'il — marfū' Visitor = subject; nakira after نَفْي = generalization ("no visitor") غَيْرَ Mustathna — mansūb Scenario #7 (ghayр mujab, muttasil with mustasnā minh implied); ghayر takes nasb الطَّبِيبِ Mudhāf ilayhi — majrūr Mudhāf ilayhi of غَيْر

docs.title: Class Exercise — Mustasna Sentences (identifying scenarios)
docs.text: Sentence Translation Scenario I'rāb of Mustasna/Ghayр رَأَيْتُ جُنُودًا إِلَّا الْقَائِدَ I saw soldiers except the commander #2 (mujab, muttasil — mustasna minh is part of same group) Mansūb صَامَ الْوَلَدُ رَمَضَانَ غَيْرَ يَوْمٍ The boy fasted Ramadan except for one day #7 (uses غَيْر) غَيْرَ mansūb; يَوْمٍ majrūr زُرْتُ مَسَاجِدَ الْمَدِينَةِ إِلَّا مَسْجِدًا I visited the mosques of the city except one #5 (munqati') Mansūb جَاءَ الْقَوْمُ إِلَّا الْحِمَارَ The people came except the donkey #1 (mujab, munqati'... per student's identification) Mansūb دَخَلْتُ غُرَفَ الْبَيْتِ خَلَا غُرْفَةَ النَّوْمِ I entered the rooms of the house except the bedroom #6 (uses خَلَا) Per خَلَا rules مَا عَادَ الْمَرِيضَ عَائِدٌ غَيْرَ الطَّبِيبِ No visitor visited the patient except the doctor #7 (uses غَيْر) غَيْرَ mansūb قَرَأْتُ الْكِتَابَ إِلَّا صَفْحَتَيْنِ I read the book except for two pages #2 (mujab, muttasil) Mansūb مَا جَاءَ إِلَّا الْأُسْتَاذُ No one came except the teacher #4 (ghayр mujab, mufarragh) Marfū' (per 'āmil)

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. I'rāb of غَيْر is determined by substitution: Replace غَيْر with إِلَّا, identify the Mustasna scenario, give that I'rāb to غَيْر, and make the following noun majrūr as mudhāf ilayhi.

  2. سِوَى never changes because it is mabnī; غَيْر changes because it is mu'rab — this is why only غَيْر's I'rāb needs to be determined.

  3. Tawābi' is the umbrella for five concepts — tābi' (the follower) matches the matbū' (the followed) in I'rāb and potentially in ma'rifa/nakira, gender, and number.

  4. Mawsūf = Man'ūt; Sifah = Na't — these are synonymous term pairs. The teacher recommends sticking with mawsūf and sifah for clarity.

  5. Na't Haqīqī (direct description): The sifah directly describes the mawsūf and agrees with it in all four categories.

  6. When the sifah is a sentence: Two strict rules apply — (a) the mawsūf must be nakira, and (b) the sentence must contain a dhamīr referring back to the mawsūf.

  7. Three possible roles for a sentence following a noun:

  8. Follows ma'rifa + linked by copula → Khabar
  9. Follows ma'rifa + after a verb → Hāl (with dhul-hāl)

  10. Follows nakira → Sifah (na't)

  11. Next class: Na't Sababi (indirect description) — read the section in advance as it is more technical. Exercise on identifying Na't Haqīqī vs. Na't Sababi.

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session focused on Na't Sababi (Indirect Description) — a type of grammatical follower (tabi') where the descriptive word does not directly describe the word it follows, but rather describes something connected to it. The class reviewed the rules governing Na't Sababi, analyzed sentence examples in detail, and worked through exercises contrasting Na't Sababi with Na't Haqiqi. The session also covered the distinction between Ism al-Fa'il and Fa'il, and concluded with exercises distinguishing between Mausoof-Sifah, Mubtada-Khabar, and Hal constructions.

docs.title: 1. Tawabi' — Recap
docs.text:
• A tabi' (follower) is a word that follows another word and takes its grammatical case from it.
  • The word being followed is called matbu'; the word following is called tabi'.
  • Mausoof and Sifah are a type of tabi'/matbu' relationship — the Sifah (adjective) is the tabi', the Mausoof (noun described) is the matbu'.

docs.title: 2. Na't Haqiqi vs. Na't Sababi
docs.text:
Feature Na't Haqiqi Na't Sababi What does the Na't describe? Directly describes the Matbu'/Mausoof Describes something connected to the Matbu' Structure Na't — Man'ut (adjective after noun) Na't comes first, Man'ut comes second (reversed) Example جَاءَنِي وَلَدٌ عَالِمٌ جَاءَنِي وَلَدٌ عَالِمٌ أَبُوهُ Translation A learned boy came A boy whose father is learned came

Key insight: In Na't Sababi, the Na't (descriptive word) and the Man'ut (the one actually being described) swap positions — Na't comes first, Man'ut comes second. This is the reverse of Na't Haqiqi where Man'ut (= Mausoof) comes first.

docs.title: 3. Rules of Na't Sababi
docs.text:
Rule 1 — Agreement between Matbu' and Tabi' The first word (Matbu') and the second word (Tabi') must agree in only two aspects: - I'rab (case ending) — both must be in the same case - Ma'rifah/Nakirah — both must either be definite or indefinite

They do not need to agree in gender or number.

Example: وَلَدٌ (Nakirah, Raf') — عَالِمٌ (Nakirah, Raf') ✓

Rule 2 — Na't is always singular The Na't (the descriptive word/adjective) remains singular regardless of whether the Matbu' is singular, dual, or plural.

Example: صُورَتَانِ جَمِيلٌ إِطَارُهُمَا "Two pictures whose frames are beautiful." — صُورَتَانِ is dual (Matbu'), but جَمِيلٌ remains singular (Na't)

Rule 3 — Na't agrees in gender with the word after it (the Man'ut) The Na't must agree in gender with the Man'ut (the word it actually describes — the connected noun), not with the Matbu'.

Example: جَاءَتِ السَّيِّدَةُ الْعَاقِلُ وَلَدُهَا "The lady whose son is intelligent came." — الْعَاقِلُ is masculine because وَلَدُهَا (son) is masculine, even though السَّيِّدَةُ is feminine.

Summary of Na't Sababi agreement:

Aspect Agrees with I'rab Matbu' (first word) Ma'rifah/Nakirah Matbu' (first word) Gender Man'ut (the connected word after Na't) Number Neither — Na't is always singular

docs.title: 4. Ism al-Fa'il vs. Fa'il
docs.text:
This distinction arose during sentence analysis:

  • Fa'il = the doer of the action in a sentence (the subject of the verb)
  • Ism al-Fa'il = any noun on the pattern فَاعِلٌ — regardless of whether it is actually the doer of the action in that sentence

Example: رَأَيْتُ سَاجِدًا — "I saw a prostrating person." - أَنَا (I) is the Fa'il (doer — I am the one who saw) - سَاجِدٌ is the Ism al-Fa'il (on the فَاعِل pattern, but not the doer of the verb رَأَيْتُ)

Application in Na't Sababi: When an Ism al-Fa'il appears as the Na't, it can take its own Fa'il (the Man'ut), forming a Jumlah Fi'liyyah or making the following noun its Fa'il.

Example: جَاءَتِ امْرَأَةٌ عَالِمٌ ابْنُهَا - عَالِمٌ is Ism al-Fa'il; ابْنُهَا is its Fa'il (in Raf' case) - Together عَالِمٌ ابْنُهَا = the Tabi' of امْرَأَةٌ

docs.title: 5. Identifying Na't Haqiqi vs. Na't Sababi — Practical Test
docs.text:
Look at the middle word in the construction: - If the middle word is an adjective → likely Na't Sababi (the adjective describes the following noun, not the first noun) - If the middle word is a regular noun (not an adjective) → likely Na't Haqiqi using a Jumlah Ismiyyah as the Sifah

Example: شَاهَدْنَا قِطَارًا سَيْرُهُ سَرِيعٌ - سَيْرٌ (speed/movement) = regular noun, not an adjective - سَيْرُهُ سَرِيعٌ = Jumlah Ismiyyah acting as Sifah of قِطَارًا - This is Na't Haqiqi with a sentential description

docs.title: 6. Hal — Brief Review
docs.text:
During the final exercise, Hal was revisited: - Hal = a circumstantial accusative describing the state of the doer/object at the time of the action - Usually Nakirah and in the Nasb case - Always requires a Dhul-Hal (the person/thing in that state) preceding it

Example: جَاءَ الْوَلَدُ ضَاحِكًا - الْوَلَدُ = Dhul-Hal; ضَاحِكًا = Hal (Nakirah, Nasb) - Together → Fa'il of جَاءَ

docs.title: 7. Distinguishing Mubtada-Khabar from Mausoof-Sifah
docs.text:
The session highlighted how word order and the insertion of "is/are" in translation reveals the structure:

هَذَا الْوَلَدُ ضَاحِكٌ - "This boy is laughing." - هَذَا الْوَلَدُ = Ism Ishara + Musha'run Ilayh → together = Mubtada - ضَاحِكٌ = Khabar

هَذَا وَلَدٌ ضَاحِكٌ - "This is a laughing boy." - هَذَا = Mubtada - وَلَدٌ ضَاحِكٌ = Mausoof-Sifah → together = Khabar

Tip: Where you insert "is" in the English translation tells you what the Mubtada is — everything before "is" = Mubtada.

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning تَابِع (Tabi') The following/dependent word مَتْبُوع (Matbu') The word being followed نَعْت (Na't) Adjective/descriptive word مَنْعُوت (Man'ut) The word being described by the Na't مَوْصُوف (Mausoof) The described noun (synonym for Man'ut in Na't Haqiqi) صِفَة (Sifah) Adjective/description (synonym for Na't) نَعْت حَقِيقِي (Na't Haqiqi) Direct description — adjective directly describes the Matbu' نَعْت سَبَبِي (Na't Sababi) Indirect description — adjective describes something connected to Matbu' اسْمُ الْفَاعِل (Ism al-Fa'il) A noun on the فَاعِل pattern فَاعِل (Fa'il) The doer of the verb in a sentence جُمْلَة اسْمِيَّة (Jumlah Ismiyyah) Nominal sentence (Mubtada + Khabar) جُمْلَة فِعْلِيَّة (Jumlah Fi'liyyah) Verbal sentence (Fi'l + Fa'il) حَال (Hal) Circumstantial accusative (state of doer/object) ذُو الْحَال (Dhul-Hal) The person/thing whose state is described by Hal مَعْرِفَة (Ma'rifah) Definite noun نَكِرَة (Nakirah) Indefinite noun تَوَابِع (Tawabi') Followers (grammatical dependents) — plural of Tabi' مُهَذَّب (Muhazzab) Polite, refined, well-mannered

docs.title: Example 1: جَاءَتِ امْرَأَةٌ عَالِمٌ ابْنُهَا
docs.text:
"A woman whose son is learned came."

Word Role جَاءَتْ Fi'l (verb) امْرَأَةٌ Matbu' عَالِمٌ Na't (Ism al-Fa'il) ابْنُهَا Man'ut — also Fa'il of عَالِمٌ (Mudaf + Mudaf Ilayh, in Raf') عَالِمٌ ابْنُهَا (together) Tabi' of امْرَأَةٌ امْرَأَةٌ + عَالِمٌ ابْنُهَا (together) Fa'il of جَاءَتْ

Agreement check: عَالِمٌ agrees with امْرَأَةٌ in I'rab (Raf') and Nakirah. عَالِمٌ agrees with ابْنٌ in gender (both masculine).

docs.title: Example 2: هَاتَانِ صُورَتَانِ جَمِيلٌ إِطَارُهُمَا
docs.text:
"These are two pictures whose frames are beautiful."

Word Role هَاتَانِ Mubtada (Ism Ishara) صُورَتَانِ Matbu' جَمِيلٌ Na't (singular, masculine — agrees with إِطَار in gender) إِطَارُهُمَا Man'ut — Fa'il of جَمِيلٌ (Mudaf + Mudaf Ilayh, Raf') جَمِيلٌ إِطَارُهُمَا (together) Tabi' of صُورَتَانِ صُورَتَانِ + جَمِيلٌ إِطَارُهُمَا Khabar of هَاتَانِ

Note: صُورَتَانِ is dual but جَمِيلٌ remains singular — Rule 2 in action.

docs.title: Example 3: جَاءَتِ السَّيِّدَةُ الْعَاقِلُ وَلَدُهَا
docs.text:
"The lady whose son is intelligent came."

Word Role جَاءَتْ Fi'l السَّيِّدَةُ Matbu' (Ma'rifah, Raf') الْعَاقِلُ Na't (Ma'rifah, Raf', masculine — agrees with وَلَد in gender) وَلَدُهَا Man'ut — Fa'il of الْعَاقِلُ السَّيِّدَةُ + الْعَاقِلُ وَلَدُهَا Fa'il of جَاءَتْ

docs.title: Example 4: شَاهَدْنَا قِطَارًا سَيْرُهُ سَرِيعٌ
docs.text:
"We saw a train whose movement is fast."

Word Role شَاهَدْنَا Fi'l + Fa'il قِطَارًا Mausoof (Nakirah, Nasb) سَيْرُهُ Mubtada (Mudaf + Mudaf Ilayh) — Raf' سَرِيعٌ Khabar سَيْرُهُ سَرِيعٌ Jumlah Ismiyyah acting as Sifah of قِطَارًا قِطَارًا سَيْرُهُ سَرِيعٌ Maf'ul Bihi of شَاهَدْنَا

Note: This is Na't Haqiqi with a sentential Sifah — سَيْرٌ is a regular noun (not an adjective), so the middle word is not an adjective, indicating it is not Na't Sababi.

docs.title: Exercise Set 1 — Translate and Identify Na't Type
docs.text:
1. رَكِبْتُ الْحِصَانَ الْجَمِيلَ سَرْجُهُ "I rode the horse whose saddle is beautiful." - رَكِبْتُ = Fi'l + Fa'il - الْحِصَانَ = Matbu' (Ma'rifah, Nasb) - الْجَمِيلَ = Na't (Ma'rifah, Nasb — agrees with حِصَان in I'rab and Ma'rifah; agrees with سَرْجٌ in gender — masculine) - سَرْجُهُ = Man'ut / Fa'il of الْجَمِيل (Raf') - الْحِصَانَ + الْجَمِيلَ سَرْجُهُ = Maf'ul Bihi → Na't Sababi

2. هُوَ رَجُلٌ عَالِمَةٌ ابْنَتُهُ "He is a man whose daughter is learned." - هُوَ = Mubtada - رَجُلٌ = Matbu' (Nakirah, Raf') - عَالِمَةٌ = Na't (Nakirah, Raf', feminine — agrees with ابْنَة in gender) - ابْنَتُهُ = Man'ut / Fa'il of عَالِمَة (Raf') - رَجُلٌ + عَالِمَةٌ ابْنَتُهُ = Khabar of هُوَ → Na't Sababi

3. هَؤُلَاءِ بَنَاتٌ عَاقِلَاتٌ "These are intelligent girls." - هَؤُلَاءِ = Mubtada - بَنَاتٌ = Mausoof (Nakirah, Raf') - عَاقِلَاتٌ = Sifah (Nakirah, Raf', feminine plural — agrees with بَنَات) - بَنَاتٌ عَاقِلَاتٌ = Khabar → Na't Haqiqi

4. شَاهَدْنَا قِطَارًا سَيْرُهُ سَرِيعٌ (Analyzed above) — Na't Haqiqi with Jumlah Ismiyyah as Sifah

5. أَوْقَدْتُ مِصْبَاحًا نُورُهُ قَوِيٌّ "I lit a lamp whose light was strong." - أَوْقَدْتُ = Fi'l + Fa'il - مِصْبَاحًا = Man'ut / Mausoof (Nakirah, Nasb) - نُورُهُ = Mubtada (Mudaf + Mudaf Ilayh) - قَوِيٌّ = Khabar - نُورُهُ قَوِيٌّ = Jumlah Ismiyyah → Sifah of مِصْبَاح → Na't Haqiqi with sentential Sifah

6. هَذَا عَمَلٌ يَنْفَعُ "This is a beneficial deed." - هَذَا = Mubtada - عَمَلٌ = Mausoof (Nakirah, Raf') - يَنْفَعُ = Fi'l + Fa'il (Fa'il = Damir mustatir) → Jumlah Fi'liyyah acting as Sifah - عَمَلٌ يَنْفَعُ = Mausoof + Sifah → Khabar → Na't Haqiqi with Jumlah Fi'liyyah as Sifah

7. هَذَا مَنْزِلٌ ضَيِّقٌ "This is a narrow house." - هَذَا = Mubtada - مَنْزِلٌ = Mausoof - ضَيِّقٌ = Sifah - مَنْزِلٌ ضَيِّقٌ = Khabar → Na't Haqiqi

8. جَاءَ الرَّجُلُ الْمُهَذَّبُ أَخُوهُ "The man whose brother is polite came." - جَاءَ = Fi'l - الرَّجُلُ = Matbu' (Ma'rifah, Raf') - الْمُهَذَّبُ = Na't (Ma'rifah, Raf', masculine — agrees with أَخٌ in gender) - أَخُوهُ = Man'ut / Fa'il of الْمُهَذَّب (Raf') - الرَّجُلُ + الْمُهَذَّبُ أَخُوهُ = Fa'il of جَاءَ → Na't Sababi

docs.title: Exercise Set 2 — Distinguish Between Sentences
docs.text:
هَذَا الْوَلَدُ ضَاحِكٌ "This boy is laughing." - هَذَا = Ism Ishara; الْوَلَدُ = Musha'run Ilayh - هَذَا الْوَلَدُ together = Mubtada - ضَاحِكٌ = Khabar

هَذَا وَلَدٌ ضَاحِكٌ "This is a laughing boy." - هَذَا = Mubtada - وَلَدٌ = Mausoof; ضَاحِكٌ = Sifah - وَلَدٌ ضَاحِكٌ together = Khabar

هَؤُلَاءِ الْبَنَاتُ عَاقِلَاتٌ "These girls are intelligent." - هَؤُلَاءِ = Ism Ishara; الْبَنَاتُ = Musha'run Ilayh - هَؤُلَاءِ الْبَنَاتُ together = Mubtada - عَاقِلَاتٌ = Khabar

هَؤُلَاءِ بَنَاتٌ عَاقِلَاتٌ "These are intelligent girls." - هَؤُلَاءِ = Mubtada - بَنَاتٌ عَاقِلَاتٌ = Mausoof + Sifah → Khabar

جَاءَ الْوَلَدُ ضَاحِكًا "The boy came laughing." - جَاءَ = Fi'l - الْوَلَدُ = Dhul-Hal + Fa'il of جَاءَ - ضَاحِكًا = Hal (Nakirah, Nasb)

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Na't Sababi is indirect description. The Na't (adjective) does not describe the Matbu' (first noun) — it describes a noun connected to the Matbu'. The connection is typically through possession or relation (e.g., his father, her son, its frame).

  2. Reversed word order. Unlike Na't Haqiqi where Mausoof comes before Sifah, in Na't Sababi the Na't comes first and the Man'ut comes second. Remember: Na'at Sababi → Na't then Man'ut.

  3. Three agreement rules for Na't Sababi:

  4. Na't agrees with Matbu' in I'rab and Ma'rifah/Nakirah
  5. Na't is always singular (regardless of Matbu' number)

  6. Na't agrees with Man'ut in gender only

  7. Ism al-Fa'il ≠ Fa'il. A word can be on the فَاعِل pattern (Ism al-Fa'il) without being the actual doer (Fa'il) of the main verb in the sentence. An Ism al-Fa'il can govern its own Fa'il, which becomes the Man'ut in Na't Sababi.

  8. Quick identification test: Look at the middle word. If it is an adjective → Na't Sababi. If it is a regular noun (non-adjective) → the following Jumlah is a Sifah (Na't Haqiqi with a sentential description).

  9. Mubtada-Khabar vs. Mausoof-Sifah: Translate and insert "is/are" — what comes before "is" is the Mubtada. If a demonstrative pronoun shares its referent with the following noun (هَذَا الْوَلَدُ), both form the Mubtada together as Ism Ishara + Musha'run Ilayh.

  10. Ta'kid (Emphasis) section (3.10) was not covered — scheduled for the next session (Thursday, 2026-06-25), with approximately two more classes remaining after that to complete the book.

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text:
This session covered the concept of Ta'kid (التأكيد) — emphasis in Arabic grammar. Ta'kid is a type of Tabi' (a word that follows another), falling under the broader umbrella of Tawabi'. The class distinguished between two major types of ta'kid — lafzi (verbal/repetition-based) and ma'nawi (meaning-based) — and explored the specific words used for ma'nawi emphasis in detail. The session concluded with extensive sentence analysis exercises applying all concepts learned.

Previous topic: Na't Sababi (the final discussion on it was completed last class).

Next topic: Al-Badl (3.10.3) — to be covered Monday.

Homework: Exercises on pages 104 and 107 (page 108 is minimal).

docs.title: 1. Definition of Ta'kid (التأكيد)
docs.text:
• Ta'kid is a Tabi' (follower) that either:
  • Emphasizes the Matbu' (the word being followed) in the matter related to it, or
  • Emphasizes the inclusion of all members of the Matbu' in the matter related to it.
  • The word doing the emphasizing = Ta'kid (تأكيد) → it is the Tabi'
  • The word being emphasized = Mu'akkad (مؤكد) → it is the Matbu'
  • Ta'kid always follows the Mu'akkad in i'rab (grammatical case).

docs.title: A. Ta'kid Lafzi (التأكيد اللفظي) — Verbal/Repetition Emphasis
docs.text:
• Emphasis is achieved by repeating the Mu'akkad.
  • The repeated word can be: a noun, a verb, a harf (particle), or a dhamir (pronoun).
  • Rule for repeating a Dhamir: If the Mu'akkad is a Dhamir Muttasil (attached pronoun) or Mustatir (hidden pronoun), you must use a Dhamir Munfasil (detached pronoun) for the Ta'kid — you cannot repeat two attached pronouns back to back.
Mu'akkad Type Example Notes Noun جاء زيدٌ زيدٌ Repeat the noun Verb غاب الغائبُ فحضر Repeat the verb Harf لا لا أنقضُ العهدَ Repeat the particle Dhamir Muttasil افتح أنتَ النافذةَ Use Dhamir Munfasil instead of repeating attached dhamir

docs.title: B. Ta'kid Ma'nawi (التأكيد المعنوي) — Semantic/Meaning-Based Emphasis
docs.text: Emphasis through specific designated words. Divided into four groups:

docs.title: Group 1: نَفْس (Nafs) and عَيْن (Ayn) — "himself / herself / itself"
docs.text:
Rules: 1. Can be used for singular, dual, or plural Mu'akkad. 2. Must always be used as Mudaf (first term of a genitive construction). 3. The Mudaf Ilayh must be a Dhamir (pronoun). 4. The Dhamir must agree with the Mu'akkad in gender and number. 5. Exception: The plural forms (أنفس / أعيُن) are used for both dual AND plural Mu'akkad — the dual form (نَفْسَانِ / عَيْنَانِ) is not used. The distinction between dual vs. plural Mu'akkad is shown only through the Dhamir.

Mu'akkad Ta'kid Form Example Singular (m) نَفْسُهُ / عَيْنُهُ قام زيدٌ نفسُه Singular (f) نَفْسُهَا / عَيْنُهَا جاءت المعلمةُ عينُها Dual (m) أنفسُهُمَا / أعيُنُهُمَا قام الزيدان أنفسُهُمَا Dual (f) أنفسُهُمَا / أعيُنُهُمَا جاءت المعلمتان أعيُنُهُمَا Plural (m) أنفسُهُمْ / أعيُنُهُمْ قام الزيدون أنفسُهُمْ Plural (f) أنفسُهُنَّ / أعيُنُهُنَّ جاءت المعلمات أعيُنُهُنَّ

docs.title: Group 2: كِلا (Kila) and كِلْتَا (Kilta) — "both"
docs.text:
Rules: 1. Can only be used for dual Mu'akkad — "both" = exactly two. 2. Originally: كِلانِ (m) and كِلْتَانِ (f) — Tathniya forms. 3. Always used as Mudaf → noon of Tathniya is dropped (standard rule when Tathniya is Mudaf). 4. Therefore: كِلانِ → كِلا and كِلْتَانِ → كِلْتَا. 5. Must be Mudaf to a dual Dhamir (هُمَا for third person, كُمَا for second person, نَا for first person). 6. كِلا = masculine | كِلْتَا = feminine. 7. In Rafa': كِلاهُمَا / كِلْتَاهُمَا; in Nasb/Jarr: كِلَيْهِمَا / كِلْتَيْهِمَا (ya replaces alif).

Gender Rafa' Nasb/Jarr Masculine كِلاهُمَا كِلَيْهِمَا Feminine كِلْتَاهُمَا كِلْتَيْهِمَا

Examples: - قام الزيدان كِلاهُمَا — Both Zaids stood. - قامت المعلمتان كِلْتَاهُمَا — Both female teachers stood.

docs.title: Group 3: كُلّ (Kull), جَمِيع (Jami'), and أجْمَع (Ajma') — "all / entire"
docs.text:
General rule: Can be used for singular and plural (not restricted to dual). Emphasis on completeness/totality.

Rules for كُلّ and جَمِيع: - Must be Mudaf to a Dhamir that agrees with the Mu'akkad in number and gender. - Singular Mu'akkad → كُلُّهُ / جَمِيعُهُ - Plural Mu'akkad → كُلُّهُمْ / جَمِيعُهُمْ

Rules for أجْمَع (and its feminine جَمْعَاء): - For singular: follows the same rule as كُلّ/جَمِيع → must be Mudaf to a Dhamir (e.g., قرأتُ الكتابَ أجمعَهُ). - For plural: do not use a Dhamir. Instead, convert أجمع into Jama' Muzakkar Salim by adding واو/نون → أجمعون (m) / جُمَع (f). - Important restriction: كُلّ, جَمِيع, and أجمع can only be used for things that have parts or can be divided — not for indivisible entities (e.g., you cannot say اللهُ كُلُّهُ — incorrect).

Mu'akkad كُلّ form جَمِيع form أجمع form Singular كُلُّهُ جَمِيعُهُ أجمعَهُ Plural (m) كُلُّهُمْ جَمِيعُهُمْ أجمعون Plural (f) كُلُّهُنَّ جَمِيعُهُنَّ جُمَع

Examples: - قرأتُ الكتابَ كُلَّهُ — I read the entire book. - جاء الرجالُ كُلُّهُمْ — All the men came. - جاء الناسُ أجمعون — All the people came. - فَسجد الملائكةُ كُلُّهُمْ أجمعون — All the angels prostrated.

docs.title: Group 4: أكْتَع / أبْتَع / أبْصَع (and feminines كُتَع / بُتَع / بُصَع) — Additional Emphasis
docs.text:
• Used after أجمعون / جُمَع for extra/intensified emphasis.
  • Only come after أجمع — they are not used alone.
  • For plural masculine: أكتعون / أبتعون / أبصعون
  • For plural feminine: كُتَع / بُتَع / بُصَع

Examples: - جاء القومُ أجمعون أكتعون — All the people came (emphatic). - قامت النساءُ جُمَعُ كُتَعُ — All the women stood (emphatic).

docs.title: 3. Multiple Ta'kid in One Sentence
docs.text:
It is permissible to use two or more Ta'kid words for a single Mu'akkad:

جاء القومُ كُلُّهُمْ أجمعون - Al-Qawmu = Mu'akkad - كُلُّهُمْ = Ta'kid #1 - أجمعون = Ta'kid #2 - All three together = Fa'il of جاء

docs.title: 4. I'rab Position of Ta'kid
docs.text: Ta'kid always follows its Mu'akkad in i'rab case: - Mu'akkad in Rafa' → Ta'kid in Rafa' - Mu'akkad in Nasb → Ta'kid in Nasb (e.g., نفسَهُ، كُلَّهُ) - Mu'akkad in Jarr → Ta'kid in Jarr (e.g., نفسِهِ، كُلِّهِ)

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning التأكيد (at-ta'kid) Emphasis التوكيد (at-tawkid) Emphasis (alternate form) التأكيد اللفظي Verbal/repetition-based emphasis التأكيد المعنوي Meaning/semantic-based emphasis المؤكَّد (al-mu'akkad) The word being emphasized; the Matbu' التأكيد / التوكيد (as Tabi') The word doing the emphasizing; the Tabi' نَفْس (nafs) Self / himself / herself (used for ta'kid) عَيْن (ayn) Same / himself / herself (used for ta'kid) أنفُس (anfus) Plural of nafs (used for dual AND plural Mu'akkad) أعيُن (a'yun) Plural of ayn (used for dual AND plural Mu'akkad) كِلا (kila) Both (masculine dual) كِلْتَا (kilta) Both (feminine dual) كُلّ (kull) All / entire جَمِيع (jami') All / entirety أجمع (ajma') All (can shift to Jama' Muzakkar Salim for plural) جُمَع (juma') Feminine plural form of أجمع أكتع / أبتع / أبصع Extra emphasis words used after أجمعون كُتَع / بُتَع / بُصَع Feminine forms of the extra emphasis words ضمير مُتَّصِل (dhamir muttasil) Attached pronoun ضمير مُنفصل (dhamir munfasil) Detached/separate pronoun ضمير مُستتِر (dhamir mustatir) Hidden/implied pronoun مضاف (mudaf) First term in a genitive construction مضاف إليه (mudaf ilayh) Second term in a genitive construction (possessor) مرجع (marja') Reference point of a pronoun اسم الفعل (ism al-fi'l) Noun acting as a verb (e.g., حَذَارِ) النَّمِيمة (an-namima) Gossip / tale-bearing حَذَارِ (hadhari) Beware! (ism al-fi'l) إيَّاكَ (iyyaka) Beware of / avoid (used with hidden verb) مستثنى (mustathna) The excepted item (after إلا) مستثنى منه (mustathna minh) What is being excepted from حرف الاستثناء Particle of exception (e.g., إلا)

docs.title: Example 1: قام زيدٌ نفسُهُ / عينُهُ
docs.text: Zayd himself stood. - قام — Fi'l (past tense verb) - زيدٌ — Mu'akkad → Fa'il (subject), marfu', tanwin damm - نفسُهُ — Ta'kid (of Zayd); Nafs = Mudaf, Hu = Mudaf Ilayh (refers to Zayd: masculine singular); marfu' following the Mu'akkad

docs.title: Example 2: جاء القومُ كُلُّهُمْ أجمعون
docs.text: All the people came. - جاء — Fi'l (Jumlah Fi'liyyah Khabariyyah) - القومُ — Mu'akkad - كُلُّهُمْ — Ta'kid #1; Kull = Mudaf, Hum = Mudaf Ilayh (plural, agreeing with al-Qawm) - أجمعون — Ta'kid #2; Jama' Muzakkar Salim form because Mu'akkad is plural - Al-Qawmu + كُلُّهُمْ + أجمعون together = Fa'il of جاء

docs.title: Example 3: فسجد الملائكةُ كُلُّهُمْ أجمعون إلا إبليسَ
docs.text: All the angels prostrated except Iblis. - سجد — Fi'l - الملائكةُ — Mu'akkad - كُلُّهُمْ — Ta'kid #1 - أجمعون — Ta'kid #2 - إلا — Harf al-Istithna' - إبليسَ — Mustathna (منصوب) - الملائكةُ + كُلُّهُمْ + أجمعون = Mustathna Minh - The full combination (Mustathna Minh + إلا + Mustathna) = Fa'il of سجد

docs.title: Example 4: إيَّاكَ إيَّاكَ والنَّمِيمةَ
docs.text: Beware! Beware of gossip. - Hidden Fi'l + Hidden Fa'il (implied "I warn you") - إيَّاكَ (first) — Mu'akkad → Maf'ul Awwal (first object) - إيَّاكَ (second) — Ta'kid Lafzi (repetition) - والنَّمِيمةَ — Second Maf'ul (واو = 'atf or addition) - Both إيَّاكَ (Mu'akkad + Ta'kid) and النميمة are Maf'ul Bih to the hidden verb

docs.title: Example 5: غَرَبَتْ غَرَبَتِ الشمسُ
docs.text: The sun has indeed set. - غَرَبَتْ (first) — Fi'l → Mu'akkad - غَرَبَتِ (second) — Fi'l → Ta'kid Lafzi (verbal repetition) - الشمسُ — Fa'il for both (for the combined Mu'akkad + Ta'kid)

docs.title: Example 6: قَطَعْنَا نَحْنُ أنفسَنَا الطريقَ كُلَّهُ
docs.text: We ourselves covered the entire path. - قَطَعْنَا — Fi'l + Dhamir (implied Fa'il "we") - نَحْنُ — Ta'kid Lafzi #1 (Dhamir Munfasil repeating the hidden نا) - أنفسَنَا — Ta'kid Ma'nawi #2 (Anfus = Mudaf, Na = Mudaf Ilayh referring to "we") - نحنُ + أنفسَنا together = Fa'il (Ta'kid of the implied Fa'il in قطعنا) - الطريقَ — Mu'akkad → Maf'ul Bih - كُلَّهُ — Ta'kid #3 of الطريق (Kull = Mudaf, Hu = Mudaf Ilayh) - الطريقَ + كُلَّهُ = Maf'ul Bih of قطعنا

docs.title: Example 7: المُلكُ كُلُّهُ لله
docs.text: All sovereignty belongs to Allah. - المُلكُ — Mu'akkad → Mubtada' - كُلُّهُ — Ta'kid (Mudaf + Mudaf Ilayh); follows Mubtada' in Rafa' - المُلكُ + كُلُّهُ together = Mubtada' - لِلَّهِ — Khabar (predicate)

docs.title: Practice Examples
docs.text:
(Exercises from class — translated and analyzed)

1. قابلتُ الوزيرَ نفسَهُ I met the minister himself. - قابلتُ = Fi'l + Fa'il - الوزيرَ = Mu'akkad → Maf'ul Bih - نفسَهُ = Ta'kid (Nafs=Mudaf, Hu=Mudaf Ilayh, agrees with الوزير: masculine singular) - الوزيرَ + نفسَهُ together = Maf'ul Bih

2. ذَبَحْنَا الكَبْشَيْنِ كِلَيْهِمَا We slaughtered both the rams. - ذبحنا = Fi'l + Fa'il - الكبشَيْنِ = Mu'akkad → Maf'ul Bih (dual, Nasb case) - كِلَيْهِمَا = Ta'kid (in Nasb/Jarr form because Mu'akkad is Mansub; Huma = dual dhamir) - الكبشين + كليهما = Maf'ul Bih

3. فسجد الملائكةُ كُلُّهُمْ أجمعون إلا إبليسَ (Analyzed above in I'rab section)

4. إيَّاكَ إيَّاكَ والنَّمِيمةَ (Analyzed above in I'rab section)

5. رَجَع القائدُ عَيْنُهُ The commander himself returned. - رجع = Fi'l - القائدُ = Mu'akkad → Fa'il - عَيْنُهُ = Ta'kid (Ayn=Mudaf, Hu=Mudaf Ilayh, masculine singular) - القائدُ + عينُهُ = Fa'il

6. رأيتُ التمساحَ التمساحَ I saw the crocodile — the actual crocodile. (Ta'kid Lafzi — noun repeated) - رأيتُ = Fi'l + Fa'il - التمساحَ (first) = Mu'akkad → Maf'ul Bih - التمساحَ (second) = Ta'kid Lafzi - Both التمساحَ together = Maf'ul Bih

7. المُلكُ كُلُّهُ لله (Analyzed above in I'rab section)

8. غَرَبَتْ غَرَبَتِ الشمسُ (Analyzed above in I'rab section)

9. حَذَارِ حَذَارِ مِنَ الإهمال Beware, beware of negligence. - حَذَارِ (first) = Ism al-Fi'l (acts as a verb: "beware!") → Mu'akkad - حَذَارِ (second) = Ta'kid Lafzi - مِنَ الإهمال = connected to حذاري (specifies what to beware of)

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Ta'kid is a Tabi' — it always follows the Mu'akkad in grammatical case (i'rab). This is the unifying principle across all types.

  2. Ta'kid Lafzi = repetition. Just repeat the word — noun, verb, harf, or dhamir. If repeating a Dhamir Muttasil/Mustatir, use a Dhamir Munfasil instead.

  3. Ta'kid Ma'nawi uses four groups of special words:

  4. نفس / عين (himself/itself) — Mudaf + Dhamir; use plural form (أنفس/أعيُن) for both dual and plural Mu'akkad; singular form for singular.
  5. كِلا / كِلْتَا (both) — dual only; always Mudaf + dual Dhamir; noon dropped because always Mudaf.
  6. كُلّ / جَمِيع / أجمع (all/entire) — for divisible things only; كُلّ and جَمِيع always take a Dhamir; أجمع takes a Dhamir for singular but becomes Jama' Muzakkar Salim (أجمعون/جُمَع) for plural.

  7. أكتع / أبتع / أبصع (and feminines) — only used after أجمعون/جُمَع for extra emphasis.

  8. The Dhamir in Ma'nawi Ta'kid always refers back to the Mu'akkad and must agree with it in gender and number — this is how you decode what the Mu'akkad is.

  9. كُلّ, جَمِيع, and أجمع cannot be used with indivisible things — do not use them to refer to Allah, a person (as a person), or anything that cannot be divided into parts.

  10. Multiple Ta'kid words can be stacked on a single Mu'akkad (e.g., كُلُّهُمْ أجمعون أكتعون) for increasing levels of emphasis.

  11. حَذَارِ is an Ism al-Fi'l, not a regular noun — it functions like a verb and governs its context accordingly.

  12. When analyzing complex sentences with Ta'kid + Istithna', identify layers: Mu'akkad → Ta'kid(s) → Mustathna Minh (all together) → إلا → Mustathna → then the whole unit fills its syntactic role (Fa'il, Maf'ul, etc.).

docs.title: SARF 102 (Spring 2026)
docs.text:
Sarf 102

Date Topic Jan 30, 2026 Grammar rules Feb 06, 2026 Vowel marks Feb 15, 2026 Awzan Apr 03, 2026 Fundamental three-letter ve... Apr 10, 2026 Jumla ismiyya Apr 19, 2026 Patterns May 03, 2026 Analyzing the book's title ... May 08, 2026 Sigha May 15, 2026 ما May 22, 2026 Fi'l Mudari' Majhul Jun 05, 2026 The nature of Alif and Hamz... Jun 12, 2026 Jazm-giving particles

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session focused on understanding the fundamental structure of Arabic grammar by exploring the two main branches: Nahu (grammar rules) and Sarf (word patterns/morphology). The class used the analogy of building a house to explain how Sarf provides the "materials" (word patterns) while Nahu provides the "blueprint" (rules for usage). The primary emphasis was on trilateral roots and how the verb "kataba" (to write) demonstrates the systematic formation of Arabic words through different patterns and combinations.

docs.title: Trilateral Root System
docs.text:
• Trilateral roots (al-huruf thulathi): Three-letter combinations forming the basis of most Arabic words (~80%)
  • Root letters: K-T-B as example for writing-related words
  • Six possible combinations: kataba, kabata, bakata, bataka, takaba, tabaka

docs.title: Verb Conjugation Patterns
docs.text:
• 14 total patterns for active voice verbs
  • Distribution breakdown:
  • Third person: 6 patterns (masculine/feminine, singular/dual/plural)
  • Second person: 6 patterns (masculine/feminine, singular/dual/plural)
  • First person: 2 patterns only (ana/nahnu - no dual or gender distinction)

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning kataba he wrote (past tense, 3rd person singular masculine) katabat she wrote (past tense, 3rd person singular feminine) yaktubu he writes (present tense) taktubu she writes (present tense) katib writer (masculine) katiba writer (feminine) katibun writers (masculine plural) kutiba it was written (passive voice) kitab book kutub books (plural) al-huruf thulathi trilateral letters/roots awzan patterns/scales maruf active voice majhul passive voice ghaib third person (literally "absent")

docs.title: Past Tense Active Voice (Kataba Root)
docs.text:
Third Person (6 patterns): - kataba (he wrote) - katabat (she wrote) - kataba (they two males wrote) - katabata (they two females wrote) - katabu (they males wrote) - katabna (they females wrote)

Second Person (6 patterns): - katabta (you masculine wrote) - katabti (you feminine wrote) - katabtuma (you two wrote) - katabtum (you males wrote) - katabtunna (you females wrote)

First Person (2 patterns only): - katabtu (I wrote) - katabna (we wrote)

docs.title: K-T-B Root Family
docs.text:
• Base verb: kataba (to write)
  • Active participle: katib/katiba (writer)
  • Noun form: kitab (book)
  • Plural noun: kutub (books)
  • Passive form: kutiba (was written)

docs.title: Pattern Formation Rules
docs.text:
• General meaning: Three root letters in specific order (K-T-B) give general sense of writing
  • Specific meaning: Harakat (vowel marks) create precise meanings within the general category
  • Order significance: Only one of the six possible letter combinations (kataba) produces the intended meaning

docs.title: House Building Analogy
docs.text:
• Sarf = Building materials: Pre-made components (doors, windows, tiles) with limited standard sizes
  • Nahu = Building codes: Rules governing how materials can be used and assembled
  • Application: Same materials can build different structures (house, school, store) following appropriate codes

docs.title: English Comparison
docs.text:
• Go/went/gone: Similar pattern variation as Arabic trilateral roots
  • Writer pattern: Adding "-er" suffix parallels Arabic active participle formation (katib)
  • Usage rules: Grammar determines when to use "he goes" vs "he go" (similar to Arabic grammatical requirements)

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Dual Number Limitation in First Person: Unlike third and second person, first person cannot have dual forms because "I" cannot be duplicated - only singular (ana) and plural (nahnu) exist.

  2. Gender Independence in First Person: First person forms are gender-neutral because the speaker's gender is already known, eliminating the need for separate masculine/feminine patterns.

  3. Pattern Recognition Importance: Understanding root letters and their patterns enables vocabulary expansion - from one three-letter root, multiple related words can be derived and recognized.

  4. Sarf vs Nahu Division: Sarf handles internal word structure and pattern formation, while Nahu governs word endings and sentence-level grammar rules.

  5. Trilateral Root Dominance: Approximately 80% of Arabic vocabulary stems from three-letter root combinations, making this system central to language comprehension.

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This foundational session established the crucial relationship between Sarf and Nahw through practical analogies. The core focus was understanding that Sarf provides the available word structures and patterns (like building materials in a store), while Nahw provides the grammatical rules for how to use these structures correctly (like building codes and construction rules). The session emphasized the primary function of Nahw: determining the haraka (vowel marks) on the last letter of words, which affects meaning and grammatical correctness.

docs.title: Tri-letter Root System (Fa-Ayn-Lam)
docs.text:
• Base structure: فَعَلَ (fa'ala) - three letter foundation
  • Pattern variations for past tense:
  • فَعَلَ (fa'ala) - fatha on middle letter
  • فَعِلَ (fa'ila) - kasra on middle letter
  • فَعُلَ (fa'ula) - dhamma on middle letter

docs.title: Position terminology:
docs.text:
• Makan al-fa (مكان الفاء) - First letter position
  • Makan al-ayn (مكان العين) - Middle letter position
  • Makan al-lam (مكان اللام) - Last letter position

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning صرف (Sarf) Morphology - deals with word structures and patterns نحو (Nahw) Grammar - deals with rules for using words in sentences حركة (Haraka) Vowel mark (fatha, kasra, dhamma) إعراب (I'rab) Case endings - haraka on the last letter of words فعل (Fi'l) Verb اسم (Ism) Noun حرف (Harf) Particle/preposition فاء (Fa) First letter of tri-letter root عين (Ayn) Middle letter of tri-letter root لام (Lam) Last letter of tri-letter root ماضي (Madi) Past tense مضارع (Mudari') Present/future tense لحن جلي (Lahn Jali) Clear grammatical mistake that changes meaning

docs.title: Past Tense Patterns (Third Person, Male, Singular)
docs.text:
Pattern Example Translation فَعَلَ (fa'ala) كَتَبَ (kataba) He wrote فَعِلَ (fa'ila) شَرِبَ (shariba) He drank فَعُلَ (fa'ula) كَرُمَ (karuma) He was noble

Note: The first and last letters maintain consistent harakas in Sarf; only the middle letter's haraka varies between these patterns.

docs.title: Tri-letter System Fundamentals
docs.text:
• Foundation: Most Arabic words built on three-letter roots
  • Reference form: Past tense, third person, male, singular
  • Dictionary entry: This reference form is the primary entry in Arabic dictionaries
  • Variables involved:
  • Person (first, second, third)
  • Gender (masculine, feminine)
  • Number (singular, dual, plural)

docs.title: Haraka Distribution
docs.text:
• Sarf responsibility: Determines harakas for first and middle letters
  • Nahw responsibility: Determines haraka for the last letter only
  • Sukun: Absence of haraka, not counted as a haraka itself

docs.title: Quranic Example Analysis
docs.text:
وَقَتَلَ دَاوُودُ جَالُوتَ (Wa qatala Dawudu Jaluta) - دَاوُودُ (Dawudu) - dhamma on final dal = subject (doer) - جَالُوتَ (Jaluta) - fatha on final ta = object - Meaning: "And Dawud killed Jalut"

Incorrect version: دَاوُودَ جَالُوتُ (Dawuda Jalutun) - دَاوُودَ (Dawuda) - fatha on final dal = object - جَالُوتُ (Jalutu) - dhamma on final ta = subject - Meaning: "And Jalut killed Dawud" (completely opposite and false)

This demonstrates how case endings (i'rab) completely change meaning and why proper Nahw is essential for Quranic recitation.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Sarf vs Nahw relationship: Sarf provides the "building materials" (word structures), while Nahw provides the "building codes" (grammatical rules)

  2. Primary Nahw function: Determining the correct haraka on the last letter of words based on their grammatical role

  3. Case ending importance: Incorrect harakas can completely change meaning, making prayers invalid and misrepresenting Quranic content

  4. Learning hierarchy: Sarf is generally easier to master than Nahw because it deals with finite, memorizable patterns

  5. Tri-letter foundation: Most Arabic words derive from three-letter roots following the fa-ayn-lam pattern

  6. Dictionary usage: Understanding that past tense, third person, male, singular forms serve as dictionary reference entries

  7. Practical application: Every Muslim needs solid Sarf and Nahw knowledge for proper Quranic recitation and prayer validity

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session focused on understanding Arabic verb patterns (awzan) through dictionary usage and the systematic study of harakat (vowel markings) on the middle letter of three-letter verb roots. The class emphasized practical dictionary skills and the mathematical patterns underlying Arabic verb conjugation, particularly examining how past tense middle letter harakat determine present/future tense possibilities.

docs.title: Basic Verb Pattern Structure
docs.text:
• Fa'ala - Past tense, singular, masculine, active voice pattern
  • Mudari' - Present/future tense forms
  • Root Pattern: Fa-Ayn-Lam (ف-ع-ل) as the template for three-letter verbs

docs.title: Six Valid Combinations
docs.text: The Arabic language allows only 6 combinations (not 9) for verb patterns: 1. Fatha in past → Dhamma in present: fa'ala → yaf'ulu 2. Fatha in past → Fatha in present: fa'ala → yaf'alu 3. Fatha in past → Kasra in present: fa'ala → yaf'ilu 4. Kasra in past → Fatha in present: fa'ila → yaf'alu 5. Kasra in past → Kasra in present: fa'ila → yaf'ilu 6. Dhamma in past → Dhamma in present: fa'ula → yaf'ulu

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Masdar (مصدر) Infinitive/verbal noun - origin of all verb forms Madhi (ماضي) Past tense Mudari' (مضارع) Present/future tense Fa-Ayn-Lam (ف-ع-ل) Template letters representing root positions Harakat (حركات) Vowel markings (fatha, kasra, dhamma) Awzan (أوزان) Patterns/measures Mutalakka (متعلق) Preposition attached to verb

docs.title: Examples from Dictionary Research:
docs.text: Past Tense Present Tense Masdar Meaning fa'ala (فعل) yaf'alu (يفعل) fi'l/fa'l (فعل/فال) he did/acted kataba (كتب) yaktubu (يكتب) kitab/kitaba (كتاب/كتابة) he wrote daraba (ضرب) yadribu (يضرب) darb (ضرب) he hit/struck sami'a (سمع) yasma'u (يسمع) sam' (سمع) he heard karuma (كرم) yakrumu (يكرم) karam (كرام) he was noble

docs.title: Dictionary Usage Pattern:
docs.text:
1. Enter root letters without harakat
  2. Identify middle letter haraka from transliteration
  3. Check present tense pattern indicator
  4. Verify masdar forms in brackets
  5. Note attached prepositions (harf mutalakka)

docs.title: Harakat Restrictions:
docs.text:
• Middle letter fatha in past → allows all three harakat in present
  • Middle letter kasra in past → only allows fatha or kasra in present (no dhamma)
  • Middle letter dhamma in past → only allows dhamma in present (no fatha or kasra)

docs.title: Dictionary Exercises Completed:
docs.text:
1. fa'ala - confirmed fatha middle letter, present tense yaf'alu
  2. daraba - confirmed fatha middle letter, present tense yadribu (kasra)
  3. kataba - confirmed fatha middle letter, present tense yaktubu (dhamma)
  4. sami'a - confirmed kasra middle letter, present tense yasma'u (fatha)
  5. karuma - confirmed dhamma middle letter, present tense yakrumu (dhamma)

docs.title: Preposition Variations:
docs.text:
• daraba bi - hit with (something)
  • daraba ila - throw to (somewhere)
  • daraba ala - pour over (something/someone)

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Always start with dictionary research - don't guess harakat patterns
  2. Past tense determines present tense possibilities - not all combinations exist
  3. Only 6 valid combinations exist out of theoretically possible 9
  4. Language has organic restrictions - certain patterns simply don't exist in Arabic
  5. Dictionary efficiency is crucial - use transliteration to identify harakat
  6. Multiple masadir possible - one verb can have several infinitive forms
  7. Prepositions change meaning - same verb + different harf = different meaning

Recommended dictionaries: sedra.net, almaany.com

Next focus: After Ramadan - continue with remaining verb patterns and conjugation rules.

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: Post-Ramadan review session focusing on fundamental three-letter verb patterns in Arabic morphology. The class emphasized understanding why Sarf is studied separately from Nahw, and how three-letter verb combinations transform from past tense (madhi) to present tense (mudari). Key focus on the six valid combinations out of nine theoretical possibilities, and proper dictionary usage for determining harakat (vowel markings).

docs.title: Three-Letter Verb Patterns (Past Tense - Madhi):
docs.text:
1. فَعَلَ (fa'ala) - middle letter has fatha
  2. فَعِلَ (fa'ila) - middle letter has kasra
  3. فَعُلَ (fa'ula) - middle letter has dhamma

docs.title: Present Tense (Mudari) Possibilities:
docs.text:
• From فَعَلَ: يَفْعُلُ (yaf'ulu), يَفْعَلُ (yaf'alu), يَفْعِلُ (yaf'ilu) - all three harakat possible
  • From فَعِلَ: يَفْعَلُ (yaf'alu), يَفْعِلُ (yaf'ilu) only - dhamma not allowed
  • From فَعُلَ: يَفْعُلُ (yaf'ulu) only - same haraka maintained

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning صرف (Sarf) Morphology/word patterns نحو (Nahw) Grammar/sentence structure ماضي (Madhi) Past tense مضارع (Mudari) Present/future tense حركات (Harakat) Vowel markings فتحة (Fatha) Short 'a' vowel كسرة (Kasra) Short 'i' vowel ضمة (Dhamma) Short 'u' vowel فعل (Fi'l) Verb اسم (Ism) Noun حرف (Harf) Particle مذكر (Mudhakkar) Masculine مؤنث (Mu'annath) Feminine مصدر (Masdar) Verbal noun

docs.title: Six Valid Combinations (Active Voice, Third Person Masculine Singular):
docs.text: Past Tense Present Tense Example Dictionary Form فَعَلَ يَفْعُلُ كَتَبَ → يَكْتُبُ kataba/yaktubu فَعَلَ يَفْعَلُ فَتَحَ → يَفْتَحُ fataha/yaftahu فَعَلَ يَفْعِلُ ضَرَبَ → يَضْرِبُ daraba/yadribu فَعِلَ يَفْعَلُ سَمِعَ → يَسْمَعُ sami'a/yasma'u فَعِلَ يَفْعِلُ حَسِبَ → يَحْسِبُ hasiba/yahsibu فَعُلَ يَفْعُلُ رَحُبَ → يَرْحُبُ rahuba/yarhubu

docs.title: Rule Summary:
docs.text:
• Past tense: First and last letters always have fatha
  • Middle letter variations: Determines present tense possibilities
  • Nine theoretical combinations reduced to six: Language doesn't allow فَعِلَ → يَفْعُلُ, فَعُلَ → يَفْعَلُ, or فَعُلَ → يَفْعِلُ

docs.title: Three-Letter Root System:
docs.text:
• Most Arabic verbs built on three-letter roots (approximately 70-80%)
  • فعل (fa'l) as reference pattern: ف (fa) = 1st letter, ع (ayn) = 2nd letter, ل (lam) = 3rd letter
  • Root letters can be any Arabic letters - فعل is just a placeholder system
  • Each root carries core meaning that extends through all derived forms

docs.title: Examples Analyzed:
docs.text:
• ك-ت-ب (k-t-b): Writing/recording
  • ن-ص-ر (n-s-r): Helping/supporting
  • ع-ل-م ('a-l-m): Knowing/learning

docs.title: Dictionary Usage Examples:
docs.text:
كتب (kataba) - Past: كَتَبَ (kataba) - he wrote - Present: يَكْتُبُ (yaktubu) - he writes/will write - Masdar: كِتَابَة (kitaba) - writing

نصر (nasara) - Past: نَصَرَ (nasara) - he helped - Present: يَنْصُرُ (yansuru) - he helps/will help - Masdar: نَصْر (nasr) - help

علم ('alima) - Past: عَلِمَ ('alima) - he knew - Present: يَعْلَمُ (ya'lamu) - he knows/will know - Masdar: عِلْم ('ilm) - knowledge

docs.title: Advanced Patterns:
docs.text: رحب (rahiba/rahuba) - رَحِبَ (rahiba) → يَرْحَبُ (yarhabu) - to be spacious - رَحُبَ (rahuba) → يَرْحُبُ (yarhubu) - to be wide

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Foundation First: Sarf deals with word patterns (building blocks), while Nahw governs how these blocks combine into sentences

  2. Six Sacred Combinations: Memorize the six valid past-to-present transformations - this is fundamental to reading classical texts and Quran

  3. Dictionary Skills Essential: Learn to read Arabic dictionaries to determine correct harakat, especially for middle letters in verbs

  4. Gender and Number Awareness: Everything in Arabic has gender (masculine/feminine) and number (singular/dual/plural)

  5. Practice Reading: Regular exposure to correctly vowelized texts builds intuitive understanding of patterns

  6. Root Recognition: Understanding three-letter roots unlocks vocabulary expansion and meaning connections

  7. No Guesswork: Harakat placement follows rules - if unknown, consult dictionary rather than guess

  8. Active Learning Required: Weekly meetings demand consistent review of recordings and materials between classes

  9. Practical Application: Focus on understanding methodology rather than memorizing isolated examples

  10. Long-term Goal: Solid Sarf foundation enables independent reading of classical Islamic texts and Quranic comprehension

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session began with a recap of three-letter verb patterns and their conjugations, then evolved into an extensive discussion of Arabic sentence structure and grammatical parsing. The class covered verb conjugation tables for نَصَرَ (nasara - "he helped"), distinguished between جملة اسمية (jumla ismiyya) and جملة فعلية (jumla fi'liyya), and conducted detailed grammatical analysis of Surat Al-Fatiha. The instructor emphasized the importance of understanding حركات (harakaat) on final letters to determine grammatical states.

docs.title: نَصَرَ Pattern (Basic Three-Letter Verb)
docs.text:
• Root: ن-ص-ر (n-s-r)
  • Meaning: To help/assist
  • Pattern: فَعَلَ (fa'ala) in past tense
  • Middle letter: Can take فتحة (fatha), كسرة (kasra), or ضمة (dhamma)

docs.title: Conjugation Structure
docs.text:
• Past tense serves as the reference point (غائب مذكر واحد - third person masculine singular)
  • Dictionary entries use this base form
  • Different حركات on middle letter create distinct verb families

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning نَصَرَ He helped نَصَرَا They two (males) helped نَصَرُوا They (males) helped نَصَرَتْ She helped نَصَرَتَا They two (females) helped نَصَرْنَ They (females) helped نَصَرْتَ You (male) helped نَصَرْتُمَا You two helped نَصَرْتُم You (males) helped نَصَرْتُنَّ You (females) helped نَصَرْتُ I helped نَصَرْنَا We helped ضمير مستتر Hidden pronoun مبتدأ Subject (in nominal sentence) خبر Predicate جملة اسمية Nominal sentence جملة فعلية Verbal sentence مضاف First term in possessive construction مضاف إليه Second term in possessive construction

docs.title: Past Tense (الماضي) - نَصَرَ Pattern
docs.text: Person Singular Dual Plural Third (Male) نَصَرَ نَصَرَا نَصَرُوا Third (Female) نَصَرَتْ نَصَرَتَا نَصَرْنَ Second (Male) نَصَرْتَ نَصَرْتُمَا نَصَرْتُم Second (Female) نَصَرْتِ نَصَرْتُمَا نَصَرْتُنَّ First نَصَرْتُ نَصَرْنَا نَصَرْنَا

docs.title: Key Notes on Conjugation:
docs.text:
• Basic form نَصَرَ contains hidden pronoun (ضمير مستتر)
  • Dual forms use الف for masculine, تا for feminine
  • Plural forms use واو for masculine, ن for feminine
  • Second and first person forms add تاء (ta) suffixes
  • Arabic distinguishes three numbers: singular, dual, plural (unlike English)

docs.title: ن-ص-ر Root Family
docs.text:
• Base meaning: Help, assistance, victory
  • Pattern variations: Different middle vowels create distinct meanings
  • Morphological principle: Three-letter roots form foundation of Arabic word formation

docs.title: Grammatical State Analysis
docs.text:
• رفع (Rafa): Nominative case - marked by ضمة
  • نصب (Nasb): Accusative case - marked by فتحة
  • جر (Jarr): Genitive case - marked by كسرة
  • Final letter حركة indicates grammatical function in sentence

docs.title: Sentence Structure Analysis
docs.text:
Example 1: موسى ذهب (Musa dhahab - "Musa went") - موسى = مبتدأ (definite noun starting sentence → jumla ismiyya) - ذهب = خبر (predicate in form of jumla fi'liyya) - Translation: "Musa went" (emphasis on Musa)

Example 2: الحمد لله رب العالمين - الحمد = مبتدأ (definite, starting sentence) - لله رب العالمين = خبر (entire phrase serves as predicate) - Multiple صفات (adjectives) modify الله in jarr case

Example 3: فصل المدرس جيد (The teacher's class is good) - فصل المدرس = مبتدأ (mudaf-mudaf ilayh construction) - جيد = خبر - Mudaf must be analyzed for case, not mudaf ilayh

docs.title: Fundamental Principles:
docs.text:
1. Every Arabic verb is a complete sentence - contains implicit subject
  2. Definite nouns beginning sentences → jumla ismiyya (stronger emphasis)
  3. Final letter harakaat are crucial - indicate grammatical relationships
  4. Mudaf-mudaf ilayh constructions - mudaf ilayh always in jarr case
  5. Context determines translation nuance - Arabic conveys emphasis patterns

docs.title: Parsing Strategy:
docs.text:
1. Identify sentence type by opening word
  2. Locate subject-predicate boundaries
  3. Analyze حركات on final letters
  4. Check for possessive constructions (إضافة)
  5. Consider implicit pronouns in verbs

docs.title: Quranic Application:
docs.text:
• Surat Al-Fatiha demonstrates complex grammatical structures
  • Single sentences can span multiple verses
  • حرف جر (prepositions) affect entire chains of connected nouns
  • Understanding grammar enhances comprehension of meaning and emphasis

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session provided an in-depth exploration of the six fundamental babs (patterns) for three-letter verbs in Arabic. The primary focus was understanding how babs are formed through unique combinations of harakat (vowel marks) on the middle letter in past tense (madi) and present/future tense (mudariya). The teacher emphasized the technical definition of "bab" and practical dictionary usage for identifying verb patterns. The session included live derivation of why there are only six babs instead of the theoretical nine possibilities.

docs.title: The Six Fundamental Babs:
docs.text:
1. Fatha-Dhamma Pattern (نَصَرَ يَنْصُرُ)
  2. Past tense middle letter: Fatha
  3. Present tense middle letter: Dhamma

  4. Example: kataba-yaktubu (كَتَبَ يَكْتُبُ)

  5. Fatha-Fatha Pattern (فَتَحَ يَفْتَحُ)

  6. Past tense middle letter: Fatha
  7. Present tense middle letter: Fatha

  8. Example: fataha-yaftahu (فَتَحَ يَفْتَحُ)

  9. Fatha-Kasra Pattern (ضَرَبَ يَضْرِبُ)

  10. Past tense middle letter: Fatha
  11. Present tense middle letter: Kasra

  12. Example: daraba-yadribu (ضَرَبَ يَضْرِبُ)

  13. Kasra-Fatha Pattern (سَمِعَ يَسْمَعُ)

  14. Past tense middle letter: Kasra
  15. Present tense middle letter: Fatha

  16. Example: samia-yasma'u (سَمِعَ يَسْمَعُ)

  17. Kasra-Kasra Pattern (حَسِبَ يَحْسِبُ)

  18. Past tense middle letter: Kasra
  19. Present tense middle letter: Kasra

  20. Example: hasiba-yahsabu (حَسِبَ يَحْسِبُ)

  21. Dhamma-Dhamma Pattern (كَرُمَ يَكْرُمُ)

  22. Past tense middle letter: Dhamma
  23. Present tense middle letter: Dhamma
  24. Example: karuma-yakrumu (كَرُمَ يَكْرُمُ)

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning باب (Bab) Pattern/category - unique combination of past and present tense harakat ماضي (Madi) Past tense مضارع (Mudariya) Present/future tense حركة (Haraka) Vowel mark فتحة (Fatha) Short 'a' vowel كسرة (Kasra) Short 'i' vowel ضمة (Dhamma) Short 'u' vowel عين الكلمة (Ayn al-Kalima) Middle letter of the word مصدر (Masdar) Verbal noun/infinitive اصطلاحي (Istilahi) Technical term مُصَرِّفون (Musarrifun) Grammarians who study morphology لسان العرب (Lisan al-Arab) Authoritative Arabic dictionary

docs.title: Example: نَصَرَ (Nasara) - Past Tense (14 Patterns)
docs.text: Person Singular Dual Plural 3rd Masc نَصَرَ نَصَرَا نَصَرُوا 3rd Fem نَصَرَتْ نَصَرَتَا نَصَرْنَ 2nd Masc نَصَرْتَ نَصَرْتُمَا نَصَرْتُمْ 2nd Fem نَصَرْتِ نَصَرْتُمَا نَصَرْتُنَّ 1st نَصَرْتُ نَصَرْنَا نَصَرْنَا

docs.title: Present Tense Pattern: يَنْصُرُ (Yansuru)
docs.text:
• Root: ن-ص-ر
  • Middle letter (ص) has dhamma in present tense
  • Belongs to Fatha-Dhamma bab

docs.title: Three-Letter Verb Structure:
docs.text:
• All six babs apply only to three-letter root verbs
  • Focus on middle letter harakat to determine bab membership
  • Dictionary provides both past and present tense harakat information

docs.title: Hamza Transformation Rules:
docs.text:
• When hamza appears with certain vowel combinations, spelling changes occur
  • Example: أَكَلَ (akala) - hamza + fatha + alif becomes modified spelling
  • Original form undergoes transformation for pronunciation ease

docs.title: Missing Combinations:
docs.text:
• Theoretically should have 9 babs (3 past × 3 present combinations)
  • Arabic language doesn't support these 3 combinations:
  • Kasra-Dhamma (doesn't exist)
  • Dhamma-Fatha (doesn't exist)
  • Dhamma-Kasra (doesn't exist)

docs.title: Dictionary Usage Examples:
docs.text:
1. أَكَلَ (Akala) - "to eat"
  2. Past: أَكَلَ (middle letter ك has fatha)
  3. Present: يَأْكُلُ (middle letter ك has dhamma)

  4. Bab: Fatha-Dhamma (like kataba-yaktubu)

  5. ذَهَبَ (Dhahaba) - "to go"

  6. Past: ذَهَبَ (middle letter ه has fatha)
  7. Present: يَذْهَبُ (middle letter ه has fatha)
  8. Bab: Fatha-Fatha (like fataha-yaftahu)

docs.title: Advanced Babs (Roman Numerals):
docs.text:
• Bab II: تَفْعِيل pattern (ta'leem) - أَكْرَمَ يُكْرِمُ
  • Bab IV: إِفْعَال pattern - أَذْهَبَ يُذْهِبُ (causative: "to make go away")

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Technical Definition of Bab: A bab is formed through the unique combination of harakat on the middle letter in past tense and the harakat on the same letter in present tense.

  2. Dictionary Dependency: Cannot memorize all verb patterns - must use Arabic dictionaries like almaany.com or traditional sources like Lisan al-Arab to determine harakat combinations.

  3. Six vs Nine: Arabic naturally developed only 6 fundamental babs instead of the theoretical 9 because certain harakat combinations don't exist in the language as established by classical grammarians.

  4. Practical Application: When encountering a new three-letter verb:

  5. Identify the middle letter
  6. Check dictionary for past tense harakat
  7. Check dictionary for present tense harakat

  8. Determine which of the six babs it belongs to

  9. Foundation for Advanced Study: These six fundamental babs serve as the foundation for understanding advanced verb patterns (Babs II-XIV) which involve additional letters and meanings.

  10. Linguistic Authority: The patterns were established by classical Arabic grammarians (musarrifun) through extensive study of authentic Arabic usage, not arbitrary rules.

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session began the study of "Min Kunuz as-Sarf" (From the Treasures of Arabic Morphology). The class focused on analyzing the book's title to demonstrate proper Arabic grammatical analysis, covering fundamental morphological concepts including mudaf constructions, plural patterns, verb classifications, and the distinction between different types of negation and commands. The instructor emphasized the importance of understanding grammatical relationships between words rather than just memorizing vocabulary.

docs.title: Word Patterns (Awzan) Covered
docs.text:
Plural Patterns: - Kanz → Kunuz (treasure → treasures) - Qalb → Qulub (heart → hearts) - Asl → Usul (principle → principles)

Morphological Analysis Patterns: - Fa'ala pattern structure: fa kalima, ain kalima, lam kalima (first letter, middle letter, last letter) - Mudaf-Mudaf Ilayhi constructions demonstrated in "Kunuz as-Sarf"

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Min From Man Who (relative pronoun) Kanz Treasure Kunuz Treasures (plural) Sarf Morphology/patterns Mudaf First part of possessive construction Mudaf Ilayhi Second part of possessive construction Mutaharrik Letter with haraka (vowel mark) Maskun Letter without haraka Haraka Vowel mark (fatha, kasra, dhamma) Fa'il Subject/doer Maf'ul bihi Direct object Mutaadi Transitive verb (needs object) Lazim Intransitive verb (doesn't need object) Ma'ruf Active voice Majhul Passive voice Amr Positive command Nahi Negative command/prohibition Nafi Simple negation

docs.title: Verb Conjugation Tables
docs.text:
Voice Classifications: - Ma'ruf (Active Voice): Subject explicitly mentioned - Majhul (Passive Voice): Subject omitted or unknown

Verb Types by Object Requirements: - Mutaadi (Transitive): Requires direct object - Example: Kataba (wrote) - "wrote what?" - Lazim (Intransitive): Does not require direct object - Example: Dhahaba (went) - complete meaning without object

docs.title: Root Analysis
docs.text:
Book Title Analysis: "Min Kunuz as-Sarf" - Min: Preposition meaning "from" - Kunuz: Plural of "kanz" (treasure), mudaf (first part of possessive) - as-Sarf: "The morphology," mudaf ilayhi (second part of possessive) - Translation: "From the Treasures of Arabic Morphology"

Key Grammatical Points: - Mudaf cannot have "al" prefix - Mudaf ilayhi cannot have tanwin - Context determines whether مِن is "min" (from) or "man" (who)

docs.title: Practice Examples
docs.text:
Mudaf Constructions: - Kunuz as-Sarf (treasures of morphology) - Usul al-Fiqh (principles of jurisprudence) - Usul at-Tafsir (principles of exegesis)

Command vs Negation Examples: - Amr (Positive Command): If'al (do it) - Nahi (Prohibition): La taf'al (don't do it) - Nafi (Simple Negation): La ilaha illa Allah (there is no god but Allah)

Typography Notes: - ar-Rahman (الرحمن) - no alif written after ra - ar-Rahim (الرحيم) - alif written after ra due to different vowel pattern

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Analytical Approach: Every word in Arabic must connect meaningfully within phrases and sentences
  2. Context is Crucial: Grammar analysis requires understanding word relationships, not just individual meanings
  3. Pattern Recognition: Understanding morphological patterns (like plural formations) is more important than memorizing individual vocabulary
  4. Three Types of Words: Ism (noun), Fi'l (verb), and Harf (particle)
  5. Question-Based Learning: Students should actively ask questions to deepen understanding
  6. Structural Understanding: Focus on grammatical relationships rather than just translation
  7. Command Classifications: Distinguish between amr (positive command), nahi (negative command), and nafi (simple negation)

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text:
This session focused on understanding the trilateral verb system in Arabic morphology, specifically the 14 verb conjugation patterns (sigha) of the past tense (madhi), and a critical rule governing how verbs interact with their doers (fa'il). The class worked through the distinction between verbs with built-in (attached/muttasil) pronouns versus verbs that can accept an explicit external doer, using multiple examples and student Q&A to clarify common points of confusion.

Key themes: - Why the musarrифун (morphologists) begin their reference tables with the madhi - The 14 patterns of the active past tense (fi'l madhi ma'ruf) - The 14 patterns of the passive past tense (fi'l madhi majhul) - The rule that a verb cannot have two fa'ils simultaneously - When to use entry 1 (fa'ala) vs. entry 4 (fa'alat) with explicit external doers

docs.title: The Six Babs (Categories) of the Trilateral Verb — Middle Letter Haraka Combinations
docs.text:
Madhi Middle Haraka Mudari Middle Haraka Example (Madhi) Example (Mudari) Fatha Dhamma nasara yansuru Fatha Kasra daraba yadribu Fatha Fatha fatha'a yaftha'u Dhamma Dhamma karuma yakrumu Kasra Kasra hasiba yahsibu Kasra Fatha (less common) —

The musarrифун derived these six babs by observing that not all nine theoretical combinations of (fatha/dhamma/kasra) × (fatha/dhamma/kasra) exist in the language — only six unique combinations appear.

docs.title: Active Past Tense — The 14 Sighas (fa'ala as model)
