docs.text: # Sigha Person Number Gender 1 fa'ala 3rd Singular Masculine (Gha'ib) 2 fa'ala (with alif) 3rd Dual Masculine (Gha'ib) 3 fa'alu 3rd Plural Masculine (Gha'ib) 4 fa'alat 3rd Singular Feminine (Gha'ib) 5 fa'alata 3rd Dual Feminine (Gha'ib) 6 fa'alna 3rd Plural Feminine (Gha'ib) 7 fa'alta 2nd Singular Masculine (Hadir/Mukhatab) 8 fa'altuma 2nd Dual Masculine (Hadir) 9 fa'altum 2nd Plural Masculine (Hadir) 10 fa'alti 2nd Singular Feminine (Hadir) 11 fa'altuma 2nd Dual Feminine (Hadir) 12 fa'altunna 2nd Plural Feminine (Hadir) 13 fa'altu 1st Singular Any (Mutakallim) 14 fa'alna 1st Plural Any (Mutakallim)

docs.title: Passive Past Tense — The 14 Sighas (nusira as model)
docs.text:
Formed by placing dhamma on the first letter and kasra on the second letter of the madhi pattern:

  • nasara (active, he helped) → nusira (passive, he was helped)
  • The same 14-pattern structure applies: nusira, nusiru, nusirat, nusirata, nusirna … etc.

Goal: A student should be able to recite all 28 forms (14 active + 14 passive) in one breath for a given root.

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning fa'ala He did (model verb; also: he wrote — kataba is the classic example) kataba He wrote yaktubu He writes / will write saraqa He stole sa'ala He asked nasara He helped nusira He was helped (passive) karuma He was noble/generous hasiba He considered/thought naama (ni'am) To benefit; to be in blessing jahada To strive/struggle qabila To accept / to approach someone mabni Built-in, fixed (from bana-yabni: to build) — refers to patterns that do not change awzan Patterns/weights (sing. wazn) sigha Form/conjugation (pl. sighat) madhi Past tense verb mudari Present/future tense verb ma'ruf Active voice (lit. known) majhul Passive voice (lit. unknown) fa'il The doer; subject of the verb fa'il mustatir Hidden/implicit doer (pronoun not visible) fa'il baariz Explicit/visible doer fa'il muttasil Attached doer (built-in pronoun within the verb form) dhamir Pronoun dhamir muttasil Attached pronoun dhamir munfasil Detached (separate) pronoun dhamir mustatir Hidden/implicit pronoun marfu Nominative case (the case of the fa'il) mansub Accusative case majrur Genitive case gha'ib Absent/third person hadir / mukhatab Present/addressed — second person mutakallim Speaker — first person wahid Singular muthanna Dual jam' Plural mudhakkar Masculine mu'annath Feminine jumla fi'liyya Verbal sentence mudaf Possessive construct (the first noun) mudaf ilayh The second noun in possessive construct sifa Adjective/descriptor mawsuf The noun being described abwab Chapters/categories (sing. bab) tadribat / tamrin Exercises/practice jam' taksir Broken plural muqim Resident (used as analogy for built-in fa'il) musafir Traveler (used as analogy for external fa'il) nisa' Women tullab Students annas / an-nas The people ana I (first person singular pronoun) nahnu We (first person plural pronoun) huwa He (third person masculine singular pronoun) hiya She (third person feminine singular pronoun) nun al-nuswa Nun indicating an all-female group (e.g., fa'alna for 3rd person plural feminine) alif al-ithnayn Alif indicating duality (e.g., fa'ala for 3rd person dual masculine) waw al-jama'a Waw indicating masculine plural (e.g., fa'alu) ta' al-ta'nith Ta indicating feminine (e.g., fa'alat) ta' al-sukun The ta' with sukun that marks certain conjugations — NOT itself a dhamir

docs.title: Why Start with the Madhi?
docs.text:
The musarrифун (Arabic morphologists) always begin conjugation tables with the madhi (past tense) for two reasons: 1. The madhi is mabni (fixed/built) — like a constructed building, its forms do not change based on grammatical role. Once you know it, it stays. 2. The singular 3rd person masculine (gha'ib) is the simplest — it has no extra letters attached (unlike fa'alat which has ta' al-sukun). Starting with the simplest form makes it easier to derive others.

By contrast, the mudari has variations (i'rab — case endings that change), making it more complex to use as a starting reference.

docs.title: The Built-in Fa'il in Each Sigha
docs.text:
Of the 14 past tense sighas, 12 have an explicit, visible (baariz) fa'il attached directly to the verb:

Sigha Built-in Fa'il (Dhamir) Refers to fa'ala (dual) alif al-ithnayn they two (masc.) fa'alu waw al-jama'a they (masc. plural) fa'alata alif al-ithnayn they two (fem.) fa'alna nun al-nuswa they (fem. plural) fa'alta ta' (mutakallim form) you (masc. sing.) fa'altuma ta' + uma you two fa'altum ta' + um you all (masc.) fa'alti ta' + i you (fem. sing.) fa'altuma ta' + uma you two (fem.) fa'altunna ta' + unna you all (fem.) fa'altu tu I (any gender) fa'alna na we (any gender)

Entries 1 and 4 only (fa'ala and fa'alat) have no explicit attached fa'il. Their implicit (mustatir) doers are: - Entry 1 (fa'ala): mustatir = huwa - Entry 4 (fa'alat): mustatir = hiya

docs.title: Dhamir Count: Why There Are 60 Dhameers
docs.text:
The total count of Arabic pronouns (dhamir) = 60, derived as follows:

Type Marfu (Nominative) Mansub (Accusative) Majrur (Genitive) Muttasil (attached) 12 12 12 Munfasil (detached) 12 12 — (no detached majrur dhamir) Subtotal 24 24 12 Total 60

docs.title: The Dhamir "tu" in fa'altu
docs.text:
A student asked why the 13th sigha (first person singular) uses "tu" and not "ni" or "i" (as seen in dhamir muttasil tables).

Answer: The dhamir muttasil table distinguishes by grammatical role: - Marfu muttasil (subject/fa'il attached to verb): the form for "I" is tu — because the fa'il is always in the rafa' case (marfu), and the marfu attached form of "ana" is "tu" - Mansub muttasil (object attached to verb): the form for "me" is ni/ya — because the object is in the nasb case

These are different rows in the dhamir table. "fa'altu" uses "tu" because the dhamir is functioning as the fa'il (marfu), not as an object.

Analogy: In English, "I" (subject) vs. "me" (object) are two different forms of the same pronoun. Similarly in Arabic, "tu" (doer/subject attached) vs. "ni" (object attached) are two different forms.

docs.title: Broken Plural (Jam' Taksir) + Verb Gender Agreement
docs.text: A Quranic example was referenced: even when a broken plural refers to males (e.g., rusul — messengers, all male), Arabic grammar allows treating the broken plural as a feminine singular for verb agreement purposes. This is because grammatical agreement can operate at the word level (rusul is a broken plural, which takes the feminine singular verb) rather than the semantic/referential level (the messengers are all male).

docs.title: The Golden Rule — Illustrated with Examples
docs.text:
Rule: When a verb already has an attached (muttasil) fa'il, it cannot accept any additional external fa'il. The built-in fa'il always takes priority. A single verb cannot have two fa'ils.

Consequence: Whenever you use an explicit external doer (a noun or detached pronoun placed after the verb), you must use either entry 1 (fa'ala) or entry 4 (fa'alat) — the only two forms without a built-in fa'il.

docs.title: Example 1: "The people did" — an-nasu fa'alu
docs.text:
Attempt Form Verdict Reason fa'alu an-nasu fa'alu + an-nasu Wrong fa'alu has built-in waw (they masc.), which is its fa'il. an-nasu cannot be a second fa'il fa'ala an-nasu fa'ala + an-nasu Correct fa'ala has no explicit fa'il; an-nasu (nominative/marfu) is the fa'il fa'alat an-nasu fa'alat + an-nasu Wrong fa'alat implies a singular female doer; an-nasu (people) is masculine or mixed fa'alan nasa fa'ala + an-nasa (accusative) Wrong an-nasa is in the nasb case (accusative) — a fa'il must be marfu (dhamma)

Correct: fa'ala an-nasu (فَعَلَ النَّاسُ)

docs.title: Example 2: "The students did" — fa'ala at-tullaabu
docs.text:
• "The students" is explicit → must use entry 1 or 4
  • Students are masculine (or gender unknown) → use entry 1 (fa'ala)
  • Correct: fa'ala at-tullabu (فَعَلَ الطُّلَّابُ)

docs.title: Example 3: "The women did" — fa'alat an-nisa'u
docs.text:
• "The women" is explicit → must use entry 1 or 4
  • All female → use entry 4 (fa'alat)
  • Correct: fa'alat an-nisa'u (فَعَلَتِ النِّسَاءُ)

docs.title: Example 4: "Fatima did it" — fa'alat fatimatu
docs.text:
• Fatima is explicit → must use entry 1 or 4
  • Fatima is female → use entry 4 (fa'alat)
  • Correct: fa'alat fatimatu (فَعَلَتْ فَاطِمَةُ)

docs.title: Example 5: "The car did it" — fa'alat as-sayyaratu
docs.text:
• As-sayyara (car) is feminine in Arabic (ends in ta' marbuTa)
  • Explicit doer → must use entry 1 or 4
  • Female word → use entry 4 (fa'alat)
  • Correct: fa'alat as-sayyaratu

docs.title: Example 6: When the doer is a dhamir (built-in)
docs.text:
• "They (two males) did it" → fa'ala — the alif IS the fa'il, no additional word needed
  • "They (plural males) did it" → fa'alu — the waw IS the fa'il
  • "We did it" → fa'alna — the na IS the fa'il
  • "I did it" → fa'altu — the tu IS the fa'il

In all these cases, no external noun or pronoun is added because the verb form itself already encodes the fa'il.

docs.title: Active vs. Passive — Oral Fluency Goal
docs.text:
Students should practice reciting all 28 forms (14 active + 14 passive) for a given root in one breath. Example for nasara/nusira:

Active: nasara, nasara (dual), nasaru, nasarat, nasarata, nasarna, nasarta, nasartuma, nasartum, nasarti, nasartuma, nasartunna, nasartu, nasarna

Passive: nusira, nusiru, nusirat, nusirata, nusirna … (same 14-pattern structure with dhamma on first letter and kasra on second)

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Why start with madhi: The past tense is mabni (fixed) — it does not change based on grammatical role. This makes it the most stable reference point, like a building's foundation. The mudari changes with i'rab, so it is less ideal as a starting reference.

  2. The 14 sighas of the madhi: The verb system has 14 patterns covering all combinations of person (3rd/2nd/1st), number (singular/dual/plural), and gender (masculine/feminine). First person (mutakallim) has no gender distinction — only 2 forms.

  3. 12 sighas have explicit built-in fa'ils; 2 do not: Only entries 1 (fa'ala, huwa implicit) and 4 (fa'alat, hiya implicit) lack an attached doer. All other 12 carry their fa'il visibly within the word.

  4. The cardinal rule — one verb, one fa'il: A verb cannot simultaneously hold a built-in fa'il and an external fa'il. The built-in fa'il always takes priority and rejects all external candidates. Violating this rule produces grammatically corrupt (and communicatively meaningless) Arabic.

  5. Choosing entry 1 vs. 4 with explicit doers: When you have any explicit external doer — singular, dual, or plural — always use entry 1 (fa'ala) for masculine/mixed and entry 4 (fa'alat) for exclusively feminine. The number of the doer does NOT affect this choice; only the gender does.

  6. Dhamir "tu" vs. "ni/ya": The attached dhamir for "I" when acting as fa'il (marfu, subject) is "tu." The attached dhamir for "me" when acting as an object (mansub) is "ni/ya." These come from different rows of the dhamir table and must not be confused.

  7. Passive formation rule: To form the passive (majhul) of any madhi verb, place dhamma on the first letter and kasra on the second letter. The same 14 sigha structure then applies.

  8. Broken plural (jam' taksir) grammar: Broken plurals can take feminine singular verb forms in Arabic grammar — this is a word-level agreement (the form of the word), not semantic agreement (the meaning of the word). This appears in Quranic Arabic (e.g., with rusul).

  9. Grammar serves communication: Grammar rules exist not as arbitrary restrictions but to prevent miscommunication. If a verb could take two fa'ils simultaneously, the sentence would be ambiguous and the communication would break down. The rule enforces clarity.

  10. Study method reminder: Mastery requires revisiting recordings, asking questions in the group, and repeated practice. The goal is to reach a level where verb selection and fa'il identification become immediate and automatic — not effortful recall.

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session focused on the passive voice (Majhul) forms of past tense verbs in Arabic morphology, including how to form the passive from the active (Ma'ruf), how to negate verbs using ما and لا, and an extensive practical exercise parsing Arabic sentences. The teacher led a detailed grammatical analysis of ten practice sentences, covering Jumlatul Ismiyah vs. Jumlatul Fi'liyah identification, Mubtada-Khabar structures, and the critical rule that when the Khabar is a full sentence it must contain a pronoun (Dhamir) referring back to the Mubtada.

docs.title: Active (Ma'ruf) → Passive (Majhul) — Madi Form
docs.text:
The conversion rules for the past tense verb from active to passive:

Rule Detail First letter Always takes a Dhamma (ضَمَّة) in Majhul Middle (second root) letter Always takes a Kasra (كَسْرَة) in Majhul Last letter Unchanged — keeps the same Haraka as the active form

docs.title: Conversion Examples
docs.text:
Active (Ma'ruf) Passive (Majhul) Meaning (Active → Passive) نَصَرَ (nasara) نُصِرَ (nusira) He helped → He was helped سَمِعَ (sami'a) سُمِعَ (sumi'a) He heard → He was heard كَرُمَ (karuma) كُرِمَ (kurima) He was noble → He was honored

Note: Regardless of whether the active form has Fatha, Kasra, or Dhamma on the middle letter, the Majhul form always standardizes to Dhamma on first letter + Kasra on middle letter.

docs.title: Negation Patterns (Manfi)
docs.text:
Particle Used With Condition ما (ma) Madi (past tense) Can be used freely before the verb لا (la) Madi (past tense) Must be repeated (cannot appear only once)

Examples: - نَصَرَ → مَا نَصَرَ (He did not help) - نُصِرَ → مَا نُصِرَ (He was not helped)

docs.title: Vocabulary Multiplication Insight
docs.text: Knowing one root pattern multiplies vocabulary systematically: - 1 active verb pattern × 14 conjugations = 14 forms - + passive (Majhul) = 28 forms - + negation with ما = 28 more → 56 forms - + negation with لا = even more

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning مَعْرُوف (Ma'ruf) Known / Active voice verb مَجْهُول (Majhul) Unknown / Passive voice verb مُثْبَت (Muthabbat) Affirmative (positive) statement مَنْفِي (Manfi) Negative statement فِعْل (Fi'l) Verb فَاعِل (Fa'il) Doer / Subject (of active verb) نَائِب فَاعِل (Na'ib Fa'il) Deputy doer / Subject of passive verb ضَمِير مُسْتَتِر (Dhamir Mustatir) Hidden/implied pronoun ضَمِير مُتَّصِل (Dhamir Muttasil) Attached pronoun ضَمِير مُنْفَصِل (Dhamir Munfasil) Detached/independent pronoun جُمْلَة اسْمِيَّة (Jumlatul Ismiyah) Nominal sentence جُمْلَة فِعْلِيَّة (Jumlatul Fi'liyah) Verbal sentence مُبْتَدَأ (Mubtada) Subject of nominal sentence خَبَر (Khabar) Predicate of nominal sentence مَفْعُول بِه (Maf'ul bihi) Direct object مُتَعَلِّق (Mutaliq) A Jar-Majroor phrase connected/attached to a verb إِضَافَة (Idhafa) Possessive/genitive construction مُضَاف (Mudaf) First word in Idhafa construction مُضَاف إِلَيْه (Mudaf ilayh) Second word in Idhafa construction طَالِب (Talib) Student (lit. one who seeks) طَلَبَ (talaba) To seek, to request, to call upon مَرْفُوع (Marfu) Nominative case (Dhamma) مَجْرُور (Majrur) Genitive case (Kasra) مَنْصُوب (Mansub) Accusative case (Fatha) حَرْف نَفْي (Harf Nafi) Negating particle حَرْف جَرّ (Harf Jarr) Preposition particle

docs.title: Madi Majhul — Full Pattern
docs.text:
The Majhul Madi follows the same conjugation slots as the Ma'ruf Madi. Only the voweling changes (Dhamma on first letter, Kasra on middle letter). Example root: ن-ص-ر

Pronoun Ma'ruf (Active) Majhul (Passive) هُوَ نَصَرَ نُصِرَ هِيَ نَصَرَتْ نُصِرَتْ هُمَا (m) نَصَرَا نُصِرَا هُمَا (f) نَصَرَتَا نُصِرَتَا هُمْ نَصَرُوا نُصِرُوا هُنَّ نَصَرْنَ نُصِرْنَ أَنْتَ نَصَرْتَ نُصِرْتَ أَنْتِ نَصَرْتِ نُصِرْتِ أَنْتُمَا نَصَرْتُمَا نُصِرْتُمَا أَنْتُمْ نَصَرْتُمْ نُصِرْتُمْ أَنْتُنَّ نَصَرْتُنَّ نُصِرْتُنَّ أَنَا نَصَرْتُ نُصِرْتُ نَحْنُ نَصَرْنَا نُصِرْنَا

The last letter retains its Haraka from the Ma'ruf pattern. Only first and middle letters change.

docs.title: Na'ib Fa'il — The Deputy Doer
docs.text:
• In a Ma'ruf (active) verb sentence, the verb requires a Fa'il (doer). If not explicitly stated, it is a hidden pronoun (Dhamir Mustatir).
  • In a Majhul (passive) verb sentence, the actual doer is unknown or omitted. The grammatical subject position is filled by the Na'ib Fa'il (deputy doer).
  • The Na'ib Fa'il is always Marfu (nominative), just like a Fa'il.
  • The Na'ib Fa'il is typically what was the Maf'ul Bihi in the active sentence — i.e., the receiver of the action is promoted to the grammatical subject position, but it is still semantically the receiver.

Example: - Active: هُوَ قَرَأَ الْقُرْآنَ → He read the Quran (الْقُرْآنَ = Maf'ul bihi, Mansub) - Passive: قُرِئَ الْقُرْآنُ → The Quran was read (الْقُرْآنُ = Na'ib Fa'il, Marfu — note Dhamma)

docs.title: Critical Rule: Khabar as a Sentence
docs.text:
When the Khabar of a Jumlatul Ismiyah is itself a full sentence (Jumlatul Fi'liyah or Jumlatul Ismiyah), that sentence must contain a Dhamir (pronoun) that refers back to the Mubtada. Without this back-reference pronoun, the sentence is grammatically broken.

Example from class: - هُوَ قَرَأَ الْقُرْآنَ - هُوَ = Mubtada - قَرَأَ الْقُرْآنَ = Khabar (Jumlatul Fi'liyah) - The hidden Fa'il (هُوَ) inside قَرَأَ is the back-reference pronoun → valid - If you wrote هُوَ قَرَأَتْ الْقُرْآنَ → the Ta refers to a female (هِيَ), not هُوَ → invalid

docs.title: Why هُوَ قَرَأَ and Not Just قَرَأَ?
docs.text:
• قَرَأَ الْقُرْآنَ alone is a valid complete sentence (Jumlatul Fi'liyah): "He read the Quran."
  • Adding هُوَ at the start (making it Jumlatul Ismiyah) adds emphasis and specificity: it was specifically he — no one else — who did the reading. The Mubtada asserts exclusivity or confirmation.

docs.title: Sentence Parsing Exercises (from class)
docs.text:
Sentence 1: هُوَ قَرَأَ الْقُرْآنَ - Type: Jumlatul Ismiyah (starts with detached pronoun هُوَ) - هُوَ = Mubtada (Marfu, detached pronoun) - قَرَأَ الْقُرْآنَ = Khabar (Jumlatul Fi'liyah) - قَرَأَ = Fi'l Madi, Mabni - Fa'il = Dhamir Mustatir (هُوَ) — back-reference to Mubtada ✓ - الْقُرْآنَ = Maf'ul Bihi (Mansub)

Sentence 2: قُرِئَ الْقُرْآنُ (Majhul) - Type: Jumlatul Fi'liyah (starts with verb) - قُرِئَ = Fi'l Madi Majhul - الْقُرْآنُ = Na'ib Fa'il (Marfu — note Dhamma on Nun, vs. Fatha in sentence 1)

Sentence 3: هُمَا كَتَبَتَا مَكْتُوبَيْن - Type: Jumlatul Ismiyah - هُمَا = Mubtada - كَتَبَتَا مَكْتُوبَيْن = Khabar (Jumlatul Fi'liyah) - كَتَبَتَا = Fi'l + Fa'il built-in (Alif of duality = Na'ib reference back to هُمَا) ✓ - مَكْتُوبَيْن = Maf'ul Bihi (Mansub — duality in Mansub/Majrur form, hence يْن not ان) - Why كَتَبَتَا and not كَتَبَ? Because هُمَا (two females) governs the verb; the Alif duality ending refers back to the Mubtada هُمَا.

Sentence 4: اَلْبِنْتَانِ طُلِبَتَا - Type: Jumlatul Ismiyah - اَلْبِنْتَانِ = Mubtada (definite noun, Marfu — duality form with ان) - طُلِبَتَا = Khabar (Jumlatul Fi'liyah — Fi'l Majhul) - طُلِبَتَا = Fi'l Majhul; Alif duality = Na'ib Fa'il (refers back to البنتان) ✓ - Translation: "The two daughters were sought/called." - Why طُلِبَتَا and not طُلِبَتْ? The duality Alif is the Na'ib Fa'il and must match البنتان.

Sentence 5: أَنْتَ أَكَلْتَ التُّفَّاحَةَ - Type: Jumlatul Ismiyah (starts with detached pronoun أَنْتَ) - أَنْتَ = Mubtada - أَكَلْتَ التُّفَّاحَةَ = Khabar (Jumlatul Fi'liyah) - أَكَلْتَ = Fi'l + attached Ta (Dhamir Muttasil, Marfu, Fa'il — refers back to أَنْتَ) ✓ - التُّفَّاحَةَ = Maf'ul Bihi (Mansub) - Note: تَ in أَكَلْتَ is the attached pronoun for أَنْتَ and is always Marfu as the Fa'il.

Sentence 6: أَنْتُمْ بُعِثْتُمْ إِلَى مَكَّةَ - Type: Jumlatul Ismiyah - أَنْتُمْ = Mubtada - بُعِثْتُمْ إِلَى مَكَّةَ = Khabar (Jumlatul Fi'liyah) - بُعِثْتُمْ = Fi'l Majhul; تُمْ = Na'ib Fa'il (Dhamir Muttasil, Marfu — refers back to أَنْتُمْ) ✓ - إِلَى مَكَّةَ = Harf Jarr + Majrur (Jar-Majrur phrase); Mutaliq (connected) to بُعِثَ - Translation: "You all were sent to Mecca." (The sentence-initial أَنْتُمْ implies: you — specifically, no choice — must go)

Sentence 7: أَنْتِ طَلَبْتِ الْعِلْمَ - Type: Jumlatul Ismiyah - أَنْتِ = Mubtada - طَلَبْتِ الْعِلْمَ = Khabar (Jumlatul Fi'liyah) - طَلَبْتِ = Fi'l + تِ (Dhamir Muttasil, Marfu, Fa'il — refers back to أَنْتِ) ✓ - الْعِلْمَ = Maf'ul Bihi (Mansub) - Any conjugation other than طَلَبْتِ would break the back-reference to أَنْتِ.

Sentence 8: أَنْتُنَّ بُعِثْتُنَّ إِلَى الْمُسْتَوَى - Type: Jumlatul Ismiyah - أَنْتُنَّ = Mubtada - بُعِثْتُنَّ إِلَى الْمُسْتَوَى = Khabar (Jumlatul Fi'liyah) - بُعِثْتُنَّ = Fi'l Majhul; نَّ = Na'ib Fa'il (Dhamir Muttasil, Marfu — refers back to أَنْتُنَّ) ✓ - إِلَى الْمُسْتَوَى = Harf Jarr + Majrur; Mutaliq to بُعِثَ

Sentence 9: نَحْنُ شَرِبْنَا لَبَنًا - Type: Jumlatul Ismiyah - نَحْنُ = Mubtada - شَرِبْنَا لَبَنًا = Khabar (Jumlatul Fi'liyah) - شَرِبْنَا = Fi'l + نَا (Dhamir Muttasil, Marfu, Fa'il — refers back to نَحْنُ) ✓ - لَبَنًا = Maf'ul Bihi (Mansub, indefinite)

Sentence 10: مَا فَهِمْنَا كَلَامَكَ - Type: Jumlatul Fi'liyah (negated verbal sentence) - مَا = Harf Nafi (negating particle) — does not determine sentence type; look at what follows - فَهِمْنَا = Fi'l Madi (negated by ما); نَا = Fa'il (Dhamir Muttasil, Marfu) - كَلَامَكَ = Maf'ul Bihi (Mansub) - Translation: "We did not understand your speech." - Note: ما here is one of its 10+ uses; context determines which use applies.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Majhul formation is mechanical: Dhamma on first letter + Kasra on middle letter, last letter unchanged. Apply this to any Ma'ruf verb to get its Majhul.

  2. Na'ib Fa'il is not a Fa'il: It is the receiver of the action, not the doer — but it fills the grammatical doer position and is always Marfu. This distinction matters for meaning and parsing.

  3. Every detached pronoun (Dhamir Munfasil) beginning a sentence = Jumlatul Ismiyah, and that pronoun is always the Mubtada. No analysis needed — it's definitive. Examples: هُوَ، هِيَ، هُمَا، أَنْتَ، أَنْتِ، أَنْتُمْ، نَحْنُ etc.

  4. The Mubtada is the "king": Once the Mubtada is established, everything in the sentence — especially the Khabar — must comply with it in terms of number, gender, and pronoun back-reference.

  5. Critical rule — Khabar as a sentence: If the Khabar is a Jumlatul Fi'liyah (or Jumlatul Ismiyah), it must contain a pronoun that refers back to the Mubtada. The verb ending (attached pronoun as Fa'il or Na'ib Fa'il) serves as this back-reference. Using the wrong conjugation breaks this link and invalidates the sentence.

  6. Sentence type is determined by what comes after a particle: Particles like ما and لا do not themselves indicate whether a sentence is nominal or verbal — look at the word that follows the particle.

  7. Jar-Majrur phrases (حرف جرّ + مجرور) are Mutaliq: They must attach to a verb or verb-like entity. They are not standalone sentence components.

  8. Maf'ul Bihi takes Mansub; Na'ib Fa'il takes Marfu: This is the key distinction seen in الْقُرْآنَ (Maf'ul, active sentence) vs. الْقُرْآنُ (Na'ib Fa'il, passive sentence).

  9. Vocabulary compounds rapidly: One root × patterns × passive × negation = dozens of derivable forms. Mastering structure, not memorization, is the goal.

  10. Serious study outside class is essential: Class sessions are primers. Solid Arabic acquisition requires dedicated personal study time beyond scheduled classes.

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session focused on the Fi'l Mudari' Majhul (Imperfect Passive Voice), reviewing how to form the passive from the active imperfect, and applying it to sentence parsing and translation exercises. The teacher connected Sarf concepts heavily with Nahw (grammar), emphasizing that the two disciplines are inseparable. The session also briefly introduced the Huruf Nasb (particles that render nasb to the imperfect verb) and previewed the upcoming topic of Huruf Jazm.

docs.title: Fi'l Mudari' Majhul (Imperfect Passive Voice)
docs.text:
Two rules to form the Mudari' Majhul from the Mudari' Ma'ruf:

  1. Prefix letter → give it a Dhamma (ضمة)
  2. Original middle letter of the root → give it a Fatha (فتحة)
Ma'ruf (Active) Majhul (Passive) Notes يَنْصُرُ (yanṣuru) يُنْصَرُ (yunṣaru) Prefix ya gets ḍamma; middle ṣad gets fatḥa يَسْمَعُ (yasma'u) يُسْمَعُ (yusma'u) Fatḥa already on middle letter; only change prefix يَشْرَبُ (yashrabu) يُشْرَبُ (yushrabu) Prefix ya gets ḍamma; middle rā gets fatḥa يُرْسَلُ (yursalu) يُرْسَلُ (yursalu) Advanced pattern (Akrama/Ukrimu bāb); second-to-last letter gets fatḥa

Key principle: If the middle letter already has a fatḥa in the Ma'ruf, only change the prefix to ḍamma — no second change needed.

docs.title: Huruf Nasb (Particles Rendering Nasb to the Mudari')
docs.text:
The particles أنْ، لنْ، كَيْ، إذَن (an, lan, kay, idhan) render nasb to the following Fi'l Mudari'. The sign of nasb is:

Form Ma'ruf After Huruf Nasb (Nasb) Change Singular (مفرد) يَفْعَلُ يَفْعَلَ Dhamma → Fatḥa on last letter Dual (مثنى) يَفْعَلَانِ يَفْعَلَا Nūn dropped Masc. Plural (جمع مذكر سالم) يَفْعَلُونَ يَفْعَلُوا Nūn dropped; alif added for clarity Fem. Plural (جمع مؤنث سالم) يَفْعَلْنَ يَفْعَلْنَ No change — nūn is not dropped

Note: The nūns of the two female plurality forms (Ghā'ib and Ḥāḍir) are not dropped because dropping them would cause confusion with other patterns. This brings the total to all 14 conjugation forms.

لَنْ (lan): Turns the positive imperfect into a negative future — "will never / shall not." E.g., لَنْ يَفْعَلَ (lan yaf'ala) = "he will never do."

docs.title: Huruf Jazm (Preview — Next Class)
docs.text: Particles that give jazm (sukūn) to the Fi'l Mudari'. E.g., يَفْعَلُ → يَفْعَلْ. Will be covered in the next session.

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning الفعل المضارع المجهول Imperfect passive voice verb الفعل المضارع المعروف Imperfect active voice verb الفاعل (fā'il) The doer / subject (active voice) نائب الفاعل (nā'ib al-fā'il) Deputy doer / subject (passive voice) حرف نافٍ (ḥarf nāfi) Negating particle جملة فعلية (jumla fi'liyya) Verbal sentence جملة اسمية (jumla ismiyya) Nominal sentence مبتدأ (mubtada') Subject of a nominal sentence خبر (khabar) Predicate of a nominal sentence مركّب (murakkab) Phrase / compound صفة (ṣifa) Adjective / descriptor موصوف (mawṣūf) The noun being described مجرور (majrūr) Noun in genitive state (with kasra) حرف جر (ḥarf jarr) Preposition ظرف زمان (ẓarf zamān) Adverb of time مفعول فيه (maf'ūl fīhi) Adverbial of time or place مفعول به (maf'ūl bihi) Direct object متعلق (muta'alliq) Jar-majrūr phrase connected to the verb منفصل (munfaṣil) Detached pronoun ضمير (ḍamīr) Pronoun مرفوع (marfū') Nominative state (ḍamma) منصوب (manṣūb) Accusative state (fatḥa) مجزوم (majzūm) Jussive state (sukūn) عسكري ('askarī) Soldier / military محاربة (muḥāraba) Battle / war حروف الناصبة Particles that give nasb to the mudari' حروف الجازمة Particles that give jazm to the mudari' مُؤَنَّث (mu'annath) Feminine gender مُذَكَّر (mudhakkar) Masculine gender غائب (ghā'ib) Third person (absent) حاضر (ḥāḍir) Second person (present/addressed)

docs.title: Forming Majhul from Ma'ruf — Summary Rule
docs.text: Prefix → ḍamma | Original middle root letter → fatḥa

docs.title: Example: نَصَرَ — يَنْصُرُ → يُنْصَرُ
docs.text: Person Ma'ruf (Active) Majhul (Passive) هو (3rd m. sg.) يَنْصُرُ يُنْصَرُ هي (3rd f. sg.) تَنْصُرُ تُنْصَرُ

docs.title: Example: يُسْأَلُ (from سَأَلَ)
docs.text: Used in the Quranic context: لَا يُسْأَلُ (he will not be asked) — passive voice, with لا as a negating particle (ḥarf nāfi).

docs.title: Huruf Nasb — Effect on 14 Conjugation Forms
docs.text: # Description Before (Ma'ruf) After أنْ/لنْ/كي 1 3rd m. sg. يَفْعَلُ يَفْعَلَ 2–3 3rd m. dual يَفْعَلَانِ يَفْعَلَا (nūn dropped) 4–6 3rd m. plural يَفْعَلُونَ يَفْعَلُوا (nūn dropped) 7 3rd f. sg. تَفْعَلُ تَفْعَلَ 8–9 3rd f. dual تَفْعَلَانِ تَفْعَلَا (nūn dropped) 10 3rd f. plural يَفْعَلْنَ يَفْعَلْنَ (unchanged) 11 2nd m. sg. تَفْعَلُ تَفْعَلَ 12–13 2nd m. dual/plural تَفْعَلُونَ تَفْعَلُوا (nūn dropped) 14 2nd f. plural تَفْعَلْنَ تَفْعَلْنَ (unchanged)

docs.title: يُسْأَلُ
docs.text:
• Root: س-أ-ل
  • Bāb: نَصَرَ pattern
  • Mudari' Ma'ruf: يَسْأَلُ
  • Mudari' Majhul: يُسْأَلُ
  • Used in: لَا يُسْأَلُ عَمَّا يَفْعَلُ وَهُمْ يُسْأَلُونَ (Quranic reference)

docs.title: يُشْرَبُ
docs.text:
• Root: ش-ر-ب
  • Bāb: نَصَرَ pattern
  • Mudari' Ma'ruf: يَشْرَبُ
  • Mudari' Majhul: يُشْرَبُ (prefix ḍamma, rā gets fatḥa)

docs.title: يُؤْكَلُ / يُقْتَلُ / يُفْهَمُ / يُتْرَكُ / يُرْسَلُ / يُهْزَمُ / يُفْتَحُ / يُقْرَأُ
docs.text: All follow the same two-change rule (prefix ḍamma + middle root letter fatḥa).

docs.title: يُرْسَلُ (from أَرْسَلَ)
docs.text:
• Root: ر-س-ل — but verb is from the Akrama/Ukrimu bāb (Form IV — not yet formally studied)
  • Mudari' Ma'ruf: يُرْسِلُ (sin has kasra)
  • Mudari' Majhul: يُرْسَلُ (sin gets fatḥa — second-to-last letter rule still applies)

docs.title: Arabic → English Parsing (from class)
docs.text: Arabic Sentence Translation Key Parsing Points لَا يُسْأَلُ عَمَّا يَفْعَلُ He will not be asked about what He does لا = ḥarf nāfi; يُسْأَلُ = fi'l mudari' majhūl وَهُمْ يُسْأَلُونَ While they will be asked وَ = ḥarf 'aṭf; هُمْ = mubtada'; يُسْأَلُونَ = fi'l mudari' majhūl (khabar jumla fi'liyya) يُشْرَبُ اللَّبَنُ The milk is being drunk يُشْرَبُ = majhūl; اللَّبَنُ = nā'ib fā'il (marfū') يُؤْكَلُ الْيَوْمَ السَّمَكُ وَالْأُرُزُّ Today, fish and rice are being eaten الْيَوْمَ = ẓarf zamān/maf'ūl fīhi; السَّمَكُ، الْأُرُزُّ = nā'ib fā'il يُقْتَلُ الْعَسْكَرِيُّ فِي الْمُحَارَبَةِ The soldier is killed in battle فِي الْمُحَارَبَةِ = jar-majrūr (muta'alliq) هَلْ يُفْهَمُ اللِّسَانُ الْهِنْدِيُّ فِي مَكَّةَ؟ Is Hindi understood in Makkah? هَلْ = ḥarf istifhām; اللِّسَانُ الْهِنْدِيُّ = nā'ib fā'il (ṣifa-mawṣūf); مَكَّةَ = majrūr but shows with fatḥa (ممنوع من الصرف)

docs.title: English → Arabic Translation Exercises
docs.text:
English Arabic Notes The door of the school is being opened يُفْتَحُ الْبَابُ الْبَابُ = nā'ib fā'il, must be marfū' (ḍamma) He is being sent to Medina يُرْسَلُ إِلَى الْمَدِينَةِ From Akrama bāb; second-to-last letter gets fatḥa The disbelievers will be defeated يُهْزَمُ الْكَافِرُونَ Verb stays singular even though nā'ib fā'il is plural (verb-first rule) The car will be left on the road تُتْرَكُ السَّيَّارَةُ عَلَى الطَّرِيقِ Verb is tā' (4th form) because سَيَّارَة is feminine (mu'annath); third person, not second The book will be read today يُقْرَأُ الْكِتَابُ الْيَوْمَ الْيَوْمَ = ẓarf zamān

Critical Nahw rule applied: When the nā'ib fā'il (or fā'il) comes after the verb, the verb is always singular — it does not take a plural form. The plural of the noun is shown on the noun itself, not on the verb.

Gender rule: When the nā'ib fā'il is feminine, use the 4th conjugation form (tā' prefix) for the verb — e.g., تُتْرَكُ for سَيَّارَة.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Two-change rule for Mudari' Majhul: Prefix → ḍamma; original middle root letter → fatḥa. If the fatḥa is already there, only change the prefix.

  2. Haraka changes are critical: A single vowel change can flip an active verb to passive (or vice versa), completely changing meaning — or produce gibberish. This makes precision in tajwīd and Arabic reading essential.

  3. Nā'ib Fā'il is always marfū': In a passive sentence, the deputy doer takes the place of the fā'il and must carry a ḍamma (or equivalent).

  4. Verb stays singular before an explicit nā'ib fā'il: Even if the nā'ib fā'il is plural or dual, the verb preceding it remains in the singular form.

  5. Huruf Nasb (أن، لن، كي، إذن): These particles govern the following mudari' verb, rendering it manṣūb. Sign of nasb: ḍamma → fatḥa on last letter; or deletion of the nūn (for dual and sound masculine plural). The two feminine plural forms do not lose their nūn.

  6. لَنْ specifically negates the imperfect and pushes the action into the future with certainty: "will never / shall not."

  7. Sarf and Nahw are inseparable: Sarf produces the word pattern; Nahw governs its role in the sentence. Neither discipline is complete without the other.

  8. Makkah is ghayr munsarif: مَكَّة does not take tanwīn and shows kasra-state with a fatḥa — a foundational Nahw concept applied in these exercises.

  9. Coming next: Huruf Jazm — particles that give sukūn (jazm) to the mudari' verb.

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text:
This session covered two major interconnected topics:

  1. The nature of Alif and Hamzatul Wasl — A foundational linguistic discussion clarifying why the "Alif" seen at the beginning of words in Arabic is not a true Alif but rather a Hamzatul Wasl (helping Hamza). This included a deep dive into Alif's unique characteristics as a letter, its relationship to Tajweed, and practical examples from Quranic recitation.

  2. Huruf Nasiba (حروف الناصبة) — The particles that render nasb (accusative case) on the Fi'l Mudari' (present/imperfect verb), including how they affect the five variable patterns of the 14-entry conjugation table.

The session also briefly introduced Huruf Jazima (حروف الجازمة) — the particles that render jazm on the Fi'l Mudari' — as a preview for the next class.

docs.title: The Five Patterns Affected by Huruf Nasiba
docs.text:
Out of the 14 patterns (entries) in the full conjugation table, the Huruf Nasiba affect five specific pattern-types:

# Pattern Notes 1 يَفْعَلُوا (Yaf'alū) Noon of masculine plurality drops 2 يَفْعَلَانِ (Yaf'alāni) Noon of duality drops; grammatical Alif added with sukun 3 تَفْعَلُوا (Taf'alū) Noon of masculine plurality drops 4 تَفْعَلَانِ (Taf'alāni) Noon of duality drops 5 تَفْعَلِينَ (Taf'alīna) Noon of femininity — does NOT drop (would create ambiguity with other patterns)

The general rule for patterns not in the above five: wherever there is a Dhamma at the end of the Fi'l Mudari', it changes to a Fatha as the sign of Nasb (e.g., يَنْصُرُ → يَنْصُرَ).

The general rule for the five patterns: the Noon of I'rab (نون الإعراب) drops. The exception is the Noon of femininity (نون التأنيث), which does not drop — its Nasb state is understood from context.

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning الأَلِف (Al-Alif) The letter Alif; unique in Arabic — cannot take direct Haraka الهَمْزَة (Al-Hamza) The glottal stop; a full letter that can take Haraka هَمْزَةُ الوَصْل (Hamzatul Wasl) "Connecting/Helping Hamza" — the pseudo-Alif written at the start of words to allow pronunciation of an initial Sukun letter هَمْزَةُ القَطْع (Hamzatul Qat') "Cutting Hamza" — a real Hamza that is never dropped المَدّ (Al-Madd) Prolongation of a vowel sound مَدّ طَبِيعِي (Madd Tabi'i) Natural/original prolongation مُتَحَرِّك (Mutaharrik) A letter that carries a Haraka (vowel); opposite of Sakin السُّكُون (Al-Sukun) Absence of a vowel on a letter الفِعْل المُضَارِع (Al-Fi'l Al-Mudari') The present/imperfect verb حُرُوفُ النَّاصِبَة (Huruf An-Nasiba) Particles that render Nasb (accusative) on the Mudari' verb حُرُوفُ الجَازِمَة (Huruf Al-Jazima) Particles that render Jazm (jussive) on the Mudari' verb النَّصْب (An-Nasb) Accusative/subjunctive grammatical state; sign is Fatha الجَزْم (Al-Jazm) Jussive grammatical state; sign is Sukun الجَرّ (Al-Jarr) Genitive state in nouns; sign is Kasra مَجْزُوم (Majzum) A verb rendered into Jazm state مَنْصُوب (Mansub) A word in the Nasb state مَجْرُور (Majrur) A noun in the Jarr state نُونُ الإِعْرَاب (Nunu al-I'rab) The Noon at the end of certain Mudari' patterns that drops with Nasib/Jazim particles نُونُ التَّثْنِيَة (Nunu at-Tathniya) Noon of duality نُونُ جَمْعِ المُذَكَّر (Nunu Jam' al-Mudhakkar) Noon of masculine plural نُونُ المُؤَنَّث (Nunu al-Mu'annath) Noon of femininity — never drops المَجْهُول (Al-Majhul) Passive voice المَعْرُوف (Al-Ma'ruf) Active voice المُرَكَّب (Al-Murakkab) Compound word / phrase (less than a full sentence) المُضَاف (Al-Mudaf) The first term of a genitive construction (possessor construct) المُضَافُ إِلَيْه (Al-Mudaf Ilayh) The second term of a genitive construction; takes Kasra الجُمْلَةُ الفِعْلِيَّة (Al-Jumla Al-Fi'liyya) Verbal sentence — must begin with a verb (Fi'l) الجُمْلَةُ الاسْمِيَّة (Al-Jumla Al-Ismiyya) Nominal sentence — begins with a noun الفِعْل (Al-Fi'l) Verb — mandatory first element of a Jumla Fi'liyya الفَاعِل (Al-Fa'il) Subject/doer — mandatory second element of a Jumla Fi'liyya إِيَّا (Iyyā) A detached pronoun base used exclusively with attached pronouns (e.g., إِيَّاكَ); particle of exclusivity (ikhtiṣāṣ) الاخْتِصَاص (Al-Ikhtiṣaṣ) Exclusivity — grammatical emphasis (e.g., إِيَّاكَ = "You alone") التَّقْدِيم (At-Taqdim) Fronting a grammatical element for emphasis لاَمُ الكَلِمَة (Lamu al-Kalima) The last root letter of a word عَيْنُ الكَلِمَة ('Aynu al-Kalima) The middle root letter of a word

docs.title: Passive Voice (Majhul) of Fi'l Mudari' — Rule & Examples
docs.text:
Rule for forming Majhul from Ma'ruf: 1. The sign of Mudari' (one of: ي، ت، أ، ن) receives a Dhamma 2. The original middle letter (Ayn al-Kalima) receives a Fatha 3. Everything else remains unchanged

Ma'ruf (Active) Majhul (Passive) Root Meaning (Passive) يَنْصُرُ يُنْصَرُ ن-ص-ر He is helped/given victory يَسْمَعُ يُسْمَعُ س-م-ع He is heard (Fatha already present — no change to middle) يَضْرِبُ يُضْرَبُ ض-ر-ب He is struck يَرْجِعُ يُرْجَعُ ر-ج-ع He is returned يُرْزَقُ يُرْزَقُ ر-ز-ق He is provided with sustenance يَشْكُرُ يُشْكَرُ ش-ك-ر He is thanked يَهْزِمُ يُهْزَمُ ه-ز-م He is defeated/overpowered

docs.title: Effect of Huruf Nasiba on Mudari' Conjugation
docs.text:
Before (Ma'ruf / Marfu'):

Pattern Form 3rd masc. sing. يَفْعَلُ 3rd masc. dual يَفْعَلَانِ 3rd masc. plural يَفْعَلُونَ

After Huruf Nasiba (e.g., أَنْ):

Pattern Form Change 3rd masc. sing. يَفْعَلَ Dhamma → Fatha 3rd masc. dual يَفْعَلَا Noon drops; grammatical Alif with Sukun added 3rd masc. plural يَفْعَلُوا Noon drops 2nd fem. sing. تَفْعَلِي Noon drops 2nd fem. plural تَفْعَلْنَ Noon of femininity does NOT drop; state known from context

docs.title: Effect of Huruf Jazima on Mudari' Conjugation
docs.text:
Rule: The last letter of the Mudari' receives Sukun (Jazm). The same five patterns as Nasiba apply — Noon of I'rab drops; Noon of femininity does not drop.

Pattern Before (Marfu') After Jazima (e.g., لَمْ) يَفْعَلُ يَفْعَلُ يَفْعَلْ تَفْعَلُ تَفْعَلُ تَفْعَلْ يَفْعَلُونَ يَفْعَلُونَ يَفْعَلُوا (Noon drops)

docs.title: Why Alif Cannot Begin a Word — Linguistic Root
docs.text:
• Alif is unique among Arabic letters: it cannot take a direct Haraka (Fatha, Kasra, or Dhamma)
  • Because it has no Haraka, it produces no independent sound — confirmed by Tajweed scholars
  • Because it produces no sound, it cannot open a word (Arabic requires a voweled letter to begin pronunciation)
  • Its primary function is as a Madd letter — it extends/prolongs the Fatha sound
  • This is why some scholars count 28 Arabic letters (excluding Alif) and others say 29 (28 + Alif as a special case)

docs.title: Iya vs. Waw as Madd Letters
docs.text:
Letter Sound Dual Role ي (Ya) Extends Kasra (ī sound) Also functions as a regular consonant with Haraka و (Waw) Extends Dhamma (ū sound) Also functions as a regular consonant with Haraka ا (Alif) Extends Fatha (ā sound) Only functions as a Madd letter — no independent consonantal role

This is why Iya and Waw are called Huruf al-Leen (weak/soft letters) — they have dual behavior, sometimes silent (as Madd letters) and sometimes voiced (as consonants).

docs.title: Hamzatul Wasl — Mechanism
docs.text:
• When Arabic encounters a word beginning with a Sukun letter, it cannot be pronounced directly (Arabic rule: you cannot begin any pronunciation with a Sukun)
  • Solution: a Hamzatul Wasl is inserted, carrying a Haraka, to enable pronunciation
  • If a Mutaharrik (voweled) letter precedes it in continuous speech, the Hamzatul Wasl drops entirely — it is no longer needed
  • Dropping a Hamzatul Wasl is not a grammatical error because it is not a real letter
  • Dropping a real letter (Hamzatul Qat') would be a Lahnu Jalli (grave mistake)

Haraka on Hamzatul Wasl: - For a definite noun (with ال): receives Fatha → اَلـ - For other contexts: Kasra or Dhamma depending on specific rules

Example — اَلْحَمْدُ لِلّٰهِ: - Starting fresh: اَلْحَمْدُ — Hamzatul Wasl pronounced with Fatha - Continuing from Basmala: ...الرَّحِيمِ ∙ الْحَمْدُ → pronounced مِلْحَمْدُ — Hamzatul Wasl drops because مِيم carries a Kasra (Mutaharrik)

docs.title: Exercise: Convert to Majhul
docs.text: Given (Active) Majhul (Passive) Meaning يَرْجِعُ (he returns) يُرْجَعُ he is returned يُرْزَقُ (he is provided) يُرْزَقُ he is given sustenance يَشْكُرُ (he thanks) يُشْكَرُ he is thanked يَهْزِمُ (he defeats) يُهْزَمُ he is defeated

docs.title: Exercise: Identify the Jumla Fi'liyya Components
docs.text:
A Jumla Fi'liyya requires exactly two mandatory elements: 1. Fi'l (فِعْل) — the verb, must come first 2. Fa'il (فَاعِل) — the subject/doer, must come after the verb

If Fa'il precedes Fi'l → it is no longer a Jumla Fi'liyya; it becomes a Jumla Ismiyya

docs.title: Exercise: إِيَّاكَ Analysis (from Surah Al-Fatiha)
docs.text:
• إِيَّاكَ is NOT a Fi'l and NOT a Fa'il
  • It is a Maf'ul Bihi (مَفْعُول بِه) brought forward (Taqdim) for Ikhtiṣaṣ (exclusivity)
  • إِيَّا on its own has no independent meaning — it only functions with an attached pronoun
  • The Taqdim (fronting) conveys: "You and You alone we worship" — highest degree of exclusivity

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Alif is not a regular letter. It cannot take any Haraka and cannot begin a word. What appears as an Alif at the start of words is always a Hamzatul Wasl.

  2. Hamzatul Wasl is a helper. It exists purely to allow pronunciation of an initial Sukun letter. When the preceding letter is already Mutaharrik (has a Haraka), the Hamzatul Wasl drops with no error.

  3. Huruf Nasiba (أَنْ لَنْ كَيْ إِذَنْ) render Nasb on the Mudari'. The sign of Nasb is: Fatha on the final letter for most patterns; dropping of the Noon of I'rab for the five affected patterns.

  4. Noon of femininity (نُون التَّأْنِيث) never drops with Huruf Nasiba or Jazima — the Nasb/Jazm state is understood from context (similar to how مَمْنُوع مِن الصَّرْف uses Fatha for both Nasb and Jarr).

  5. Huruf Jazima (إِنْ لَمْ لَمَّا لَامُ الأَمْر لَا النَّاهِيَة) render Jazm. Sign of Jazm is Sukun on the final letter (or dropping of Noon of I'rab). This is the verb equivalent of Jarr in nouns — but the state is called Majzum, not Majrur.

  6. Jumla Fi'liyya rule is absolute: Fi'l must precede Fa'il. No exceptions. If the noun comes first, it is automatically a Jumla Ismiyya.

  7. Self-directed Arabic exposure is essential for online students. Deliberately read Arabic sources first before turning to English translations — even partial understanding builds fluency over time.

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text:
This session focused on the governing particles (حروف عاملة) that affect the فعل مضارع (present/future tense verb) — specifically the حروف جازمة (jazm-giving particles) and their effects on the 14 verb patterns. The class covered:

  • Review of non-human plural treated as feminine singular (a Nahw rule)
  • How جازم particles render the مضارع مجزوم (with sukoon or dropped letter)
  • The نون الإعراب (noon of i'rab) and when it drops
  • لام الأمر (lam of command) for 3rd and 1st person
  • لا النهي (la of prohibition) as a negative command
  • لَمْ and لَمَّا and their specific meanings
  • Weak-letter (حروف العلة) verbs and how jazm affects them
  • The distinction between حرف جر and لام العامل

docs.title: The Five Patterns Affected by حروف ناصبة / حروف جازمة
docs.text:
From the 14 entries of the صرف table, five patterns are directly affected by the vowel change (the remaining ones are governed through نون الإعراب):

Pattern Example (base) After جازم (e.g., لَمْ) يَفْعَلُ (3rd m. sg.) يَفْعَلُ لَمْ يَفْعَلْ تَفْعَلُ (3rd f. sg.) تَفْعَلُ لَمْ تَفْعَلْ تَفْعَلُ (2nd m. sg.) تَفْعَلُ لَمْ تَفْعَلْ أَفْعَلُ (1st sg.) أَفْعَلُ لَمْ أَفْعَلْ نَفْعَلُ (1st pl.) نَفْعَلُ لَمْ نَفْعَلْ

The last letter receives سكون (sukoon) / jazm in all five cases.

docs.title: The Seven Patterns Where نون الإعراب Drops
docs.text:
For the remaining seven patterns (those whose مرفوع form ends in نون as the sign of رفع), the نون drops when preceded by a جازم or ناصب particle:

  • يَفْعَلُونَ → لَمْ يَفْعَلُوا (with protective الف)
  • تَفْعَلُونَ → لَمْ تَفْعَلُوا
  • يَفْعَلَانِ → لَمْ يَفْعَلَا
  • تَفْعَلَانِ → لَمْ تَفْعَلَا
  • تَفْعَلِينَ → لَمْ تَفْعَلِي
  • And the corresponding dual/plural patterns

docs.title: The Two Patterns Where نون Does NOT Drop
docs.text:
These two patterns retain their noon even when governed by a جازم:

Pattern Description Example يَفْعَلْنَ 3rd person feminine plural لَمْ يَفْعَلْنَ تَفْعَلْنَ 2nd person feminine plural لَمْ تَفْعَلْنَ

Reason: The نون here is نون النسوة (noon of femininity), not نون الإعراب. Dropping it would cause confusion with other patterns (it would merge with يَفْعَلْ). Although the noon is retained, these verbs are still grammatically مجزوم — the jazm is present but not visibly marked (مجزوم في محل / مجزوم بسبب حرف الجازمة). This is analogous to مُوسى, which never changes its written form but is still considered مرفوع / منصوب / مجرور depending on its grammatical role.

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning حروف Plural of حرف (particle/letter) حرف Singular: a particle or letter حروف جازمة Jazm-giving particles (render the مضارع مجزوم) حروف ناصبة Nasb-giving particles (render the مضارع منصوب with فتحة) حروف عاملة / عوامل Governing particles — those that act upon (exert grammatical influence over) what follows مجزوم In the state of jazm (sukoon on final letter, or dropped نون / حرف علة) منصوب In the state of nasb (فتحة on final letter, or dropped نون) مرفوع In the state of raf' (ضمة on final letter, or presence of نون) نون الإعراب The noon that signals the grammatical state (رفع) in certain مضارع patterns نون النسوة The noon of femininity (never dropped) لام الأمر The lam of command — used to issue commands to 3rd and 1st persons لا النهي The prohibiting lا — used for negative commands (prohibition) لَمْ Negates the مضارع and pushes its meaning into the past ("did not") لَمَّا "Not yet" — negates with implication that the action is still expected حروف العلة Weak letters: و، ي، ا (Alif, Waw, Ya) ألف مقصورة The standing alif written as ى (without dots) مجهول Passive voice معروف Active voice مصدر Verbal noun / infinitive-like noun مصدر مشتق A derived verbal noun تقييد / تأكيد Emphasis/affirmation نون التوكيد الثقيلة Heavy noon of emphasis (نّ) نون التوكيد الخفيفة Light noon of emphasis (نْ) مُرَكَّب Compound phrase (can stand in place of an اسم) مُضاف إليه The second noun in an إضافة (genitive) phrase مركب توصيفي Adjectival/descriptive phrase (adjective-noun phrase)

docs.title: لام الأمر + يَرْكَعُ (to bow/do rukoo')
docs.text:
Person Base مضارع With لام الأمر 3rd m. sg. يَرْكَعُ لِيَرْكَعْ 3rd f. sg. تَرْكَعُ لِتَرْكَعْ 3rd m. dual يَرْكَعَانِ لِيَرْكَعَا 3rd f. dual تَرْكَعَانِ لِتَرْكَعَا 3rd m. pl. يَرْكَعُونَ لِيَرْكَعُوا 3rd f. pl. يَرْكَعْنَ لِيَرْكَعْنَ 1st sg. أَرْكَعُ لِأَرْكَعْ 1st pl. نَرْكَعُ لِنَرْكَعْ

(The عين receives sukoon in all cases where the ending is not a weak letter or نون نسوة)

docs.title: Exercise Conjugations from Class (using لام الأمر)
docs.text: Root Verb Conjugated Form Note يَفْجُرُ (to transgress) لِيَفْجُرْ ضمة on ر drops → سكون يَرْكَعُ (to bow) لِيَرْكَعْ فتحة on ع drops → سكون يَعْبُدُ (to worship) لِيَعْبُدْ ضمة on د drops → سكون يَمْلِكُ (to own/possess) لِيَمْلِكْ كسرة on ك drops → سكون

docs.title: Weak-Letter Verb Examples (حروف العلة dropped by جازم)
docs.text:
Original مضارع Root letters Effect of جازم Result يَخْشَى خ-ش-ي Alif maqsoorah dropped لَمْ يَخْشَ يَرْمِي ر-م-ي ياء dropped لَمْ يَرْمِ (kasra on م retained to mark dropped ي) يَدْعُو د-ع-و واو dropped لَمْ يَدْعُ (damma on ع retained to mark dropped و)

Key principle: When a jazm-giving particle precedes a مضارع whose final letter is a حرف علة (و، ي، or ألف مقصورة), that weak letter is completely deleted. The remaining vowel on the letter before it signals what letter was originally there.

docs.title: The 14 Entries and How Commands Are Distributed
docs.text:
The 14 entries of the صرف table break down as: - 6 patterns for 2nd person (المخاطب) — these use the direct أمر form (command conjugation); no لام needed - 6 patterns for 3rd person (الغائب) — command issued via لام الأمر - 2 patterns for 1st person (المتكلم) — command issued via لام الأمر

This is why لام الأمر is essential: the direct أمر form only exists for the 2nd person. To issue a command to an absent (3rd) person or to include oneself (1st person), لام الأمر + مضارع is the grammatical tool.

docs.title: لَمْ vs. لَمَّا
docs.text: Particle Meaning Effect Example لَمْ "did not" (past negation) Gives jazm; pushes مضارع meaning into past لَمْ يَفْعَلْ = "he did not do" لَمَّا "not yet" (negation with expectation) Gives jazm; implies action is still pending لَمَّا يَفْعَلْ = "he has not yet done (but is expected to)"

docs.title: لام الأمر vs. لا النهي
docs.text:
Tool Arabic Meaning Example لام الأمر لِ + مضارع Positive command (3rd/1st person) لِيَفْعَلْ = "let him do / he should do" لا النهي لَا + مضارع Prohibition / negative command لَا يَفْعَلْ = "let him not do / he must not do"

Note on لام الأمر voweling: لام الأمر always carries a كسرة (kasra). The لام with فتحة (لَ) is the لام of affirmation/emphasis (e.g., لَأَذْهَبَنَّ = "I will surely go"), a completely different particle.

docs.title: The حرف جر Rule — A Critical Distinction
docs.text:
حرف الجر can ONLY precede an اسم (or a phrase acting in place of an اسم). It can NEVER precede a فعل directly.

The لام in لِيَفْعَلَ is not حرف الجر — it is لام العامل (a governing particle). The distinction: - If you claim something is حرف جر, you must be able to identify the اسم following it - No اسم follows لِ in لِيَفْعَلَ — the فعل follows directly - Therefore it is لام الأمر, a governing particle from the حروف جازمة category - What can follow حرف جر after a verb: convert the verb into مصدر form (verbal noun), then حرف جر applies to that مصدر

docs.title: Identifying Jazm in Weak-Letter Verbs
docs.text:
Q: In لَمْ يَخْشَ — the verb appears to end in only two letters (يَخْشَ). Where is the third root letter?

A: The third root letter is ي (from خ-ش-ي). The مضارع was originally يَخْشَى (with ألف مقصورة). When لَمْ preceded it, the ألف مقصورة was deleted as the sign of jazm. The student must consult the dictionary to confirm the original pattern.

Q: In لَمْ يَرْمِ — why does the مeem have a kasra if the verb is مجزوم?

A: The original form was يَرْمِي. The ياء was deleted by jazm. The kasra on the مeem is not the i'rab vowel — it is a remnant marker indicating the deleted ياء. Reading it as sukoon on the مeem would be wrong because one cannot drop two letters simultaneously.

docs.title: Applying لام الأمر
docs.text:
Given يَعْبُدُ (he worships):

  • لِيَعْبُدْ — let him worship (3rd m. sg.)
  • لِيَعْبُدُوا — let them worship (3rd m. pl.) — نون dropped, protective الف added
  • لِأَعْبُدْ — let me worship (1st sg.)
  • لِنَعْبُدْ — let us worship (1st pl.)

docs.title: Non-Human Plural + Feminine Singular Agreement (Nahw connection)
docs.text:
Example phrase: حروف جازمة

  • حروف = plural (non-human)
  • جازمة = feminine singular adjective

Why? Non-human plurals are grammatically treated as feminine singular in Arabic. Therefore the adjective/attribute agrees as feminine singular (مفرد مؤنث), not as a masculine plural.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. حروف جازمة render the مضارع مجزوم — the sign of jazm is: sukoon on the final letter (for the 5 base patterns), or dropping of نون الإعراب (for the 7 noon-bearing patterns).

  2. نون النسوة is never dropped — even when governed by a جازم. These verbs are still مجزوم grammatically, but the jazm is "hidden" (مجزوم محلاً), just as مُوسى is always written the same but changes grammatical role depending on context.

  3. لام الأمر vs. لا النهي: لام الأمر (with kasra) = positive command for 3rd and 1st persons. لا النهي = prohibition/negative command for all persons. Both are حروف جازمة.

  4. لَمْ vs. لَمَّا: لَمْ simply negates (past meaning). لَمَّا means "not yet" — the action is negated but still anticipated.

  5. Weak-letter verbs lose their حرف علة under jazm: ألف مقصورة, ياء, or واو at the end of a مضارع is completely deleted when a جازم governs it. The remaining vowel on the preceding letter is a trace indicating which letter was dropped.

  6. حرف جر ONLY governs اسم — never فعل. Whenever you identify something as حرف جر, you must be able to point to the اسم it governs. The لام in لِيَفْعَلَ is لام العامل (governing particle), not حرف جر.

  7. The 14 patterns and command distribution: Direct أمر form exists only for 2nd person (6 patterns). For 3rd person (6 patterns) and 1st person (2 patterns), لام الأمر + مضارع is the mechanism.

  8. Dropping نون with ناصب vs. جازم: The نون of إعراب drops with both حروف ناصبة AND حروف جازمة, but the resulting state is different — منصوب (nasb, فتحة meaning) vs. مجزوم (jazm, sukoon meaning). The visual form may look the same; the grammatical ruling differs based on which governing particle precedes it.

  9. The grammarians' methodology: The rules of Nahw and Sarf were derived by the early Muslim grammarians (مصرّفون/نحاة) through data analysis of the language — scanning the Quran and Arabic literature, identifying patterns, and codifying them. These rules are descriptive of how the language works, not prescriptive inventions.

  10. Next topic: نون التوكيد (noon of emphasis) — الثقيلة (heavy: نَّ) and الخفيفة (light: نْ) — to be covered in the following class. Study the relevant pages independently before the next session.

docs.title: TFS 101 (Fall 2023)
docs.text:
Tafseer Foundation Series 101

Date Topic Sep 21, 2023 Surah Al-Duha Sep 28, 2023 Surah Al-Duha (contd.) Oct 05, 2023 Surah Al-Inshirah Nov 09, 2023 Al-Alaq (Chapter 96) -- First five ayat Nov 16, 2023 Surah Al-Alaq (contd.) Nov 23, 2023 Surah Al-Alaq Nov 30, 2023 Surah Al-Alaq (Part 3) Dec 07, 2023 Surah Al-Qadr Dec 14, 2023 Surah Al-Bayyinah (Ayat 1-3)

docs.title: TFS 101 - Study Notes
docs.text: Date: 2023-09-21 Surah Covered: Surah Al-Duha (Chapter 93), with references to Surah Al-Inshirah / Alam Nashrah (Chapter 94)

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session introduces Surah Al-Duha and its relationship to Surah Al-Inshirah as twin surahs (tawamain). The primary theme is Allah's consolation of the Prophet (sallallahu alayhi wasallam) during a period of sadness. The session also includes an important academic discussion on a narration in Sahih al-Bukhari regarding the period of Fatrat al-Wahi.

docs.title: Key Themes and Topics
docs.text:
• Consolation of the Prophet (SAW): Both Surah Al-Duha and Surah Al-Inshirah were revealed to console the Prophet during a difficult period.
  • The pain of criticism from one's own people: The emotional weight of being attacked by family members, not strangers.
  • Fatrat al-Wahi (the pause in revelation): The period when revelation ceased, causing sadness to the Prophet (SAW).
  • Sadness vs. Depression: An important distinction -- sadness is a normal human emotion; depression is a mental illness. The Prophet experienced sadness, never depression.
  • Hadith criticism and academic integrity: A detailed discussion on a narration in Sahih al-Bukhari, the concept of mursal narrations, and the principle that even Sahih al-Bukhari contains narrations that scholars have critiqued.
  • The Principles of Khair and Yusr: Two complementary principles given across the twin surahs.
  • Night and day as metaphors for hardship and relief.

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Tawamain Twins; used to describe the twin/sister relationship between Surah Al-Duha and Surah Al-Inshirah Fatrat al-Wahi The pause/cessation of revelation Majnun Mad, insane; the accusation leveled against the Prophet (SAW) by Abu Lahab Wahi Divine revelation Mursal A hadith where a Tabi'i (successor) narrates something attributed to the Prophet without mentioning the intermediary link (the Sahabi) Duha Morning brightness; the time after sunrise Layl Night Waw al-Qasam The Arabic letter "waw" used for oath-taking Jar case The grammatical case in Arabic caused by certain particles, including waw al-qasam; typically indicated by kasra Kasra The vowel mark indicating the jar (genitive) case in Arabic Khair Goodness, that which is beneficial Yusr Ease Al-Akhira The Hereafter; also can mean "the subsequent/later time" Al-Ula The first; can refer to this worldly life (dunya) or the current/present time Tasawwuf Islamic mysticism/spirituality (Sufism) Muhaddisin Scholars of hadith Fuqaha Jurists; scholars of Islamic jurisprudence Mansoukh Abrogated Salat al-Duha The forenoon/mid-morning prayer (sunnah prayer, 4 rakat) Salat al-Ishraq The post-sunrise prayer; according to muhaddisin, this is the same as Salat al-Duha

docs.title: Twin Surahs (Tawamain)
docs.text:
• Surah Al-Duha and Surah Al-Inshirah share the same subject matter and address the same issue.
  • Both were revealed to console the Prophet (SAW).

docs.title: Context of Revelation
docs.text:
• After the Prophet (SAW) began giving da'wah in Mecca, he faced harsh resistance.
  • His own uncle Abu Lahab would walk behind him in public calling him "Majnun" (mad).
  • Criticism from one's own family is more painful than criticism from strangers.
  • Additionally, the Prophet was saddened by Fatrat al-Wahi -- the pause in revelation.
  • Two scholarly opinions on its duration: six months or two and a half years.
  • Both opinions are supported by historical narrations; it is a matter of historical record, not creed.
  • The Prophet had no indication of when (or whether) revelation would resume.
  • He would wait every day and night for Jibreel (AS) to return.

docs.title: Timeline Clarification
docs.text:
• The pause in revelation (Fatrat al-Wahi) occurred right after the first revelation (Iqra/Surah Al-Alaq).
  • After the pause, Surah Al-Muddaththir was revealed.
  • Surah Al-Duha was revealed sometime after Surah Al-Muddaththir.
  • The consolation did not have to be immediate.

docs.title: Discussion on the Sahih al-Bukhari Narration
docs.text:
• A narration in Sahih al-Bukhari, reported by Sayyida Aisha (RA), describes the early period of Islam.
  • Within this narration, there is a portion stating that the Prophet attempted to take his own life during Fatrat al-Wahi and was stopped by Jibreel (AS).
  • This portion is NOT from Sayyida Aisha's narration. It is the inserted statement of Imam al-Zuhri, a Tabi'i in the chain of transmission.
  • Imam al-Zuhri's statement is classified as mursal -- he did not provide the links between himself and the Prophet (SAW).
  • It is the consensus of the muhaddisin that Imam al-Zuhri's mursal narrations are not accepted as valid.
  • Furthermore, this claim contradicts the Quran directly: Surah Al-Qalam states that the Prophet is not majnun (mad) by the blessing of his Lord.
  • Suicidal tendencies are symptoms of mental illness, which is incompatible with prophethood, since prophets must have sound decision-making ability to guide their nations.
  • Imam al-Zuhri was not lying; he was a reliable and distinguished scholar who helped compile hadith during the time of Umar ibn Abdul Aziz. However, even reliable scholars can be less cautious at times, narrating information without full verification.

docs.title: Critique of Sahih al-Bukhari -- Academic Perspective
docs.text:
• Imam Bukhari was human and capable of mistakes.
  • Imam al-Daraqutni criticized 82 ahadith in Sahih al-Bukhari.
  • Various schools of thought (Hanafi, Shafi'i, Maliki, Hanbali) have not accepted certain narrations in Sahih al-Bukhari, either considering them abrogated (mansoukh) or having stronger counter-narrations.
  • The popular belief that every hadith in Sahih al-Bukhari is beyond question is a common public perception but is not academically sound.
  • The first person to establish the rule of giving Sahih al-Bukhari supreme preference was Ibn al-Salah, and his position has been critiqued by other scholars.

docs.title: Ayah 1: "Wa al-Duha" (By the morning brightness)
docs.text:
• The "waw" is waw al-qasam (oath particle), placing the following noun in the jar (genitive) case.
  • Al-Duha refers to the morning brightness, the time starting approximately 15-20 minutes after sunrise.
  • The word "duha" cannot accept a kasra but is still grammatically in the jar case (kasra is the most common but not the only sign of the jar case).

docs.title: Ayah 2: "Wa al-Layli idha saja" (And by the night when it covers with darkness)
docs.text: • Also uses waw al-qasam; "al-layl" is in the jar case.

docs.title: Connection of the Oaths to the Consolation
docs.text:
• Night never stays forever; morning always follows. Similarly, morning passes and night returns.
  • This is a subtle indication to the Prophet (SAW): the phase of sadness and hardship will end, just as night gives way to dawn.
  • Duha symbolizes a new beginning -- brightness after darkness.
  • The message: this long phase of sadness will come to an end, and a brighter time will follow.

docs.title: Ayah 3: "Ma wadda'aka Rabbuka wa ma qala"
docs.text:
• "Your Lord has not forsaken you, nor has He been displeased with you."
  • Direct consolation: Allah has not abandoned the Prophet, and revelation will return.

docs.title: Ayah 4: "Wa la-l-akhiratu khayrun laka min al-ula"
docs.text:
• First meaning (general): The Hereafter is better for you than this worldly life (dunya). This applies to all believers. The hadith states: "Dunya is a prison for the believer and a paradise for the disbeliever."
  • Second meaning (specific to the Prophet): The subsequent time -- the future -- will be better for you than this current time of hardship. This was a promise that his situation would improve. In this reading, "akhira" means "the later time" and "ula" means "the current/present time."

docs.title: The Two Principles Across the Twin Surahs
docs.text:
1. Principle of Khair (Surah Al-Duha): The hereafter/future is better -- the principle of goodness.
  2. Principle of Yusr (Surah Al-Inshirah): "With hardship comes ease" -- the principle of ease.
  3. Both khair and yusr are needed for success in dunya and akhira.
  4. Sometimes a person has khair but not yusr (e.g., wealth is good but hard to earn; knowledge is good but requires effort).
  5. Sometimes a person has yusr but not khair (e.g., haram money is easy to earn but is not good; overnight fame through inappropriate means is easy but not beneficial).

docs.title: Salat al-Duha and Salat al-Ishraq
docs.text:
• It is sunnah to offer four rakat at the duha time.
  • The time for Salat al-Duha begins approximately 15-20 minutes after sunrise.
  • Muhaddisin and majority of fuqaha: Ishraq and Duha are the same prayer -- one prayer in the morning after sunrise.
  • Sufi scholars: There are two separate prayers -- Salat al-Ishraq (earlier, right after sunrise) and Salat al-Duha/Chasht (later, about an hour after). The muhaddisin's position is considered stronger.

docs.title: Lessons and Practical Takeaways
docs.text:
• Sadness is normal and human. The Prophet (SAW) experienced sadness, which validates that feeling sad during hardship is a natural and acceptable response. Not being affected by anything would be abnormal.
  • Hardship is temporary. Just as night always gives way to morning, difficult periods in life will pass. This is a universal Quranic principle.
  • Criticism from loved ones hurts most. The surah acknowledges that the deepest pain often comes from those closest to us, not from strangers.
  • Allah does not abandon the believer. The message "Your Lord has not forsaken you" is a source of comfort for anyone going through difficulty.
  • The future holds goodness. Whether understood as the akhira or simply the times ahead, the believer is assured that better days are coming.
  • Success requires both khair and yusr. Goodness without ease is incomplete, and ease without goodness is hollow. Strive for both.
  • Academic rigor in hadith study. Not every narration in a famous collection is beyond critique. Scholars have always exercised critical analysis, and students of knowledge should do the same rather than accepting everything uncritically.
  • Distinguish between sadness and depression. Sadness is a temporary emotional state; depression is a clinical illness. The distinction matters both theologically and practically.

docs.title: Historical Context and Stories Mentioned
docs.text:
• Abu Lahab's public harassment: The Prophet's uncle would follow him in public, calling him "Majnun" (mad), causing significant emotional pain.
  • Fatrat al-Wahi: The period after the first revelation when Jibreel (AS) did not return for an extended time (six months or two and a half years), leaving the Prophet waiting anxiously.
  • Imam al-Zuhri: A prominent Tabi'i and one of the earliest compilers of hadith during the era of Umar ibn Abdul Aziz. His mursal narrations are not accepted by consensus of muhaddisin.
  • Imam al-Daraqutni's critique: He identified and critiqued 82 ahadith in Sahih al-Bukhari, demonstrating that scholarly criticism of even the most respected collections is a legitimate and established practice.
  • Ibn al-Salah: The scholar who first established the principle of giving Sahih al-Bukhari supreme preference over other hadith collections. His position has been critiqued by subsequent scholars.
  • Am al-Huzn (Year of Sadness): Briefly referenced as the year the Prophet's son died -- described in hadith as the "year of sadness," not "year of depression," reinforcing the distinction.

docs.title: Surah Covered: Surah Al-Duha (Ayaat 5-11) with continuation into closing discussion
Review: Sources of Tafseer (Recap from Previous Classes)
docs.text:
1. The Quran - Quran explains itself
  2. Hadith - Prophetic narrations
  3. Statements of the Sahaba - Companions' explanations
  4. Statements of the Tabi'een - Next generation's explanations
  5. The Arabic Language - Linguistic analysis
  6. Tadabbur / Istinbat - Contemplation and deriving meaning

docs.title: Ruling on Biblical Narrations (Isra'iliyyat)
docs.text:
• Many tafseer books contain narrations from Jewish and Christian sources because of shared stories (e.g., names of Ashabul Kahf are found only in biblical sources, not in Quran or Sunnah).
  • Ruling: We neither confirm nor deny biblical narrations.
  • We do not confirm them because they could be false.
  • We do not deny them because they could be true.
  • We simply do not rely on them.

docs.title: Tafseer bir Ra'i (Interpretation by Personal Opinion)
docs.text:
• This is when a person gives an interpretation that contradicts the established sources (Quran, Hadith, statements of Sahaba/Tabi'een, language).
  • Tafseer bir Ra'i is not acceptable -- it is not the same as Istinbat.

docs.title: Surah Al-Duha: Context and Overview
docs.text:
• Surah Al-Duha and Surah Al-Inshirah (Al-Nashrah) are twin surahs addressing the same theme: consoling the Prophet (SAW).
  • Reason for revelation: The Prophet (SAW) was saddened because:
  • Revelation (Wahi) had paused and he did not know when or if it would resume.
  • He faced harsh treatment from his own family members.

docs.title: Two Promises Across Two Surahs
docs.text:
Surah Promise Al-Duha Promise of Khayr (goodness) Al-Inshirah Promise of Yusr (ease)
  • Sometimes a person has khayr without ease (e.g., earning halal money through hard work but not having enough).
  • Sometimes a person has ease without khayr (e.g., earning haram money easily).
  • The Prophet (SAW) was promised both khayr and yusr, which were fulfilled after the migration to Madinah.

docs.title: Ayah 5: Promise of Al-Maqam Al-Mahmoud
docs.text:
Arabic: wa la sawfa yu'teeka rabbuka fa tarda

Translation: And surely your Lord will give you, and then you will be satisfied.

docs.title: Key Grammatical Points
docs.text:
• Lam (la) = for emphasis, indicating certainty
  • Sawfa + present tense (yu'teeka) = indicates far future (i.e., the Akhirah, not this dunya)
  • If it were near future, the particle Seen (sa) would be used instead (e.g., sa yu'teeka)
  • Fa tarda = then you will be satisfied -- this links to al-Maqam al-Mahmoud

docs.title: What Will Allah Give?
docs.text:
• Not specified in this ayah. Explained in Surah Al-Isra (17:79): "It may be that your Lord will raise you to Maqaman Mahmooda (a praiseworthy station)."
  • Al-Maqam Al-Mahmoud = the praiseworthy position where the Prophet (SAW) will intercede (shafa'ah) for his entire Ummah.
  • The Prophet (SAW) will not enter Jannah until the last person of his Ummah -- even with the weakest iman -- enters Jannah.
  • A narration states that when this ayah was revealed, the Prophet (SAW) said: "I will not be satisfied while even one person from my Ummah is in the Fire."

docs.title: Practical Connection: Du'a After Adhan
docs.text:
• The Prophet (SAW) instructed that after hearing the adhan, one should make du'a including: "...wab'athhu maqaman mahmoodan alladhi wa'adtah" (and raise him to the praiseworthy station You promised him).
  • Reflect: if we consistently make this du'a for the Prophet (SAW), will he forget us on the Day of Judgment when we need his intercession?

docs.title: Ayahs 6-11: Three Blessings and Three Responsibilities
docs.text: Core Principle: Nothing is free in this dunya. Every blessing comes with a corresponding responsibility. This applies to divine law and even worldly systems (e.g., welfare states provide free education/healthcare but raise taxes).

docs.title: Structure: Blessings Paired with Responsibilities
docs.text: Ayah Blessing Ayah Corresponding Responsibility 6 Orphanhood -- Allah gave shelter 9 Do not oppress the orphan 7 Lost -- Allah guided 10 Do not repel the one who asks 8 Dependent -- Allah made self-sufficient 11 Proclaim the favor of your Lord

docs.title: Ayah 6 (Blessing): a lam yajidka yateeman fa aawa
docs.text:
Translation: Did He not find you an orphan and gave you shelter?

  • The Prophet's father passed away before his birth; his mother passed away after; his grandfather by age 8; then raised by uncle Abu Talib.
  • Orphans in harsh societies typically cannot reach leadership positions and remain dependent.
  • Allah protected the Prophet (SAW) from psychological trauma -- his intellectual ability was never compromised despite these losses.

docs.title: Ayah 9 (Responsibility): fa ammal yateema fa la taqhar
docs.text:
Translation: So as for the orphan, do not oppress him.

  • Because the Prophet (SAW) experienced orphanhood, he understands an orphan's pain.
  • Hadith: "I and the caretaker of an orphan will be like this in Jannah" (indicating closeness with two fingers).

docs.title: Ayah 7 (Blessing): wa wajadaka dallan fa hada
docs.text: Translation: And He found you lost, so He guided.

docs.title: Clarification on the Word "Dall"
docs.text:
• Dall does NOT mean "astray" or "misguided" in this context -- it means lost (knowing the destination but not knowing how to get there).
  • Before prophethood, the Prophet (SAW):
  • Felt an intuitive urge to worship his Creator.
  • Knew the idols were false.
  • Did not know how to properly worship the one true God.
  • Would go to the Cave of Hira for days and weeks, contemplating and sometimes weeping at the Ka'bah.
  • Cross-reference: Surah Al-Shura -- "You did not know what was the Book and Iman" -- the details of faith (belief in angels, all books, Jannah, Jahannam, Qadr) were not yet revealed to him.

docs.title: Ayah 10 (Responsibility): wa ammas saa'ila fa la tanhar
docs.text:
Translation: And as for the one who asks, do not repel him.

  • Sa'il = anyone who asks -- whether for money, knowledge, or guidance.
  • Connection: Just as the Prophet (SAW) was lost and Allah guided him, his responsibility is to guide everyone who comes seeking.
  • If someone does not know how to pray, teach them. If someone asks for financial help and you can help, help them.

docs.title: Ayah 8 (Blessing): wa wajadaka aa'ilan fa aghna
docs.text:
Translation: And He found you in need/dependent, so He made you self-sufficient.

  • The Prophet (SAW) did not inherit wealth and was financially dependent on his uncle Abu Talib.
  • Allah made him self-sufficient in two ways:
  • Through marriage to Khadijah (RA) -- she was the main businesswoman of Makkah, and through managing her trade, he became financially independent.
  • Through internal contentment (ghina al-nafs) -- true richness is richness of the heart.

docs.title: Key Hadith on True Wealth
docs.text:
• "Being rich is not having many possessions; rather, richness is the richness of the soul (heart)."
  • Example: Sayyida Aisha (RA) had only a single date at home. A woman came with two daughters asking for food. Aisha gave away the only date she had, and the woman split it between her daughters. This illustrates the ghina (contentment) the Prophet (SAW) trained his household with.
  • A person who does not chase the dunya and is happy with whatever little they have possesses a priceless form of happiness.

docs.title: Ayah 11 (Responsibility): wa amma bi ni'mati rabbika fa haddith
docs.text: Translation: And as for the favor of your Lord, proclaim/narrate it.

docs.title: Meanings and Applications
docs.text:
1. Share blessings openly -- do not hide the favors Allah has given you.
  2. Use the blessings Allah gave you -- if you can afford good, healthy food, eat it; if you can afford nice clothes, wear them. Keeping blessings locked away and living miserably despite having means is stinginess (bukhl).
  3. Narrating blessings is a form of gratitude (shukr) to Allah.

docs.title: Supporting Hadith
docs.text: • A man came to the Prophet (SAW) wearing patched clothes. The Prophet asked if he had better clothes. The man confirmed he had wealth (sheep, camels, etc.). The Prophet (SAW) said: "Indeed Allah loves to see the effect/impact (athar) of His blessing on His servant."

docs.title: Important Distinction: Gratitude vs. Showing Off
docs.text:
• Thin line between expressing gratitude and riya (showing off).
  • Driving a nice car because you can afford it and are grateful to Allah = permissible.
  • Driving a nice car to show off in front of those who cannot afford it = riya/sum'ah (sinful).
  • Only the individual truly knows their own intention -- no one else can judge.

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Tadabbur Contemplation, reflection on the Quran Istinbat Deriving meaning/rulings through reasoning Tafseer bir Ra'i Interpretation based on personal opinion (rejected if contradicts established sources) Isra'iliyyat Biblical/Judeo-Christian narrations found in tafseer literature Sawfa Particle indicating far future (used with present tense verb) Seen (sa) Particle indicating near future Al-Maqam Al-Mahmoud The Praiseworthy Station -- position of intercession on Day of Judgment Shafa'ah Intercession Dall Lost (not misguided) -- knowing the destination but not the path Aa'il One who is in need, dependent Ghina Self-sufficiency, contentment, richness of the heart Bukhl Stinginess, miserliness Riya Showing off (in worship or worldly matters) Sum'ah Doing things to be heard/praised by people Haddith Narrate, proclaim, share Athar Effect, impact, trace Khayr Goodness, benefit Yusr Ease, facilitation Zahir ul-Riwayah Relied-upon narrations in the Hanafi school (from six books of Imam Muhammad) Nadir ul-Riwayah Less-relied-upon narrations (from other books of Imam Muhammad)

docs.title: Lessons and Practical Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Every blessing carries a responsibility. When Allah gives us something, we are accountable for how we use it and what we do in return.

  2. Care for orphans. The Prophet (SAW) experienced orphanhood firsthand. The Ummah is instructed never to oppress orphans and to actively care for them.

  3. Never turn away someone who asks. Whether they seek knowledge or material help, be open and generous.

  4. True wealth is contentment. Richness is not measured by possessions but by satisfaction of the heart. No matter how much a greedy person accumulates, they will never feel rich.

  5. Use and enjoy Allah's blessings. It is not piety to hoard wealth and live miserably. Use what Allah has given you -- good food, good clothing, good shelter -- with gratitude, not with the intention to show off.

  6. Make du'a after the adhan. Specifically the du'a requesting al-Maqam al-Mahmoud for the Prophet (SAW) -- this is directly connected to his intercession for us.

  7. Be grateful openly. Expressing gratitude through words and through visibly using blessings is encouraged, so long as the intention is thankfulness, not arrogance.

  8. Follow a madhhab with discipline. For ordinary Muslims without deep scholarly knowledge, following one madhhab consistently is necessary for discipline. Switching opinions based on personal convenience (desire) is not acceptable, but switching for genuine excuses or after thorough scholarly evaluation is permitted.

docs.title: On Following Madhahib (Schools of Thought)
docs.text:
• It is mandatory for ordinary Muslims to follow a madhhab for the sake of discipline.
  • Picking opinions from different madhahib based on personal comfort = following desire, not scholarship.
  • Switching is permissible when:
  • There is a genuine excuse (e.g., living in a country where only one madhhab is practiced).
  • One has thoroughly studied and compared the evidences of both positions.
  • Switching constantly and inconsistently = against the principle of discipline.

docs.title: On the Prophet's Voluntary Poverty
docs.text:
• The Prophet (SAW) chose poverty (ikhtiyari) -- it was not imposed on him.
  • Following his exact lifestyle is virtuous but not obligatory (fard or wajib).
  • If one has wealth, they should use it, help the poor, and be grateful -- not hoard it or refuse to spend on themselves.

docs.title: On Zahir ul-Riwayah and Nadir ul-Riwayah (Hanafi Jurisprudence)
docs.text:
• Zahir ul-Riwayah: Positions found in the six books of Imam Muhammad ibn al-Hasan al-Shaybani (student of Imam Abu Hanifa). These are given preference.
  • Nadir ul-Riwayah: Positions found in Imam Muhammad's other works. These are secondary.
  • This classification was established during the time of Imam Abu Hanifa's students.

docs.title: TFS 101 - Study Notes
docs.text: Date: October 5, 2023 Surah Covered: Surah Al-Inshirah (Surah 94 - The Expansion / The Relief)

docs.title: Connection to the Previous Surah (Al-Duha)
docs.text:
• Surah Al-Inshirah and Surah Al-Duha are considered twin surahs by the ulama.
  • Both were revealed after the period when revelation (wahi) paused, a time when the Prophet (SAW) was going through difficult times.
  • The Prophet (SAW) was not expecting the harsh treatment he received from his people.
  • Structural parallels between the two surahs:
  • Both open with an affirmation in the form of a question (e.g., Al-Duha: "Alam yajidka yateeman fa aawa" / Al-Inshirah: "Alam nashrah laka sadrak").
  • Both transition from questions to positive affirmations.
  • Both contain a promise from Allah.
  • Both end with commands: Al-Duha commands three things; Al-Inshirah commands two things.

docs.title: Historical Context: Waraqa ibn Nawfil
docs.text:
