  • This link is demonstrated in the life of the Prophet (sallallahu alayhi wasallam): he proclaimed the truth, faced boycott, assassination attempts, and was eventually forced to make hijrah, but he exercised patience throughout.

docs.title: 9. Azimah vs. Rukhsah in Speaking Truth
docs.text:
• Azimah (full conformity): Speaking truth despite the threat of imprisonment or death -- this earns immense reward.
  • Rukhsah (partial conformity / concession): If there is a genuine fear for one's life or severe persecution, remaining silent while hating the wrongdoing in the heart is permissible. This exception is established in the Quran itself (one may even utter words of kufr under threat of death).
  • However, if the fear is merely losing a job or mild inconvenience, that is not a valid excuse to conceal the truth.

docs.title: 10. Surah Al-Asr as a Table of Contents
docs.text: • The four conditions (iman, amal salih, tawasaw bil-haqq, tawasaw bil-sabr) serve as a framework that Allah expands upon in the surahs that follow: Al-Humazah, Al-Fil, Quraysh, Al-Ma'un, and onward to the end of the Quran.

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Al-Asr Time (serial, irreversible time) Iman Faith; from "amn" (peace); linguistically means "to make someone at peace"; technically, belief in Allah and all fundamentals of the deen Amal Salih Righteous deeds Tawasaw From "wasiyyah" (advice/counsel); to mutually advise or enjoin one another Al-Haqq That which is both true and good simultaneously Sabr Patience; steadfastness in the face of difficulty Khasarah Loss Tawakkul Reliance and trust in Allah Ghibah Backbiting -- speaking a true but harmful thing about someone in their absence Buhtan Slander -- speaking something false about someone (worse than ghibah) Ma'bood The one worthy of worship Mu'mina A believing woman Murtad Apostate; one who leaves Islam Zalimeen Oppressors Azimah Full conformity with the ideal ruling Rukhsah Concession; partial conformity permitted due to hardship Ruqyah Shar'iyyah Quranic healing/recitation for spiritual ailments Fatrah al-Wahi The period when revelation ceased (between two prophets) Khulasah Summary; gist Dalil Evidence; proof

docs.title: Main Tafseer Points
docs.text:
1. Default state of humanity is loss. The surah establishes that every human being is in a state of loss (khusr) by default. Success must be actively earned.

  2. The four exceptions are interconnected. They form a chain: iman leads to amal salih, which includes tawasaw bil-haqq, which necessitates tawasaw bil-sabr. Each one flows into the next.

  3. Iman is the foundation. Without it, a person cannot answer the most basic existential questions and lives in perpetual confusion -- a loss in both dunya and akhirah.

  4. Amal salih is the proof of iman. Belief without action is empty, just as claiming to respect a constitution while violating its laws is contradictory.

  5. Haqq has a precise definition. It is not merely "truth" -- it must be both true and good. This filters out backbiting (true but harmful) and flattery (seemingly good but false).

  6. Sabr is the cost of haqq. Speaking truth invites hardship, so patience is the required response. Allah placed them together deliberately.

  7. The Prophetic model. The Prophet's life is the living demonstration of these four principles: he had iman, performed righteous deeds, spoke the truth in the face of opposition, and endured with patience.

  8. Hadith cited: "Ashaddu al-naasi bala'an al-anbiya, thumma al-amthal fal-amthal" -- The most severe trials befall the prophets, then those closest to their path, and so on.

docs.title: Lessons and Practical Takeaways
docs.text:
• Iman provides psychological resilience. Even in the worst worldly circumstances (loss of family, oppression, injustice), a believer's faith provides hope and a framework for meaning. Without it, despair is the logical outcome.
  • Action validates belief. Claiming faith while being passive about worship and righteous deeds weakens iman over time. A person may eventually lose iman altogether through persistent inaction.
  • Speaking truth is a communal obligation. The surah uses the reciprocal form "tawasaw" (they enjoin each other), indicating that standing for truth is not an individual act alone but a collective responsibility.
  • Expect difficulty when standing for truth. It is the natural consequence. The question is not whether hardship will come, but whether one will be patient through it.
  • Know the difference between genuine fear and mere inconvenience. Fear for one's life is a valid excuse for silence, but fear of mild professional or social consequences is not a sufficient reason to abandon truth.
  • This surah is a checklist. One can evaluate oneself regularly: Do I have iman? Am I performing righteous deeds? Am I speaking and encouraging truth? Am I patient through the difficulties that follow?

docs.title: Jinn and Ruqyah
docs.text:
• Jinn exist and can attack humans regardless of the jinn's or the human's religious background.
  • The remedy lies in Ruqyah Shar'iyyah (Quranic healing) performed by someone trained in this specific science -- not just any imam.
  • The reciter must be seeking help from Allah, not from shayateen.

docs.title: Lineage (Nasab) of the Prophets and the Father of Ibrahim
docs.text:
• The hadith about the purity of prophetic lineage refers to the absence of zina (illegitimate birth) in the chain -- purity of lineage.
  • The Quranic reference to mushrikeen as impure refers to impurity of creed (aqidah).
  • There is no contradiction: the lineage is pure in terms of legitimacy, while ancestors may have held incorrect beliefs.
  • Azar was the father of Ibrahim (alayhi al-salam), and there should be no difficulty accepting this, just as the wives of Nuh and Lut and the son of Nuh were disbelievers.

docs.title: Parents of the Prophet (sallallahu alayhi wasallam)
docs.text:
• They passed away before prophethood during the period of Fatrah al-Wahi.
  • Ash'ari and Maturidi position: People of the Fatrah period will be in Jannah.
  • Ibn Taymiyyah's position: They will be given a test on the Day of Judgment, and their fate decided accordingly.
  • Both views lead to the conclusion that the Prophet's parents will be in Jannah.

docs.title: Abu Talib's Punishment Being Reduced
docs.text:
• The Prophet initially made du'a for Abu Talib before the ayah forbidding istighfar for mushrikeen was revealed.
  • Per a hadith in Sahih Muslim, Abu Talib's punishment was lightened, but he is still in the lightest form of hellfire punishment, yet he will think he is in the worst condition.
  • This is an exceptional case due to Abu Talib's defense of the Prophet and Islam -- it is not a general rule. Du'a of istighfar for mushrikeen does not benefit them as a general principle.

docs.title: Student Loans
docs.text:
• Interest-free student loans (e.g., government loans without interest) are permissible.
  • Interest-bearing loans from banks or universities involve sin due to the interest component.
  • However, a sinful transaction does not make all subsequent earnings haram. The sin is in the interest payment itself, not in every dollar earned after obtaining the degree.

docs.title: Talismans and Numerology Plaques
docs.text:
• Decorative designs are permissible.
  • Numbers representing Quranic surahs or ayat (numerology) are not haram, though some scholars are uncomfortable with the practice.
  • If the numbers represent something unknown or involve seeking help from shayateen, then it would be haram.

docs.title: Sleep Paralysis
docs.text:
• There is no specific mention of sleep paralysis in the Quran or hadith.
  • It is possible that it occurs due to jinn, but there is no definitive Islamic ruling on it.

docs.title: Witr Prayer in Saudi Arabia (Hanafi Following Hanbali Jama'ah in Ramadan)
docs.text:
• In Ramadan, praying witr with the jama'ah (even following a different madhhab's method, including raising hands and making du'a) is preferred.
  • One can pray the additional 10 raka'at of taraweeh individually afterwards.

docs.title: Upcoming Content
docs.text:
• The surahs following Al-Asr (Al-Humazah, Al-Fil, Quraysh, Al-Ma'un, and onward) will expand on the four principles outlined in Surah Al-Asr.
  • Last 10 nights of Ramadan: daily tafseer sessions after Taraweeh (approximately 20-25 minutes), live-streamed.

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session covers the complete tafseer of Surah Al-Humazah (Chapter 104). The surah is named after the word "Humazah" (slanderer), which appears in the first ayah. The session also introduces a broader framework connecting Surah Al-Asr through the next several surahs.

docs.title: Structural Connection to Surrounding Surahs
docs.text:
A major theme of the session is how Surahs 104-107 each describe a barrier that blocks the four success factors mentioned in Surah Al-Asr (103):

Surah Barrier Blocks Al-Humazah (104) Capitalism / extreme love of wealth Iman, Amal Salih, Haq, Sabr Al-Fil (105) Imperialism / colonialism (to be covered next session) Quraysh (106) Tribalism / nationalism (future session) Al-Ma'un (107) Fake religiosity (future session)
  • The four good things from Surah Al-Asr: (1) Iman, (2) Amal Salih, (3) Tawasi bil-Haq, (4) Tawasi bil-Sabr
  • The four bad things (barriers) from Surahs 104-107 block those four good things
  • Capitalism and imperialism support each other and go side by side

docs.title: 1. The Nature of Capitalism as a Spiritual Disease
docs.text:
• The surah is not merely about backbiting in general; it is specifically about those who slander and degrade others as a result of their obsession with wealth
  • Capitalism is defined as an ideology of unlimited wealth accumulation at the expense of others
  • A person does not need to be rich to be a capitalist -- having the intention and desire to exploit others for wealth accumulation is sufficient
  • The extreme love of money is identified as the first barrier against spiritual success

docs.title: 2. The Specific Sin: Wealth-Driven Arrogance
docs.text:
• The humaza (slanderer) and lumaza (backbiter) described here are consequences of wealth obsession
  • Those who accumulate wealth look down upon the poor, deny them a voice, and degrade them
  • They believe their wealth makes them immortal (a false sense of permanence and security)
  • They are stingy and do not share wealth because they acquired it by exploiting others

docs.title: 3. The Punishment: Al-Hutamah (The Crusher)
docs.text:
• A special category of Hellfire reserved for these capitalists
  • Not ordinary fire -- ignited by Allah Himself, fueled not by firewood but by the sins (ma'asi) of the people
  • The fire reaches the hearts first because the heart is where the lust for wealth resided
  • The punishment involves being confined in extended columns (like being trapped in a tight pipe), closed from both sides, with fire burning inside and outside simultaneously, with no room to move or shift position

docs.title: 4. Islamic Economic Position
docs.text:
• Islam is neither fully capitalist nor fully socialist; it occupies a middle position
  • Private ownership is permitted (property, land, homes, transportation)
  • The government has a regulatory role in the market
  • Monopoly is prohibited; the Islamic state must prevent any single entity from dominating a market

docs.title: Ayah 1: "Waylun li kulli humazatin lumazah"
docs.text:
• Wayl = Woe (severe warning/punishment)
  • Humazah = Slanderer
  • Lumazah = Backbiter
  • Grammatically: Lam is harfu jarr; kull is mudaf; humazah and lumazah are mudaf ilayh
  • This is not about every backbiter in general; it is about those who slander as a result of wealth obsession

docs.title: Ayah 2: "Alladhi jama'a maalan wa 'addadah"
docs.text:
• Alladhi = Ism al-mawsool (relative pronoun: "the one who")
  • Jama'a = Fi'il madhi (past tense verb: collected/accumulated)
  • Maalan = Maf'ool (object: wealth)
  • 'Addadah = Counted it (constantly counting wealth, indicating obsession and stinginess)
  • The counting implies they are consumed by tracking their wealth; it is their main concern in life
  • Connected to another Quranic usage: "jama'a fa aw'a" (in the 29th Juz) -- accumulated and hoarded

docs.title: Ayah 3: "Yahsabu anna maalahu akhladah"
docs.text:
• Yahsabu = From hasiba/yahsabu, meaning to think/guess/estimate
  • Akhladah = Will make him immortal; derived from khalid (immortal, the one who does not die)
  • Connected to the recurring Quranic phrase "khalideena feeha abada" (dwelling therein forever)
  • They build empires, mansions, and pursue extreme luxury, thinking they and their wealth are permanent

docs.title: Ayah 4: "Kalla la yunbadhanna fil hutamah"
docs.text:
• Kalla = Nay! (emphatic denial/rejection)
  • La yunbadhanna = Surely he will be thrown; contains double emphasis (lam + nun of emphasis), meaning there should be no shadow of doubt
  • Al-Hutamah = The Crusher; from hatama/yahtimu (pattern of daraba/yadribu), meaning to crush

docs.title: Ayah 5: "Wa ma adraka mal hutamah"
docs.text:
• Wa ma adraka = And what will make you know?
  • When Allah uses this phrase, it directs attention to the fact that human senses, rationality, and consciousness alone cannot comprehend this reality -- it can only be understood if Allah explains it
  • Allah then explains it in the next ayah

docs.title: Ayah 6: "Naaru Allahil muqadah"
docs.text:
• Naaru Allah = The fire of Allah (attributed directly to Allah, emphasizing its severity)
  • Al-Muqadah = Ignited/kindled (by Allah Himself)
  • This is not ordinary fire; it is a divinely ignited fire

docs.title: Ayah 7: "Allati tattali'u 'alal af'idah"
docs.text:
• Allati = Ism mawsool (which)
  • Tattali'u = From ittala'a/yattali'u; meanings include "to be aware of" and "to mount up / reach"
  • Al-Af'idah = Plural of fu'ad (heart)
  • The fire targets the hearts first because the heart is where the disease of wealth-lust originated
  • Connected to the hadith: "There is an organ in the body; if it is sound, the whole body is sound, and if it is corrupt, the whole body is corrupt -- indeed it is the heart (al-qalb)"
  • Fu'ad and qalb are synonyms, both meaning heart

docs.title: Ayah 8: "Innaha 'alayhim mu'sadah"
docs.text:
• Innaha = Indeed it (referring to the fire)
  • 'Alayhim = Upon them (the slanderers, backbiters, wealth-hoarders)
  • Mu'sadah = Closed over / sealed shut
  • The fire will be closed over them with no window, opening, or gap

docs.title: Ayah 9: "Fi 'amadin mumaddadah"
docs.text:
• 'Amad = Columns/pillars
  • Mumaddadah = Extended/stretched out
  • Description: columns taller and wider than a human body but tight enough that the person inside cannot move -- like being squeezed into a pipe
  • Closed from both top and bottom, no opening or escape
  • Fire burns both inside and outside these columns
  • The person cannot even shift position to seek any relief from pain
  • In worldly pain, people naturally move and shift positions to find comfort; this will be impossible in Al-Hutamah

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Humazah Slanderer Lumazah Backbiter Jama'a Collected, accumulated 'Addadah Counted it Yahsabu He thinks, estimates, guesses Akhladah Will make him immortal Khalid / Khalideen Immortal / those who dwell forever Al-Hutamah The Crusher (a category of Hellfire) Hatama / Yahtimu To crush La yunbadhanna Surely he will be thrown (double emphasis) Wa ma adraka And what will make you know (rhetorical; indicates incomprehensibility without divine explanation) Naaru Allah The fire of Allah Al-Muqadah Ignited, kindled Tattali'u Mounts up to, reaches Al-Af'idah Hearts (plural of fu'ad) Fu'ad / Qalb Heart (synonyms) Mu'sadah Closed over, sealed shut 'Amad Columns, pillars Mumaddadah Extended, stretched out Ma'asi Sins Harfu jarr Preposition particle Mudaf / Mudaf ilayh Possessive construction (annexed / annexed to) Ism al-mawsool Relative pronoun Fi'il madhi Past tense verb Maf'ool Object (grammatical) Fa'il Subject / doer (grammatical) Harful 'atf Conjunction particle

docs.title: Hadith References
docs.text:
1. The ignition of Hellfire: Rasulullah (sallallahu alayhi wasallam) said that Allah ignited the fire in Hell and it burned for 1,000 years until it turned red, then another 1,000 years until it turned white, then another 1,000 years until it turned black. This describes the general Hellfire; Al-Hutamah is a special category within it, ignited later.

  2. The importance of the heart: Rasulullah (sallallahu alayhi wasallam) said: "There is a piece of flesh in the body; if it is sound, the whole body is sound, and if it is corrupt, the whole body will be corrupt. Indeed, it is the heart (al-qalb)."

docs.title: Grammatical Notes
docs.text:
• Lam in "li kulli" is harfu jarr (preposition)
  • Kull is mudaf; humazah and lumazah are mudaf ilayh
  • Alladhi is ism al-mawsool (relative pronoun)
  • Jama'a is fi'il madhi with hidden damir as fa'il; maalan is maf'ool
  • Waw in "wa 'addadah" is harful 'atf (conjunction particle)
  • 'Addadah is fi'il with damir as fa'il and "hu" as maf'ool
  • Akhladah follows the af'ala pattern; hidden damir is fa'il referring to malahu
  • La yunbadhanna has double emphasis: lam for emphasis + nun of emphasis
  • Allati is ism al-mawsool (feminine relative pronoun)
  • Tattali'u from ittala'a/yattali'u

docs.title: Lessons and Practical Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Wealth obsession is a spiritual barrier: The extreme love of money blocks all four pillars of success from Surah Al-Asr -- it undermines iman, prevents good deeds, stops people from speaking truth, and destroys patience.

  2. Capitalism is an ideology, not just a financial status: A person does not need to be wealthy to be a capitalist. If one has the intention to exploit others for financial gain and would do so given the opportunity, that person carries the same spiritual disease.

  3. Wealth accumulation leads to moral corruption: The sequence in the surah shows how collecting and obsessively counting wealth leads to arrogance, slandering the poor, and a delusional sense of immortality.

  4. The heart is the origin of spiritual disease: The fire of Al-Hutamah targets the hearts first because that is where the greed and lust for wealth took root. Purifying the heart is essential.

  5. The punishment fits the crime: Those who trapped others in financial slavery (debt, exploitation) will themselves be trapped in columns with no room to move. Those who hoarded and refused to share will be enclosed with no escape.

  6. Islam offers a balanced economic model: Neither pure capitalism nor pure socialism. Private ownership is allowed, but the state regulates markets and prevents monopolies to ensure justice.

  7. Self-examination is necessary: One should not assume this surah only applies to billionaires. The attitude and intention matter more than the actual amount of wealth one possesses.

  8. The four barriers are interconnected: Capitalism supports imperialism, which supports tribalism, which enables fake religiosity. Understanding and rejecting all four is necessary to achieve the success outlined in Surah Al-Asr.

docs.title: Preview of Next Session
docs.text: • Surah Al-Fil (105): Will cover the second barrier -- imperialism/colonialism, through the story of Abraha's attempt to attack Makkah and destroy the Kaaba.

docs.title: TFS 102 - Study Notes
docs.text: Date: April 25, 2024 Surah Covered: Surah Al-Feel (Chapter 105)

docs.title: Key Themes and Topics
docs.text:
• Imperialism and its consequences: The surah serves as a divine warning against all forms of imperialism -- subjugating others to one's rule, whether through dictatorship, colonialism, kingdom, or even democratic facades.
  • Connection between Surah Al-Humaza and Surah Al-Feel: Surah Al-Humaza condemned capitalism (love of wealth); Surah Al-Feel condemns imperialism. Both are linked back to Surah Al-Asr, which identifies things that block success in the akhira.
  • Neo-colonialism as modern imperialism: The concept that colonialism has evolved -- countries now invade, extract resources, and install puppet governments while maintaining a veneer of democracy.
  • Divine protection of the Ka'bah: Allah directly intervened to protect His House from Abraha's army, demonstrating that He defends what He wills.
  • The difference between Khilafah and imperialism: Khilafah is based on shura (consultation), accountability, and the sovereignty of Allah's law -- not the personal rule of a king or emperor.

docs.title: Important Vocabulary (Arabic Terms)
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Al-Feel The Elephant (name of the surah) Alam tara Have you not seen? (Istifham / rhetorical question) Alam Negation particle; when added to fi'l mudari', gives meaning of negative past tense Kayd Plan, plot, scheme Tadleel Going astray, misguidance; here meaning Allah ruined their plan Tayran Ababeel Birds in flocks; Ababeel is not a species name but means "in flocks" Tarmee To strike, to pelt Bi-hijaratin min sijjeel With stones of baked clay Ka-'asfin ma'kool Like eaten-up straw (chewed and destroyed by animals) Ashabu Al-Feel The People/Companions of the Elephant Ashabu Al-Ukhdood The People of the Trench (from Surah Al-Buruj) Rabt Connection (between surahs) Istifham Interrogative / question form Fi'l Mudari' Present/imperfect tense verb Jazm Jussive case (grammatical state of the verb after "alam") Fa'il Subject of the verb Dhamir Pronoun Harf Jarr Preposition Mudaf ilayh Possessive construction (second element) Khilafah Islamic system of governance based on shura and divine law Shura Consultation Jizya Tax paid by non-Muslims under Islamic governance Huthala / Hufala Useless remnants (like chaff filtered from grains)

docs.title: Ayah-by-Ayah Breakdown
docs.text:
1. "Alam tara kayfa fa'ala rabbuka bi-ashaabil feel?"
  2. Rhetorical question: "Have you not seen how your Lord dealt with the People of the Elephant?"
  3. "Alam" added to mudari' gives negative past tense meaning.

  4. The original verb ra'a (to see) has its last letter dropped due to the effect of "alam" -- a sarf (morphology) principle.

  5. "Alam yaj'al kaydahum fee tadleel?"

  6. "Did He not make their plan go astray?"
  7. The fa'il (subject) of yaj'al is the hidden pronoun referring back to "Rabbuka."
  8. Yaj'al is in the jazm case because of "alam."

  9. Allah completely ruined Abraha's plan.

  10. "Wa arsala 'alayhim tayran ababeel"

  11. "And He sent against them birds in flocks."
  12. Arsala (to send) is the root of Rasul (messenger).

  13. The exact species of the birds is unknown -- Allahu a'lam.

  14. "Tarmeehim bi-hijaratin min sijjeel"

  15. "Striking them with stones of baked clay."
  16. The stones were specifically described as baked clay, not ordinary stones.

  17. Each pebble acted like a bullet upon impact.

  18. "Fa ja'alahum ka-'asfin ma'kool"

  19. "And He made them like eaten-up straw."
  20. Like grass that has been grazed by animals -- completely crushed and ruined.
  21. The fa'il is Rabbuka; "hum" refers to Ashabul Feel.

docs.title: Why Details Are Brief in the Surah
docs.text:
• The surah was revealed in Makkah approximately 45 years after the event.
  • The incident occurred roughly 50 days before the birth of the Prophet (sallallahu alayhi wasallam).
  • Many people alive at the time of revelation either witnessed the event firsthand or heard about it from eyewitnesses.
  • Since everyone already knew the story, Allah did not elaborate on the details.

docs.title: Historical Context: The Story of Ashabul Feel
Background -- Ashabul Ukhdood (70 years before the Prophet's birth)
docs.text:
1. A tribe called Himyar existed in Yemen, led by Abu Nawas, a mushrik (polytheist).
  2. Over 20,000 people in his land accepted Tawheed according to the religion of Isa (alayhi salatu wasallam).
  3. Abu Nawas dug a trench (ukhdood), threw them in, and burned them alive.
  4. Two or three survivors escaped to Rome, a Christian civilization, and reported the massacre.
  5. The Roman emperor ordered the Abyssinian king to intervene.

docs.title: Abyssinian Conquest of Yemen
docs.text:
• Two army chiefs were sent: Aryat and Abraha.
  • They conquered Yemen, killed Abu Nawas, and established Abyssinian rule.
  • Yemen became an extension of the Abyssinian (Christian) empire.
  • Aryat and Abraha later fought each other; Abraha took sole control.

docs.title: Abraha's Rule and the Attack on the Ka'bah
docs.text:
1. Abraha became an oppressive ruler, similar to Abu Nawas.
  2. The people of Yemen (and all Arabs) still revered the Ka'bah and traveled to Makkah for Hajj.
  3. Abraha built a massive church as a localized substitute for the Ka'bah and ordered people to go there instead.
  4. Two incidents enraged Abraha:
  5. An Arab man entered the temple and urinated in it as a gesture of insult.
  6. An Arab caravan camped nearby and the temple caught fire (intentionally or accidentally -- both opinions exist).
  7. Abraha decided to attack and destroy the Ka'bah as punishment.

docs.title: The March to Makkah
docs.text:
• Abraha assembled a massive army featuring elephants; the plan was to tie ropes to the elephants and pull down the Ka'bah.
  • No tribe in the Arabian Peninsula attempted to stop him -- tribes were divided (pro-Persian, pro-Roman, or independent).
  • The army reached Wadi Al-Muhassar (between Muzdalifah and Makkah) and camped there.

docs.title: Abdul Muttalib's Meeting with Abraha
docs.text:
• Abraha's army seized several hundred camels belonging to Abdul Muttalib, the chief and caretaker of the Ka'bah.
  • Abdul Muttalib went to negotiate and impressed Abraha with his personality.
  • Abdul Muttalib asked only for his camels back.
  • Abraha was surprised: "I came to destroy your temple and you are asking about camels?"
  • Abdul Muttalib replied: "I am the owner of the camels, so I came for them. The Ka'bah has its own Lord (Rabb) who will protect it."

docs.title: Divine Intervention
docs.text:
• When the army marched toward Makkah, the elephants refused to move despite repeated attempts over several days.
  • Birds came in flocks carrying pebbles in their beaks and dropped them on the army.
  • Each pebble struck like a bullet, injuring upon contact.
  • Abraha was not killed on the spot but fled; every place a pebble touched his body became an open wound.
  • He died upon reaching Yemen, and everyone learned of his fate.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• ~70 years before the Prophet's birth: Incident of Ashabul Ukhdood.
  • A few years later: Abyssinian conquest of Yemen and Abraha's rise to power.
  • ~50 days before the Prophet's birth: Abraha's attack on the Ka'bah and divine destruction.
  • ~45 years after the event: Surah Al-Feel revealed in Makkah.

docs.title: Lessons and Practical Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Imperialism in all its forms leads to destruction. Whether ancient military conquest or modern neo-colonialism, subjugating others invites divine punishment and blocks success in both dunya and akhira.

  2. Even supporting imperialism is blameworthy. One does not have to be a king or dictator to fall under this warning -- supporting or enabling oppressive systems makes a person a manifestation of this story.

  3. Khilafah is not imperialism. Islamic governance is distinguished from imperialism by: shura (consultation), accountability of the leader, sovereignty of Allah's law (not the ruler's word), and the fact that conquered lands' resources stayed with their people.

  4. The spread of Islam vs. colonialism. When Muslims entered other lands historically, they did not extract resources to enrich the center. Non-Muslims were given options: peace treaty, jizya, or acceptance of Islam. Resources of conquered lands (Iraq, Sham, etc.) were not funneled back to Medina.

  5. Allah protects what He wills. The Ka'bah had no military defense, yet Allah sent birds with stones of baked clay to annihilate Abraha's army. Reliance on Allah (tawakkul) is demonstrated by Abdul Muttalib's statement: the Lord of the Ka'bah will defend it.

  6. Trust in Allah does not mean human passivity. Abdul Muttalib still went to negotiate for what was in his power (his camels) while entrusting the Ka'bah's defense to Allah.

  7. Critical thinking about modern ideologies is essential. Without understanding modern political systems, liberalism, secularism, and their mechanisms, one cannot fully comprehend 60-70% of the Quran and Sunnah, which address governance, justice, and societal order.

  8. Scholars must be evaluated by their positions, not their appearance. Having a title (Mufti, Mawlana, Sheikh), a turban, or a long beard does not validate a scholar's stance. What matters is whether their teachings align with the Quran and Sunnah.

  9. The hadith about end-times "huthala." The Prophet (sallallahu alayhi wasallam) said that near the Day of Judgment, most people will be like useless chaff -- easily manipulated, subscribing to ideologies without investigation.

docs.title: Connection to Previous and Upcoming Surahs
docs.text:
• Surah Al-Asr: Identifies obstacles to success in the akhira, including love of capital (addressed in Al-Humaza) and imperialism (addressed in Al-Feel).
  • Surah Al-Humaza (previous session): Condemned capitalism and obsessive accumulation of wealth.
  • Surah Al-Feel (this session): Condemns imperialism and the subjugation of others.
  • Surah Al-Buruj: Contains the story of Ashabul Ukhdood, which is the historical precursor to the story of Ashabul Feel.

docs.title: Discussion Points Raised in Class
docs.text:
• Is there such a thing as "Islamic imperialism"? The term was coined by Western writers. Khilafah is fundamentally different from imperialism due to shura, accountability, and divine law as the source of legislation.
  • Duty to spread Islam vs. imperialism: Muslims have a responsibility to spread the word of Allah, but this is distinct from resource extraction or governance imposition. If force was used, it was because other civilizations blocked the message, and non-Muslims were still given multiple options.
  • Response to scholars promoting problematic positions: One should engage them on evidence (daleel), not accept fatawa blindly, and measure scholars by the yardstick of whether they uphold traditional Islamic principles.

docs.title: TFS 102 - Study Notes
docs.text: Date: May 30, 2024 Surah Covered: Surah Al-Quraysh (Chapter 106)

docs.title: Key Themes and Topics
docs.text:
• Tribalism and Nationalism: The central theme of the surah is Allah's condemnation of tribalism. The Quraysh developed a sense of pride based on their noble lineage and status as guardians of the Ka'bah. Allah reminds them that their privilege is a blessing requiring gratitude, not arrogance. This critique extends to modern nationalism -- taking excessive pride in one's nation, placing national identity above all else including the Quran and Sunnah.

  • Connection of Four Surahs to Surah Al-Asr: Four consecutive surahs address barriers that prevent a person from success (as outlined in Surah Al-Asr):

  • Surah Al-Humazah -- condemns capitalism (hoarding wealth, materialism)
  • Surah Al-Feel -- condemns colonialism/imperialism (Abraha's attempt to colonize Makkah)
  • Surah Al-Quraysh -- condemns tribalism/nationalism

  • Surah Al-Ma'un -- condemns fake religiosity (to be covered in the next class)

  • Gratitude and Worship: Allah instructs the Quraysh to worship the Lord of the Ka'bah in recognition of the blessings He bestowed upon them -- provision (food) and security.

  • The Ka'bah and the Refutation of Idol Worship: An important theological clarification: Muslims face the Ka'bah in prayer but do not worship it. The distinction between facing a direction and worshipping an object.

docs.title: Important Vocabulary (Arabic Terms)
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Ilaf Familiarity; a masdar (verbal noun) Rihlah Journey, trip Shita' Winter Sayf Summer Fal ya'budu "So let them worship" -- amr (command) for 3rd person plural masculine Rabb Lord, Sustainer Al-Bayt The House (referring to the Ka'bah) At'amahum He fed them Ju' Hunger Amana To give security, to make safe Aman Security, safety Khawf Fear Ma'kul Eaten (ism al-maf'ul from akala -- to eat) Asf Straw Tasgheer Diminutive noun form; can be used for insult or honor Tijara Trade, business/commerce Ummah The Muslim community (as opposed to "nation") Darul Islam Abode of Islam Darul Kufr / Darul Harb Abode of disbelief / Abode of war

docs.title: Grammatical Points
docs.text:
• Li-ilafi -- The lam is a harf jar (preposition) and must be connected to something. Two opinions exist on what it connects to (see Tafseer Points below).
  • Ilaf is a masdar (verbal noun). Just as a verb has a fa'il (subject) and maf'ul (object), a masdar can also have its own fa'il and maf'ul.
  • Rihlata is in the nasab case because it is the maf'ul of the masdar ilaf (mudaf/mudaf ilayh construction).
  • Quraysh is a tasgheer (diminutive) form of the original word "Qurashi." Here the tasgheer is used in an honorary sense.
  • Ism al-maf'ul (passive participle) like ma'kul can take harf jar and majrur connections just as a verb can -- this is an advanced nahw (grammar) concept.
  • The Basran and Kufan schools of grammar (nahw) did contribute to differences in ijtihad among the Imams. Imam Abu Hanifa's approach aligned with the Kufan school, as he was from Kufa.

docs.title: Structure and Connection of "Li-ilaf"
docs.text:
Two scholarly opinions on what the preposition "li" in "li-ilafi Quraysh" connects to:

  1. Connected to the last word of Surah Al-Feel ("ma'kul"): Allah made the army of Abraha like eaten-up straw because of the familiarity/status of the Quraysh. The lam connects backward to the previous surah.

  2. Connected to "fal ya'budu" later in the surah: Because of the familiarity of the Quraysh with their journeys, they should worship the Lord of this House. The lam connects forward within the same surah.

docs.title: Historical Background
docs.text:
• Nadr bin Kinanah -- the 12th grandfather of the Prophet (sallallahu alayhi wasallam). His descendants are called Quraysh.
  • Hashim -- the Prophet's great-grandfather (grandfather of Abdul Muttalib). He was the one who advised the people of Makkah to adopt trade as a profession since agriculture was impossible in the barren valley of Makkah.
  • Ibrahim (alayhi salam) described Makkah as a valley with no farming produce when he settled Ismail (alayhi salam) there.
  • The Quraysh traded in two directions:
  • Yemen (south) in winter
  • Sham/Syria (north) in summer (since Sham's winters were harsh with snow)
  • Some Quraysh built large commercial empires. They had an equitable profit-sharing system that controlled the wealth gap among them.
  • The nearby market in Jeddah was where they purchased grains (rice, wheat, barley).

docs.title: Two Blessings Mentioned in the Surah
docs.text:
1. "At'amahum min ju'" (Fed them against hunger): Before Hashim's recommendation of trade, the people of Makkah lived in dire economic conditions. Their only income came from the Hajj season. Hashim's advice to pursue trade, blessed by Allah, solved their hunger problem.

  2. "Amanahum min khawf" (Gave them security from fear): After the defeat of Abraha, the entire Arabian Peninsula recognized Makkah as a special, divinely protected place. The Quraysh were respected everywhere. Their caravans traveled without guards -- simply identifying themselves as Qurayshi was enough to guarantee safe passage.

docs.title: The Ka'bah -- Direction vs. Object of Worship
docs.text:
• Allah says "Rabb hadhal bayt" (Lord of this House), not just "this House." Even the Ka'bah has a Lord above it.
  • Evidence that Muslims do not worship the Ka'bah:
  • Throughout the entire salah, from takbir to taslim, the word "Ka'bah" is never mentioned in praise. All praise is directed to Allah.
  • If the Ka'bah were moved from its location, Muslims would continue facing the original direction, not follow the structure. Idol worshippers, by contrast, follow the idol wherever it is placed.
  • If the Ka'bah were destroyed, prayer would not be interrupted. The Ka'bah has been rebuilt multiple times in history, and Muslims continued praying during reconstruction.
  • What matters is the direction (qiblah), not the physical structure itself.

docs.title: Lessons and Practical Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Privilege demands gratitude, not pride. Being born into a noble family, a powerful nation, or favorable circumstances is a blessing from Allah. Greater blessings carry greater responsibility. Taking pride in them is a form of ingratitude.

  2. The Prophet's example of humility: He said he will be the leader of the children of Adam on the Day of Judgment, "wa la fakhr" -- but he takes no pride in it. Leadership and honor are met with humility, not arrogance.

  3. Nationalism is the modern form of tribalism. Just as the Quraysh took excessive pride in their tribal identity, modern people do the same with their national identities. The Quranic condemnation of tribalism applies equally to nationalism.

  4. "My country first" is a problematic statement when it means placing national interest above the Sharia, the Quran, and the Sunnah. Borders are man-made and temporary. The land belongs to Allah. People routinely leave their nations for better opportunities, which exposes nationalism as an inconsistent ideology.

  5. The Islamic framework recognizes Ummah, not nation. The concept of nation-state is a modern construct. While living in a nation-state is a practical reality, one's belief system should maintain that Islam and its teachings are superior to any national allegiance.

  6. The four barriers to success (capitalism, colonialism, tribalism, fake religiosity) are interconnected obstacles that prevent practicing the four pillars of success in Surah Al-Asr (iman, righteous deeds, enjoining truth, enjoining patience).

  7. Story of Abu Jahl at Badr illustrates extreme tribalism: even while mortally wounded and defeated, Abu Jahl mocked Abdullah ibn Mas'ud (radiyallahu anhu) as "the son of a shepherd" and boasted of his own Qurayshi lineage. Tribalistic pride can persist even in the face of death.

  8. Worship must be directed to the Creator, not the creation. The Ka'bah is honored as a direction of prayer, but all worship, praise, and devotion belong exclusively to Allah -- the Lord of the House, not the House itself.

docs.title: Q&A Highlights
docs.text:
• Q: Can one say "my Muslim nation is superior because it aligns more closely with Sharia"? A: The word "nation" carries specific modern connotations tied to the nation-state concept. It is better to use the word "Ummah." One can say that a particular country is closer to the Sharia, and therefore living there is preferable -- that is not haram. But the framing of national superiority should be avoided.

  • Q: Did the differences between the Basran and Kufan schools of grammar affect the ijtihad of the Imams? A: Yes, in some cases. Imam Abu Hanifa's grammatical understanding aligned with the Kufan school, as he was from Kufa.

docs.title: TFS 102 - Study Notes
docs.text: Date: June 6, 2024 Surah Covered: Surah Al-Ma'un (Chapter 107)

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text:
• Surah Al-Ma'un consists of 7 ayat
  • Named after the last word of the surah: "Al-Ma'un"
  • Position of the majority of Mufassirin: it is a Makki (Meccan) surah
  • Some Mufassirin hold that the surah was revealed in two parts (first part in Makkah, second in Madinah), because the latter portion deals with Munafiqeen who did not exist in Makkah. However, the majority view is that the entire surah is Makki, since Allah knew Munafiqeen would later exist and wanted to prepare the believers in advance.

docs.title: Connection to Previous Surahs
docs.text:
This surah is part of a sequence following Surah Al-Asr, identifying obstacles that prevent a person from acting upon its message (iman, good deeds, mutual enjoining of truth, and mutual enjoining of patience):

# Obstacle Surah 1 Capitalism - excessive love of wealth/capital Surah Al-Humazah 2 Imperialism / Colonialism - love of power and domination Surah Al-Fil 3 Tribalism / Nationalism - pride in tribe or nation Surah Quraysh 4 Fake Religiosity - insincere worship and showing off Surah Al-Ma'un

Direct connection to Surah Quraysh: The Quraysh were given guardianship (tawliyah) of the Ka'bah and commanded to worship its Lord. Over time, they developed tribal pride from this guardianship while maintaining a pretense of religiousness. This insincerity (nifaq) eventually led to shirk.

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Al-Ma'un Small kindness / trivial assistance / minor favor Ikhlas Sincerity (in worship and practice) Nifaq Hypocrisy Munafiqeen Hypocrites Yadu' al-Yateem Repulses / pushes away the orphan Al-Miskeen The poor / needy person Yura'oon They show off / engage in ostentation (riya') Sahoon Neglectful / heedless Wayl Woe / severe punishment Al-Musalleen Those who pray Fajirun Shakiyyun Sinful and wretched/miserable Barrun Taqiyyun Righteous and God-fearing Irtifaqat Shah Waliullah's theory of civilizational progression

docs.title: Ayah 1: "Have you seen the one who denies the Day of Judgment?"
docs.text:
• A person who practices religion in a fake manner is, through his behavior (not necessarily his words), denying the Day of Judgment (Yawm al-Din)
  • If he truly believed in accountability before Allah, he would not merely pretend to be religious
  • Fake religiosity is itself an implicit denial of divine accountability

docs.title: Ayah 2: "That is the one who repulses the orphan"
docs.text:
• First side effect of fake religiosity: Loss of mercy and kindness
  • When a person believes there is no accountability, he becomes tyrannical (like Fir'awn, Haman, Shaddad)
  • He pushes away orphans and refuses to help them

docs.title: Ayah 3: "And does not encourage the feeding of the poor"
docs.text:
• Second side effect: Extreme stinginess/miserliness
  • Two meanings: (1) He does not feel the urge to feed the poor himself, (2) He does not even encourage others to feed the poor
  • Encouraging others to spend is far easier than spending oneself, yet he refuses even that -- indicating the highest level of stinginess

docs.title: Ayah 4-6: "Woe to those who pray, who are neglectful of their prayer, who show off"
docs.text:
• Third side effect: Prayer without sincerity; showing off (riya')
  • They pray but have no heart in their prayer; they simply go through the motions
  • No consideration of sunan (recommended acts), mustahabbat (desirable acts), or adab (etiquette) of salah
  • Allah condemns them despite their praying because the prayer is not for His sake

docs.title: Ayah 7: "And they deny small kindnesses (Al-Ma'un)"
docs.text:
• Fourth side effect: Withholding even trivial favors
  • Examples: refusing to give someone a cup of water, refusing to make dua for someone, refusing to use one's influence to recommend someone when it would be easy to do so
  • Demonstrates extreme harshness and selfishness

docs.title: Nahw (Grammar) Analysis of Ayat 4-6
docs.text:
• "Fa" is a conjunction particle (harf 'atf)
  • "Waylun" is khabar muqaddam (fronted predicate)
  • "Lil-musalleen alladhina hum 'an salatihim sahoon" is mubtada' mu'akhkhar (delayed subject)
  • "Alladhina" is ism mawsul (relative pronoun); "hum 'an salatihim sahoon" is its silah (relative clause)
  • "Hum" is mubtada'; "sahoon 'an salatihim" is the khabar

docs.title: Abu Jahl and the Orphans
docs.text:
• Abu Jahl (whose real name was Abu al-Hakam) would exploit dying men: he would offer to become the caretaker of their wealth and children
  • After the person's death, he would seize the entire wealth and give nothing to the orphan
  • One such orphan came to Rasulullah (sallallahu alayhi wa sallam) and complained
  • The Prophet went to Abu Jahl and advised him; Allah placed such awe (ru'b) in Abu Jahl's heart that he returned the wealth to the orphan
  • This illustrates the behavior of those who "repulse the orphan"

docs.title: Hadith: Developing Kindness Through Caring for Orphans
docs.text:
• A sahabi complained to the Prophet that he felt harshness in his heart
  • The Prophet advised him to place his hand on the head of an orphan (i.e., show kindness and care for orphans)
  • Over time, this practice would develop genuine kindness in one's heart

docs.title: Hadith: Two Types of People
docs.text:
• The Prophet said that Allah has removed the pride of Jahiliyyah (pre-Islamic ignorance)
  • Now people are only of two types:
  • Barrun Taqiyyun Kareemun 'ala Allah -- righteous, God-fearing, and noble in the sight of Allah
  • Fajirun Shakiyyun -- sinful, wretched, and non-practicing

docs.title: Summary: Four Side Effects of Fake Religiosity
docs.text:
1. Repulsing the orphan -- loss of mercy and kindness
  2. Not feeding / not encouraging feeding the poor -- extreme stinginess
  3. Showing off in prayer -- worship without sincerity (riya')
  4. Denying small favors (al-ma'un) -- withholding even trivial kindnesses

These can also be condensed into two core problems: - Not having genuine kindness - Being fake in one's religious practice

docs.title: Lessons and Practical Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Sincerity (ikhlas) is the foundation of all worship. Without it, even prayer becomes condemnable. Religiosity without sincerity is worse than it appears, because it is a form of implicit denial of the Day of Judgment.

  2. Self-check against fake religiosity. If a person finds in himself the combination of showing off, stinginess, harshness toward the weak, and unwillingness to do even small favors, this is a warning sign.

  3. Caring for orphans softens the heart. The Prophet's practical advice to develop kindness was to spend time with and care for orphans.

  4. Even small acts of kindness matter. Islam emphasizes not only major acts of charity but also trivial daily favors -- giving someone water, making dua for someone, or using one's influence to help others.

  5. Surah Al-Ma'un is the fourth in a series of warnings about what prevents a person from fulfilling the program of Surah Al-Asr (faith, good deeds, enjoining truth, and enjoining patience). After greed, imperialism, and tribalism, fake religiosity is the final obstacle identified.

  6. Fake religiosity leads to spiritual degeneration. The Quraysh example shows that insincerity (nifaq) eventually degenerates into outright shirk over time.

  7. There is no middle ground. As the hadith states, a person is either righteous and God-fearing, or sinful and wretched. Pretending to be religious while lacking sincerity places one in the latter category.

docs.title: Shah Waliullah's Theory of Irtifaqat (Civilizational Progression)
docs.text:
• Found in his books Hujjatullah al-Balighah and Al-Budur al-Bazighah
  • Four levels (irtifaqat):
  • Individual level -- personal conduct and development
  • Societal level -- how a society should behave
  • Regional level -- how different societies in the same region interact; includes formation of regional government
  • International level -- how nations deal with each other internationally; includes the concept of an international governing body (similar to the United Nations) -- proposed roughly 300 years ago

docs.title: Brief Discussion on Attitude Toward Muslim Rulers Who Secularize
docs.text:
• First approach (majority position): One should not rebel against rulers or cause anarchy, even if one disagrees with their policies
  • Second approach: Based on the hadith that obedience to the ruler is required unless one witnesses "kufran bawahan" (open, undeniable disbelief) -- some scholars argue secularization constitutes open kufr
  • Key principle: Even legitimate criticism of rulers must not lead to anarchy, bloodshed, or loss of life and property, which the Sharia strictly opposes
  • Abu Hanifa's position: He gave moral support to rebellion against unjust rulers but did not openly participate or (to the shaykh's knowledge) provide material support

docs.title: TFS 102 - Session 102 Study Notes
docs.text: Date: June 13, 2024 Surah Covered: Surah Al-Kawthar (108)

docs.title: 1. Introduction: The Miraculous Nature of Surah Al-Kawthar
docs.text:
• Surah Al-Kawthar is the smallest surah in the Quran, yet mufassireen have written entire books on its tafsir.
  • It serves as a proof of the Quran's miraculous nature: when Allah challenged mankind to produce anything like the Quran, even a single chapter, this smallest surah stands as the benchmark. If no one could match even this brief surah, the Quran is undeniably a miracle.
  • Analogy given: Just as most people do not understand Einstein's theory of relativity yet accept it on the authority of experts, those who do not know Arabic should accept the Quran's miraculous nature on the testimony of those who understand it. One must learn the sciences behind it before raising objections.
  • The teacher criticized the double standard (called "scientistic" rather than scientific): people accept scientific claims on expert authority without question but become skeptical about the Quran's miraculous nature simply because they personally do not understand Arabic or the relevant background sciences.

docs.title: Connection with the Previous Surah (Al-Ma'un, 107)
docs.text:
• Surah Al-Ma'un discussed fake religiosity -- people who pretend to be religious but are not sincere, with specific focus on the Quraysh.
  • Surah Al-Kawthar follows by giving the glad tiding that the Quraysh will lose guardianship of the Ka'bah due to their fake religiosity, and it will be given to Rasulullah (SAW), who was the most sincere and true servant of Allah.
  • Universal rule: Whoever has fake religiosity does not deserve guardianship of Ka'batullah. This applies in every era -- when rulers become insincere and move away from the deen, their guardianship will eventually be taken away, just as it was taken from the Quraysh (though it took approximately 21 years after the announcement of prophethood).

docs.title: Connection with the Next Surah (Al-Kafirun, 109)
docs.text:
• The next surah contains a subtle indication toward hijrah (migration).
  • The sequence communicates: to achieve guardianship of the Ka'bah, a period of hardship (hijrah) must come first.
  • The glad tiding is mentioned before the hardship -- a rhetorical strategy. Good news is given first, then the difficulty required to achieve it. When the Sahabah understood this sequence, they were ready to endure the hardship because they knew the promise that awaited them.
  • This ordering demonstrates the fasahah (eloquence) of the Quran.

docs.title: 3. Key Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Al-Kawthar Sigatul mubalagha (hyperbolic form) from "kathir" (abundant); means abundant good (al-khayr al-kathir); also refers to Al-Hawdh Al-Kawthar (the Basin/Pool on the Day of Judgment) Sigatul mubalagha The grammatical form of exaggeration/hyperbole in Arabic Fasahah Eloquence Salli Pray / offer salah (imperative form) Wanhar Sacrifice (specifically: sacrifice a camel; nahara/yanharu = to slaughter a camel) Al-Abtar The one who is cut off (from progeny, legacy, remembrance) Huwal abtar "He is the one cut off" -- uses damir al-fasl (pronoun of separation) for emphasis/restriction Damir al-fasl Pronoun of separation -- limits the khabar to the mubtada and vice versa (grammatical restriction) Taqyeed Restriction/limitation (grammatical concept) Tadabbur Deep contemplation of Quranic meanings, understanding subtle indications (permissible for scholars) Tafsir bi al-ra'y Interpretation by personal opinion (contrasted with tadabbur; tafsir requires evidence from Rasulullah, Sahabah, or Tabi'in) Muluk Kings/rulers Nahar Specifically the slaughter of a camel (distinct from general sacrifice)

docs.title: Ayah 1: "Indeed, We have given you Al-Kawthar"
docs.text:
Two scholarly opinions on the meaning of Al-Kawthar:

  1. Sayyida Aisha (RA): Al-Kawthar refers specifically to Al-Hawdh Al-Kawthar -- the Pool/Basin on the Day of Judgment, where Rasulullah (SAW) will give water to the believers.
  2. Ibn Abbas (RA): Al-Kawthar means al-khayr al-kathir (abundant good) -- a general term that includes Al-Hawdh Al-Kawthar but also encompasses:
  3. The Quran
  4. Wisdom

  5. Guardianship of the Ka'bah, Makkah, Madinah, and the entire Hijaz region

  6. Ibn Abbas's interpretation is broader: Aisha (RA) limited it to the Pool, while Ibn Abbas generalized it to include all abundant blessings.

The Ka'bah as the worldly representation of Al-Hawdh Al-Kawthar:

The teacher drew extensive parallels between the Ka'bah and Al-Hawdh:

In the Dunya (Ka'bah) On the Day of Judgment (Al-Hawdh) Our direction (qiblah) is the Ka'bah Our direction will be Al-Hawdh We go around Ka'batullah (tawaf) People will go around the shores of Al-Hawdh Muslims journey toward the Ka'bah Muslims will walk toward Al-Hawdh seeking Rasulullah (SAW)

The Hadith of Rawdah and Al-Hawdh:

  • Rasulullah (SAW) said: "Between my house (the house of Aisha where he is buried) and my minbar is a garden from the gardens of Paradise (Rawdah)."
  • He also said: "My minbar is upon my Hawdh" -- even though the Hawdh is not physically in Madinah, this indicates the connection between that blessed land and Al-Hawdh.
  • In another hadith, the size of Al-Hawdh Al-Kawthar is described as the distance between Makkah and Madinah.
  • Based on these narrations, many commentators believe the entire land from Makkah to Madinah will become the land of Al-Hawdh on the Day of Judgment.
  • Therefore, "We have given you Al-Kawthar" includes both the eschatological Pool and the worldly guardianship of this blessed land.

docs.title: Ayah 2: "So pray to your Lord and sacrifice"
docs.text:
Every blessing comes with a responsibility. Immediately after the glad tiding, Allah states the obligation -- nothing in this world is free.

The teacher identified three words in this ayah that oppose three different ideologies:

docs.title: (a) "Fasalli" (So pray) -- Opposing the Christian Way
docs.text:
• Salah was originally commanded to Christians, Jews, and Muslims alike.
  • Christians completely abandoned salah; it no longer exists in their practice.
  • By commanding Rasulullah (SAW) to pray, Allah is sending a message to preserve the identity that Christians lost.
  • This means: do not follow the ways, culture, or lifestyle of those who abandoned salah.

docs.title: (b) "Li Rabbika" (To your Lord) -- Opposing the Mushrikeen
docs.text:
• The mushrikeen (polytheists) also performed acts of worship that superficially resembled Islamic worship (they had forms of ruku and sujud).
  • However, their prostration was complete lying down before idols.
  • "To your Lord" specifies: pray only to Allah, not to idols or multiple deities.
  • This distinguishes Muslim worship from polytheistic worship.

docs.title: (c) "Wanhar" (And sacrifice) -- Opposing the Jewish Way
docs.text:
• Notably, wherever Allah mentions salah in the Quran, He usually pairs it with zakat. Here, instead of zakat, He mentions nahar (sacrificing a camel).
  • This is the only place in the entire Quran where Allah specifically commands sacrificing a camel.
  • The camel was haram in the Shariah of Bani Israel (the Jews). By commanding its sacrifice, Allah is telling Muslims not to be intimidated by Jewish dietary laws or to honor those restrictions out of deference to them.
  • This preserves Muslim identity distinct from Jewish practice.

Implication for guardianship of Ka'bah: - Any ruler who wants to retain guardianship over Ka'bah must uphold all three of these principles. - If rulers adopt elements of these three ideologies (Christian lifestyle, polytheistic practices, normalization with those who oppose Islamic principles) instead of opposing them, they will eventually lose guardianship. - This is supported by Surah Muhammad (47:38): "If you turn away, Allah will replace you with another nation, and they will not be like you."

docs.title: Ayah 3: "Indeed, your enemy is the one cut off (al-abtar)"
docs.text:
Historical context: - Abu Jahl and Abu Lahab used to mock Rasulullah (SAW) because his sons died in infancy: - Ibrahim, Qasim, and Tayyib (or Tahir -- both names are narrated) all died young. - Rasulullah (SAW) had only daughters who survived. - Abu Lahab had 10 sons and was proud of his lineage, mocking the Prophet by saying his progeny was "cut off" (abtar) and no one would remember him.

Allah's response and prediction: - Allah declared that it is the enemy who is cut off, not Rasulullah (SAW). - Grammatical emphasis: "Huwa" is damir al-fasl (pronoun of separation), which restricts the predicate: "HE (your enemy) is the one cut off -- no one else."

Fulfillment of the prophecy: - Today, virtually no one knows the names of Abu Lahab's or Abu Jahl's sons (except those who accepted Islam and became Sahabah). - If anyone discovers Abu Lahab in their family tree, they hide it out of embarrassment. - Meanwhile, Rasulullah (SAW) -- despite having no surviving sons -- has: - Descendants who proudly identify as Ahlul Bayt - The entire Ummah that mentions his name multiple times every single day - This prediction came true not after 14 centuries, but within Rasulullah's own lifetime and during the Khilafah al-Rashidah.

docs.title: 5. Structure of the Surah: Two Glad Tidings and Two Responsibilities
docs.text:
Glad Tidings Responsibilities 1. You will be given al-khayr al-kathir (including guardianship of Ka'bah) 1. Fasalli -- Offer salah (and oppose the ways of those who abandoned it) 2. Your enemy is the one cut off, not you; you will be remembered forever 2. Wanhar -- Sacrifice (and preserve your distinct Islamic identity)
  • "Li Rabbika" is not an independent third responsibility but is connected to and qualifies "Fasalli" (pray specifically to your Lord alone).

docs.title: 6. Lessons and Practical Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Do not reject what you do not understand. Just as laypeople accept scientific theories on expert authority, one should accept the Quran's miraculous nature on the authority of those who have studied it. If skeptical, the answer is to learn -- not to dismiss.

  2. Fake religiosity leads to loss of blessings. This is a universal divine principle: insincere religiosity will eventually cause the loss of privilege and guardianship, whether for individuals, communities, or rulers.

  3. Blessings come with responsibilities. Every divine gift is accompanied by obligations. The guardianship of sacred spaces requires active maintenance of Islamic identity and worship.

  4. Preserve your distinct identity. Muslims are commanded not to assimilate into the religious practices or lifestyles of other communities. The three commands in Ayah 2 collectively mandate maintaining a distinct Islamic way of life.

  5. Rulers bear the greatest responsibility. The proverb was cited: "People follow the religion of their kings." When rulers are corrupt, corruption spreads. When rulers are pious, piety spreads.

  6. Ulama should remain close to rulers. Even if rulers are oppressive and do not listen, the presence of scholars provides hope that the next generation may change, as happened when Imam Muhammad's influence led a subsequent caliph to abandon the Mu'tazili ideology.

  7. Allah's promises are certain. The prediction that Rasulullah's enemies would be "cut off" while he would be remembered forever has been undeniably fulfilled -- a living proof of the Quran's truth.

  8. The worldly and the eschatological are connected. The Ka'bah and its surrounding blessed land serve as the worldly counterpart to Al-Hawdh Al-Kawthar on the Day of Judgment, showing that sacred geography in this life has real significance in the next.

docs.title: 7. Methodological Note: Tafsir vs. Tadabbur
docs.text:
• Tafsir = Explaining what Allah intended. This requires evidence from Rasulullah (SAW), the Sahabah, and the Tabi'in. Without such evidence, it would be tafsir bi al-ra'y (interpretation by personal opinion), which is not permissible.
  • Tadabbur = Contemplating and identifying subtle indications, hidden messages, and deeper connections in the ayat. This is permissible for scholars and does not claim to be "the intent of Allah" but rather additional layers of meaning that the text supports.
  • The three-ideology reading of Ayah 2 (opposing Christians, mushrikeen, and Jews) falls under tadabbur, not tafsir. It is found in books of tafsir as a scholarly contemplation, not as a definitive statement of divine intent.

docs.title: 8. Q&A Highlights
docs.text:
• Camel sacrifice and opposing the Jews: The camel was haram in the Shariah of Bani Israel; commanding its sacrifice in Islam is a deliberate distinction.
  • Guardianship and history: Even when the Ka'bah was ruled by deviant groups (e.g., the Fatimids), such rule did not last permanently. A century is a small time in the history of nations.
  • Bayt al-Maqdis (Jerusalem): The certain promise in hadith is that when Sayyiduna Isa ibn Maryam (AS) returns, the Jews will lose Bayt al-Maqdis. Some modern Arab mufassireen believe a second expulsion may occur before that, but this is speculative -- neither confirmed nor denied.
  • Moon sighting and Eid: Following local moon sighting is acknowledged by scholars as closest to the Sunnah. Those who follow global/Saudi sighting do so for the sake of Ummah unity, but true unity in prayer times is impossible across time zones. The teacher's personal practice: follow local sighting. If the entire community follows one method, follow them; if there are options, follow the one closest to the Sunnah.
  • Day of Arafah fasting and Eid sacrifice: Should be determined by the local 10th of Dhul Hijjah, not by Saudi Arabia's calendar.

docs.title: TFS 102 - Tafseer of Surah Al-Kafirun (Chapter 109)
docs.text: Date: June 27, 2024 Session: TFS 102 Surah Covered: Surah Al-Kafirun (The Disbelievers) - Chapter 109, 6 Ayat

docs.title: 1. Context Within the Curriculum
docs.text:
• This is the final surah in the year's curriculum.
  • Students had already completed recordings for the last five surahs: Al-Nasr, Al-Masad, Al-Ikhlas, Al-Falaq, and Al-Nas.

docs.title: 2. Text of the Surah
docs.text:
1. Qul ya ayyuhal kafirun -- Say, O disbelievers
  2. Laa a'budu maa ta'budun -- I do not worship what you worship
  3. Wa laa antum 'aabiduna maa a'bud -- Nor are you worshippers of what I worship
  4. Wa laa ana 'aabidun maa 'abadtum -- Nor will I be a worshipper of what you worship
  5. Wa laa antum 'aabiduna maa a'bud -- Nor will you be worshippers of what I worship
  6. Lakum deenukum wa liya deen -- For you is your religion, and for me is my religion

docs.title: A. No Coexistence Between Tawhid and Shirk
docs.text:
• The central theme of the surah is that coexistence between the believers (Muslims) and the mushrikeen on matters of worship and creed is not possible.
  • A firm line is drawn: there is no middle ground between worshipping Allah alone and worshipping false gods.

docs.title: B. Warning and Preparation for Difficult Times
docs.text:
• The surah is not a glad tiding; it is a warning to the kuffar and a signal to the Muslims that challenging times are approaching.
  • It served as a hidden hint that a major command (hijrah) would be coming.

docs.title: C. Rejection of the Compromise Deal
docs.text:
• The mushrikeen of Makkah proposed a deal: they would add Allah to their gods if Muhammad (sallallahu alayhi wa sallam) would stop condemning their idols.
  • Rasulullah (sallallahu alayhi wa sallam) rejected this categorically. He was a true messenger and would not compromise the message.

docs.title: D. Misrepresentation of "Lakum Deenukum Wa Liya Deen"
docs.text:
• This ayah is widely misquoted to support the idea of religious pluralism or peaceful mutual acceptance of all faiths.
  • The correct understanding within the surah's context is the opposite: it is a declaration of total separation, not harmonious coexistence.
  • Muslims are obligated to continue giving da'wah and declaring falsehood as falsehood. The ayah does not mean "you enjoy your religion, I enjoy mine, and we leave each other alone."
  • Disconnecting this ayah from the rest of the surah leads to misrepresentation.

docs.title: E. Interfaith Dialogue Critique
docs.text:
• Interfaith is described as a "scam" and a multi-million dollar industry.
  • No prophet from Adam to Muhammad (sallallahu alayhi wa sallam) engaged in interfaith; all of them engaged in da'wah.
  • The purpose of engaging with people of other faiths should be to teach them about Islam, not to learn about their religions as equals.
  • An anecdote was shared about a synagogue interfaith meeting where disrespectful depictions of a prophet were distributed.

docs.title: 4. Important Vocabulary (Arabic Grammar Points)
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning / Explanation Sighatul Amr Command form (imperative); "Qul" (Say) is in this form Nida Vocative particle; "Ya ayyuha" is used for calling/addressing Laa Particle of negation A'budu Fi'l (verb); "I worship" Maa Mausoola Relative pronoun meaning "what" or "that which" (not to be confused with maa naafiya) Maa Naafiya "Maa" used for negation (NOT the usage here) Sila The clause that follows the relative pronoun (maa mausoola) Jumlah Fi'liyya Verbal sentence; linked to time (past, present, or future) Jumlah Ismiyya Nominal sentence; NOT linked to time, therefore more powerful and permanent Mubtada Subject in a nominal sentence Khabar Predicate in a nominal sentence Khabar Muqaddam Predicate placed before the subject (fronted predicate) Mubtada Mu'akhkhar Subject placed after the predicate (delayed subject) Fi'l Mudari' Present/future tense verb; used for haal (present) and future Waw Particle of conjunction Hijrah Migration (from Makkah to Madinah) Khitab Address; who is being addressed Da'wah Inviting to Islam Aqidah Creed/belief system Batil Falsehood Haqq Truth Fathu Makkah The Conquest of Makkah

docs.title: A. Connection Between Surahs (Munasabah)
docs.text:
• Surah Al-Kawthar (108): Glad tiding that Muslims would receive the guardianship of the Ka'bah, with responsibilities (pray and sacrifice).
  • Surah Al-Kafirun (109): A warning placed between two glad tidings; it declares the impossibility of coexistence and hints at difficult times ahead.
  • Surah Al-Nasr (110): Glad tiding of victory and people entering Islam in multitudes.
  • The order: Promise (Al-Kawthar) --> Warning and preparation for hardship (Al-Kafirun) --> Victory and result (Al-Nasr). This is exactly how events unfolded historically.

docs.title: B. Grammatical Shift: Jumlah Fi'liyya vs. Jumlah Ismiyya
docs.text:
• Ayah 2 ("Laa a'budu maa ta'budun"): Uses jumlah fi'liyya (verbal sentence), which is restricted to time (present/future). Meaning: "I will not worship what you worship" -- a rejection of their deal.
  • Ayah 3 ("Wa laa antum 'aabiduna maa a'bud"): Shifts to jumlah ismiyya (nominal sentence). Allah did NOT say "wa laa ta'buduna" (which would be fi'liyya). Instead He used "antum 'aabidun" (mubtada + khabar).
  • Significance of the shift: Jumlah ismiyya is timeless and more powerful. It means: you NEVER worshipped what I worship, you do NOT now, and you NEVER will. It is a permanent divine declaration.
  • Ayahs 4-5: Repeat the declaration using jumlah ismiyya for emphasis on both sides (the Prophet will never worship what they worship, and they will never worship what he worships).

docs.title: C. Why the Kuffar Can Never Claim to Worship Allah
docs.text:
• The mushrikeen offered to worship Allah alongside their other gods.
  • Worshipping Allah means worshipping Allah ALONE (tawhid). Adding other deities negates worship of Allah entirely.
  • Analogy given: A person cannot claim two biological fathers. Claiming a second one is disrespectful to the actual father.

docs.title: D. Grammatical Analysis of the Final Ayah
docs.text:
• "Lakum deenukum": Lakum = khabar muqaddam (fronted predicate); deenukum = mubtada mu'akhkhar (delayed subject).
  • "Wa liya deen": Deen = mubtada mu'akhkhar; liya = khabar muqaddam.
  • This structure emphasizes exclusivity and finality.

docs.title: E. The Khitab (Address) is to the Society, Not Individuals
docs.text:
• The address is to Makkan society as a whole, not to each individual.
  • Some individuals did accept Islam, but the society as a whole did not accept through da'wah alone.
  • The society accepted Islam as a result of Fathu Makkah (conquest/force), not willingly through da'wah.
  • This reconciles the ayah's declaration with the historical fact that most Makkans eventually became Muslim.

docs.title: 6. Lessons and Practical Takeaways
docs.text:
1. No compromise on Tawhid: The fundamental creed of worshipping Allah alone is non-negotiable and cannot be diluted for social harmony or political convenience.

  2. Difficulty precedes victory: The surah's placement between Al-Kawthar and Al-Nasr teaches that hardship and struggle come before divine victory. Muslims should expect and prepare for challenging times.

  3. Hijrah was mandatory: When practicing the fundamentals of the deen became impossible due to torture, hijrah was obligatory upon all Muslims. This demonstrates the principle that Muslims must take action, not passively endure oppression.

  4. Correct understanding of "Lakum deenukum wa liya deen": This ayah is a declaration of complete separation, not a verse endorsing religious pluralism. Muslims should not use it to justify a "live and let live" attitude toward falsehood.

  5. Da'wah, not interfaith: The prophetic model is da'wah (calling to truth), not interfaith (mutual exchange of beliefs as equals). No prophet practiced interfaith.

  6. Standing firm has consequences: Rasulullah (sallallahu alayhi wa sallam) could have accepted the deal and potentially reduced the persecution of Muslims, but he chose truth over comfort. Followers of truth must be ready to face challenges.

  7. When is hijrah applicable today? Hijrah becomes obligatory when Muslims are tortured for practicing the FUNDAMENTALS of their deen (prayer, fasting, openly giving da'wah, criticizing false gods). If these fundamentals can be practiced freely, hijrah is not obligatory, even if not every aspect of Islamic law can be implemented.

docs.title: The Deal Proposed by the Quraysh
docs.text:
• The mushrikeen of Makkah came to the Prophet (sallallahu alayhi wa sallam) with a proposal: stop condemning their gods and goddesses, and they would be willing to add Allah to their pantheon.
  • Their logic: "We worship thousands of gods; adding one more is no problem."
  • This deal was categorically rejected, and this surah was revealed in response.

docs.title: The Hijrah
docs.text:
• After the declaration in this surah, Muslims were eventually commanded to migrate from Makkah to Madinah.
  • Hijrah was mandatory for all Muslims at that time.
  • It involved leaving behind homes, businesses, and sometimes even family members with no certainty of return.
  • Compared to modern-day refugees who flee with uncertainty about their future.
  • Reference made to the 2021 Afghanistan evacuation as a contemporary parallel of desperate migration.

docs.title: Fathu Makkah (Conquest of Makkah)
docs.text:
• Within eight years of hijrah, Muslims returned to Makkah victorious.
  • The Makkan society accepted Islam after the conquest, but this was not coexistence; it was the result of force/conquest.
  • Individuals later became true believers, but the initial acceptance was not through da'wah.

docs.title: The Story of Musa (Alayhis Salam) and the Rock (Q&A)
docs.text:
• A student asked about the hadith in which Musa (alayhis salam) placed his clothes on a rock, and the rock ran away, causing him to briefly appear uncovered before Bani Isra'il.
  • Explanation: Bani Isra'il bathed publicly, but Musa did not, leading them to suspect he had a physical defect. Allah planned this incident to briefly disprove their suspicion without compromising his dignity.
  • The rock running with clothes is a miracle (mu'jizah), no different from the splitting of the moon or the she-camel emerging from a boulder. Miracles should not be considered "strange."

docs.title: 8. Theological Discussion: Good and Evil (Husn wa Qubh)
docs.text:
A question was raised about how different schools understand the nature of good and evil:

School Position Mu'tazilah Good and evil are purely rational; human reason alone determines them Ash'ari Good and evil are purely textual; something becomes good or evil only when Allah declares it so Maturidi Middle position: good and evil are both rational AND textual; rationality confirms the text and text confirms rationality

docs.title: 9. Announcement: Burhan Academy / Islamic School Project
docs.text:
• A major Islamic school project was announced in Elgin, Illinois.
  • 18-acre campus with 8 buildings, from daycare to high school.
  • Formerly a prestigious 186-year-old private school; acquired as a foreclosure property for $3.7 million (valued at $35-40 million).
  • Closing date: August 29.
  • Two unique features of the planned curriculum:
  • Integrated Alim course: Students completing high school would automatically complete 3 years of an Alim course, needing only 3 more years to become a full Alim.
  • Islamization of secular sciences: Teaching biology, chemistry, physics, and other subjects from an Islamic paradigm rather than copy-pasting secular curricula.
  • Plans to register as a national accreditation agency with the state Department of Education so Muslim schools can be accredited by a Muslim body with an Islamic curriculum.
  • Website: burhanacademy.us
  • Promotional video available on YouTube (search: "Burhan Academy Elgin").
